functions.name: fluids.two_phase.Beggs_Brill
documentation:
Calculates the two-phase pressure drop according to the Beggs-Brill
correlation ([^footnote-1], [^footnote-2], [^footnote-3]).

# Parameters

m

: Mass flow rate of fluid, [kg/s]

x

: Mass quality of fluid, [-]

rhol

: Liquid density, [kg/m^3]

rhog

: Gas density, [kg/m^3]

mul

: Viscosity of liquid, [Pa\*s]

mug

: Viscosity of gas, [Pa\*s]

sigma

: Surface tension, [N/m]

P

: Pressure of fluid (used only if `acceleration=True`), [Pa]

D

: Diameter of pipe, [m]

angle

: The angle of the pipe with respect to the horizontal, [degrees]

roughness

: Roughness of pipe for use in calculating friction factor, [m]

L

: Length of pipe, [m]

g

: Acceleration due to gravity, [m/s^2]

acceleration

: Whether or not to include the original acceleration component, [-]

# Returns

dP

: Pressure drop of the two-phase flow, [Pa]

# Notes

The original acceleration formula is fairly primitive and normally
neglected. The model was developed assuming smooth pipe, so leaving
`roughness` to zero may be wise.

Note this is a "mechanistic" pressure drop model - the gravitational
pressure drop cannot be separated from the frictional pressure drop.

# Examples

```pycon
>>> Beggs_Brill(m=0.6, x=0.1, rhol=915., rhog=2.67, mul=180E-6, mug=14E-6,
... sigma=0.0487, P=1E7, D=0.05, angle=0, roughness=0.0, L=1.0)
686.9724506803469
```

# References

[^footnote-1]: Beggs, D.H., and J.P. Brill. "A Study of Two-Phase Flow in Inclined
    Pipes." Journal of Petroleum Technology 25, no. 05 (May 1, 1973):
    607-17. <https://doi.org/10.2118/4007-PA>.

[^footnote-2]: Brill, James P., and Howard Dale Beggs. Two-Phase Flow in Pipes,
    1994\.

[^footnote-3]: Shoham, Ovadia. Mechanistic Modeling of Gas-Liquid Two-Phase Flow in
    Pipes. Pap/Cdr edition. Richardson, TX: Society of Petroleum Engineers,
    2006\.

functions.name: fluids.two_phase.Friedel
documentation:
Calculates two-phase pressure drop with the Friedel correlation.

$$
\Delta P_{friction} = \Delta P_{lo} \phi_{lo}^2
$$

$$
\phi_{lo}^2 = E + \frac{3.24FH}{Fr^{0.0454} We^{0.035}}
$$

$$
H = \left(\frac{\rho_l}{\rho_g}\right)^{0.91}\left(\frac{\mu_g}{\mu_l} \right)^{0.19}\left(1 - \frac{\mu_g}{\mu_l}\right)^{0.7}
$$

$$
F = x^{0.78}(1 - x)^{0.224}
$$

$$
E = (1-x)^2 + x^2\left(\frac{\rho_l f_{d,go}}{\rho_g f_{d,lo}}\right)
$$

$$
Fr = \frac{G_{tp}^2}{gD\rho_H^2}
$$

$$
We = \frac{G_{tp}^2 D}{\sigma \rho_H}
$$

$$
\rho_H = \left(\frac{x}{\rho_g} + \frac{1-x}{\rho_l}\right)^{-1}
$$

# Parameters

m

: Mass flow rate of fluid, [kg/s]

x

: Quality of fluid, [-]

rhol

: Liquid density, [kg/m^3]

rhog

: Gas density, [kg/m^3]

mul

: Viscosity of liquid, [Pa\*s]

mug

: Viscosity of gas, [Pa\*s]

sigma

: Surface tension, [N/m]

D

: Diameter of pipe, [m]

roughness

: Roughness of pipe for use in calculating friction factor, [m]

L

: Length of pipe, [m]

# Returns

dP

: Pressure drop of the two-phase flow, [Pa]

# Notes

Applicable to vertical upflow and horizontal flow. Known to work poorly
when mul/mug > 1000. Gives mean errors on the order of 40%. Tested on data
with diameters as small as 4 mm.

The power of 0.0454 is given as 0.045 in [^footnote-2], [^footnote-3], [^footnote-4], and [^footnote-5]; [^footnote-6]
and [^footnote-2] give 0.0454 and [^footnote-2] also gives a similar correlation said to be
presented in [^footnote-1], so it is believed this 0.0454 was the original power.
[^footnote-6] also gives an expression for friction factor claimed to be presented
in [^footnote-1]; it is not used here.

# Examples

Example 4 in [^footnote-6]:

```pycon
>>> Friedel(m=0.6, x=0.1, rhol=915., rhog=2.67, mul=180E-6, mug=14E-6,
... sigma=0.0487, D=0.05, roughness=0.0, L=1.0)
738.6500525002241
```

# References

[^footnote-1]: Friedel, L. "Improved Friction Pressure Drop Correlations for
    Horizontal and Vertical Two-Phase Pipe Flow." , in: Proceedings,
    European Two Phase Flow Group Meeting, Ispra, Italy, 1979: 485-481.

[^footnote-2]: Whalley, P. B. Boiling, Condensation, and Gas-Liquid Flow. Oxford:
    Oxford University Press, 1987.

[^footnote-3]: Triplett, K. A., S. M. Ghiaasiaan, S. I. Abdel-Khalik, A. LeMouel,
    and B. N. McCord. "Gas-liquid Two-Phase Flow in Microchannels: Part II:
    Void Fraction and Pressure Drop.” International Journal of Multiphase
    Flow 25, no. 3 (April 1999): 395-410. doi:10.1016/S0301-9322(98)00055-X.

[^footnote-4]: Mekisso, Henock Mateos. "Comparison of Frictional Pressure Drop
    Correlations for Isothermal Two-Phase Horizontal Flow." Thesis, Oklahoma
    State University, 2013. <https://shareok.org/handle/11244/11109>.

[^footnote-5]: Thome, John R. "Engineering Data Book III." Wolverine Tube Inc
    (2004). <http://www.wlv.com/heat-transfer-databook/>

[^footnote-6]: Ghiaasiaan, S. Mostafa. Two-Phase Flow, Boiling, and Condensation:
    In Conventional and Miniature Systems. Cambridge University Press, 2007.

functions.name: fluids.two_phase.Gronnerud
documentation:
Calculates two-phase pressure drop with the Gronnerud correlation as
presented in [^footnote-2], [^footnote-3], and [^footnote-4].

$$
\Delta P_{friction} = \Delta P_{gd} \phi_{lo}^2
$$

$$
\phi_{gd} = 1 + \left(\frac{dP}{dL}\right)_{Fr}\left[ \frac{\frac{\rho_l}{\rho_g}}{\left(\frac{\mu_l}{\mu_g}\right)^{0.25}} -1\right]
$$

$$
\left(\frac{dP}{dL}\right)_{Fr} = f_{Fr}\left[x+4(x^{1.8}-x^{10} f_{Fr}^{0.5})\right]
$$

$$
f_{Fr} = Fr_l^{0.3} + 0.0055\left(\ln \frac{1}{Fr_l}\right)^2
$$

$$
Fr_l = \frac{G_{tp}^2}{gD\rho_l^2}
$$

# Parameters

m

: Mass flow rate of fluid, [kg/s]

x

: Quality of fluid, [-]

rhol

: Liquid density, [kg/m^3]

rhog

: Gas density, [kg/m^3]

mul

: Viscosity of liquid, [Pa\*s]

mug

: Viscosity of gas, [Pa\*s]

D

: Diameter of pipe, [m]

roughness

: Roughness of pipe for use in calculating friction factor, [m]

L

: Length of pipe, [m]

# Returns

dP

: Pressure drop of the two-phase flow, [Pa]

# Notes

Developed for evaporators. Applicable from 0 < x < 1.

In the model, if `Fr_l` is more than 1, `f_Fr` is set to 1.

# Examples

```pycon
>>> Gronnerud(m=0.6, x=0.1, rhol=915., rhog=2.67, mul=180E-6, mug=14E-6,
... D=0.05, roughness=0.0, L=1.0)
384.12541144474085
```

# References

[^footnote-1]: Gronnerud, R. "Investigation of Liquid Hold-Up, Flow Resistance and
    Heat Transfer in Circulation Type Evaporators. 4. Two-Phase Flow
    Resistance in Boiling Refrigerants." Proc. Freudenstadt Meet., IIR/C.
    R. Réun. Freudenstadt, IIF. 1972-1: 127-138. 1972.

[^footnote-2]: ASHRAE Handbook: Fundamentals. American Society of Heating,
    Refrigerating and Air-Conditioning Engineers, Incorporated, 2013.

[^footnote-3]: Mekisso, Henock Mateos. "Comparison of Frictional Pressure Drop
    Correlations for Isothermal Two-Phase Horizontal Flow." Thesis, Oklahoma
    State University, 2013. <https://shareok.org/handle/11244/11109>.

[^footnote-4]: Thome, John R. "Engineering Data Book III." Wolverine Tube Inc
    (2004). <http://www.wlv.com/heat-transfer-databook/>

functions.name: fluids.two_phase.Chisholm
documentation:
Calculates two-phase pressure drop with the Chisholm (1973) correlation
from [^footnote-1], also in [^footnote-2] and [^footnote-3].

$$
\frac{\Delta P_{tp}}{\Delta P_{lo}} = \phi_{ch}^2
$$

$$
\phi_{ch}^2 = 1 + (\Gamma^2 -1)\left\{B x^{(2-n)/2} (1-x)^{(2-n)/2} + x^{2-n} \right\}
$$

$$
\Gamma ^2 = \frac{\left(\frac{\Delta P}{L}\right)_{go}}{\left(\frac{ \Delta P}{L}\right)_{lo}}
$$

For Gamma < 9.5:

$$
B = \frac{55}{G_{tp}^{0.5}} \text{ for } G_{tp} > 1900
$$

$$
B = \frac{2400}{G_{tp}} \text{ for } 500 < G_{tp} < 1900
$$

$$
B = 4.8 \text{ for } G_{tp} < 500
$$

For 9.5 < Gamma < 28:

$$
B = \frac{520}{\Gamma G_{tp}^{0.5}} \text{ for } G_{tp} < 600
$$

$$
B = \frac{21}{\Gamma} \text{ for } G_{tp} > 600
$$

For Gamma > 28:

$$
B = \frac{15000}{\Gamma^2 G_{tp}^{0.5}}
$$

If `rough_correction` is True, the following correction to B is applied:

$$
\frac{B_{rough}}{B_{smooth}} = \left[0.5\left\{1+ \left(\frac{\mu_g} {\mu_l}\right)^2 + 10^{-600\epsilon/D}\right\}\right]^{\frac{0.25-n} {0.25}}
$$

$$
n = \frac{\ln \frac{f_{d,lo}}{f_{d,go}}}{\ln \frac{Re_{go}}{Re_{lo}}}
$$

# Parameters

m

: Mass flow rate of fluid, [kg/s]

x

: Quality of fluid, [-]

rhol

: Liquid density, [kg/m^3]

rhog

: Gas density, [kg/m^3]

mul

: Viscosity of liquid, [Pa\*s]

mug

: Viscosity of gas, [Pa\*s]

D

: Diameter of pipe, [m]

roughness

: Roughness of pipe for use in calculating friction factor, [m]

L

: Length of pipe, [m]

rough_correction

: Whether or not to use the roughness correction proposed in the 1968
  version of the correlation

# Returns

dP

: Pressure drop of the two-phase flow, [Pa]

# Notes

Applicable for 0 < x < 1. n = 0.25, the exponent in the Blassius equation.
Originally developed for smooth pipes, a roughness correction is included
as well from the Chisholm's 1968 work [^footnote-4]. Neither [^footnote-2] nor [^footnote-3] have any
mention of the correction however.

# Examples

```pycon
>>> Chisholm(m=0.6, x=0.1, rhol=915., rhog=2.67, mul=180E-6,
... mug=14E-6, D=0.05, roughness=0.0, L=1.0)
1084.148992292
```

# References

[^footnote-1]: Chisholm, D. "Pressure Gradients due to Friction during the Flow of
    Evaporating Two-Phase Mixtures in Smooth Tubes and Channels."
    International Journal of Heat and Mass Transfer 16, no. 2 (February
    1973): 347-58. doi:10.1016/0017-9310(73)90063-X.

[^footnote-2]: Mekisso, Henock Mateos. "Comparison of Frictional Pressure Drop
    Correlations for Isothermal Two-Phase Horizontal Flow." Thesis, Oklahoma
    State University, 2013. <https://shareok.org/handle/11244/11109>.

[^footnote-3]: Thome, John R. "Engineering Data Book III." Wolverine Tube Inc
    (2004). <http://www.wlv.com/heat-transfer-databook/>

[^footnote-4]: Chisholm, D. "Research Note: Influence of Pipe Surface Roughness on
    Friction Pressure Gradient during Two-Phase Flow." Journal of Mechanical
    Engineering Science 20, no. 6 (December 1, 1978): 353-354.
    doi:10.1243/JMES_JOUR_1978_020_061_02.

functions.name: fluids.two_phase.Baroczy_Chisholm
documentation:
Calculates two-phase pressure drop with the Baroczy (1966) model.
It was presented in graphical form originally; Chisholm (1973) made the
correlation non-graphical. The model is also shown in [^footnote-3].

$$
\frac{\Delta P_{tp}}{\Delta P_{lo}} = \phi_{ch}^2
$$

$$
\phi_{ch}^2 = 1 + (\Gamma^2 -1)\left\{B x^{(2-n)/2} (1-x)^{(2-n)/2} + x^{2-n} \right\}
$$

$$
\Gamma ^2 = \frac{\left(\frac{\Delta P}{L}\right)_{go}}{\left(\frac{ \Delta P}{L}\right)_{lo}}
$$

For Gamma < 9.5:

$$
B = \frac{55}{G_{tp}^{0.5}}
$$

For 9.5 < Gamma < 28:

$$
B = \frac{520}{\Gamma G_{tp}^{0.5}}
$$

For Gamma > 28:

$$
B = \frac{15000}{\Gamma^2 G_{tp}^{0.5}}
$$

# Parameters

m

: Mass flow rate of fluid, [kg/s]

x

: Quality of fluid, [-]

rhol

: Liquid density, [kg/m^3]

rhog

: Gas density, [kg/m^3]

mul

: Viscosity of liquid, [Pa\*s]

mug

: Viscosity of gas, [Pa\*s]

D

: Diameter of pipe, [m]

roughness

: Roughness of pipe for use in calculating friction factor, [m]

L

: Length of pipe, [m]

# Returns

dP

: Pressure drop of the two-phase flow, [Pa]

# Notes

Applicable for 0 < x < 1. n = 0.25, the exponent in the Blassius equation.
The `Chisholm_1973` function should be used in preference to this.

# Examples

```pycon
>>> Baroczy_Chisholm(m=0.6, x=0.1, rhol=915., rhog=2.67, mul=180E-6,
... mug=14E-6, D=0.05, roughness=0.0, L=1.0)
1084.148992292
```

# References

[^footnote-1]: Baroczy, C. J. "A systematic correlation for two-phase pressure
    drop." In Chem. Eng. Progr., Symp. Ser., 62: No. 64, 232-49 (1966).

[^footnote-2]: Chisholm, D. "Pressure Gradients due to Friction during the Flow of
    Evaporating Two-Phase Mixtures in Smooth Tubes and Channels."
    International Journal of Heat and Mass Transfer 16, no. 2 (February
    1973): 347-58. doi:10.1016/0017-9310(73)90063-X.

[^footnote-3]: Mekisso, Henock Mateos. "Comparison of Frictional Pressure Drop
    Correlations for Isothermal Two-Phase Horizontal Flow." Thesis, Oklahoma
    State University, 2013. <https://shareok.org/handle/11244/11109>.

functions.name: fluids.two_phase.Muller_Steinhagen_Heck
documentation:
Calculates two-phase pressure drop with the Muller-Steinhagen and Heck
(1986) correlation from [^footnote-1], also in [^footnote-2] and [^footnote-3].

$$
\Delta P_{tp} = G_{MSH}(1-x)^{1/3} + \Delta P_{go}x^3
$$

$$
G_{MSH} = \Delta P_{lo} + 2\left[\Delta P_{go} - \Delta P_{lo}\right]x
$$

# Parameters

m

: Mass flow rate of fluid, [kg/s]

x

: Quality of fluid, [-]

rhol

: Liquid density, [kg/m^3]

rhog

: Gas density, [kg/m^3]

mul

: Viscosity of liquid, [Pa\*s]

mug

: Viscosity of gas, [Pa\*s]

D

: Diameter of pipe, [m]

roughness

: Roughness of pipe for use in calculating friction factor, [m]

L

: Length of pipe, [m]

# Returns

dP

: Pressure drop of the two-phase flow, [Pa]

# Notes

Applicable for 0 < x < 1. Developed to be easily integrated. The
contribution of each term to the overall pressure drop can be
understood in this model.

# Examples

```pycon
>>> Muller_Steinhagen_Heck(m=0.6, x=0.1, rhol=915., rhog=2.67, mul=180E-6,
... mug=14E-6, D=0.05, roughness=0.0, L=1.0)
793.446545743
```

# References

[^footnote-1]: Müller-Steinhagen, H, and K Heck. "A Simple Friction Pressure Drop
    Correlation for Two-Phase Flow in Pipes." Chemical Engineering and
    Processing: Process Intensification 20, no. 6 (November 1, 1986):
    297-308. doi:10.1016/0255-2701(86)80008-3.

[^footnote-2]: Mekisso, Henock Mateos. "Comparison of Frictional Pressure Drop
    Correlations for Isothermal Two-Phase Horizontal Flow." Thesis, Oklahoma
    State University, 2013. <https://shareok.org/handle/11244/11109>.

[^footnote-3]: Thome, John R. "Engineering Data Book III." Wolverine Tube Inc
    (2004). <http://www.wlv.com/heat-transfer-databook/>

functions.name: fluids.two_phase.Lombardi_Pedrocchi
documentation:
Calculates two-phase pressure drop with the Lombardi-Pedrocchi (1972)
correlation from [^footnote-1] as shown in [^footnote-2] and [^footnote-3].

$$
\Delta P_{tp} = \frac{0.83 G_{tp}^{1.4} \sigma^{0.4} L}{D^{1.2} \rho_{h}^{0.866}}
$$

# Parameters

m

: Mass flow rate of fluid, [kg/s]

x

: Quality of fluid, [-]

rhol

: Liquid density, [kg/m^3]

rhog

: Gas density, [kg/m^3]

sigma

: Surface tension, [N/m]

D

: Diameter of pipe, [m]

L

: Length of pipe, [m]

# Returns

dP

: Pressure drop of the two-phase flow, [Pa]

# Notes

This is a purely empirical method. [^footnote-3] presents a review of this and other
correlations. It did not perform best, but there were also correlations
worse than it.

# Examples

```pycon
>>> Lombardi_Pedrocchi(m=0.6, x=0.1, rhol=915., rhog=2.67, sigma=0.045,
... D=0.05, L=1.0)
1567.328374498781
```

# References

[^footnote-1]: Lombardi, C., and E. Pedrocchi. "Pressure Drop Correlation in Two-
    Phase Flow." Energ. Nucl. (Milan) 19: No. 2, 91-99, January 1, 1972.

[^footnote-2]: Mekisso, Henock Mateos. "Comparison of Frictional Pressure Drop
    Correlations for Isothermal Two-Phase Horizontal Flow." Thesis, Oklahoma
    State University, 2013. <https://shareok.org/handle/11244/11109>.

[^footnote-3]: Turgut, Oğuz Emrah, Mustafa Turhan Çoban, and Mustafa Asker.
    "Comparison of Flow Boiling Pressure Drop Correlations for Smooth
    Macrotubes." Heat Transfer Engineering 37, no. 6 (April 12, 2016):
    487-506. doi:10.1080/01457632.2015.1060733.

functions.name: fluids.two_phase.Theissing
documentation:
Calculates two-phase pressure drop with the Theissing (1980)
correlation as shown in [^footnote-2] and [^footnote-3].

$$
\Delta P_{{tp}} = \left[ {\Delta P_{{lo}}^{{1/{n\epsilon}}} \left({1 - x} \right)^{{1/\epsilon}} + \Delta P_{{go}}^{{1/ {(n\epsilon)}}} x^{{1/\epsilon}}} \right]^{n\epsilon}
$$

$$
\epsilon = 3 - 2\left({\frac{{2\sqrt {{{\rho_{{l}}}/ {\rho_{{g}}}}}}}{{1 + {{\rho_{{l}}}/{\rho_{{g}}}}}}} \right)^{{{0.7}/n}}
$$

$$
n = \frac{{n_1 + n_2 \left({{{\Delta P_{{g}}}/{\Delta P_{{l}}}}} \right)^{0.1}}}{{1 + \left({{{\Delta P_{{g}}} / {\Delta P_{{l}}}}} \right)^{0.1}}}
$$

$$
n_1 = \frac{{\ln \left({{{\Delta P_{{l}}}/ {\Delta P_{{lo}}}}} \right)}}{{\ln \left({1 - x} \right)}}
$$

$$
n_2 = \frac{\ln \left({\Delta P_{{g}} / \Delta P_{{go}}} \right)}{{\ln x}}
$$

# Parameters

m

: Mass flow rate of fluid, [kg/s]

x

: Quality of fluid, [-]

rhol

: Liquid density, [kg/m^3]

rhog

: Gas density, [kg/m^3]

mul

: Viscosity of liquid, [Pa\*s]

mug

: Viscosity of gas, [Pa\*s]

D

: Diameter of pipe, [m]

roughness

: Roughness of pipe for use in calculating friction factor, [m]

L

: Length of pipe, [m]

# Returns

dP

: Pressure drop of the two-phase flow, [Pa]

# Notes

Applicable for 0 < x < 1. Notable, as it can be used for two-phase liquid-
liquid flow as well as liquid-gas flow.

# Examples

```pycon
>>> Theissing(m=0.6, x=.1, rhol=915., rhog=2.67, mul=180E-6, mug=14E-6,
... D=0.05, roughness=0.0, L=1.0)
497.6156370699538
```

# References

[^footnote-1]: Theissing, Peter. "Eine Allgemeingültige Methode Zur Berechnung Des
    Reibungsdruckverlustes Der Mehrphasenströmung (A Generally Valid Method
    for Calculating Frictional Pressure Drop on Multiphase Flow)." Chemie
    Ingenieur Technik 52, no. 4 (January 1, 1980): 344-345.
    doi:10.1002/cite.330520414.

[^footnote-2]: Mekisso, Henock Mateos. "Comparison of Frictional Pressure Drop
    Correlations for Isothermal Two-Phase Horizontal Flow." Thesis, Oklahoma
    State University, 2013. <https://shareok.org/handle/11244/11109>.

[^footnote-3]: Greco, A., and G. P. Vanoli. "Experimental Two-Phase Pressure
    Gradients during Evaporation of Pure and Mixed Refrigerants in a Smooth
    Horizontal Tube. Comparison with Correlations." Heat and Mass Transfer
    42, no. 8 (April 6, 2006): 709-725. doi:10.1007/s00231-005-0020-7.

functions.name: fluids.two_phase.Jung_Radermacher
documentation:
Calculates two-phase pressure drop with the Jung-Radermacher (1989)
correlation, also shown in [^footnote-2] and [^footnote-3].

$$
\frac{\Delta P_{tp}}{\Delta P_{lo}} = \phi_{tp}^2
$$

$$
\phi_{tp}^2 = 12.82X_{tt}^{-1.47}(1-x)^{1.8}
$$

# Parameters

m

: Mass flow rate of fluid, [kg/s]

x

: Quality of fluid, [-]

rhol

: Liquid density, [kg/m^3]

rhog

: Gas density, [kg/m^3]

mul

: Viscosity of liquid, [Pa\*s]

mug

: Viscosity of gas, [Pa\*s]

D

: Diameter of pipe, [m]

roughness

: Roughness of pipe for use in calculating friction factor, [m]

L

: Length of pipe, [m]

# Returns

dP

: Pressure drop of the two-phase flow, [Pa]

# Notes

Applicable for 0 < x < 1. Developed for the annular flow regime in
turbulent-turbulent flow.

# Examples

```pycon
>>> Jung_Radermacher(m=0.6, x=0.1, rhol=915., rhog=2.67, mul=180E-6,
... mug=14E-6, D=0.05, roughness=0.0, L=1.0)
552.0686123725568
```

# References

[^footnote-1]: Jung, D. S., and R. Radermacher. "Prediction of Pressure Drop during
    Horizontal Annular Flow Boiling of Pure and Mixed Refrigerants."
    International Journal of Heat and Mass Transfer 32, no. 12 (December 1,
    1989): 2435-46. doi:10.1016/0017-9310(89)90203-2.

[^footnote-2]: Kim, Sung-Min, and Issam Mudawar. "Universal Approach to Predicting
    Two-Phase Frictional Pressure Drop for Adiabatic and Condensing Mini/
    Micro-Channel Flows." International Journal of Heat and Mass Transfer
    55, no. 11-12 (May 2012): 3246-61.
    doi:10.1016/j.ijheatmasstransfer.2012.02.047.

[^footnote-3]: Filip, Alina, Florin Băltăreţu, and Radu-Mircea Damian. "Comparison
    of Two-Phase Pressure Drop Models for Condensing Flows in Horizontal
    Tubes." Mathematical Modelling in Civil Engineering 10, no. 4 (2015):
    19-27. doi:10.2478/mmce-2014-0019.

functions.name: fluids.two_phase.Tran
documentation:
Calculates two-phase pressure drop with the Tran (2000) correlation,
also shown in [^footnote-2] and [^footnote-3].

$$
\Delta P = dP_{lo} \phi_{lo}^2
$$

$$
\phi_{lo}^2 = 1 + (4.3\Gamma^2-1)[\text{Co} \cdot x^{0.875} (1-x)^{0.875}+x^{1.75}]
$$

$$
\Gamma ^2 = \frac{\left(\frac{\Delta P}{L}\right)_{go}}{\left(\frac {\Delta P}{L}\right)_{lo}}
$$

# Parameters

m

: Mass flow rate of fluid, [kg/s]

x

: Quality of fluid, [-]

rhol

: Liquid density, [kg/m^3]

rhog

: Gas density, [kg/m^3]

mul

: Viscosity of liquid, [Pa\*s]

mug

: Viscosity of gas, [Pa\*s]

sigma

: Surface tension, [N/m]

D

: Diameter of pipe, [m]

roughness

: Roughness of pipe for use in calculating friction factor, [m]

L

: Length of pipe, [m]

# Returns

dP

: Pressure drop of the two-phase flow, [Pa]

# Notes

Developed for boiling refrigerants in channels with hydraulic diameters of
2.4 mm to 2.92 mm.

# Examples

```pycon
>>> Tran(m=0.6, x=0.1, rhol=915., rhog=2.67, mul=180E-6, mug=14E-6,
... sigma=0.0487, D=0.05, roughness=0.0, L=1.0)
423.2563312951
```

# References

[^footnote-1]: Tran, T. N, M. -C Chyu, M. W Wambsganss, and D. M France. "Two-Phase
    Pressure Drop of Refrigerants during Flow Boiling in Small Channels: An
    Experimental Investigation and Correlation Development." International
    Journal of Multiphase Flow 26, no. 11 (November 1, 2000): 1739-54.
    doi:10.1016/S0301-9322(99)00119-6.

[^footnote-2]: Kim, Sung-Min, and Issam Mudawar. "Universal Approach to Predicting
    Two-Phase Frictional Pressure Drop for Adiabatic and Condensing Mini/
    Micro-Channel Flows." International Journal of Heat and Mass Transfer
    55, no. 11-12 (May 2012): 3246-61.
    doi:10.1016/j.ijheatmasstransfer.2012.02.047.

[^footnote-3]: Choi, Kwang-Il, A. S. Pamitran, Chun-Young Oh, and Jong-Taek Oh.
    "Two-Phase Pressure Drop of R-410A in Horizontal Smooth Minichannels."
    International Journal of Refrigeration 31, no. 1 (January 2008): 119-29.
    doi:10.1016/j.ijrefrig.2007.06.006.

functions.name: fluids.two_phase.Chen_Friedel
documentation:
Calculates two-phase pressure drop with the Chen modification of the
Friedel correlation, as given in [^footnote-1] and also shown in [^footnote-2] and [^footnote-3].

$$
\Delta P = \Delta P_{Friedel}\Omega
$$

For Bo < 2.5:

$$
\Omega = \frac{0.0333Re_{lo}^{0.45}}{Re_g^{0.09}(1 + 0.4\exp(-Bo))}
$$

For Bo >= 2.5:

$$
\Omega = \frac{We^{0.2}}{2.5 + 0.06Bo}
$$

# Parameters

m

: Mass flow rate of fluid, [kg/s]

x

: Quality of fluid, [-]

rhol

: Liquid density, [kg/m^3]

rhog

: Gas density, [kg/m^3]

mul

: Viscosity of liquid, [Pa\*s]

mug

: Viscosity of gas, [Pa\*s]

sigma

: Surface tension, [N/m]

D

: Diameter of pipe, [m]

roughness

: Roughness of pipe for use in calculating friction factor, [m]

L

: Length of pipe, [m]

# Returns

dP

: Pressure drop of the two-phase flow, [Pa]

# Notes

Applicable ONLY to mini/microchannels; yields drastically too low
pressure drops for larger channels. For more details, see the `Friedel`
correlation.

It is not explicitly stated in [^footnote-1] how to calculate the liquid mixture
density for use in calculation of Weber number; the homogeneous model is
assumed as it is used in the Friedel model.

The bond number used here is 1/4 the normal value, i.e.:

$$
Bo = \frac{g(\rho_l-\rho_g)D^2}{4\sigma}
$$

# Examples

```pycon
>>> Chen_Friedel(m=.0005, x=0.9, rhol=950., rhog=1.4, mul=1E-3, mug=1E-5,
... sigma=0.02, D=0.003, roughness=0.0, L=1.0)
6249.247540
```

# References

[^footnote-1]: Chen, Ing Youn, Kai-Shing Yang, Yu-Juei Chang, and Chi-Chung Wang.
    "Two-Phase Pressure Drop of Air-water and R-410A in Small Horizontal
    Tubes." International Journal of Multiphase Flow 27, no. 7 (July 2001):
    1293-99. doi:10.1016/S0301-9322(01)00004-0.

[^footnote-2]: Kim, Sung-Min, and Issam Mudawar. "Universal Approach to Predicting
    Two-Phase Frictional Pressure Drop for Adiabatic and Condensing Mini/
    Micro-Channel Flows." International Journal of Heat and Mass Transfer
    55, no. 11-12 (May 2012): 3246-61.
    doi:10.1016/j.ijheatmasstransfer.2012.02.047.

[^footnote-3]: Choi, Kwang-Il, A. S. Pamitran, Chun-Young Oh, and Jong-Taek Oh.
    "Two-Phase Pressure Drop of R-410A in Horizontal Smooth Minichannels."
    International Journal of Refrigeration 31, no. 1 (January 2008): 119-29.
    doi:10.1016/j.ijrefrig.2007.06.006.

functions.name: fluids.two_phase.Zhang_Webb
documentation:
Calculates two-phase pressure drop with the Zhang-Webb (2001)
correlation as shown in [^footnote-1] and also given in [^footnote-2].

$$
\phi_{lo}^2 = (1-x)^2 + 2.87x^2\left(\frac{P}{P_c}\right)^{-1} + 1.68x^{0.8}(1-x)^{0.25}\left(\frac{P}{P_c}\right)^{-1.64}
$$

# Parameters

m

: Mass flow rate of fluid, [kg/s]

x

: Quality of fluid, [-]

rhol

: Liquid density, [kg/m^3]

mul

: Viscosity of liquid, [Pa\*s]

P

: Pressure of fluid, [Pa]

Pc

: Critical pressure of fluid, [Pa]

D

: Diameter of pipe, [m]

roughness

: Roughness of pipe for use in calculating friction factor, [m]

L

: Length of pipe, [m]

# Returns

dP

: Pressure drop of the two-phase flow, [Pa]

# Notes

Applicable for 0 < x < 1. Corresponding-states method developed with
R-134A, R-22 and R-404A in tubes of hydraulic diameters of 2.13 mm,
6.25 mm, and 3.25 mm. For the author's 119 data points, the mean deviation
was 11.5%. Recommended for reduced pressures larger than 0.2 and tubes of
diameter 1-7 mm.

Does not require known properties for the gas phase.

# Examples

```pycon
>>> Zhang_Webb(m=0.6, x=0.1, rhol=915., mul=180E-6, P=2E5, Pc=4055000,
... D=0.05, roughness=0.0, L=1.0)
712.0999804205617
```

# References

[^footnote-1]: Zhang, Ming, and Ralph L. Webb. "Correlation of Two-Phase Friction
    for Refrigerants in Small-Diameter Tubes." Experimental Thermal and
    Fluid Science 25, no. 3-4 (October 2001): 131-39.
    doi:10.1016/S0894-1777(01)00066-8.

[^footnote-2]: Choi, Kwang-Il, A. S. Pamitran, Chun-Young Oh, and Jong-Taek Oh.
    "Two-Phase Pressure Drop of R-410A in Horizontal Smooth Minichannels."
    International Journal of Refrigeration 31, no. 1 (January 2008): 119-29.
    doi:10.1016/j.ijrefrig.2007.06.006.

functions.name: fluids.two_phase.Bankoff
documentation:
Calculates two-phase pressure drop with the Bankoff (1960) correlation,
as shown in [^footnote-2], [^footnote-3], and [^footnote-4].

$$
\Delta P_{tp} = \phi_{l}^{7/4} \Delta P_{l}
$$

$$
\phi_l = \frac{1}{1-x}\left[1 - \gamma\left(1 - \frac{\rho_g}{\rho_l} \right)\right]^{3/7}\left[1 + x\left(\frac{\rho_l}{\rho_g} - 1\right) \right]
$$

$$
\gamma = \frac{0.71 + 2.35\left(\frac{\rho_g}{\rho_l}\right)} {1 + \frac{1-x}{x} \cdot \frac{\rho_g}{\rho_l}}
$$

# Parameters

m

: Mass flow rate of fluid, [kg/s]

x

: Quality of fluid, [-]

rhol

: Liquid density, [kg/m^3]

rhog

: Gas density, [kg/m^3]

mul

: Viscosity of liquid, [Pa\*s]

mug

: Viscosity of gas, [Pa\*s]

D

: Diameter of pipe, [m]

roughness

: Roughness of pipe for use in calculating friction factor, [m]

L

: Length of pipe, [m]

# Returns

dP

: Pressure drop of the two-phase flow, [Pa]

# Notes

This correlation is not actually shown in [^footnote-1]. Its origin is unknown.
The author recommends against using this.

# Examples

```pycon
>>> Bankoff(m=0.6, x=0.1, rhol=915., rhog=2.67, mul=180E-6, mug=14E-6,
... D=0.05, roughness=0.0, L=1.0)
4746.0594424533965
```

# References

[^footnote-1]: Bankoff, S. G. "A Variable Density Single-Fluid Model for Two-Phase
    Flow With Particular Reference to Steam-Water Flow." Journal of Heat
    Transfer 82, no. 4 (November 1, 1960): 265-72. doi:10.1115/1.3679930.

[^footnote-2]: Thome, John R. "Engineering Data Book III." Wolverine Tube Inc
    (2004). <http://www.wlv.com/heat-transfer-databook/>

[^footnote-3]: Moreno Quibén, Jesús. "Experimental and Analytical Study of Two-
    Phase Pressure Drops during Evaporation in Horizontal Tubes," 2005.
    doi:10.5075/epfl-thesis-3337.

[^footnote-4]: Mekisso, Henock Mateos. "Comparison of Frictional Pressure Drop
    Correlations for Isothermal Two-Phase Horizontal Flow." Thesis, Oklahoma
    State University, 2013. <https://shareok.org/handle/11244/11109>.

functions.name: fluids.two_phase.Xu_Fang
documentation:
Calculates two-phase pressure drop with the Xu and Fang (2013)
correlation. Developed after a comprehensive review of available
correlations, likely meaning it is quite accurate.

$$
\Delta P = \Delta P_{lo} \phi_{lo}^2
$$

$$
\phi_{lo}^2 = Y^2x^3 + (1-x^{2.59})^{0.632}[1 + 2x^{1.17}(Y^2-1) + 0.00775x^{-0.475} Fr_{tp}^{0.535} We_{tp}^{0.188}]
$$

$$
Y^2 = \frac{\Delta P_{go}}{\Delta P_{lo}}
$$

$$
Fr_{tp} = \frac{G_{tp}^2}{gD\rho_{tp}^2}
$$

$$
We_{tp} = \frac{G_{tp}^2 D}{\sigma \rho_{tp}}
$$

$$
\frac{1}{\rho_{tp}} = \frac{1-x}{\rho_l} + \frac{x}{\rho_g}
$$

# Parameters

m

: Mass flow rate of fluid, [kg/s]

x

: Quality of fluid, [-]

rhol

: Liquid density, [kg/m^3]

rhog

: Gas density, [kg/m^3]

mul

: Viscosity of liquid, [Pa\*s]

mug

: Viscosity of gas, [Pa\*s]

sigma

: Surface tension, [N/m]

D

: Diameter of pipe, [m]

roughness

: Roughness of pipe for use in calculating friction factor, [m]

L

: Length of pipe, [m]

# Returns

dP

: Pressure drop of the two-phase flow, [Pa]

# Notes

# Examples

```pycon
>>> Xu_Fang(m=0.6, x=0.1, rhol=915., rhog=2.67, mul=180E-6, mug=14E-6,
... sigma=0.0487, D=0.05, roughness=0.0, L=1.0)
604.059563211
```

# References

[^footnote-1]: Xu, Yu, and Xiande Fang. "A New Correlation of Two-Phase Frictional
    Pressure Drop for Condensing Flow in Pipes." Nuclear Engineering and
    Design 263 (October 2013): 87-96. doi:10.1016/j.nucengdes.2013.04.017.

functions.name: fluids.two_phase.Yu_France
documentation:
Calculates two-phase pressure drop with the Yu, France, Wambsganss,
and Hull (2002) correlation given in [^footnote-1] and reviewed in [^footnote-2] and [^footnote-3].

$$
\Delta P = \Delta P_{l} \phi_{l}^2
$$

$$
\phi_l^2 = X^{-1.9}
$$

$$
X = 18.65\left(\frac{\rho_g}{\rho_l}\right)^{0.5}\left(\frac{1-x}{x} \right)\frac{Re_{g}^{0.1}}{Re_l^{0.5}}
$$

# Parameters

m

: Mass flow rate of fluid, [kg/s]

x

: Quality of fluid, [-]

rhol

: Liquid density, [kg/m^3]

rhog

: Gas density, [kg/m^3]

mul

: Viscosity of liquid, [Pa\*s]

mug

: Viscosity of gas, [Pa\*s]

D

: Diameter of pipe, [m]

roughness

: Roughness of pipe for use in calculating friction factor, [m]

L

: Length of pipe, [m]

# Returns

dP

: Pressure drop of the two-phase flow, [Pa]

# Notes

# Examples

```pycon
>>> Yu_France(m=0.6, x=.1, rhol=915., rhog=2.67, mul=180E-6, mug=14E-6,
... D=0.05, roughness=0.0, L=1.0)
1146.9833225539571
```

# References

[^footnote-1]: Yu, W., D. M. France, M. W. Wambsganss, and J. R. Hull. "Two-Phase
    Pressure Drop, Boiling Heat Transfer, and Critical Heat Flux to Water in
    a Small-Diameter Horizontal Tube." International Journal of Multiphase
    Flow 28, no. 6 (June 2002): 927-41. doi:10.1016/S0301-9322(02)00019-8.

[^footnote-2]: Kim, Sung-Min, and Issam Mudawar. "Universal Approach to Predicting
    Two-Phase Frictional Pressure Drop for Adiabatic and Condensing Mini/
    Micro-Channel Flows." International Journal of Heat and Mass Transfer
    55, no. 11-12 (May 2012): 3246-61.
    doi:10.1016/j.ijheatmasstransfer.2012.02.047.

[^footnote-3]: Xu, Yu, Xiande Fang, Xianghui Su, Zhanru Zhou, and Weiwei Chen.
    "Evaluation of Frictional Pressure Drop Correlations for Two-Phase Flow
    in Pipes." Nuclear Engineering and Design, SI : CFD4NRS-3, 253 (December
    2012): 86-97. doi:10.1016/j.nucengdes.2012.08.007.

functions.name: fluids.two_phase.Wang_Chiang_Lu
documentation:
Calculates two-phase pressure drop with the Wang, Chiang, and Lu (1997)
correlation given in [^footnote-1] and reviewed in [^footnote-2] and [^footnote-3].

$$
\Delta P = \Delta P_{g} \phi_g^2
$$

$$
\phi_g^2 = 1 + 9.397X^{0.62} + 0.564X^{2.45} \text{ for } G >= 200 kg/m^2/s
$$

$$
\phi_g^2 = 1 + CX + X^2 \text{ for lower mass fluxes}
$$

$$
C = 0.000004566X^{0.128}Re_{lo}^{0.938}\left(\frac{\rho_l}{\rho_g} \right)^{-2.15}\left(\frac{\mu_l}{\mu_g}\right)^{5.1}
$$

$$
X^2 = \frac{\Delta P_l}{\Delta P_g}
$$

# Parameters

m

: Mass flow rate of fluid, [kg/s]

x

: Quality of fluid, [-]

rhol

: Liquid density, [kg/m^3]

rhog

: Gas density, [kg/m^3]

mul

: Viscosity of liquid, [Pa\*s]

mug

: Viscosity of gas, [Pa\*s]

D

: Diameter of pipe, [m]

roughness

: Roughness of pipe for use in calculating friction factor, [m]

L

: Length of pipe, [m]

# Returns

dP

: Pressure drop of the two-phase flow, [Pa]

# Notes

# Examples

```pycon
>>> Wang_Chiang_Lu(m=0.6, x=0.1, rhol=915., rhog=2.67, mul=180E-6,
... mug=14E-6, D=0.05, roughness=0.0, L=1.0)
448.2998197863
```

# References

[^footnote-1]: Wang, Chi-Chuan, Ching-Shan Chiang, and Ding-Chong Lu. "Visual
    Observation of Two-Phase Flow Pattern of R-22, R-134a, and R-407C in a
    6.5-Mm Smooth Tube." Experimental Thermal and Fluid Science 15, no. 4
    (November 1, 1997): 395-405. doi:10.1016/S0894-1777(97)00007-1.

[^footnote-2]: Kim, Sung-Min, and Issam Mudawar. "Universal Approach to Predicting
    Two-Phase Frictional Pressure Drop for Adiabatic and Condensing Mini/
    Micro-Channel Flows." International Journal of Heat and Mass Transfer
    55, no. 11-12 (May 2012): 3246-61.
    doi:10.1016/j.ijheatmasstransfer.2012.02.047.

[^footnote-3]: Xu, Yu, Xiande Fang, Xianghui Su, Zhanru Zhou, and Weiwei Chen.
    "Evaluation of Frictional Pressure Drop Correlations for Two-Phase Flow
    in Pipes." Nuclear Engineering and Design, SI : CFD4NRS-3, 253 (December
    2012): 86-97. doi:10.1016/j.nucengdes.2012.08.007.

functions.name: fluids.two_phase.Hwang_Kim
documentation:
Calculates two-phase pressure drop with the Hwang and Kim (2006)
correlation as in [^footnote-1], also presented in [^footnote-2] and [^footnote-3].

$$
\Delta P = \Delta P_{l} \phi_{l}^2
$$

$$
C = 0.227 Re_{lo}^{0.452} X^{-0.32} Co^{-0.82}
$$

$$
\phi_l^2 = 1 + \frac{C}{X} + \frac{1}{X^2}
$$

$$
X^2 = \frac{\Delta P_l}{\Delta P_g}
$$

# Parameters

m

: Mass flow rate of fluid, [kg/s]

x

: Quality of fluid, [-]

rhol

: Liquid density, [kg/m^3]

rhog

: Gas density, [kg/m^3]

mul

: Viscosity of liquid, [Pa\*s]

mug

: Viscosity of gas, [Pa\*s]

sigma

: Surface tension, [N/m]

D

: Diameter of pipe, [m]

roughness

: Roughness of pipe for use in calculating friction factor, [m]

L

: Length of pipe, [m]

# Returns

dP

: Pressure drop of the two-phase flow, [Pa]

# Notes

Developed with data for microtubes of diameter 0.244 mm and 0.792 mm only.
Not likely to be suitable to larger diameters.

# Examples

```pycon
>>> Hwang_Kim(m=0.0005, x=0.1, rhol=915., rhog=2.67, mul=180E-6, mug=14E-6,
... sigma=0.0487, D=0.003, roughness=0.0, L=1.0)
798.302774184557
```

# References

[^footnote-1]: Hwang, Yun Wook, and Min Soo Kim. "The Pressure Drop in Microtubes
    and the Correlation Development." International Journal of Heat and
    Mass Transfer 49, no. 11-12 (June 2006): 1804-12.
    doi:10.1016/j.ijheatmasstransfer.2005.10.040.

[^footnote-2]: Kim, Sung-Min, and Issam Mudawar. "Universal Approach to Predicting
    Two-Phase Frictional Pressure Drop for Adiabatic and Condensing Mini/
    Micro-Channel Flows." International Journal of Heat and Mass Transfer
    55, no. 11-12 (May 2012): 3246-61.
    doi:10.1016/j.ijheatmasstransfer.2012.02.047.

[^footnote-3]: Xu, Yu, Xiande Fang, Xianghui Su, Zhanru Zhou, and Weiwei Chen.
    "Evaluation of Frictional Pressure Drop Correlations for Two-Phase Flow
    in Pipes." Nuclear Engineering and Design, SI : CFD4NRS-3, 253 (December
    2012): 86-97. doi:10.1016/j.nucengdes.2012.08.007.

functions.name: fluids.two_phase.Zhang_Hibiki_Mishima
documentation:
Calculates two-phase pressure drop with the Zhang, Hibiki, Mishima and
(2010) correlation as in [^footnote-1], also presented in [^footnote-2] and [^footnote-3].

$$
\Delta P = \Delta P_{l} \phi_{l}^2
$$

$$
\phi_l^2 = 1 + \frac{C}{X} + \frac{1}{X^2}
$$

$$
X^2 = \frac{\Delta P_l}{\Delta P_g}
$$

For adiabatic liquid-vapor two-phase flow:

$$
C = 21[1 - \exp(-0.142/Co)]
$$

For adiabatic liquid-gas two-phase flow:

$$
C = 21[1 - \exp(-0.674/Co)]
$$

For flow boiling:

$$
C = 21[1 - \exp(-0.358/Co)]
$$

# Parameters

m

: Mass flow rate of fluid, [kg/s]

x

: Quality of fluid, [-]

rhol

: Liquid density, [kg/m^3]

rhog

: Gas density, [kg/m^3]

mul

: Viscosity of liquid, [Pa\*s]

mug

: Viscosity of gas, [Pa\*s]

sigma

: Surface tension, [N/m]

D

: Diameter of pipe, [m]

roughness

: Roughness of pipe for use in calculating friction factor, [m]

L

: Length of pipe, [m]

flowtype

: One of 'adiabatic vapor', 'adiabatic gas', or 'flow boiling'

# Returns

dP

: Pressure drop of the two-phase flow, [Pa]

# Notes

Seems fairly reliable.

# Examples

```pycon
>>> Zhang_Hibiki_Mishima(m=0.0005, x=0.1, rhol=915., rhog=2.67, mul=180E-6,
... mug=14E-6, sigma=0.0487, D=0.003, roughness=0.0, L=1.0)
444.9718476894804
```

# References

[^footnote-1]: Zhang, W., T. Hibiki, and K. Mishima. "Correlations of Two-Phase
    Frictional Pressure Drop and Void Fraction in Mini-Channel."
    International Journal of Heat and Mass Transfer 53, no. 1-3 (January 15,
    2010): 453-65. doi:10.1016/j.ijheatmasstransfer.2009.09.011.

[^footnote-2]: Kim, Sung-Min, and Issam Mudawar. "Universal Approach to Predicting
    Two-Phase Frictional Pressure Drop for Adiabatic and Condensing Mini/
    Micro-Channel Flows." International Journal of Heat and Mass Transfer
    55, no. 11-12 (May 2012): 3246-61.
    doi:10.1016/j.ijheatmasstransfer.2012.02.047.

[^footnote-3]: Xu, Yu, Xiande Fang, Xianghui Su, Zhanru Zhou, and Weiwei Chen.
    "Evaluation of Frictional Pressure Drop Correlations for Two-Phase Flow
    in Pipes." Nuclear Engineering and Design, SI : CFD4NRS-3, 253 (December
    2012): 86-97. doi:10.1016/j.nucengdes.2012.08.007.

functions.name: fluids.two_phase.Mishima_Hibiki
documentation:
Calculates two-phase pressure drop with the Mishima and Hibiki (1996)
correlation as in [^footnote-1], also presented in [^footnote-2] and [^footnote-3].

$$
\Delta P = \Delta P_{l} \phi_{l}^2
$$

$$
C = 21[1 - \exp(-319D)]
$$

$$
\phi_l^2 = 1 + \frac{C}{X} + \frac{1}{X^2}
$$

$$
X^2 = \frac{\Delta P_l}{\Delta P_g}
$$

# Parameters

m

: Mass flow rate of fluid, [kg/s]

x

: Quality of fluid, [-]

rhol

: Liquid density, [kg/m^3]

rhog

: Gas density, [kg/m^3]

mul

: Viscosity of liquid, [Pa\*s]

mug

: Viscosity of gas, [Pa\*s]

sigma

: Surface tension, [N/m]

D

: Diameter of pipe, [m]

roughness

: Roughness of pipe for use in calculating friction factor, [m]

L

: Length of pipe, [m]

# Returns

dP

: Pressure drop of the two-phase flow, [Pa]

# Notes

# Examples

```pycon
>>> Mishima_Hibiki(m=0.6, x=0.1, rhol=915., rhog=2.67, mul=180E-6,
... mug=14E-6, sigma=0.0487, D=0.05, roughness=0.0, L=1.0)
732.4268200606
```

# References

[^footnote-1]: Mishima, K., and T. Hibiki. "Some Characteristics of Air-Water Two-
    Phase Flow in Small Diameter Vertical Tubes." International Journal of
    Multiphase Flow 22, no. 4 (August 1, 1996): 703-12.
    doi:10.1016/0301-9322(96)00010-9.

[^footnote-2]: Kim, Sung-Min, and Issam Mudawar. "Universal Approach to Predicting
    Two-Phase Frictional Pressure Drop for Adiabatic and Condensing Mini/
    Micro-Channel Flows." International Journal of Heat and Mass Transfer
    55, no. 11-12 (May 2012): 3246-61.
    doi:10.1016/j.ijheatmasstransfer.2012.02.047.

[^footnote-3]: Xu, Yu, Xiande Fang, Xianghui Su, Zhanru Zhou, and Weiwei Chen.
    "Evaluation of Frictional Pressure Drop Correlations for Two-Phase Flow
    in Pipes." Nuclear Engineering and Design, SI : CFD4NRS-3, 253 (December
    2012): 86-97. doi:10.1016/j.nucengdes.2012.08.007.

functions.name: fluids.two_phase.Kim_Mudawar
documentation:
Calculates two-phase pressure drop with the Kim and Mudawar (2012)
correlation as in [^footnote-1], also presented in [^footnote-2].

$$
\Delta P = \Delta P_{l} \phi_{l}^2
$$

$$
\phi_l^2 = 1 + \frac{C}{X} + \frac{1}{X^2}
$$

$$
X^2 = \frac{\Delta P_l}{\Delta P_g}
$$

For turbulent liquid, turbulent gas:

$$
C = 0.39Re_{lo}^{0.03} Su_{go}^{0.10}\left(\frac{\rho_l}{\rho_g} \right)^{0.35}
$$

For turbulent liquid, laminar gas:

$$
C = 8.7\times 10^{-4} Re_{lo}^{0.17} Su_{go}^{0.50}\left(\frac{\rho_l} {\rho_g}\right)^{0.14}
$$

For laminar liquid, turbulent gas:

$$
C = 0.0015 Re_{lo}^{0.59} Su_{go}^{0.19}\left(\frac{\rho_l}{\rho_g} \right)^{0.36}
$$

For laminar liquid, laminar gas:

$$
C = 3.5\times 10^{-5} Re_{lo}^{0.44} Su_{go}^{0.50}\left(\frac{\rho_l} {\rho_g}\right)^{0.48}
$$

This model has its own friction factor calculations, to be consistent with
its Reynolds number transition. As their model was regressed with these
equations, more error is obtained when using any other friction factor
calculation. The laminar equation 64/Re is used up to Re=2000, then the
Blasius equation with a coefficient of 0.316, and above Re = 20000,

$$
f_d = \frac{0.184}{Re^{0.2}}
$$

# Parameters

m

: Mass flow rate of fluid, [kg/s]

x

: Quality of fluid, [-]

rhol

: Liquid density, [kg/m^3]

rhog

: Gas density, [kg/m^3]

mul

: Viscosity of liquid, [Pa\*s]

mug

: Viscosity of gas, [Pa\*s]

sigma

: Surface tension, [N/m]

D

: Diameter of pipe, [m]

L

: Length of pipe, [m]

# Returns

dP

: Pressure drop of the two-phase flow, [Pa]

# Notes

The critical Reynolds number in this model is 2000, with a Reynolds number
definition using actual liquid and gas flows. This model also requires
liquid-only Reynolds number to be calculated.

No attempt to incorporate roughness into the model was made in [^footnote-1].

The model was developed with hydraulic diameter from 0.0695 to 6.22 mm,
mass velocities 4 to 8528 kg/m^2/s, flow qualities from 0 to 1, reduced
pressures from 0.0052 to 0.91, superficial liquid Reynolds numbers up to
79202, superficial gas Reynolds numbers up to 253810, liquid-only Reynolds
numbers up to 89798, 7115 data points from 36 sources and working fluids
air, CO2, N2, water, ethanol, R12, R22, R134a, R236ea, R245fa, R404A, R407C,
propane, methane, and ammonia.

# Examples

```pycon
>>> Kim_Mudawar(m=0.6, x=0.1, rhol=915., rhog=2.67, mul=180E-6, mug=14E-6,
... sigma=0.0487, D=0.05, L=1.0)
840.41377967
```

# References

[^footnote-1]: Kim, Sung-Min, and Issam Mudawar. "Universal Approach to Predicting
    Two-Phase Frictional Pressure Drop for Adiabatic and Condensing Mini/
    Micro-Channel Flows." International Journal of Heat and Mass Transfer
    55, no. 11-12 (May 2012): 3246-61.
    doi:10.1016/j.ijheatmasstransfer.2012.02.047.

[^footnote-2]: Kim, Sung-Min, and Issam Mudawar. "Review of Databases and
    Predictive Methods for Pressure Drop in Adiabatic, Condensing and
    Boiling Mini/Micro-Channel Flows." International Journal of Heat and
    Mass Transfer 77 (October 2014): 74-97.
    doi:10.1016/j.ijheatmasstransfer.2014.04.035.

functions.name: fluids.two_phase.Lockhart_Martinelli
documentation:
Calculates two-phase pressure drop with the Lockhart and Martinelli
(1949) correlation as presented in non-graphical form by Chisholm (1967).

$$
\Delta P = \Delta P_{l} \phi_{l}^2
$$

$$
\phi_l^2 = 1 + \frac{C}{X} + \frac{1}{X^2}
$$

$$
X^2 = \frac{\Delta P_l}{\Delta P_g}
$$

| Liquid    | Gas       | C   |
| --------- | --------- | --- |
| Turbulent | Turbulent | 20  |
| Laminar   | Turbulent | 12  |
| Turbulent | Laminar   | 10  |
| Laminar   | Laminar   | 5   |

This model has its own friction factor calculations, to be consistent with
its Reynolds number transition and the procedure specified in the original
work. The equation 64/Re is used up to Re_c, and above it the Blasius
equation is used as follows:

$$
f_d = \frac{0.184}{Re^{0.2}}
$$

# Parameters

m

: Mass flow rate of fluid, [kg/s]

x

: Quality of fluid, [-]

rhol

: Liquid density, [kg/m^3]

rhog

: Gas density, [kg/m^3]

mul

: Viscosity of liquid, [Pa\*s]

mug

: Viscosity of gas, [Pa\*s]

D

: Diameter of pipe, [m]

L

: Length of pipe, [m]

Re_c

: Transition Reynolds number, used to decide which friction factor
  equation to use and which C value to use from the table above.

# Returns

dP

: Pressure drop of the two-phase flow, [Pa]

# Notes

Developed for horizontal flow. Very popular. Many implementations of this
model assume turbulent-turbulent flow.

The original model proposed that the transition Reynolds number was 1000
for laminar flow, and 2000 for turbulent flow; it proposed no model
for Re_l < 1000 and Re_g between 1000 and 2000 and also Re_g < 1000 and
Re_l between 1000 and 2000.

No correction is available in this model for rough pipe.

[^footnote-3] examined the original data in [^footnote-1] again, and fit more curves to the
data, separating them into different flow regimes. There were 229 datum
in the turbulent-turbulent regime, 9 in the turbulent-laminar regime, 339
in the laminar-turbulent regime, and 42 in the laminar-laminar regime.
Errors from [^footnote-3]'s curves were 13.4%, 3.5%, 14.3%, and 12.0% for the above
regimes, respectively. [^footnote-2]'s fits provide further error.

# Examples

```pycon
>>> Lockhart_Martinelli(m=0.6, x=0.1, rhol=915., rhog=2.67, mul=180E-6,
... mug=14E-6, D=0.05, L=1.0)
716.469565488
```

# References

[^footnote-1]: Lockhart, R. W. & Martinelli, R. C. (1949), "Proposed correlation of
    data for isothermal two-phase, two-component flow in pipes", Chemical
    Engineering Progress 45 (1), 39-48.

[^footnote-2]: Chisholm, D."A Theoretical Basis for the Lockhart-Martinelli
    Correlation for Two-Phase Flow." International Journal of Heat and Mass
    Transfer 10, no. 12 (December 1967): 1767-78.
    doi:10.1016/0017-9310(67)90047-6.

[^footnote-3]: Cui, Xiaozhou, and John J. J. Chen."A Re-Examination of the Data of
    Lockhart-Martinelli." International Journal of Multiphase Flow 36, no.
    10 (October 2010): 836-46. doi:10.1016/j.ijmultiphaseflow.2010.06.001.

[^footnote-4]: Kim, Sung-Min, and Issam Mudawar. "Universal Approach to Predicting
    Two-Phase Frictional Pressure Drop for Adiabatic and Condensing Mini/
    Micro-Channel Flows." International Journal of Heat and Mass Transfer
    55, no. 11-12 (May 2012): 3246-61.
    doi:10.1016/j.ijheatmasstransfer.2012.02.047.

functions.name: fluids.two_phase.two_phase_dP_methods
documentation:
This function returns a list of names of correlations for two-phase
liquid-gas pressure drop for flow inside channels.
24 calculation methods are available, with varying input requirements.

# Parameters

x

rhol

: Liquid density, [kg/m^3]

D

L

: Length of pipe, [m]

rhog

: Gas density, [kg/m^3]

mul

: Viscosity of liquid, [Pa\*s]

mug

: Viscosity of gas, [Pa\*s]

sigma

: Surface tension, [N/m]

P

: Pressure of fluid, [Pa]

Pc

: Critical pressure of fluid, [Pa]

: Roughness of pipe for use in calculating friction factor, [m]

# Examples

```pycon
>>> len(two_phase_dP_methods(m=0.6, x=0.1, rhol=915., rhog=2.67, mul=180E-6, mug=14E-6, sigma=0.0487, D=0.05, L=1.0, angle=30.0, roughness=1e-4, P=1e5, Pc=1e6))

```

functions.name: fluids.two_phase.two_phase_dP
documentation:
This function handles calculation of two-phase liquid-gas pressure drop
for flow inside channels. 23 calculation methods are available, with
varying input requirements. A correlation will be automatically selected if
none is specified. The full list of correlation can be obtained with the
`AvailableMethods` flag.

If no correlation is selected, the following rules are used, with the
earlier options attempted first:

> - If rhog, mul, mug, and sigma are specified, use the Kim_Mudawar model
> - If rhog, mul, and mug are specified, use the Chisholm model
> - If mul, P, and Pc are specified, use the Zhang_Webb model
> - If rhog and sigma are specified, use the Lombardi_Pedrocchi model

# Parameters

m

: Mass flow rate of fluid, [kg/s]

x

: Quality of fluid, [-]

rhol

: Liquid density, [kg/m^3]

D

: Diameter of pipe, [m]

L

: Length of pipe, [m]

rhog

: Gas density, [kg/m^3]

mul

: Viscosity of liquid, [Pa\*s]

mug

: Viscosity of gas, [Pa\*s]

sigma

: Surface tension, [N/m]

P

: Pressure of fluid, [Pa]

Pc

: Critical pressure of fluid, [Pa]

roughness

: Roughness of pipe for use in calculating friction factor, [m]

angle

: The angle of the pipe with respect to the horizontal, [degrees]

# Returns

dP

: Pressure drop of the two-phase flow, [Pa]

# Other Parameters

Method

: A string of the function name to use, as in the dictionary
  two_phase_correlations.

# Notes

These functions may be integrated over, with properties recalculated as
the fluid's quality changes.

This model considers only the frictional pressure drop, not that due to
gravity or acceleration.

# Examples

```pycon
>>> two_phase_dP(m=0.6, x=0.1, rhol=915., rhog=2.67, mul=180E-6, mug=14E-6,
... sigma=0.0487, D=0.05, L=1.0)
840.4137796786
```

functions.name: fluids.two_phase.two_phase_dP_acceleration
documentation:
This function handles calculation of two-phase liquid-gas pressure drop
due to acceleration for flow inside channels. This is a discrete
calculation for a segment with a known difference in quality (and ideally
known inlet and outlet pressures so density dependence can be included).

$$
\Delta P_{acc} = G^2\left\{\left[\frac{(1-x_o)^2}{\rho_{l,o} (1-\alpha_o)} + \frac{x_o^2}{\rho_{g,o}\alpha_o} \right] - \left[\frac{(1-x_i)^2}{\rho_{l,i}(1-\alpha_i)} + \frac{x_i^2}{\rho_{g,i}\alpha_i} \right]\right\}
$$

# Parameters

m

: Mass flow rate of fluid, [kg/s]

D

: Diameter of pipe, [m]

xi

: Quality of fluid at inlet, [-]

xo

: Quality of fluid at outlet, [-]

alpha_i

: Void fraction at inlet (area of gas / total area of channel), [-]

alpha_o

: Void fraction at outlet (area of gas / total area of channel), [-]

rho_li

: Liquid phase density at inlet, [kg/m^3]

rho_gi

: Gas phase density at inlet, [kg/m^3]

rho_lo

: Liquid phase density at outlet, [kg/m^3]

rho_go

: Gas phase density at outlet, [kg/m^3]

# Returns

dP

: Acceleration component of pressure drop for two-phase flow, [Pa]

# Notes

The use of different gas and liquid phase densities at the inlet and outlet
is optional; the outlet densities conditions will be assumed to be those of
the inlet if they are not specified.

There is a continuous variant of this method which can be integrated over,
at the expense of a speed. The differential form of this is as follows
([^footnote-1], [^footnote-3]):

$$
- \left(\frac{d P}{dz}\right)_{acc} = G^2 \frac{d}{dz} \left[\frac{ (1-x)^2}{\rho_l(1-\alpha)} + \frac{x^2}{\rho_g\alpha}\right]
$$

# Examples

```pycon
>>> two_phase_dP_acceleration(m=1, D=0.1, xi=0.372, xo=0.557, rho_li=827.1,
... rho_gi=3.919, alpha_i=0.992, alpha_o=0.996)
706.8560377214725
```

# References

[^footnote-1]: Rohsenow, Warren and James Hartnett and Young Cho. Handbook of Heat
    Transfer, 3E. New York: McGraw-Hill, 1998.

[^footnote-2]: Awad, M. M., and Y. S. Muzychka. "Effective Property Models for
    Homogeneous Two-Phase Flows." Experimental Thermal and Fluid Science 33,
    no. 1 (October 1, 2008): 106-13.
    doi:10.1016/j.expthermflusci.2008.07.006.

[^footnote-3]: Kim, Sung-Min, and Issam Mudawar. "Review of Databases and
    Predictive Methods for Pressure Drop in Adiabatic, Condensing and
    Boiling Mini/Micro-Channel Flows." International Journal of Heat and
    Mass Transfer 77 (October 2014): 74-97.
    doi:10.1016/j.ijheatmasstransfer.2014.04.035.

functions.name: fluids.two_phase.two_phase_dP_dz_acceleration
documentation:
This function handles calculation of two-phase liquid-gas pressure drop
due to acceleration for flow inside channels. This is a continuous
calculation, providing the differential in pressure per unit length and
should be called as part of an integration routine ([^footnote-1], [^footnote-2], [^footnote-3]).

$$
-\left(\frac{\partial P}{\partial L}\right)_{A} = G^2 \left(\left(\frac{1}{\rho_g} - \frac{1}{\rho_l}\right)\frac{\partial P} {\partial L}\frac{\partial x}{\partial P} + \frac{\partial P}{\partial L}\left[x \frac{\partial (1/\rho_g)} {\partial P}  + (1-x) \frac{\partial (1/\rho_l)}{\partial P} \right] \right) - \frac{G^2}{\rho_{hom}}\frac{1}{A}\frac{\partial A} {\partial L}
$$

# Parameters

m

: Mass flow rate of fluid, [kg/s]

D

: Diameter of pipe, [m]

x

: Quality of fluid [-]

rhol

: Liquid density, [kg/m^3]

rhog

: Gas density, [kg/m^3]

dv_dP_l

: Derivative of mass specific volume of the liquid phase with respect to
  pressure, [m^3/(kg\*Pa)]

dv_dP_g

: Derivative of mass specific volume of the gas phase with respect to
  pressure, [m^3/(kg\*Pa)]

dx_dP

: Derivative of mass quality of the two-phase fluid with respect to
  pressure (numerical derivatives may be convenient for this), [1/Pa]

dP_dL

: Pressure drop per unit length of pipe, [Pa/m]

dA_dL

: Change in area of pipe per unit length of pipe, [m^2/m]

# Returns

dP_dz

: Acceleration component of pressure drop for two-phase flow, [Pa/m]

# Notes

This calculation has the `homogeneous` model built in to it as its
derivation is shown in [^footnote-1]. The discrete calculation is more flexible as
different void fractions may be used.

# Examples

```pycon
>>> two_phase_dP_dz_acceleration(m=1, D=0.1, x=0.372, rhol=827.1,
... rhog=3.919, dv_dP_l=-5e-12, dv_dP_g=-4e-7, dx_dP=-2e-7, dP_dL=120.0,
... dA_dL=0.0001)
20.137876617489034
```

# References

[^footnote-1]: Shoham, Ovadia. Mechanistic Modeling of Gas-Liquid Two-Phase Flow in
    Pipes. Pap/Cdr edition. Richardson, TX: Society of Petroleum Engineers,
    2006\.

[^footnote-2]: Rohsenow, Warren and James Hartnett and Young Cho. Handbook of Heat
    Transfer, 3E. New York: McGraw-Hill, 1998.

[^footnote-3]: Kim, Sung-Min, and Issam Mudawar. "Review of Databases and
    Predictive Methods for Pressure Drop in Adiabatic, Condensing and
    Boiling Mini/Micro-Channel Flows." International Journal of Heat and
    Mass Transfer 77 (October 2014): 74-97.
    doi:10.1016/j.ijheatmasstransfer.2014.04.035.

functions.name: fluids.two_phase.two_phase_dP_gravitational
documentation:
This function handles calculation of two-phase liquid-gas pressure drop
due to gravitation for flow inside channels. This is a discrete
calculation for a segment with a known difference in elevation (and ideally
known inlet and outlet pressures so density dependence can be included).

$$
- \Delta P_{grav} =  g \sin \theta z \left\{\frac{ [\alpha_o\rho_{g,o} + (1-\alpha_o)\rho_{l,o}] + [\alpha_i\rho_{g,i} + (1-\alpha_i)\rho_{l,i}]} {2}\right\}
$$

# Parameters

angle

: The angle of the pipe with respect to the horizontal, [degrees]

z

: The total length of the pipe, [m]

alpha_i

: Void fraction at inlet (area of gas / total area of channel), [-]

rho_li

: Liquid phase density at inlet, [kg/m^3]

rho_gi

: Gas phase density at inlet, [kg/m^3]

alpha_o

: Void fraction at outlet (area of gas / total area of channel), [-]

rho_lo

: Liquid phase density at outlet, [kg/m^3]

rho_go

: Gas phase density at outlet, [kg/m^3]

g

: Acceleration due to gravity, [m/s^2]

# Returns

dP

: Gravitational component of pressure drop for two-phase flow, [Pa]

# Notes

The use of different gas and liquid phase densities and void fraction
at the inlet and outlet is optional; the outlet densities and void fraction
will be assumed to be those of the inlet if they are not specified. This
does not add much accuracy.

There is a continuous variant of this method which can be integrated over,
at the expense of a speed. The differential form of this is as follows
([^footnote-1], [^footnote-2]):

$$
-\left(\frac{dP}{dz} \right)_{grav} =  [\alpha\rho_g + (1-\alpha) \rho_l]g \sin \theta
$$

# Examples

Example calculation, page 13-2 from [^footnote-3]:

```pycon
>>> two_phase_dP_gravitational(angle=90, z=2, alpha_i=0.9685, rho_li=1518.,
... rho_gi=2.6)
987.237416829999
```

The same calculation, but using average inlet and outlet conditions:

```pycon
>>> two_phase_dP_gravitational(angle=90, z=2, alpha_i=0.9685, rho_li=1518.,
... rho_gi=2.6,  alpha_o=0.968, rho_lo=1517.9, rho_go=2.59)
994.5416058829999
```

# References

[^footnote-1]: Rohsenow, Warren and James Hartnett and Young Cho. Handbook of Heat
    Transfer, 3E. New York: McGraw-Hill, 1998.

[^footnote-2]: Kim, Sung-Min, and Issam Mudawar. "Review of Databases and
    Predictive Methods for Pressure Drop in Adiabatic, Condensing and
    Boiling Mini/Micro-Channel Flows." International Journal of Heat and
    Mass Transfer 77 (October 2014): 74-97.
    doi:10.1016/j.ijheatmasstransfer.2014.04.035.

[^footnote-3]: Thome, John R. "Engineering Data Book III." Wolverine Tube Inc
    (2004). <http://www.wlv.com/heat-transfer-databook/>

functions.name: fluids.two_phase.two_phase_dP_dz_gravitational
documentation:
This function handles calculation of two-phase liquid-gas pressure drop
due to gravitation for flow inside channels. This is a differential
calculation for a segment with an infinitesimal difference in elevation for
use in performing integration over a pipe as shown in [^footnote-1] and [^footnote-2].

$$
-\left(\frac{dP}{dz} \right)_{grav} =  [\alpha\rho_g + (1-\alpha) \rho_l]g \sin \theta
$$

# Parameters

angle

: The angle of the pipe with respect to the horizontal, [degrees]

alpha

: Void fraction (area of gas / total area of channel), [-]

rhol

: Liquid phase density, [kg/m^3]

rhog

: Gas phase density, [kg/m^3]

g

: Acceleration due to gravity, [m/s^2]

# Returns

dP_dz

: Gravitational component of pressure drop for two-phase flow, [Pa/m]

# Notes

# Examples

```pycon
>>> two_phase_dP_dz_gravitational(angle=90, alpha=0.9685, rhol=1518,
... rhog=2.6)
493.6187084149995
```

# References

[^footnote-1]: Rohsenow, Warren and James Hartnett and Young Cho. Handbook of Heat
    Transfer, 3E. New York: McGraw-Hill, 1998.

[^footnote-2]: Kim, Sung-Min, and Issam Mudawar. "Review of Databases and
    Predictive Methods for Pressure Drop in Adiabatic, Condensing and
    Boiling Mini/Micro-Channel Flows." International Journal of Heat and
    Mass Transfer 77 (October 2014): 74-97.
    doi:10.1016/j.ijheatmasstransfer.2014.04.035.

functions.name: fluids.two_phase.Taitel_Dukler_regime
documentation:
Classifies the regime of a two-phase flow according to Taitel and
Dukler (1976) ([^footnote-1], [^footnote-2]).

The flow regimes in this method are 'annular', 'bubbly', 'intermittent',
'stratified wavy', and 'stratified smooth'.

The four dimensionless parameters used are 'X', 'T', 'F', and 'K'.

$$
X = \left[\frac{(dP/dL)_{l,s,f}}{(dP/dL)_{g,s,f}}\right]^{0.5}
$$

$$
T = \left[\frac{(dP/dL)_{l,s,f}}{(\rho_l-\rho_g)g\cos\theta}\right]^{0.5}
$$

$$
F = \sqrt{\frac{\rho_g}{(\rho_l-\rho_g)}} \frac{v_{g,s}}{\sqrt{D g \cos\theta}}
$$

$$
K = F\left[\frac{D v_{l,s}}{\nu_l}  \right]^{0.5} = F \sqrt{Re_{l,s}}
$$

Note that 'l' refers to liquid, 'g' gas, 'f' friction-only, and 's'
superficial (i.e. if only the mass flow of that phase were flowing in the
pipe).

# Parameters

m

: Mass flow rate of fluid, [kg/s]

x

: Mass quality of fluid, [-]

rhol

: Liquid density, [kg/m^3]

rhog

: Gas density, [kg/m^3]

mul

: Viscosity of liquid, [Pa\*s]

mug

: Viscosity of gas, [Pa\*s]

D

: Diameter of pipe, [m]

angle

: The angle of the pipe with respect to the horizontal, [degrees]

roughness

: Roughness of pipe for use in calculating friction factor, [m]

g

: Acceleration due to gravity, [m/s^2]

# Returns

regime

: One of 'annular', 'bubbly', 'intermittent', 'stratified wavy',
  'stratified smooth', [-]

X

: `X` dimensionless group used in the calculation, [-]

T

: `T` dimensionless group used in the calculation, [-]

F

: `F` dimensionless group used in the calculation, [-]

K

: `K` dimensionless group used in the calculation, [-]

# Notes

The original friction factor used in this model is that of Blasius.

# Examples

```pycon
>>> Taitel_Dukler_regime(m=0.6, x=0.112, rhol=915.12, rhog=2.67,
... mul=180E-6, mug=14E-6, D=0.05, roughness=0.0, angle=0)[0]
'annular'
```

# References

[^footnote-1]: Taitel, Yemada, and A. E. Dukler. "A Model for Predicting Flow
    Regime Transitions in Horizontal and near Horizontal Gas-Liquid Flow."
    AIChE Journal 22, no. 1 (January 1, 1976): 47-55.
    doi:10.1002/aic.690220105.

[^footnote-2]: Brill, James P., and Howard Dale Beggs. Two-Phase Flow in Pipes,
    1994\.

[^footnote-3]: Shoham, Ovadia. Mechanistic Modeling of Gas-Liquid Two-Phase Flow in
    Pipes. Pap/Cdr edition. Richardson, TX: Society of Petroleum Engineers,
    2006\.

functions.name: fluids.two_phase.Mandhane_Gregory_Aziz_regime
documentation:
Classifies the regime of a two-phase flow according to Mandhane,
Gregory, and Azis (1974) flow map.

The flow regimes in this method are 'elongated bubble', 'stratified',
'annular mist', 'slug', 'dispersed bubble', and 'wave'.

The parameters used are just the superficial liquid and gas velocity (i.e.
if only the mass flow of that phase were flowing in the pipe).

# Parameters

m

: Mass flow rate of fluid, [kg/s]

x

: Mass quality of fluid, [-]

rhol

: Liquid density, [kg/m^3]

rhog

: Gas density, [kg/m^3]

mul

: Viscosity of liquid, [Pa\*s]

mug

: Viscosity of gas, [Pa\*s]

sigma

: Surface tension, [N/m]

D

: Diameter of pipe, [m]

# Returns

regime

: One of 'elongated bubble', 'stratified', 'annular mist', 'slug',
  'dispersed bubble', or 'wave', [-]

v_gs

: The superficial gas velocity in the pipe (x axis coordinate), [ft/s]

v_ls

: The superficial liquid velocity in the pipe (x axis coordinate), [ft/s]

# Notes

[^footnote-1] contains a Fortran implementation of this model, which this has been
validated against. This is a very fast flow map as all transitions were
spelled out with clean transitions.

# Examples

```pycon
>>> Mandhane_Gregory_Aziz_regime(m=0.6, x=0.112, rhol=915.12, rhog=2.67,
... mul=180E-6, mug=14E-6, sigma=0.065, D=0.05)
('slug', 0.9728397701853173, 42.05456634236875)
```

# References

[^footnote-1]: Mandhane, J. M., G. A. Gregory, and K. Aziz. "A Flow Pattern Map for
    Gas-liquid Flow in Horizontal Pipes." International Journal of
    Multiphase Flow 1, no. 4 (October 30, 1974): 537-53.
    doi:10.1016/0301-9322(74)90006-8.

functions.name: fluids.piping.nearest_pipe
documentation:
Searches for and finds the nearest standard pipe size to a given
specification. Acceptable inputs are:

- Nominal pipe size
- Nominal pipe size and schedule
- Outer diameter `Do`
- Outer diameter `Do` and schedule
- Inner diameter `Di`
- Inner diameter `Di` and schedule

Acceptable schedules are: '5', '10', '20', '30', '40', '60', '80', '100',
'120', '140', '160', 'STD', 'XS', 'XXS', '5S', '10S', '40S', '80S',
'40D1527', '80D1527',
'ABSD2680', 'PVCD2680',
'DR25C900', 'DR18C900', 'DR14C900',
'CIDR51C905', 'CIDR41C905', 'CIDR325C905', 'CIDR25C905', 'CIDR21C905', 'CIDR18C905', 'CIDR14C905',
'IPSDR21', 'IPSDR26', 'IPSDR325', 'IPSDR41',
'PS115F679', 'PS75F679', 'PS46F679',
'PVCD2665',
'40D1785', '80D1785', '120D1785',
'DR135D2241', 'DR17D2241', 'DR21D2241', 'DR26D2241', 'DR325D2241', 'DR41D2241', 'DR64D2241',
'DR21D2241CTS', 'DR17D2241CTS', 'DR135D2241CTS', 'DR11D2241CTS',
'DR21D2241PIP', 'DR26D2241PIP', 'DR325D2241PIP', 'DR35D2241PIP', 'DR41D2241PIP', 'DR51D2241PIP', 'DR81D2241PIP',
'S40F441IPS', 'S80F441IPS', 'S40F441SI', 'S80F441SI'
'DR325F2619SI', 'DR26F2619SI', 'DR21F2619SI', 'DR17F2619SI', 'DR135F2619SI', 'DR11F2619SI', 'DR9F2619SI', 'DR73F2619SI', 'DR7F2619SI',
'DR325F2619IPS', 'DR26F2619IPS', 'DR21F2619IPS', 'DR17F2619IPS', 'DR135F2619IPS', 'DR11F2619IPS', 'DR9F2619IPS', 'DR73F2619IPS', 'DR7F2619IPS',
'BS1387LIGHT', 'BS1387MEDIUM', 'BS1387HEAVY'

# Parameters

Do

: Pipe outer diameter, [m]

Di

: Pipe inner diameter, [m]

NPS

: Nominal pipe size, [-]

schedule

: String representing schedule size

# Returns

NPS

: Nominal pipe size, [-]

Di

: Pipe inner diameter, [m]

Do

: Pipe outer diameter, [m]

t

: Pipe wall thickness, [m]

# Notes

Internal units within this function are mm.
The imperial schedules are not quite identical to these value, but
all rounding differences happen in the sub-0.1 mm level.

# Examples

```pycon
>>> nearest_pipe(Di=0.021)
(1, 0.02664, 0.0334, 0.0033799999999999998)
>>> nearest_pipe(Do=.273, schedule='5S')
(10, 0.26630000000000004, 0.2731, 0.0034)
```

# References

[^footnote-1]: American National Standards Institute, and American Society of
    Mechanical Engineers. B36.10M-2004: Welded and Seamless Wrought Steel
    Pipe. New York: American Society of Mechanical Engineers, 2004.

[^footnote-2]: American National Standards Institute, and American Society of
    Mechanical Engineers. B36-19M-2004: Stainless Steel Pipe.
    New York, N.Y.: American Society of Mechanical Engineers, 2004.

[^footnote-3]: F17 Committee. "Specification for Acrylonitrile-Butadiene-Styrene
    (ABS) Plastic Pipe, Schedules 40 and 80." ASTM International.
    <https://doi.org/10.1520/D1527-99R05>.

[^footnote-4]: F17 Committee. "Specification for Acrylonitrile-Butadiene-Styrene
    (ABS) and Poly(Vinyl Chloride) (PVC) Composite Sewer Piping." ASTM
    International. <https://doi.org/10.1520/D2680-01R14>.

[^footnote-5]: AWWA-American Water Works Association. "AWWA C900-07 Polyvinyl
    Chloride (PVC) Pressure Pipe and Fabricated Fittings, 4 In. Through 12
    In. (100 Mm Through 300 Mm), for Water Transmission and Distribution."

[^footnote-6]: AWWA-American Water Works Association. "AWWA C905-97 Polyvinyl

    48 in. (350 Mm through 1,200 Mm), for Water Transmission and
    Distribution."

[^footnote-7]: F17 Committee. "Specification for Poly(Vinyl Chloride) (PVC)

    International. <https://doi.org/10.1520/F0679-16>.

[^footnote-8]: F17 Committee. "Specification for Poly(Vinyl Chloride) (PVC) Plastic
    Drain, Waste, and Vent Pipe and Fittings." ASTM International.
    <https://doi.org/10.1520/D2665-14>.

[^footnote-9]: F17 Committee. "Specification for Poly(Vinyl Chloride) (PVC) Plastic

functions.name: fluids.piping.gauge_from_t
documentation:
Looks up the gauge of a given wire thickness of given schedule.
Values are all non-linear, and tabulated internally.

# Parameters

t

: Thickness, [m]

SI

: If False, requires that the thickness is given in inches not meters

schedule

: Gauge schedule, one of 'BWG', 'AWG', 'SWG', 'MWG', 'BSWG', or 'SSWG'

# Returns

gauge

: Wire Gauge, [-]

# Notes

An internal variable, tol, is used in the selection of the wire gauge. If
the next smaller wire gauge is within 10% of the difference between it and
the previous wire gauge, the smaller wire gauge is selected. Accordingly,
this function can return a gauge with a thickness smaller than desired
in some circumstances.

- Birmingham Wire Gauge (BWG) ranges from 0.2 (0.5 inch) to 36 (0.004 inch).
- American Wire Gauge (AWG) ranges from 0.167 (0.58 inch) to 51 (0.00099
  inch). These are used for electrical wires.
- Steel Wire Gauge (SWG) ranges from 0.143 (0.49 inch) to 51 (0.0044 inch).
  Also called Washburn & Moen wire gauge, American Steel gauge, Wire Co.
  gauge, and Roebling wire gauge.
- Music Wire Gauge (MWG) ranges from 0.167 (0.004 inch) to 46 (0.18
  inch). Also called Piano Wire Gauge.
- British Standard Wire Gage (BSWG) ranges from 0.143 (0.5 inch) to
  51 (0.001 inch). Also called Imperial Wire Gage (IWG).
- Stub's Steel Wire Gage (SSWG) ranges from 1 (0.227 inch) to 80 (0.013 inch)

# Examples

```pycon
>>> gauge_from_t(.5, SI=False, schedule='BWG')
0.2
```

# References

[^footnote-1]: Oberg, Erik, Franklin D. Jones, and Henry H. Ryffel. Machinery's
    Handbook. Industrial Press, Incorporated, 2012.

- Steel Wire Gauge (SWG) ranges from 0.143 (0.49 inch) to 51 (0.0044 inch).
  Also called Washburn & Moen wire gauge, American Steel gauge, Wire Co.
  gauge, and Roebling wire gauge.
- Music Wire Gauge (MWG) ranges from 0.167 (0.004 inch) to 46 (0.18

functions.name: fluids.piping.erosional_velocity
documentation:
Calculate the erosional velocity according to the
API RP 14E equation.

$$
V_e = \frac{C}{\sqrt{\rho_m}}
$$

# Parameters

rho

: Bulk mass density of the overall fluid, [kg/m^3]

C

: Erosional velocity factor; must be provided, [sqrt(lb/(ft\*s^2))]

# Returns

V

: Erosional velocity, [m/s]

# Notes

Some suggested factors in [^footnote-1] are:

With solids, it is suggested the factor be determined from specific
studies.

For corrosive fluids in continuous service, C=100.
For corrosive fluids in intermittent service, C=125.
For non-corrosive fluids in continuous service, C=150-200.
For non-corrosive fluids in intermittent service, C=250.
For corrosive fluids with an inhibitor or a corrosion resistent alloy
in continuous service, C=150-200; and for intermittent service, C=250.

# Examples

```pycon
>>> erosional_velocity(1000, 100)
3.8576728004
```

# References

[^footnote-1]: "API RP 14E : Recommended Practice for Design and Installation
    of Offshore Production Platform Piping Systems." 5E, 2000

[^footnote-2]: Madani Sani, F., S. Huizinga, K. A. Esaklul, and S. Nesic. "Review of
    the API RP 14E Erosional Velocity Equation: Origin, Applications, Misuses,
    Limitations and Alternatives." Wear, 22nd International Conference on
    Wear of Materials, 426-427 (April 30, 2019): 620-36.
    <https://doi.org/10.1016/j.wear.2019.01.119>.
