functions.name: fluids.saltation.Rizk
documentation:
Calculates saltation velocity of the gas for pneumatic conveying,
according to [^footnote-1] as described in [^footnote-2] and many others.

$$
\mu=\left(\frac{1}{10^{1440d_p+1.96}}\right)\left(Fr_s\right)^{1100d_p+2.5}
$$

$$
Fr_s = \frac{V_{salt}}{\sqrt{gD}}
$$

$$
\mu = \frac{m_p}{\frac{\pi}{4}D^2V \rho_f}
$$

# Parameters

mp

: Solid mass flow rate, [kg/s]

dp

: Particle diameter, [m]

rhog

: Gas density, [kg/m^3]

D

: Diameter of pipe, [m]

# Returns

V

: Saltation velocity of gas, [m/s]

# Notes

Model is rearranged to be explicit in terms of saltation velocity
internally.

# Examples

Example is from [^footnote-3].

```pycon
>>> Rizk(mp=0.25, dp=100E-6, rhog=1.2, D=.078)
9.8833092829357
```

# References

[^footnote-1]: Rizk, F. "Pneumatic conveying at optimal operation conditions and a
    solution of Bath's equation." Proceedings of Pneumotransport 3,
    paper D4. BHRA Fluid Engineering, Cranfield, England (1973)

[^footnote-2]: Klinzing, G. E., F. Rizk, R. Marcus, and L. S. Leung. Pneumatic
    Conveying of Solids: A Theoretical and Practical Approach.
    Springer, 2013.

[^footnote-3]: Rhodes, Martin J. Introduction to Particle Technology. Wiley, 2013.

functions.name: fluids.saltation.Matsumoto_1974
documentation:
Calculates saltation velocity of the gas for pneumatic conveying,
according to [^footnote-1]. Also described in [^footnote-2].

$$
\mu = 0.448 \left(\frac{\rho_p}{\rho_f}\right)^{0.50}\left(\frac{Fr_p} {10}\right)^{-1.75}\left(\frac{Fr_s}{10}\right)^{3}
$$

$$
Fr_s = \frac{V_{salt}}{\sqrt{gD}}
$$

$$
Fr_p = \frac{V_{terminal}}{\sqrt{gd_p}}
$$

$$
\mu = \frac{m_p}{\frac{\pi}{4}D^2V \rho_f}
$$

# Parameters

mp

: Solid mass flow rate, [kg/s]

rhop

: Particle density, [kg/m^3]

dp

: Particle diameter, [m]

rhog

: Gas density, [kg/m^3]

D

: Diameter of pipe, [m]

Vterminal

: Terminal velocity of particle settling in gas, [m/s]

# Returns

V

: Saltation velocity of gas, [m/s]

# Notes

Model is rearranged to be explicit in terms of saltation velocity
internally.
Result looks high, something may be wrong.
For particles > 0.3 mm.

# Examples

```pycon
>>> Matsumoto_1974(mp=1., rhop=1000., dp=1E-3, rhog=1.2, D=0.1, Vterminal=5.24)
19.583617317317895
```

# References

[^footnote-1]: Matsumoto, Shigeru, Michio Kara, Shozaburo Saito, and Siro Maeda.
    "Minimum Transport Velocity for Horizontal Pneumatic Conveying."
    Journal of Chemical Engineering of Japan 7, no. 6 (1974): 425-30.
    doi:10.1252/jcej.7.425.

[^footnote-2]: Jones, Peter J., and L. S. Leung. "A Comparison of Correlations for
    Saltation Velocity in Horizontal Pneumatic Conveying." Industrial &
    Engineering Chemistry Process Design and Development 17, no. 4
    (October 1, 1978): 571-75. doi:10.1021/i260068a031

functions.name: fluids.saltation.Matsumoto_1975
documentation:
Calculates saltation velocity of the gas for pneumatic conveying,
according to [^footnote-1]. Also described in [^footnote-2].

$$
\mu = 1.11 \left(\frac{\rho_p}{\rho_f}\right)^{0.55}\left(\frac{Fr_p} {10}\right)^{-2.3}\left(\frac{Fr_s}{10}\right)^{3}
$$

$$
Fr_s = \frac{V_{salt}}{\sqrt{gD}}
$$

$$
Fr_p = \frac{V_{terminal}}{\sqrt{gd_p}}
$$

$$
\mu = \frac{m_p}{\frac{\pi}{4}D^2V \rho_f}
$$

# Parameters

mp

: Solid mass flow rate, [kg/s]

rhop

: Particle density, [kg/m^3]

dp

: Particle diameter, [m]

rhog

: Gas density, [kg/m^3]

D

: Diameter of pipe, [m]

Vterminal

: Terminal velocity of particle settling in gas, [m/s]

# Returns

V

: Saltation velocity of gas, [m/s]

# Notes

Model is rearranged to be explicit in terms of saltation velocity
internally.
Result looks high, something may be wrong.
For particles > 0.3 mm.

# Examples

```pycon
>>> Matsumoto_1975(mp=1., rhop=1000., dp=1E-3, rhog=1.2, D=0.1, Vterminal=5.24)
18.04523091703009
```

# References

[^footnote-1]: Matsumoto, Shigeru, Shundo Harada, Shozaburo Saito, and Siro Maeda.
    "Saltation Velocity for Horizontal Pneumatic Conveying." Journal of
    Chemical Engineering of Japan 8, no. 4 (1975): 331-33.
    doi:10.1252/jcej.8.331.

[^footnote-2]: Jones, Peter J., and L. S. Leung. "A Comparison of Correlations for
    Saltation Velocity in Horizontal Pneumatic Conveying." Industrial &
    Engineering Chemistry Process Design and Development 17, no. 4
    (October 1, 1978): 571-75. doi:10.1021/i260068a031

functions.name: fluids.saltation.Matsumoto_1977
documentation:
Calculates saltation velocity of the gas for pneumatic conveying,
according to [^footnote-1] and reproduced in [^footnote-2], [^footnote-3], and [^footnote-4].

First equation is used if third equation yields d\* higher than dp.
Otherwise, use equation 2.

$$
\mu = 5560\left(\frac{d_p}{D}\right)^{1.43}\left(\frac{Fr_s}{10}\right)^4
$$

$$
\mu = 0.373 \left(\frac{\rho_p}{\rho_f}\right)^{1.06}\left(\frac{Fr_p} {10}\right)^{-3.7}\left(\frac{Fr_s}{10}\right)^{3.61}
$$

$$
\frac{d_p^*}{D} = 1.39\left(\frac{\rho_p}{\rho_f}\right)^{-0.74}
$$

$$
Fr_s = \frac{V_{salt}}{\sqrt{gD}}
$$

$$
Fr_p = \frac{V_{terminal}}{\sqrt{gd_p}}
$$

$$
\mu = \frac{m_p}{\frac{\pi}{4}D^2V \rho_f}
$$

# Parameters

mp

: Solid mass flow rate, [kg/s]

rhop

: Particle density, [kg/m^3]

dp

: Particle diameter, [m]

rhog

: Gas density, [kg/m^3]

D

: Diameter of pipe, [m]

Vterminal

: Terminal velocity of particle settling in gas, [m/s]

# Returns

V

: Saltation velocity of gas, [m/s]

# Notes

Model is rearanged to be explicit in terms of saltation velocity
internally.r

# Examples

Example is only a self-test.

Course routine, terminal velocity input is from example in [2].

```pycon
>>> Matsumoto_1977(mp=1., rhop=1000., dp=1E-3, rhog=1.2, D=0.1, Vterminal=5.24)
16.64284834446686
```

# References

[^footnote-1]: Matsumoto, Shigeru, Makoto Kikuta, and Siro Maeda. "Effect of
    Particle Size on the Minimum Transport Velocity for Horizontal Pneumatic
    Conveying of Solids." Journal of Chemical Engineering of Japan 10,
    no. 4 (1977): 273-79. doi:10.1252/jcej.10.273.

[^footnote-2]: Klinzing, G. E., F. Rizk, R. Marcus, and L. S. Leung. Pneumatic
    Conveying of Solids: A Theoretical and Practical Approach.
    Springer, 2013.

[^footnote-3]: Gomes, L. M., and A. L. Amarante Mesquita. "On the Prediction of
    Pickup and Saltation Velocities in Pneumatic Conveying." Brazilian
    Journal of Chemical Engineering 31, no. 1 (March 2014): 35-46.
    doi:10.1590/S0104-66322014000100005

[^footnote-4]: Rabinovich, Evgeny, and Haim Kalman. "Threshold Velocities of
    Particle-Fluid Flows in Horizontal Pipes and Ducts: Literature Review."
    Reviews in Chemical Engineering 27, no. 5-6 (January 1, 2011).
    doi:10.1515/REVCE.2011.011.

functions.name: fluids.saltation.Schade
documentation:
Calculates saltation velocity of the gas for pneumatic conveying,
according to [^footnote-1] as described in [^footnote-2], [^footnote-3], [^footnote-4], and [^footnote-5].

$$
Fr_s = \mu^{0.11}\left(\frac{D}{d_p}\right)^{0.025}\left(\frac{\rho_p} {\rho_f}\right)^{0.34}
$$

$$
Fr_s = \frac{V_{salt}}{\sqrt{gD}}
$$

$$
\mu = \frac{m_p}{\frac{\pi}{4}D^2V \rho_f}
$$

# Parameters

mp

: Solid mass flow rate, [kg/s]

rhop

: Particle density, [kg/m^3]

dp

: Particle diameter, [m]

rhog

: Gas density, [kg/m^3]

D

: Diameter of pipe, [m]

# Returns

V

: Saltation velocity of gas, [m/s]

# Notes

Model is rearranged to be explicit in terms of saltation velocity
internally.

# Examples

```pycon
>>> Schade(mp=1., rhop=1000., dp=1E-3, rhog=1.2, D=0.1)
13.697415809497912
```

# References

[^footnote-1]: Schade, B., Zum Ubergang Sprung-Strahnen-forderung bei der
    Horizontalen Pneumatischen Feststoffordrung. Dissertation, University of
    Karlsruche (1987)

[^footnote-2]: Rabinovich, Evgeny, and Haim Kalman. "Threshold Velocities of
    Particle-Fluid Flows in Horizontal Pipes and Ducts: Literature Review."
    Reviews in Chemical Engineering 27, no. 5-6 (January 1, 2011).
    doi:10.1515/REVCE.2011.011.

[^footnote-3]: Setia, G., S. S. Mallick, R. Pan, and P. W. Wypych. "Modeling
    Minimum Transport Boundary for Fluidized Dense-Phase Pneumatic Conveying
    Systems." Powder Technology 277 (June 2015): 244-51.
    doi:10.1016/j.powtec.2015.02.050.

[^footnote-4]: Bansal, A., S. S. Mallick, and P. W. Wypych. "Investigating
    Straight-Pipe Pneumatic Conveying Characteristics for Fluidized
    Dense-Phase Pneumatic Conveying." Particulate Science and Technology
    31, no. 4 (July 4, 2013): 348-56. doi:10.1080/02726351.2012.732677.

[^footnote-5]: Gomes, L. M., and A. L. Amarante Mesquita. "On the Prediction of
    Pickup and Saltation Velocities in Pneumatic Conveying." Brazilian
    Journal of Chemical Engineering 31, no. 1 (March 2014): 35-46.
    doi:10.1590/S0104-66322014000100005

functions.name: fluids.saltation.Weber_saltation
documentation:
Calculates saltation velocity of the gas for pneumatic conveying,
according to [^footnote-1] as described in [^footnote-2], [^footnote-3], [^footnote-4], and [^footnote-5].

If Vterminal is under 3 m/s, use equation 1; otherwise, equation 2.

$$
Fr_s = \left(7 + \frac{8}{3}V_{terminal}\right)\mu^{0.25} \left(\frac{d_p}{D}\right)^{0.1}
$$

$$
Fr_s = 15\mu^{0.25}\left(\frac{d_p}{D}\right)^{0.1}
$$

$$
Fr_s = \frac{V_{salt}}{\sqrt{gD}}
$$

$$
\mu = \frac{m_p}{\frac{\pi}{4}D^2V \rho_f}
$$

# Parameters

mp

: Solid mass flow rate, [kg/s]

rhop

: Particle density, [kg/m^3]

dp

: Particle diameter, [m]

rhog

: Gas density, [kg/m^3]

D

: Diameter of pipe, [m]

Vterminal

: Terminal velocity of particle settling in gas, [m/s]

# Returns

V

: Saltation velocity of gas, [m/s]

# Notes

Model is rearranged to be explicit in terms of saltation velocity
internally.

# Examples

Examples are only a self-test.

```pycon
>>> Weber_saltation(mp=1, rhop=1000., dp=1E-3, rhog=1.2, D=0.1, Vterminal=4)
15.227445436331474
```

# References

[^footnote-1]: Weber, M. 1981. Principles of hydraulic and pneumatic conveying in
    pipes. Bulk Solids Handling 1: 57-63.

[^footnote-2]: Rabinovich, Evgeny, and Haim Kalman. "Threshold Velocities of
    Particle-Fluid Flows in Horizontal Pipes and Ducts: Literature Review."
    Reviews in Chemical Engineering 27, no. 5-6 (January 1, 2011).
    doi:10.1515/REVCE.2011.011.

[^footnote-3]: Setia, G., S. S. Mallick, R. Pan, and P. W. Wypych. "Modeling
    Minimum Transport Boundary for Fluidized Dense-Phase Pneumatic Conveying
    Systems." Powder Technology 277 (June 2015): 244-51.
    doi:10.1016/j.powtec.2015.02.050.

[^footnote-4]: Bansal, A., S. S. Mallick, and P. W. Wypych. "Investigating
    Straight-Pipe Pneumatic Conveying Characteristics for Fluidized
    Dense-Phase Pneumatic Conveying." Particulate Science and Technology
    31, no. 4 (July 4, 2013): 348-56. doi:10.1080/02726351.2012.732677.

[^footnote-5]: Gomes, L. M., and A. L. Amarante Mesquita. "On the Prediction of
    Pickup and Saltation Velocities in Pneumatic Conveying." Brazilian
    Journal of Chemical Engineering 31, no. 1 (March 2014): 35-46.
    doi:10.1590/S0104-66322014000100005

functions.name: fluids.saltation.Geldart_Ling
documentation:
Calculates saltation velocity of the gas for pneumatic conveying,
according to [^footnote-1] as described in [^footnote-2] and [^footnote-3].

if Gs/D < 47000, use equation 1, otherwise use equation 2.

$$
V_{salt} = 1.5G_s^{0.465}D^{-0.01} \mu^{0.055}\rho_f^{-0.42}
$$

$$
V_{salt} = 8.7G_s^{0.302}D^{0.153} \mu^{0.055}\rho_f^{-0.42}
$$

$$
Fr_s = 15\mu^{0.25}\left(\frac{d_p}{D}\right)^{0.1}
$$

$$
Fr_s = \frac{V_{salt}}{\sqrt{gD}}
$$

$$
\mu = \frac{m_p}{\frac{\pi}{4}D^2V \rho_f}
$$

$$
G_s = \frac{m_p}{A}
$$

# Parameters

mp

: Solid mass flow rate, [kg/s]

rhog

: Gas density, [kg/m^3]

D

: Diameter of pipe, [m]

mug

: Gas viscosity, [Pa\*s]

# Returns

V

: Saltation velocity of gas, [m/s]

# Notes

Model is rearranged to be explicit in terms of saltation velocity
internally.

# Examples

```pycon
>>> Geldart_Ling(1., 1.2, 0.1, 2E-5)
7.467495862402707
```

# References

[^footnote-1]: Weber, M. 1981. Principles of hydraulic and pneumatic conveying in
    pipes. Bulk Solids Handling 1: 57-63.

[^footnote-2]: Rabinovich, Evgeny, and Haim Kalman. "Threshold Velocities of
    Particle-Fluid Flows in Horizontal Pipes and Ducts: Literature Review."
    Reviews in Chemical Engineering 27, no. 5-6 (January 1, 2011).
    doi:10.1515/REVCE.2011.011.

[^footnote-3]: Gomes, L. M., and A. L. Amarante Mesquita. "On the Prediction of
    Pickup and Saltation Velocities in Pneumatic Conveying." Brazilian
    Journal of Chemical Engineering 31, no. 1 (March 2014): 35-46.
    doi:10.1590/S0104-66322014000100005

Chemical Engineering Design Library (ChEDL). Utilities for process modeling.

This module contains a model for a jet pump, also known as an eductor or an
ejector.

For reporting bugs, adding feature requests, or submitting pull requests,
please use the [GitHub issue tracker](https://github.com/CalebBell/fluids/)
or contact the author at <mailto:Caleb.Andrew.Bell@gmail.com>.

```{contents}
:local: true
```

# Interfaces

```
.. autofunction:: liquid_jet_pump
```

# Objective Function

```
.. autofunction:: liquid_jet_pump_ancillary
```

# Vacuum Air Leakage Estimation

```
.. autofunction:: vacuum_air_leakage_HEI2633
```

```
.. autofunction:: vacuum_air_leakage_Ryans_Croll
```


functions.name: fluids.jet_pump.liquid_jet_pump_ancillary
documentation:
Calculates the remaining variable in a liquid jet pump when solving for
one if the inlet variables only and the rest of them are known. The
equation comes from conservation of energy and momentum in the mixing
chamber.

The variable to be solved for must be one of `d_nozzle`, `d_mixing`,
`Qp`, `Qs`, `P1`, or `P2`.

$$
P_1 - P_2 = \frac{1}{2}\rho_pV_n^2(1+K_p) - \frac{1}{2}\rho_s V_3^2(1+K_s)
$$

Rearrange to express V3 in terms of Vn, and using the density ratio `C`,
the expression becomes:

$$
P_1 - P_2 = \frac{1}{2}\rho_p V_n^2\left[(1+K_p) - C(1+K_s) \left(\frac{MR}{1-R}\right)^2\right]
$$

Using the primary nozzle area and flow rate:

$$
P_1 - P_2 = \frac{1}{2}\rho_p \left(\frac{Q_p}{A_n}\right)^2 \left[(1+K_p) - C(1+K_s) \left(\frac{MR}{1-R}\right)^2\right]
$$

For `P`, `P2`, `Qs`, and `Qp`, the equation can be rearranged explicitly
for them. For `d_mixing` and `d_nozzle`, a bounded solver is used searching
between 1E-9 m and 20 times the other diameter which was specified.

# Parameters

rhop

: The density of the primary (motive) fluid, [kg/m^3]

rhos

: The density of the secondary fluid (drawn from the vacuum chamber),
  [kg/m^3]

Kp

: The primary nozzle loss coefficient, [-]

Ks

: The secondary inlet loss coefficient, [-]

d_nozzle

: The inside diameter of the primary fluid's nozle, [m]

d_mixing

: The diameter of the mixing chamber, [m]

Qp

: The volumetric flow rate of the primary fluid, [m^3/s]

Qs

: The volumetric flow rate of the secondary fluid, [m^3/s]

P1

: The pressure of the primary fluid entering its nozzle, [Pa]

P2

: The pressure of the secondary fluid at the entry of the ejector, [Pa]

# Returns

solution

: The parameter not specified (one of `d_nozzle`, `d_mixing`,
  `Qp`, `Qs`, `P1`, or `P2`), (units of `m`, `m`, `m^3/s`, `m^3/s`,
  `Pa`, or `Pa` respectively)

# Notes

The following SymPy code was used to obtain the analytical formulas (
they are not shown here due to their length):

```pycon
>>> from sympy import * # doctest: +SKIP
>>> A_nozzle, A_mixing, Qs, Qp, P1, P2, rhos, rhop, Ks, Kp = symbols('A_nozzle, A_mixing, Qs, Qp, P1, P2, rhos, rhop, Ks, Kp') # doctest: +SKIP
>>> R = A_nozzle/A_mixing # doctest: +SKIP
>>> M = Qs/Qp # doctest: +SKIP
>>> C = rhos/rhop # doctest: +SKIP
>>> rhs = rhop/2*(Qp/A_nozzle)**2*((1+Kp) - C*(1 + Ks)*((M*R)/(1-R))**2 ) # doctest: +SKIP
>>> new = Eq(P1 - P2,  rhs) # doctest: +SKIP
>>> solve(new, Qp) # doctest: +SKIP
>>> solve(new, Qs) # doctest: +SKIP
>>> solve(new, P1) # doctest: +SKIP
>>> solve(new, P2) # doctest: +SKIP
```

# Examples

Calculating primary fluid nozzle inlet pressure P1:

```pycon
>>> liquid_jet_pump_ancillary(rhop=998., rhos=1098., Ks=0.11, Kp=.04,
... P2=133600, Qp=0.01, Qs=0.01, d_mixing=0.045, d_nozzle=0.02238)
426434.60314398
```

# References

[^footnote-1]: Ejectors and Jet Pumps. Design and Performance for Incompressible
    Liquid Flow. 85032. ESDU International PLC, 1985.

functions.name: fluids.jet_pump.liquid_jet_pump
documentation:
Calculate the remaining two variables in a liquid jet pump, using a
model presented in [^footnote-1] as well as [^footnote-2], [^footnote-3], and [^footnote-4].

$$
N = \frac{2R + \frac{2 C M^2 R^2}{1-R} - R^2 (1+CM) (1+M) (1 + K_m + K_d + \alpha^2) - \frac{CM^2R^2}{(1-R)^2} (1+K_s)} {(1+K_p) - 2R - \frac{2CM^2R^2}{1-R} + R^2(1+CM)(1+M)(1+K_m + K_d + \alpha^2) + (1-j)\left(\frac{CM^2R^2}{({1-R})^2} \right)(1+K_s)}
$$

$$
P_1 - P_2 = \frac{1}{2}\rho_p \left(\frac{Q_p}{A_n}\right)^2 \left[(1+K_p) - C(1+K_s) \left(\frac{MR}{1-R}\right)^2\right]
$$

$$
\text{Pressure ratio} = N = \frac{P_5 - P_2}{P_1 - P_5}
$$

$$
\text{Volume flow ratio} = M =  \frac{Q_s}{Q_p}
$$

$$
\text{Jet pump efficiency} = \eta = M\cdot N = \frac{Q_s(P_5-P_2)}{Q_p(P_1 - P_5)}
$$

$$
R = \frac{A_n}{A_m}
$$

$$
C = \frac{\rho_s}{\rho_p}
$$

There is no guarantee a solution will be found for the provided variable
values, but every combination of two missing variables are supported.

# Parameters

rhop

: The density of the primary (motive) fluid, [kg/m^3]

rhos

: The density of the secondary fluid (drawn from the vacuum chamber),
  [kg/m^3]

Kp

: The primary nozzle loss coefficient, [-]

Ks

: The secondary inlet loss coefficient, [-]

Km

: The mixing chamber loss coefficient, [-]

Kd

: The diffuser loss coefficient, [-]

d_nozzle

: The inside diameter of the primary fluid's nozle, [m]

d_mixing

: The diameter of the mixing chamber, [m]

d_diffuser

: The diameter of the diffuser at its exit, [m]

Qp

: The volumetric flow rate of the primary fluid, [m^3/s]

Qs

: The volumetric flow rate of the secondary fluid, [m^3/s]

P1

: The pressure of the primary fluid entering its nozzle, [Pa]

P2

: The pressure of the secondary fluid at the entry of the ejector, [Pa]

P5

: The pressure at the exit of the diffuser, [Pa]

nozzle_retracted

: Whether or not the primary nozzle's exit is before the mixing chamber,
  or somewhat inside it, [-]

max_variations

: When the initial guesses do not lead to a converged solution, try this
  many more guesses at converging the problem, [-]

# Returns

solution

: Dictionary of calculated parameters, [-]

# Notes

The assumptions of the model are:

- The flows are one dimensional except in the mixing chamber.
- The mixing chamber has constant cross-sectional area.
- The mixing happens entirely in the mixing chamber, prior to entry into
  the diffuser.
- The primary nozzle is in a straight line with the middle of the mixing
  chamber.
- Both fluids are incompressible, and have no excess volume on mixing.
- Primary and secondary flows both enter the mixing throat with their
  own uniform velocity distribution; the mixed stream leaves with a uniform
  velocity profile.
- If the secondary fluid is a gas, it undergoes isothermal compression in
  the throat and diffuser.
- If the secondary fluid is a gas or contains a bubbly gas, it is
  homogeneously distributed in a continuous liquid phase.
- Heat transfer between the fluids is negligible - there is no change in
  density due to temperature changes
- The change in the solubility of a dissolved gas, if there is one, is
  negigibly changed by temperature or pressure changes.

The model can be derived from the equations in
{py:func}`~.liquid_jet_pump_ancillary` and the following:

Conservation of energy at the primary nozzle, secondary inlet, and diffuser exit:

$$
P_1 = P_3 + \frac{1}{2}\rho_p V_n^2 + K_p\left(\frac{1}{2}\rho_p V_n^2\right)
$$

$$
P_2 = P_3 + \frac{1}{2}\rho_s V_3^2 + K_s\left(\frac{1}{2}\rho_s V_3^2\right)
$$

$$
P_5 = P_4 + \frac{1}{2}\rho_d V_4^2 - K_d\left(\frac{1}{2}\rho_d V_4^2\right)
$$

The mixing chamber loss coefficient should be obtained through the following
expression, using the mixing chamber exit velocity to obtain the friction
factor.

$$
K_m = \frac{4f_d L}{D}
$$

$$
K_d = \frac{P_4 - P_5}{0.5 \rho_d V_4^2} = 1 - \left(\frac{A_4}{A_5} \right)^2 - C_{pr}
$$

$$
K_s = \frac{P_2 - P_3}{0.5\rho_s V_3^2} - 1
$$

$$
K_p = \frac{P_1 - P_n}{0.5\rho_p V_n^2} - 1
$$

Continuity of the ejector:

$$
\rho_p Q_p + \rho_s Q_s = \rho_d Q_d
$$

# Examples

```pycon
>>> ans = liquid_jet_pump(rhop=998., rhos=1098., Km=.186, Kd=0.12, Ks=0.11,
... Kp=0.04, d_mixing=0.045, Qs=0.01, Qp=.01, P2=133600,
... P5=200E3, nozzle_retracted=False, max_variations=10000)
>>> s = []
>>> for key, value in ans.items():
...     s.append('%s: %g' %(key, value))
>>> sorted(s)
['M: 1', 'N: 0.293473', 'P1: 426256', 'P2: 133600', 'P5: 200000', 'Qp: 0.01', 'Qs: 0.01', 'R: 0.247404', 'alpha: 1e-06', 'd_diffuser: 45', 'd_mixing: 0.045', 'd_nozzle: 0.0223829', 'efficiency: 0.293473']
```

# References

[^footnote-1]: Karassik, Igor J., Joseph P. Messina, Paul Cooper, and Charles C.
    Heald. Pump Handbook. 4th edition. New York: McGraw-Hill Education, 2007.

[^footnote-2]: Winoto S. H., Li H., and Shah D. A. "Efficiency of Jet Pumps."
    Journal of Hydraulic Engineering 126, no. 2 (February 1, 2000): 150-56.
    <https://doi.org/10.1061/(ASCE)0733-9429(2000)126:2(150>).

[^footnote-3]: Elmore, Emily, Khalid Al-Mutairi, Bilal Hussain, and A. Sheriff
    El-Gizawy. "Development of Analytical Model for Predicting Dual-Phase
    Ejector Performance," November 11, 2016, V007T09A013.

[^footnote-4]: Ejectors and Jet Pumps. Design and Performance for Incompressible
    Liquid Flow. 85032. ESDU International PLC, 1985.

functions.name: fluids.jet_pump.vacuum_air_leakage_Ryans_Croll
documentation:
Calculates an estimated leakage of air into a vessel using
a correlation from Ryans and Croll (1981) [^footnote-1] as given in [^footnote-2] and [^footnote-3].

if P < 10 torr:

$$
W = 0.026P^{0.34}V^{0.6}
$$

if P < 100 torr:

$$
W = 0.032P^{0.26}V^{0.6}
$$

else:

$$
W = 0.106V^{0.6}
$$

In the above equation, the units are lb/hour, torr (vacuum), and cubic feet;
they are converted in this function.

# Parameters

V

: Vessel volume, [m^3]

P

: Vessel actual absolute operating pressure - less than `P_atm`!, [Pa]

P_atm

: The atmospheric pressure surrounding the vessel, [Pa]

# Returns

m

: Air leakage flow rate, [kg/s]

# Notes

No limits are applied to this function.

# Examples

```pycon
>>> vacuum_air_leakage_Ryans_Croll(10, 10000)
0.0004512
```

# References

[^footnote-1]: Ryans, J. L. and Croll, S. "Selecting Vacuum Systems," 1981.

[^footnote-2]: Coker, Kayode. Ludwig's Applied Process Design for Chemical and
    Petrochemical Plants. 4 edition. Amsterdam ; Boston: Gulf Professional
    Publishing, 2007.

[^footnote-3]: Govoni, Patrick. "An Overview of Vacuum System Design"
    Chemical Engineering Magazine, September 2017.

functions.name: fluids.jet_pump.vacuum_air_leakage_Seider
documentation:
Calculates an estimated leakage of air into a vessel using
a correlation from Seider [^footnote-1].

$$
W = 5 + \left[ 0.0298 + 0.03088\ln P - 0.0005733(\ln P)^2 \right]V^{0.66}
$$

In the above equation, the units are lb/hour, torr (vacuum), and cubic feet;
they are converted in this function.

# Parameters

V

: Vessel volume, [m^3]

P

: Vessel actual absolute operating pressure - less than `P_atm`!, [Pa]

P_atm

: The atmospheric pressure surrounding the vessel, [Pa]

# Returns

m

: Air leakage flow rate, [kg/s]

# Notes

This formula is rough.

# Examples

```pycon
>>> vacuum_air_leakage_Seider(10, 10000)
0.0018775547
```

# References

[^footnote-1]: Seider, Warren D., J. D. Seader, and Daniel R. Lewin.
    Product and Process Design Principles: Synthesis, Analysis,
    and Evaluation. 2nd edition. New York: Wiley, 2003.

functions.name: fluids.jet_pump.vacuum_air_leakage_HEI2633
documentation:
Calculates an estimated leakage of air into a vessel using
fits to a graph of HEI-2633-00 for air leakage in commercially `tight`
vessels [^footnote-1].

There are 5 fits, for < 1 mmHg; 1-3 mmHg; 3-20 mmHg, 20-90 mmHg, and
90 mmHg to atmospheric. The fits are for `maximum` air leakage.

Actual values may be significantly larger or smaller depending on the
condition of the seals, manufacturing defects, and the application.

# Parameters

V

: Vessel volume, [m^3]

P

: Vessel actual absolute operating pressure - less than `P_atm`!, [Pa]

P_atm

: The atmospheric pressure surrounding the vessel, [Pa]

# Returns

m

: Air leakage flow rate, [kg/s]

# Notes

The volume is capped to 10 ft^3 on the low end, but extrapolation past
the maximum size of 10000 ft^3 is allowed.

It is believed {obj}`vacuum_air_leakage_Seider` was derived from this data,
so this function should be used in preference to it.

# Examples

```pycon
>>> vacuum_air_leakage_HEI2633(10, 10000)
0.001186252403781038
```

# References

[^footnote-1]: "Standards for Steam Jet Vacuum Systems", 5th Edition

functions.name: fluids.jet_pump.vacuum_air_leakage_Coker_Worthington
documentation:
Calculates an estimated leakage of air into a vessel using
a tabular lookup from Coker cited as being from Worthington Corp's
1955 Steam-Jet Ejector Application Handbook, Bulletin W-205-E21 [^footnote-1].

# Parameters

P

: Vessel actual absolute operating pressure - less than `P_atm`!, [Pa]

P_atm

: The atmospheric pressure surrounding the vessel, [Pa]

conservative

: Whether to use the high values or low values in the table, [-]

# Returns

m

: Air leakage flow rate, [kg/s]

# Notes

# Examples

```pycon
>>> vacuum_air_leakage_Coker_Worthington(10000)
0.005039915222222222
```

# References

[^footnote-1]: Coker, Kayode. Ludwig's Applied Process Design for Chemical and
    Petrochemical Plants. 4 edition. Amsterdam ; Boston: Gulf Professional
    Publishing, 2007.

functions.name: fluids.control_valve.cavitation_index
documentation:
Calculates the cavitation index of a valve with upstream and downstream
absolute pressures `P1` and `P2` for a fluid with a vapor pressure `Psat`.

$$
\sigma = \frac{P_1 - P_{sat}}{P_1 - P_2}
$$

# Parameters

P1

: Absolute pressure upstream of the valve [Pa]

P2

: Absolute pressure downstream of the valve [Pa]

Psat

: Saturation pressure of the liquid at inlet temperature [Pa]

# Returns

sigma

: Cavitation index of the valve [-]

# Notes

Larger values are safer. Models for adjusting cavitation indexes provided
by the manufacturer to the user's conditions are available, making use
of scaling the pressure differences and size differences.

Values can be calculated for incipient cavitation, constant cavitation,
maximum vibration cavitation, incipient damage, and choking cavitation.

Has also been defined as:

$$
\sigma = \frac{P_2 - P_{sat}}{P_1 - P_2}
$$

Another definition and notation series is:

$$
K = xF = \frac{1}{\sigma} = \frac{P_1 - P_2}{P_1 - P_{sat}}
$$

# Examples

```pycon
>>> cavitation_index(1E6, 8E5, 2E5)
4.0
```

# References

[^footnote-1]: ISA. "RP75.23 Considerations for Evaluating Control Valve
    Cavitation." 1995.

functions.name: fluids.control_valve.FF_critical_pressure_ratio_l
documentation:
Calculates FF, the liquid critical pressure ratio factor,
for use in IEC 60534 liquid valve sizing calculations.

$$
F_F = 0.96 - 0.28\sqrt{\frac{P_{sat}}{P_c}}
$$

# Parameters

Psat

: Saturation pressure of the liquid at inlet temperature [Pa]

Pc

: Critical pressure of the liquid [Pa]

# Returns

FF

: Liquid critical pressure ratio factor [-]

# Examples

From [^footnote-1], matching example.

```pycon
>>> FF_critical_pressure_ratio_l(70100.0, 22120000.0)
0.9442375225233299
```

# References

[^footnote-1]: IEC 60534-2-1 / ISA-75.01.01-2007

functions.name: fluids.control_valve.control_valve_choke_P_l
documentation:
Calculates either the upstream or downstream pressure at which choked
flow though a liquid control valve occurs, given either a set upstream or
downstream pressure. Implements an analytical solution of
the needed equations from the full function
{py:func}`~.size_control_valve_l`. For some pressures, no choked flow
is possible; for choked flow to occur the direction if flow must be
reversed. If `disp` is True, an exception will be raised for these
conditions.

$$
P_1 = \frac{F_{F} F_{L}^{2} P_{sat} - P_{2}}{F_{L}^{2} - 1}
$$

$$
P_2 = F_{F} F_{L}^{2} P_{sat} - F_{L}^{2} P_{1} + P_{1}
$$

# Parameters

Psat

: Saturation pressure of the liquid at inlet temperature [Pa]

Pc

: Critical pressure of the liquid [Pa]

FL

: Liquid pressure recovery factor of a control valve without attached
  fittings [-]

P1

: Absolute pressure upstream of the valve [Pa]

P2

: Absolute pressure downstream of the valve [Pa]

disp

: Whether or not to raise an exception on flow reversal, [-]

# Returns

P_choke

: Pressure at which a choke occurs in the liquid valve [Pa]

# Notes

Extremely cheap to compute.

# Examples

```pycon
>>> control_valve_choke_P_l(69682.89291024722, 22048320.0, 0.6, 680000.0)
458887.5306077305
>>> control_valve_choke_P_l(69682.89291024722, 22048320.0, 0.6, P2=458887.5306077305)
680000.0
```

functions.name: fluids.control_valve.control_valve_choke_P_g
documentation:
Calculates either the upstream or downstream pressure at which choked
flow though a gas control valve occurs, given either a set upstream or
downstream pressure. Implements an analytical solution of
the needed equations from the full function
{py:func}`~.size_control_valve_g`. A singularity arises as `xT` goes to 1
and `gamma` goes to 1.4.

$$
P_1 = - \frac{7 P_{2}}{5 \gamma x_T - 7}
$$

$$
P_2 = \frac{P_{1}}{7} \left(- 5 \gamma x_T + 7\right)
$$

# Parameters

xT

: Pressure difference ratio factor of a valve without fittings at choked
  flow [-]

gamma

: Specific heat capacity ratio [-]

P1

: Absolute pressure upstream of the valve [Pa]

P2

: Absolute pressure downstream of the valve [Pa]

# Returns

P_choke

: Pressure at which a choke occurs in the gas valve [Pa]

# Notes

Extremely cheap to compute.

# Examples

```pycon
>>> control_valve_choke_P_g(1.0, 1.3, 1E5)
7142.857142857143
>>> control_valve_choke_P_g(1.0, 1.3, P2=7142.857142857143)
100000.0
```

functions.name: fluids.control_valve.is_choked_turbulent_l
documentation:
Calculates if a liquid flow in IEC 60534 calculations is critical or
not, for use in IEC 60534 liquid valve sizing calculations.
Either FL may be provided or FLP and FP, depending on the calculation
process.

$$
\Delta P > F_L^2(P_1 - F_F P_{sat})
$$

$$
\Delta P >= \left(\frac{F_{LP}}{F_P}\right)^2(P_1 - F_F P_{sat})
$$

# Parameters

dP

: Differential pressure across the valve, with reducer/expanders [Pa]

P1

: Pressure of the fluid before the valve and reducers/expanders [Pa]

Psat

: Saturation pressure of the fluid at inlet temperature [Pa]

FF

: Liquid critical pressure ratio factor [-]

FL

: Liquid pressure recovery factor of a control valve without attached fittings [-]

FLP

: Combined liquid pressure recovery factor with piping geometry factor,
  for a control valve with attached fittings [-]

FP

: Piping geometry factor [-]

# Returns

choked

: Whether or not the flow is choked [-]

# Examples

```pycon
>>> is_choked_turbulent_l(460.0, 680.0, 70.1, 0.94, 0.9)
False
>>> is_choked_turbulent_l(460.0, 680.0, 70.1, 0.94, 0.6)
True
```

# References

[^footnote-1]: IEC 60534-2-1 / ISA-75.01.01-2007

functions.name: fluids.control_valve.is_choked_turbulent_g
documentation:
Calculates if a gas flow in IEC 60534 calculations is critical or
not, for use in IEC 60534 gas valve sizing calculations.
Either xT or xTP must be provided, depending on the calculation process.

$$
x \ge F_\gamma x_T
$$

$$
x \ge F_\gamma x_{TP}
$$

# Parameters

x

: Differential pressure over inlet pressure, [-]

Fgamma

: Specific heat ratio factor [-]

xT

: Pressure difference ratio factor of a valve without fittings at choked
  flow [-]

xTP

: Pressure difference ratio factor of a valve with fittings at choked
  flow [-]

# Returns

choked

: Whether or not the flow is choked [-]

# Examples

Example 3, compressible flow, non-choked with attached fittings:

```pycon
>>> is_choked_turbulent_g(0.544, 0.929, 0.6)
False
>>> is_choked_turbulent_g(0.544, 0.929, xTP=0.625)
False
```

# References

[^footnote-1]: IEC 60534-2-1 / ISA-75.01.01-2007

functions.name: fluids.control_valve.Reynolds_valve
documentation:
Calculates Reynolds number of a control valve for a liquid or gas
flowing through it at a specified Q, for a specified D1, FL, Fd, C, and
with kinematic viscosity `nu` according to IEC 60534 calculations.

$$
Re_v = \frac{N_4 F_d Q}{\nu \sqrt{C F_L}}\left(\frac{F_L^2 C^2} {N_2D^4} +1\right)^{1/4}
$$

# Parameters

nu

: Kinematic viscosity, [m^2/s]

Q

: Volumetric flow rate of the fluid [m^3/s]

D1

: Diameter of the pipe before the valve [m]

FL

: Liquid pressure recovery factor of a control valve without attached
  fittings []

Fd

: Valve style modifier [-]

C

: Metric Kv valve flow coefficient (flow rate of water at a pressure drop
  of 1 bar) [m^3/hr]

# Returns

Rev

: Valve reynolds number [-]

# Examples

```pycon
>>> Reynolds_valve(3.26e-07, 360, 150.0, 0.9, 0.46, 165)
2966984.7525455453
```

# References

[^footnote-1]: IEC 60534-2-1 / ISA-75.01.01-2007

functions.name: fluids.control_valve.loss_coefficient_piping
documentation:
Calculates the sum of loss coefficients from possible
inlet/outlet reducers/expanders around a control valve according to
IEC 60534 calculations.

$$
\Sigma \xi = \xi_1 + \xi_2 + \xi_{B1} - \xi_{B2}
$$

$$
\xi_1 = 0.5\left[1 -\left(\frac{d}{D_1}\right)^2\right]^2
$$

$$
\xi_2 = 1.0\left[1 -\left(\frac{d}{D_2}\right)^2\right]^2
$$

$$
\xi_{B1} = 1 - \left(\frac{d}{D_1}\right)^4
$$

$$
\xi_{B2} = 1 - \left(\frac{d}{D_2}\right)^4
$$

# Parameters

d

: Diameter of the valve [m]

D1

: Diameter of the pipe before the valve [m]

D2

: Diameter of the pipe after the valve [m]

# Returns

loss

: Sum of the four loss coefficients [-]

# Examples

In example 3, non-choked compressible flow with fittings:

```pycon
>>> loss_coefficient_piping(0.05, 0.08, 0.1)
0.6580810546875
```

# References

[^footnote-1]: IEC 60534-2-1 / ISA-75.01.01-2007

functions.name: fluids.control_valve.Reynolds_factor
documentation:
Calculates the Reynolds number factor `FR` for a valve with a Reynolds
number `Rev`, diameter `d`, flow coefficient `C`, liquid pressure recovery
factor `FL`, and with either full or reduced trim, all according to
IEC 60534 calculations.

If full trim:

$$
F_{R,1a} = 1 + \left(\frac{0.33F_L^{0.5}}{n_1^{0.25}}\right)\log_{10} \left(\frac{Re_v}{10000}\right)
$$

$$
F_{R,2} = \min(\frac{0.026}{F_L}\sqrt{n_1 Re_v},\; 1)
$$

$$
n_1 = \frac{N_2}{\left(\frac{C}{d^2}\right)^2}
$$

$$
F_R = F_{R,2} \text{ if Rev < 10 else } \min(F_{R,1a}, F_{R,2})
$$

Otherwise :

$$
F_{R,3a} = 1 + \left(\frac{0.33F_L^{0.5}}{n_2^{0.25}}\right)\log_{10} \left(\frac{Re_v}{10000}\right)
$$

$$
F_{R,4} = \frac{0.026}{F_L}\sqrt{n_2 Re_v}
$$

$$
n_2 = 1 + N_{32}\left(\frac{C}{d}\right)^{2/3}
$$

$$
F_R = F_{R,4} \text{ if Rev < 10 else } \min(F_{R,3a}, F_{R,4})
$$

# Parameters

FL

: Liquid pressure recovery factor of a control valve without attached
  fittings []

C

: Metric Kv valve flow coefficient (flow rate of water at a pressure drop
  of 1 bar) [m^3/hr]

d

: Diameter of the valve [m]

Rev

: Valve reynolds number [-]

full_trim

: Whether or not the valve has full trim

# Returns

FR

: Reynolds number factor for laminar or transitional flow []

# Examples

In Example 4, compressible flow with small flow trim sized for gas flow
(Cv in the problem was converted to Kv here to make FR match with N32, N2):

```pycon
>>> Reynolds_factor(FL=0.98, C=0.015483, d=15., Rev=1202., full_trim=False)
0.7148753122302025
```

# References

[^footnote-1]: IEC 60534-2-1 / ISA-75.01.01-2007

functions.name: fluids.control_valve.size_control_valve_l
documentation:
Calculates flow coefficient of a control valve passing a liquid
according to IEC 60534. Uses a large number of inputs in SI units. Note the
return value is not standard SI. All parameters are required.
This sizing model does not officially apply to liquid mixtures, slurries,
non-Newtonian fluids, or liquid-solid conveyance systems. For details
of the calculations, consult [^footnote-1].

# Parameters

rho

: Density of the liquid at the inlet [kg/m^3]

Psat

: Saturation pressure of the fluid at inlet temperature [Pa]

Pc

: Critical pressure of the fluid [Pa]

mu

: Viscosity of the fluid [Pa\*s]

P1

: Inlet pressure of the fluid before valves and reducers [Pa]

P2

: Outlet pressure of the fluid after valves and reducers [Pa]

Q

: Volumetric flow rate of the fluid [m^3/s]

D1

: Diameter of the pipe before the valve [m]

D2

: Diameter of the pipe after the valve [m]

d

: Diameter of the valve [m]

FL

: Liquid pressure recovery factor of a control valve without attached
  fittings (normally 0.8-0.9 at full open and decreasing as opened
  further to below 0.5; use default very cautiously!) []

Fd

: Valve style modifier (0.1 to 1; varies tremendously depending on the
  type of valve and position; do not use the default at all!) []

allow_choked

: Overrides the automatic transition into the choked regime if this is
  False and returns as if choked flow does not exist

allow_laminar

: Overrides the automatic transition into the laminar regime if this is
  False and returns as if laminar flow does not exist

full_output

: If True, returns intermediate calculation values as
  well as Kv in the form of a dictionary containing 'Kv', 'Rev', 'choked',
  'FL', 'FLP', 'FR', 'FP', and 'laminar'. Some may be None if they are
  not used in the calculation.

# Returns

Kv

: Metric Kv valve flow coefficient (flow rate of water at a pressure drop
  of 1 bar) [m^3/hr]

# Notes

It is possible to use this model without any diameters specified; in that
case, turbulent flow is assumed. Choked flow can still be modeled. This is
not recommended. All three diameters need to be None for this to work.
`FL` and `Fd` are not used by the models when the diameters are not
specified.

# Examples

From [^footnote-1], matching example 1 for a globe, parabolic plug,
flow-to-open valve.

```pycon
>>> size_control_valve_l(rho=965.4, Psat=70.1E3, Pc=22120E3, mu=3.1472E-4,
... P1=680E3, P2=220E3, Q=0.1, D1=0.15, D2=0.15, d=0.15,
... FL=0.9, Fd=0.46)
164.9954763704956
```

From [^footnote-1], matching example 2 for a ball, segmented ball,
flow-to-open valve.

```pycon
>>> size_control_valve_l(rho=965.4, Psat=70.1E3, Pc=22120E3, mu=3.1472E-4,
... P1=680E3, P2=220E3, Q=0.1, D1=0.1, D2=0.1, d=0.1,
... FL=0.6, Fd=0.98)
238.05817216710483
```

# References

[^footnote-1]: IEC 60534-2-1 / ISA-75.01.01-2007

functions.name: fluids.control_valve.size_control_valve_g
documentation:
Calculates flow coefficient of a control valve passing a gas
according to IEC 60534. Uses a large number of inputs in SI units. Note the
return value is not standard SI. All parameters are required. For details
of the calculations, consult [^footnote-1]. Note the inlet gas flow conditions.

# Parameters

T

: Temperature of the gas at the inlet [K]

MW

: Molecular weight of the gas [g/mol]

mu

: Viscosity of the fluid at inlet conditions [Pa\*s]

gamma

: Specific heat capacity ratio [-]

Z

: Compressibility factor at inlet conditions, [-]

P1

: Inlet pressure of the gas before valves and reducers [Pa]

P2

: Outlet pressure of the gas after valves and reducers [Pa]

Q

: Volumetric flow rate of the gas at *273.15 K* and 1 atm specifically
  [m^3/s]

D1

: Diameter of the pipe before the valve [m]

D2

: Diameter of the pipe after the valve [m]

d

: Diameter of the valve [m]

FL

: Liquid pressure recovery factor of a control valve without attached
  fittings (normally 0.8-0.9 at full open and decreasing as opened
  further to below 0.5; use default very cautiously!) []

Fd

: Valve style modifier (0.1 to 1; varies tremendously depending on the
  type of valve and position; do not use the default at all!) []

xT

: Pressure difference ratio factor of a valve without fittings at choked
  flow (increasing to 0.9 or higher as the valve is closed further and
  decreasing to 0.1 or lower as the valve is opened further; use default
  very cautiously!) [-]

allow_choked

: Overrides the automatic transition into the choked regime if this is
  False and returns as if choked flow does not exist

allow_laminar

: Overrides the automatic transition into the laminar regime if this is
  False and returns as if laminar flow does not exist

full_output

: If True, returns intermediate calculation values as
  well as Kv in the form of a dictionary containing 'Kv', 'Rev', 'choked',
  'Y', 'FR', 'FP', 'xTP', and 'laminar'. Some may be None if they are
  not used in the calculation.

# Returns

Kv

: Metric Kv valve flow coefficient (flow rate of water at a pressure drop
  of 1 bar) [m^3/hr]

# Notes

It is possible to use this model without any diameters specified; in that
case, turbulent flow is assumed. Choked flow can still be modeled. This is
not recommended. All three diameters need to be None for this to work.
`FL` and `Fd` are not used by the models when the diameters are not
specified, but `xT` definitely is used by the model.

When this model does not converge, the result is normally because of the
specified delta P being less than that caused by the piping diameter
changes.

# Examples

From [^footnote-1], matching example 3 for non-choked gas flow with attached
fittings and a rotary, eccentric plug, flow-to-open control valve:

```pycon
>>> size_control_valve_g(T=433., MW=44.01, mu=1.4665E-4, gamma=1.30,
... Z=0.988, P1=680E3, P2=310E3, Q=38/36., D1=0.08, D2=0.1, d=0.05,
... FL=0.85, Fd=0.42, xT=0.60)
72.5866454539105
```

From [^footnote-1], roughly matching example 4 for a small flow trim sized tapered
needle plug valve. Difference is 3% and explained by the difference in
algorithms used.

```pycon
>>> size_control_valve_g(T=320., MW=39.95, mu=5.625E-5, gamma=1.67, Z=1.0,
... P1=2.8E5, P2=1.3E5, Q=0.46/3600., D1=0.015, D2=0.015, d=0.015, FL=0.98,
... Fd=0.07, xT=0.8)
0.016498765335995726
```

# References

[^footnote-1]: IEC 60534-2-1 / ISA-75.01.01-2007

functions.name: fluids.control_valve.convert_flow_coefficient
documentation:
Convert from one flow coefficient scale to another; supports the `Kv`
`Cv`, and `Av` scales.

Other scales are `Qn` and `Cg`, but clear definitions have yet to be
found.

# Parameters

flow_coefficient

: Value of the flow coefficient to be converted, expressed in the
  original scale.

old_scale

: String specifying the original scale; one of 'Av', 'Cv', or 'Kv', [-]

new_scale

: String specifying the new scale; one of 'Av', 'Cv', or 'Kv', [-]

# Returns

converted_flow_coefficient

: Flow coefficient converted to the specified scale.

# Notes

`Qn` is a scale based on a flow of air in units of L/minute as air travels
through a valve and loses one bar of pressure (initially 7 bar absolute,
to 6 bar absolute). No consistent conversion factors have been found and
those from theory do not match what have been found. Some uses of `Qn` use
its flow rate as in normal (STP reference conditions) flow rate of air;
others use something like the 7 bar absolute condition.

# Examples

```pycon
>>> convert_flow_coefficient(10, 'Kv', 'Av')
0.0002776532068951358
```

functions.name: fluids.control_valve.control_valve_noise_l_2015
documentation:
Calculates the sound made by a liquid flowing through a control valve
according to the standard IEC 60534-8-4 (2015) [^footnote-1].

# Parameters

m

: Mass flow rate of liquid through the control valve, [kg/s]

P1

: Inlet pressure of the fluid before valves and reducers [Pa]

P2

: Outlet pressure of the fluid after valves and reducers [Pa]

Psat

: Saturation pressure of the fluid at inlet temperature [Pa]

rho

: Density of the liquid at the inlet [kg/m^3]

c

: Speed of sound of the liquid at the inlet conditions [m/s]

Kv

: Metric Kv valve flow coefficient (flow rate of water at a pressure drop
  of 1 bar) [m^3/hr]

d

: Diameter of the valve [m]

Di

: Internal diameter of the pipe before and after the valve [m]

FL

: Liquid pressure recovery factor of a control valve without attached
  fittings (normally 0.8-0.9 at full open and decreasing as opened
  further to below 0.5) [-]

Fd

: Valve style modifier [-]

t_pipe

: Wall thickness of the pipe after the valve, [m]

rho_pipe

: Density of the pipe wall material at flowing conditions, [kg/m^3]

c_pipe

: Speed of sound of the pipe wall material at flowing conditions, [m/s]

rho_air

: Density of the air surrounding the valve and pipe wall, [kg/m^3]

c_air

: Speed of sound of the air surrounding the valve and pipe wall, [m/s]

xFz

: If specified, this value `xFz` is used instead of estimated; the
  calculation is sensitive to this value, [-]

An

: Valve correction factor for acoustic efficiency

# Returns

LpAe1m

: A weighted sound pressure level 1 m from the pipe wall, 1 m distance
  dowstream of the valve (at reference sound pressure level 2E-5), [dB]

# Notes

For formulas see [^footnote-1]. This takes on the order of 100 us to compute.
This model can also tell if noise is being produced in a valve just due to
turbulent flow, or cavitation. For values of `An`, see [^footnote-1]; it is
normally -4.6 for global valves, -4.3 for butterfly valves, and -4.0 for
expanders.

This model was checked against three examples in [^footnote-1]; they match to all
given decimals.

A formula is given in [^footnote-1] for multihole trim valves to estimate `xFz`
as well; this is not implemented here and `xFz` must be calculated by the
user separately. The formula is

$$
x_{Fz} = \left(4.5 + 1650\frac{N_0d_H^2}{F_L}\right)^{-1/2}
$$

Where `N0` is the number of open channels and `dH` is the multihole trim
hole diameter.

# Examples

```pycon
>>> control_valve_noise_l_2015(m=40, P1=1E6, P2=6.5E5, Psat=2.32E3,
... rho=997, c=1400, Kv=77.848, d=0.1, Di=0.1071, FL=0.92, Fd=0.42,
... t_pipe=0.0036, rho_pipe=7800.0, c_pipe=5000.0,rho_air=1.293,
... c_air=343.0, An=-4.6)
81.58200097996
```

# References

[^footnote-1]: IEC 60534-8-4 : Industrial-Process Control Valves - Part 8-4: Noise
    Considerations - Prediction of Noise Generated by Hydrodynamic Flow.
    (2015)

functions.name: fluids.control_valve.control_valve_noise_g_2011
documentation:
Calculates the sound made by a gas flowing through a control valve
according to the standard IEC 60534-8-3 (2011) [^footnote-1].

# Parameters

m

: Mass flow rate of gas through the control valve, [kg/s]

P1

: Inlet pressure of the gas before valves and reducers [Pa]

P2

: Outlet pressure of the gas after valves and reducers [Pa]

T1

: Inlet gas temperature, [K]

rho

: Density of the gas at the inlet [kg/m^3]

gamma

: Specific heat capacity ratio [-]

MW

: Molecular weight of the gas [g/mol]

Kv

: Metric Kv valve flow coefficient (flow rate of water at a pressure drop
  of 1 bar) [m^3/hr]

d

: Diameter of the valve [m]

Di

: Internal diameter of the pipe before and after the valve [m]

t_pipe

: Wall thickness of the pipe after the valve, [m]

Fd

: Valve style modifier (0.1 to 1; varies tremendously depending on the
  type of valve and position; do not use the default at all!) [-]

FL

: Liquid pressure recovery factor of a control valve without attached
  fittings (normally 0.8-0.9 at full open and decreasing as opened
  further to below 0.5; use default very cautiously!) [-]

FLP

: Combined liquid pressure recovery factor with piping geometry factor,
  for a control valve with attached fittings [-]

FP

: Piping geometry factor [-]

rho_pipe

: Density of the pipe wall material at flowing conditions, [kg/m^3]

c_pipe

: Speed of sound of the pipe wall material at flowing conditions, [m/s]

P_air

: Pressure of the air surrounding the valve and pipe wall, [Pa]

rho_air

: Density of the air surrounding the valve and pipe wall, [kg/m^3]

c_air

: Speed of sound of the air surrounding the valve and pipe wall, [m/s]

An

: Valve correction factor for acoustic efficiency, [-]

Stp

: Strouhal number at the peak `fp`; between 0.1 and 0.3 typically, [-]

T2

: Outlet gas temperature; assumed `T1` if not provided (a PH flash
  should be used to obtain this if possible), [K]

beta

: Valve outlet / expander inlet contraction coefficient, [-]

# Returns

LpAe1m

: A-weighted sound pressure level 1 m from the pipe wall, 1 m distance
  dowstream of the valve (at reference sound pressure level 2E-5), [dB]

# Notes

For formulas see [^footnote-1]. This takes on the order of 100 us to compute.
For values of `An`, see [^footnote-1].

This model was checked against six examples in [^footnote-1]; they match to all
given decimals.

Several additional formulas are given for multihole trim valves,
control valves with two or more fixed area stages, and multipath,
multistage trim valves.

# Examples

```pycon
>>> control_valve_noise_g_2011(m=2.22, P1=1E6, P2=7.2E5, T1=450, rho=5.3,
... gamma=1.22, MW=19.8, Kv=77.85,  d=0.1, Di=0.2031, FL=None, FLP=0.792,
... FP=0.98, Fd=0.296, t_pipe=0.008, rho_pipe=8000.0, c_pipe=5000.0,
... rho_air=1.293, c_air=343.0, An=-3.8, Stp=0.2)
91.67702674629604
```

# References

[^footnote-1]: IEC 60534-8-3 : Industrial-Process Control Valves - Part 8-3: Noise
    Considerations - Control Valve Aerodynamic Noise Prediction Method."

This module contains correlations and functions for calculating pressure drop
from packings and demisters; separation efficiency of demisters; demister
pressure drop; and demister geometry.

For reporting bugs, adding feature requests, or submitting pull requests,
please use the [GitHub issue tracker](https://github.com/CalebBell/fluids/)
or contact the author at <mailto:Caleb.Andrew.Bell@gmail.com>.

```{contents}
:local: true
```

functions.name: fluids.packed_tower.dP_demister_dry_Setekleiv_Svendsen
documentation:
Calculates dry pressure drop across a demister, using the
correlation in [^footnote-1]. This model is for dry demisters with no holdup only.

$$
\frac{\Delta P \epsilon^2}{\rho_f v^2} = 10.29 - \frac{565} {69.6SL - (SL)^2 - 779} - \frac{74.9}{160.9 - 4.85SL} + 45.33\left( \frac{\mu_f \epsilon S^2 L}{\rho_f v}\right)^{0.75}
$$

# Parameters

S

: Specific area of the demister, normally ~250-1000 [m^2/m^3]

voidage

: Voidage of bed of the demister material, normally ~0.98 []

vs

: Superficial velocity of fluid, Q/A [m/s]

rho

: Density of fluid [kg/m^3]

mu

: Viscosity of fluid [Pa\*s]

L

: Length of the demister [m]

# Returns

dP

: Pressure drop across a dry demister [Pa]

# Notes

Useful at startup and in modeling. Dry pressure drop is normally negligible
compared to wet pressure drop. Coefficients obtained by evolutionary
programming and may not fit data outside of the limits of the variables.

# Examples

```pycon
>>> dP_demister_dry_Setekleiv_Svendsen(S=250, voidage=.983, vs=1.2, rho=10, mu=3E-5, L=1)
320.3280788941329
```

# References

[^footnote-1]: Setekleiv, A. Eddie, and Hallvard F. Svendsen. "Dry Pressure Drop in
    Spiral Wound Wire Mesh Pads at Low and Elevated Pressures." Chemical
    Engineering Research and Design 109 (May 2016): 141-149.
    doi:10.1016/j.cherd.2016.01.019.
## Demister

functions.name: fluids.packed_tower.dP_demister_dry_Setekleiv_Svendsen_lit
documentation:
Calculates dry pressure drop across a demister, using the
correlation in [^footnote-1]. This model is for dry demisters with no holdup only.
Developed with literature data included as well as their own experimental
data.

$$
\frac{\Delta P \epsilon^2}{\rho_f v^2} = 7.3 - \frac{320} {69.6SL - (SL)^2 - 779} - \frac{52.4}{161 - 4.85SL} + 27.2\left( \frac{\mu_f \epsilon S^2 L}{\rho_f v}\right)^{0.75}
$$

# Parameters

S

: Specific area of the demister, normally ~250-1000 [m^2/m^3]

voidage

: Voidage of bed of the demister material, normally ~0.98 []

vs

: Superficial velocity of fluid, Q/A [m/s]

rho

: Density of fluid [kg/m^3]

mu

: Viscosity of fluid [Pa\*s]

L

: Length of the demister [m]

# Returns

dP

: Pressure drop across a dry demister [Pa]

# Notes

Useful at startup and in modeling. Dry pressure drop is normally negligible
compared to wet pressure drop. Coefficients obtained by evolutionary
programming and may not fit data outside of the limits of the variables.

# Examples

```pycon
>>> dP_demister_dry_Setekleiv_Svendsen_lit(S=250, voidage=.983, vs=1.2, rho=10, mu=3E-5, L=1.0)
209.083848658307
```

# References

[^footnote-1]: Setekleiv, A. Eddie, and Hallvard F. Svendsen. "Dry Pressure Drop in
    Spiral Wound Wire Mesh Pads at Low and Elevated Pressures." Chemical
    Engineering Research and Design 109 (May 2016): 141-149.
    doi:10.1016/j.cherd.2016.01.019.

functions.name: fluids.packed_tower.dP_demister_wet_ElDessouky
documentation:
Calculates wet pressure drop across a demister, using the
correlation in [^footnote-1]. Uses only their own experimental data.

$$
\frac{\Delta P}{L} = 0.002357(1-\epsilon)^{0.375798}(V)^{0.81317} (d_w)^{-1.56114147}
$$

# Parameters

vs

: Superficial velocity of fluid, Q/A [m/s]

voidage

: Voidage of bed of the demister material, normally ~0.98 []

d_wire

: Diameter of mesh wire,[m]

L

: Length of the demister [m]

# Returns

dP

: Pressure drop across a dry demister [Pa]

# Notes

No dependency on the liquid properties is included here. Because of the
exponential nature of the correlation, the limiting pressure drop as V
is lowered is 0 Pa. A dry pressure drop correlation should be compared with
results from this at low velocities, and the larger of the
two pressure drops used.

The correlation in [^footnote-1] was presented as follows, with wire diameter in
units of mm, density in kg/m^3, V in m/s, and dP in Pa/m.

$$
\Delta P = 3.88178(\rho_{mesh})^{0.375798}(V)^{0.81317} (d_w)^{-1.56114147}
$$

Here, the correlation is converted to base SI units and to use voidage;
not all demisters are stainless steel as in [^footnote-1]. A density of 7999 kg/m^3
was used in the conversion.

In [^footnote-1], V ranged from 0.98-7.5 m/s, rho from 80.317-208.16 kg/m^3, depth
from 100 to 200 mm, wire diameter of 0.2mm to 0.32 mm, and particle
diameter from 1 to 5 mm.

# Examples

```pycon
>>> dP_demister_wet_ElDessouky(6, 0.978, 0.00032)
688.9216420105029
```

# References

[^footnote-1]: El-Dessouky, Hisham T, Imad M Alatiqi, Hisham M Ettouney, and Noura
    S Al-Deffeeri. "Performance of Wire Mesh Mist Eliminator." Chemical
    Engineering and Processing: Process Intensification 39, no. 2 (March
    2000): 129-39. doi:10.1016/S0255-2701(99)00033-1.

functions.name: fluids.packed_tower.separation_demister_ElDessouky
documentation:
Calculates droplet removal by a demister as a fraction from 0 to 1,
using the correlation in [^footnote-1]. Uses only their own experimental data.

$$
\eta = 0.85835(d_w)^{-0.28264}(1-\epsilon)^{0.099625}(V)^{0.106878} (d_p)^{0.383197}
$$

# Parameters

vs

: Superficial velocity of fluid, Q/A [m/s]

voidage

: Voidage of bed of the demister material, normally ~0.98 []

d_wire

: Diameter of mesh wire,[m]

d_drop

: Drop diameter, [m]

# Returns

eta

: Fraction droplets removed by mass [-]

# Notes

No dependency on the liquid properties is included here. Because of the
exponential nature of the correlation, for smaller diameters separation
quickly lowers. This correlation can predict a separation larger than 1
for higher velocities, lower voidages, lower wire diameters, and large
droplet sizes. This function truncates these larger values to 1.

The correlation in [^footnote-1] was presented as follows, with wire diameter in
units of mm, density in kg/m^3, V in m/s, separation in %, and particle
diameter in mm.

$$
\eta = 17.5047(d_w)^{-0.28264}(\rho_{mesh})^{0.099625}(V)^{0.106878} (d_p)^{0.383197}
$$

Here, the correlation is converted to base SI units and to use voidage;
not all demisters are stainless steel as in [^footnote-1]. A density of 7999 kg/m^3
was used in the conversion.

In [^footnote-1], V ranged from 0.98-7.5 m/s, rho from 80.317-208.16 kg/m^3, depth
from 100 to 200 mm, wire diameter of 0.2 mm to 0.32 mm, and particle
diameter from 1 to 5 mm.

# Examples

```pycon
>>> separation_demister_ElDessouky(1.35, 0.974, 0.0002, 0.005)
0.8982892997640582
```

# References

[^footnote-1]: El-Dessouky, Hisham T, Imad M Alatiqi, Hisham M Ettouney, and Noura
    S Al-Deffeeri. "Performance of Wire Mesh Mist Eliminator." Chemical
    Engineering and Processing: Process Intensification 39, no. 2 (March
    2000): 129-39. doi:10.1016/S0255-2701(99)00033-1.

functions.name: fluids.packed_tower.voidage_experimental
documentation:
Calculates voidage of a bed or mesh given an experimental weight and
fixed density, diameter, and height, as shown in [^footnote-1]. The formula is also
self-evident.

$$
\epsilon = 1 - \frac{\frac{m_{mesh}}{\frac{\pi}{4}d_{column}^2 L_{mesh}}}{\rho_{material}}
$$

# Parameters

m

: Mass of mesh or bed particles weighted, [kg]

rho

: Density of solid particles or mesh [kg/m^3]

D

: Diameter of the cylindrical bed [m]

H

: Height of the demister or bed [m]

# Returns

voidage

: Voidage of bed of the material []

# Notes

Should be trusted over manufacturer data.

# Examples

```pycon
>>> voidage_experimental(m=126, rho=8000, D=1, H=1)
0.9799464771704212
```

# References

[^footnote-1]: Helsør, T., and H. Svendsen. "Experimental Characterization of
    Pressure Drop in Dry Demisters at Low and Elevated Pressures." Chemical
    Engineering Research and Design 85, no. 3 (2007): 377-85.
    doi:10.1205/cherd06048.

functions.name: fluids.packed_tower.specific_area_mesh
documentation:
Calculates the specific area of a wire mesh, as used in demisters or
filters. Shown in [^footnote-1], and also self-evident and non-empirical.
Makes the ideal assumption that wires never touch.

$$
S = \frac{4(1-\epsilon)}{d_{wire}}
$$

# Parameters

voidage

: Voidage of the mesh []

d

: Diameter of the wires making the mesh, [m]

# Returns

S

: Specific area of the mesh [m^2/m^3]

# Notes

Should be preferred over manufacturer data. Can also be used to show that
manufacturer's data is inconsistent with their claimed voidage and wire
diameter.

# Examples

```pycon
>>> specific_area_mesh(voidage=.934, d=3e-4)
879.9999999999994
```

# References

[^footnote-1]: Helsør, T., and H. Svendsen. "Experimental Characterization of
    Pressure Drop in Dry Demisters at Low and Elevated Pressures." Chemical
    Engineering Research and Design 85, no. 3 (2007): 377-85.
    doi:10.1205/cherd06048.

functions.name: fluids.packed_tower.Stichlmair_dry
documentation:
Calculates dry pressure drop across a packed column, using the
Stichlmair [^footnote-1] correlation. Uses three regressed constants for each
type of packing, and voidage and specific area.

Pressure drop is given by:

$$
\Delta P_{dry} = \frac{3}{4} f_0 \frac{1-\epsilon}{\epsilon^{4.65}} \rho_G \frac{H}{d_p}V_g^2
$$

$$
f_0 = \frac{C_1}{Re_g} + \frac{C_2}{Re_g^{0.5}} + C_3
$$

$$
d_p = \frac{6(1-\epsilon)}{a}
$$

# Parameters

Vg

: Superficial velocity of gas, Q/A [m/s]

rhog

: Density of gas [kg/m^3]

mug

: Viscosity of gas [Pa\*s]

voidage

: Voidage of bed of packing material []

specific_area

: Specific area of the packing material [m^2/m^3]

C1

: Packing-specific constant []

C2

: Packing-specific constant []

C3

: Packing-specific constant []

H

: Height of packing [m]

# Returns

dP_dry

: Pressure drop across dry packing [Pa]

# Notes

This model is used by most process simulation tools. If H is not provided,
it defaults to 1. If Z is not provided, it defaults to 1.

# Examples

```pycon
>>> Stichlmair_dry(Vg=0.4, rhog=5., mug=5E-5, voidage=0.68,
... specific_area=260., C1=32., C2=7.0, C3=1.0)
236.80904286559885
```

# References

[^footnote-1]: Stichlmair, J., J. L. Bravo, and J. R. Fair. "General Model for
    Prediction of Pressure Drop and Capacity of Countercurrent Gas/liquid
    Packed Columns." Gas Separation & Purification 3, no. 1 (March 1989):
    19-28. doi:10.1016/0950-4214(89)80016-7.
## Packing

functions.name: fluids.packed_tower.Stichlmair_wet
documentation:
Calculates dry pressure drop across a packed column, using the
Stichlmair [^footnote-1] correlation. Uses three regressed constants for each
type of packing, and voidage and specific area. This model is for irrigated
columns only.

Pressure drop is given by:

$$
\frac{\Delta P_{irr}}{H} = \frac{\Delta P_{dry}}{H}\left(\frac {1-\epsilon + h_T}{1-\epsilon}\right)^{(2+c)/3} \left(\frac{\epsilon}{\epsilon-h_T}\right)^{4.65}
$$

$$
h_T = h_0\left[1 + 20\left(\frac{\Delta Pirr}{H\rho_L g}\right)^2\right]
$$

$$
Fr_L = \frac{V_L^2 a}{g \epsilon^{4.65}}
$$

$$
h_0 = 0.555 Fr_L^{1/3}
$$

$$
c = \frac{-C_1/Re_g - C_2/(2Re_g^{0.5})}{f_0}
$$

$$
\Delta P_{dry} = \frac{3}{4} f_0 \frac{1-\epsilon}{\epsilon^{4.65}} \rho_G \frac{H}{d_p}V_g^2
$$

$$
f_0 = \frac{C_1}{Re_g} + \frac{C_2}{Re_g^{0.5}} + C_3
$$

$$
d_p = \frac{6(1-\epsilon)}{a}
$$

# Parameters

Vg

: Superficial velocity of gas, Q/A [m/s]

Vl

: Superficial velocity of liquid, Q/A [m/s]

rhog

: Density of gas [kg/m^3]

rhol

: Density of liquid [kg/m^3]

mug

: Viscosity of gas [Pa\*s]

voidage

: Voidage of bed of packing material []

specific_area

: Specific area of the packing material [m^2/m^3]

C1

: Packing-specific constant []

C2

: Packing-specific constant []

C3

: Packing-specific constant []

H

: Height of packing [m]

# Returns

dP

: Pressure drop across irrigated packing [Pa]

# Notes

This model is used by most process simulation tools. If H is not provided,
it defaults to 1. If Z is not provided, it defaults to 1.
A numerical solver is used and needed by this model. Its initial guess
is the dry pressure drop. Convergence problems may occur.
The model as described in [^footnote-1] appears to have a typo, and could not match
the example. As described in [^footnote-2], however, the model works.

# Examples

Example is from [^footnote-1], matches.

```pycon
>>> Stichlmair_wet(Vg=0.4, Vl = 5E-3, rhog=5., rhol=1200., mug=5E-5,
... voidage=0.68, specific_area=260., C1=32., C2=7., C3=1.)
539.876823725352
```

# References

[^footnote-1]: Stichlmair, J., J. L. Bravo, and J. R. Fair. "General Model for
    Prediction of Pressure Drop and Capacity of Countercurrent Gas/liquid
    Packed Columns." Gas Separation & Purification 3, no. 1 (March 1989):
    19-28. doi:10.1016/0950-4214(89)80016-7.

[^footnote-2]: Piche, Simon R., Faical Larachi, and Bernard P. A. Grandjean.
    "Improving the Prediction of Irrigated Pressure Drop in Packed
    Absorption Towers." The Canadian Journal of Chemical Engineering 79,
    no. 4 (August 1, 2001): 584-94. doi:10.1002/cjce.5450790417.

functions.name: fluids.packed_tower._Stichlmair_flood_f
documentation:
Internal function which calculates the errors of the two Stichlmair
objective functions, and their jacobian.

functions.name: fluids.packed_tower._Stichlmair_flood_f_and_jac
documentation:
Internal function which calculates the errors of the two Stichlmair
objective functions, and their jacobian.

Derived using SymPy on the main flooding function.

functions.name: fluids.packed_tower.Stichlmair_flood
documentation:
Calculates gas rate for flooding of a packed column, using the
Stichlmair [^footnote-1] correlation. Uses three regressed constants for each
type of packing, and voidage and specific area.

Pressure drop is given by:

$$
\frac{\Delta P_{irr}}{H} = \frac{\Delta P_{dry}}{H}\left(\frac {1-\epsilon + h_T}{1-\epsilon}\right)^{(2+c)/3} \left(\frac{\epsilon}{\epsilon-h_T}\right)^{4.65}
$$

$$
h_T = h_0\left[1 + 20\left(\frac{\Delta Pirr}{H\rho_L g}\right)^2\right]
$$

$$
Fr_L = \frac{V_L^2 a}{g \epsilon^{4.65}}
$$

$$
h_0 = 0.555 Fr_L^{1/3}
$$

$$
c = \frac{-C_1/Re_g - C_2/(2Re_g^{0.5})}{f_0}
$$

$$
\Delta P_{dry} = \frac{3}{4} f_0 \frac{1-\epsilon}{\epsilon^{4.65}} \rho_G \frac{H}{d_p}V_g^2
$$

$$
f_0 = \frac{C_1}{Re_g} + \frac{C_2}{Re_g^{0.5}} + C_3
$$

$$
d_p = \frac{6(1-\epsilon)}{a}
$$

# Parameters

Vl

: Superficial velocity of liquid, Q/A [m/s]

rhog

: Density of gas [kg/m^3]

rhol

: Density of liquid [kg/m^3]

mug

: Viscosity of gas [Pa\*s]

voidage

: Voidage of bed of packing material []

specific_area

: Specific area of the packing material [m^2/m^3]

C1

: Packing-specific constant []

C2

: Packing-specific constant []

C3

: Packing-specific constant []

H

: Height of packing [m]

# Returns

Vg

: Superficial velocity of gas, Q/A [m/s]

# Notes

A numerical solver is used to solve this model.

# Examples

Example is from [^footnote-1], matches.

```pycon
>>> Stichlmair_flood(Vl = 5E-3, rhog=5., rhol=1200., mug=5E-5,
... voidage=0.68, specific_area=260., C1=32., C2=7., C3=1.)
0.6394323542746928
```

# References

[^footnote-1]: Stichlmair, J., J. L. Bravo, and J. R. Fair. "General Model for
    Prediction of Pressure Drop and Capacity of Countercurrent Gas/liquid
    Packed Columns." Gas Separation & Purification 3, no. 1 (March 1989):
    19-28. doi:10.1016/0950-4214(89)80016-7.

functions.name: fluids.packed_tower.Robbins
documentation:
Calculates pressure drop across a packed column, using the Robbins
equation.

Pressure drop is given by:

$$
\Delta P = C_3 G_f^2 10^{C_4L_f}+0.4[L_f/20000]^{0.1}[C_3G_f^210^{C_4L_f}]^4
$$

$$
G_f=G[0.075/\rho_g]^{0.5}[F_{pd}/20]^{0.5}=986F_s[F_{pd}/20]^{0.5}
$$

$$
L_f=L[62.4/\rho_L][F_{pd}/20]^{0.5}\mu^{0.1}
$$

$$
F_s=V_s\rho_g^{0.5}
$$

# Parameters

L

: Specific liquid mass flow rate [kg/s/m^2]

G

: Specific gas mass flow rate [kg/s/m^2]

rhol

: Density of liquid [kg/m^3]

rhog

: Density of gas [kg/m^3]

mul

: Viscosity of liquid [Pa\*s]

H

: Height of packing [m]

Fpd

: Robbins packing factor (tabulated for packings) [1/ft]

# Returns

dP

: Pressure drop across packing [Pa]

# Notes

Perry's displayed equation has a typo in a superscript.
This model is based on the example in Perry's.

# Examples

```pycon
>>> Robbins(L=12.2, G=2.03, rhol=1000., rhog=1.1853, mul=0.001, H=2.0, Fpd=24.0)
619.6624593438102
```

# References

[^footnote-1]: Robbins \[Chem. Eng. Progr., p. 87 (May 1991) Improved Pressure Drop
    Prediction with a New Correlation.

is_critical_flow is broken

Basic function which returns a tuple of arguments of a function or

functions.name: fluids.units.check_args_order
documentation:
Reads a numpydoc function and compares the Parameters and Other
Parameters with the input arguments of the actual function signature. Raises
an exception if not correctly defined.

getargspec is used for Python 2.7 compatibility and is deprecated in Python
3\.

```pycon
>>> check_args_order(fluids.core.Reynolds)
```

functions.name: fluids.units.kwargs_to_args
documentation:
Accepts an *args and **kwargs and a signature
like ['rho', 'mu', 'nu'] which is an ordered list of
all accepted arguments.

Returns a list containing all the arguments, sorted, and
left as None if not specified

OUT OF OR IN CONNECTION WITH THE SOFTWARE OR THE USE OR OTHER DEALINGS IN THE
SOFTWARE.

This module contains equations for modeling flow where density changes
significantly during the process - compressible flow. Also included are
equations for choked flow - the phenomenon where the velocity of a fluid
reaches its speed of sound.

For reporting bugs, adding feature requests, or submitting pull requests,
please use the [GitHub issue tracker](https://github.com/CalebBell/fluids/)
or contact the author at <mailto:Caleb.Andrew.Bell@gmail.com>.

```{contents}
:local: true
```
