functions.name: fluids.compressible.isothermal_work_compression
documentation:
Calculates the work of compression or expansion of a gas going through
an isothermal process.

$$
W = zRT\ln\left(\frac{P_2}{P_1}\right)
$$

# Parameters

P1

: Inlet pressure, [Pa]

P2

: Outlet pressure, [Pa]

T

: Temperature of the gas going through an isothermal process, [K]

Z

: Constant compressibility factor of the gas, [-]

# Returns

W

: Work performed per mole of gas compressed/expanded [J/mol]

# Notes

The full derivation with all forms is as follows:

$$
W = \int_{P_1}^{P_2} V dP = zRT\int_{P_1}^{P_2} \frac{1}{P} dP
$$

$$
W = zRT\ln\left(\frac{P_2}{P_1}\right) = P_1 V_1 \ln\left(\frac{P_2} {P_1}\right) = P_2 V_2 \ln\left(\frac{P_2}{P_1}\right)
$$

The substitutions are according to the ideal gas law with compressibility:

% math:
% PV = ZRT

The work of compression/expansion is the change in enthalpy of the gas.
Returns negative values for expansion and positive values for compression.

An average compressibility factor can be used where Z changes. For further
accuracy, this expression can be used repeatedly with small changes in
pressure and the work from each step summed.

This is the best possible case for compression; all actual compresssors
require more work to do the compression.

By making the compression take a large number of stages and cooling the gas
between stages, this can be approached reasonable closely. Integrally
geared compressors are often used for this purpose.

# Examples

```pycon
>>> isothermal_work_compression(1E5, 1E6, 300)
5743.427304244769
```

# References

[^footnote-1]: Couper, James R., W. Roy Penney, and James R. Fair. Chemical Process
    Equipment: Selection and Design. 2nd ed. Amsterdam ; Boston: Gulf
    Professional Publishing, 2009.

functions.name: fluids.compressible.isentropic_work_compression
documentation:
Calculation function for dealing with compressing or expanding a gas
going through an isentropic, adiabatic process assuming constant Cp and Cv.
The polytropic model is the same equation; just provide `n` instead of `k`
and use a polytropic efficiency for `eta` instead of a isentropic
efficiency. Can calculate any of the following, given all the other inputs:

- W, Work of compression
- P2, Pressure after compression
- P1, Pressure before compression
- eta, isentropic efficiency of compression

$$
W = \left(\frac{k}{k-1}\right)ZRT_1\left[\left(\frac{P_2}{P_1} \right)^{(k-1)/k}-1\right]/\eta_{isentropic}
$$

# Parameters

T1

: Initial temperature of the gas, [K]

k

: Isentropic exponent of the gas (Cp/Cv) or polytropic exponent `n` to
  use this as a polytropic model instead [-]

Z

: Constant compressibility factor of the gas, [-]

P1

: Inlet pressure, [Pa]

P2

: Outlet pressure, [Pa]

W

: Work performed per mole of gas compressed/expanded [J/mol]

eta

: Isentropic efficiency of the process or polytropic efficiency of the
  process to use this as a polytropic model instead [-]

# Returns

W, P1, P2, or eta

: The missing input which was solved for [base SI]

# Notes

For the same compression ratio, this is always of larger magnitude than the
isothermal case.

The full derivation is as follows:

For constant-heat capacity "isentropic" fluid,

$$
V = \frac{P_1^{1/k}V_1}{P^{1/k}}
$$

$$
W = \int_{P_1}^{P_2} V dP = \int_{P_1}^{P_2}\frac{P_1^{1/k}V_1} {P^{1/k}}dP
$$

$$
W = \frac{P_1^{1/k} V_1}{1 - \frac{1}{k}}\left[P_2^{1-1/k} - P_1^{1-1/k}\right]
$$

After performing the integration and substantial mathematical manipulation
we can obtain:

$$
W = \left(\frac{k}{k-1}\right) P_1 V_1 \left[\left(\frac{P_2}{P_1} \right)^{(k-1)/k}-1\right]
$$

Using PV = ZRT:

$$
W = \left(\frac{k}{k-1}\right)ZRT_1\left[\left(\frac{P_2}{P_1} \right)^{(k-1)/k}-1\right]
$$

The work of compression/expansion is the change in enthalpy of the gas.
Returns negative values for expansion and positive values for compression.

An average compressibility factor should be used as Z changes. For further
accuracy, this expression can be used repeatedly with small changes in
pressure and new values of isentropic exponent, and the work from each step
summed.

For the polytropic case this is not necessary, as `eta` corrects for the
simplification.

# Examples

```pycon
>>> isentropic_work_compression(P1=1E5, P2=1E6, T1=300, k=1.4, eta=0.78)
10416.876986384483
```

# References

[^footnote-1]: Couper, James R., W. Roy Penney, and James R. Fair. Chemical Process
    Equipment: Selection and Design. 2nd ed. Amsterdam ; Boston: Gulf
    Professional Publishing, 2009.

functions.name: fluids.compressible.isentropic_T_rise_compression
documentation:
Calculates the increase in temperature of a fluid which is compressed
or expanded under isentropic, adiabatic conditions assuming constant
Cp and Cv. The polytropic model is the same equation; just provide `n`
instead of `k` and use a polytropic efficienty for `eta` instead of a
isentropic efficiency.

$$
T_2 = T_1 + \frac{\Delta T_s}{\eta_s} = T_1 \left\{1 + \frac{1} {\eta_s}\left[\left(\frac{P_2}{P_1}\right)^{(k-1)/k}-1\right]\right\}
$$

# Parameters

T1

: Initial temperature of gas [K]

P1

: Initial pressure of gas [Pa]

P2

: Final pressure of gas [Pa]

k

: Isentropic exponent of the gas (Cp/Cv) or polytropic exponent `n` to
  use this as a polytropic model instead [-]

eta

: Isentropic efficiency of the process or polytropic efficiency of the
  process to use this as a polytropic model instead [-]

# Returns

T2

: Final temperature of gas [K]

# Notes

For the ideal case of `eta` = 1, the model simplifies to:

$$
\frac{T_2}{T_1} = \left(\frac{P_2}{P_1}\right)^{(k-1)/k}
$$

# Examples

```pycon
>>> isentropic_T_rise_compression(286.8, 54050, 432400, 1.4)
519.5230938217768
```

# References

[^footnote-1]: Couper, James R., W. Roy Penney, and James R. Fair. Chemical Process
    Equipment: Selection and Design. 2nd ed. Amsterdam ; Boston: Gulf
    Professional Publishing, 2009.

[^footnote-2]: GPSA. GPSA Engineering Data Book. 13th edition. Gas Processors
    Suppliers Association, Tulsa, OK, 2012.

functions.name: fluids.compressible.isentropic_efficiency
documentation:
Calculates either isentropic or polytropic efficiency from the other
type of efficiency.

$$
\eta_s = \frac{(P_2/P_1)^{(k-1)/k}-1} {(P_2/P_1)^{\frac{k-1}{k\eta_p}}-1}
$$

$$
\eta_p = \frac{\left(k - 1\right) \ln{\left (\frac{P_{2}}{P_{1}} \right )}}{k \ln{\left (\frac{1}{\eta_{s}} \left(\eta_{s} + \left(\frac{P_{2}}{P_{1}}\right)^{\frac{1}{k} \left(k - 1\right)} - 1\right) \right )}}
$$

# Parameters

P1

: Initial pressure of gas [Pa]

P2

: Final pressure of gas [Pa]

k

: Isentropic exponent of the gas (Cp/Cv) [-]

eta_s

: Isentropic (adiabatic) efficiency of the process, [-]

eta_p

: Polytropic efficiency of the process, [-]

# Returns

eta_s or eta_p

: Isentropic or polytropic efficiency, depending on input, [-]

# Notes

The form for obtained `eta_p` from `eta_s` was derived with SymPy.

# Examples

```pycon
>>> isentropic_efficiency(1E5, 1E6, 1.4, eta_p=0.78)
0.7027614191263858
```

# References

[^footnote-1]: Couper, James R., W. Roy Penney, and James R. Fair. Chemical Process
    Equipment: Selection and Design. 2nd ed. Amsterdam ; Boston: Gulf
    Professional Publishing, 2009.

functions.name: fluids.compressible.polytropic_exponent
documentation:
Calculates one of:

> - Polytropic exponent from polytropic efficiency
> - Polytropic efficiency from the polytropic exponent

$$
n = \frac{k\eta_p}{1 - k(1-\eta_p)}
$$

$$
\eta_p = \frac{\left(\frac{n}{n-1}\right)}{\left(\frac{k}{k-1} \right)} = \frac{n(k-1)}{k(n-1)}
$$

# Parameters

k

: Isentropic exponent of the gas (Cp/Cv) [-]

n

: Polytropic exponent of the process [-]

eta_p

: Polytropic efficiency of the process, [-]

# Returns

n or eta_p

: Polytropic exponent or polytropic efficiency, depending on input, [-]

# Notes

# Examples

```pycon
>>> polytropic_exponent(1.4, eta_p=0.78)
1.5780346820809246
```

# References

[^footnote-1]: Couper, James R., W. Roy Penney, and James R. Fair. Chemical Process
    Equipment: Selection and Design. 2nd ed. Amsterdam ; Boston: Gulf
    Professional Publishing, 2009.

functions.name: fluids.compressible.T_critical_flow
documentation:
Calculates critical flow temperature `Tcf` for a fluid with the
given isentropic coefficient. `Tcf` is in a flow (with Ma=1) whose
stagnation conditions are known. Normally used with converging/diverging
nozzles.

$$
\frac{T^*}{T_0} = \frac{2}{k+1}
$$

# Parameters

T

: Stagnation temperature of a fluid with Ma=1 [K]

k

: Isentropic coefficient []

# Returns

Tcf

: Critical flow temperature at Ma=1 [K]

# Notes

Assumes isentropic flow.

# Examples

Example 12.4 in [^footnote-1]:

```pycon
>>> T_critical_flow(473, 1.289)
413.2809086937528
```

# References

[^footnote-1]: Cengel, Yunus, and John Cimbala. Fluid Mechanics: Fundamentals and
    Applications. Boston: McGraw Hill Higher Education, 2006.

functions.name: fluids.compressible.P_critical_flow
documentation:
Calculates critical flow pressure `Pcf` for a fluid with the
given isentropic coefficient. `Pcf` is in a flow (with Ma=1) whose
stagnation conditions are known. Normally used with converging/diverging
nozzles.

$$
\frac{P^*}{P_0} = \left(\frac{2}{k+1}\right)^{k/(k-1)}
$$

# Parameters

P

: Stagnation pressure of a fluid with Ma=1 [Pa]

k

: Isentropic coefficient []

# Returns

Pcf

: Critical flow pressure at Ma=1 [Pa]

# Notes

Assumes isentropic flow.

# Examples

Example 12.4 in [^footnote-1]:

```pycon
>>> P_critical_flow(1400000, 1.289)
766812.9022792266
```

# References

[^footnote-1]: Cengel, Yunus, and John Cimbala. Fluid Mechanics: Fundamentals and
    Applications. Boston: McGraw Hill Higher Education, 2006.

functions.name: fluids.compressible.P_isothermal_critical_flow
documentation:
Calculates critical flow pressure `Pcf` for a fluid flowing
isothermally and suffering pressure drop caused by a pipe's friction factor.

$$
P_2 = P_{1} e^{\frac{1}{2 D} \left(D \left(\operatorname{LambertW} {\left (- e^{\frac{1}{D} \left(- D - L f_d\right)} \right )} + 1\right) + L f_d\right)}
$$

# Parameters

P

: Inlet pressure [Pa]

fd

: Darcy friction factor for flow in pipe [-]

D

: Diameter of pipe, [m]

L

: Length of pipe, [m]

# Returns

Pcf

: Critical flow pressure of a compressible gas flowing from `P1` to `Pcf`
  in a tube of length L and friction factor `fd` [Pa]

# Notes

Assumes isothermal flow. Developed based on the `isothermal_gas` model,
using SymPy.

The isothermal gas model is solved for maximum mass flow rate; any pressure
drop under it is impossible due to the formation of a shock wave.

# Examples

```pycon
>>> P_isothermal_critical_flow(P=1E6, fd=0.00185, L=1000., D=0.5)
389699.73176
```

# References

[^footnote-1]: Wilkes, James O. Fluid Mechanics for Chemical Engineers with
    Microfluidics and CFD. 2 edition. Upper Saddle River, NJ: Prentice Hall,
    2005\.

functions.name: fluids.compressible.P_upstream_isothermal_critical_flow
documentation:
Not part of the public API. Reverses `P_isothermal_critical_flow`.

# Examples

```pycon
>>> P_upstream_isothermal_critical_flow(P=389699.7317645518, fd=0.00185,
... L=1000., D=0.5)
1000000.00000
```

functions.name: fluids.compressible.is_critical_flow
documentation:
Determines if a flow of a fluid driven by pressure gradient
P1 - P2 is critical, for a fluid with the given isentropic coefficient.
This function calculates critical flow pressure, and checks if this is
larger than P2. If so, the flow is critical and choked.

# Parameters

P1

: Higher, source pressure [Pa]

P2

: Lower, downstream pressure [Pa]

k

: Isentropic coefficient []

# Returns

flowtype

: True if the flow is choked; otherwise False

# Notes

Assumes isentropic flow. Uses P_critical_flow function.

# Examples

Examples 1-2 from API 520.

```pycon
>>> is_critical_flow(670E3, 532E3, 1.11)
False
>>> is_critical_flow(670E3, 101E3, 1.11)
True
```

# References

[^footnote-1]: API. 2014. API 520 - Part 1 Sizing, Selection, and Installation of
    Pressure-relieving Devices, Part I - Sizing and Selection, 9E.

functions.name: fluids.compressible.stagnation_energy
documentation:
Calculates the increase in enthalpy `dH` which is provided by a fluid's
velocity `V`.

$$
\Delta H = \frac{V^2}{2}
$$

# Parameters

V

: Velocity [m/s]

# Returns

dH

: Incease in enthalpy [J/kg]

# Notes

The units work out. This term is pretty small, but not trivial.

# Examples

```pycon
>>> stagnation_energy(125)
7812.5
```

# References

[^footnote-1]: Cengel, Yunus, and John Cimbala. Fluid Mechanics: Fundamentals and
    Applications. Boston: McGraw Hill Higher Education, 2006.

functions.name: fluids.compressible.P_stagnation
documentation:
Calculates stagnation flow pressure `Pst` for a fluid with the
given isentropic coefficient and specified stagnation temperature and
normal temperature. Normally used with converging/diverging nozzles.

$$
\frac{P_0}{P}=\left(\frac{T_0}{T}\right)^{\frac{k}{k-1}}
$$

# Parameters

P

: Normal pressure of a fluid [Pa]

T

: Normal temperature of a fluid [K]

Tst

: Stagnation temperature of a fluid moving at a certain velocity [K]

k

: Isentropic coefficient []

# Returns

Pst

: Stagnation pressure of a fluid moving at a certain velocity [Pa]

# Notes

Assumes isentropic flow.

# Examples

Example 12-1 in [^footnote-1].

```pycon
>>> P_stagnation(54050., 255.7, 286.8, 1.4)
80772.80495900588
```

# References

[^footnote-1]: Cengel, Yunus, and John Cimbala. Fluid Mechanics: Fundamentals and
    Applications. Boston: McGraw Hill Higher Education, 2006.

functions.name: fluids.compressible.T_stagnation
documentation:
Calculates stagnation flow temperature `Tst` for a fluid with the
given isentropic coefficient and specified stagnation pressure and
normal pressure. Normally used with converging/diverging nozzles.

$$
T=T_0\left(\frac{P}{P_0}\right)^{\frac{k-1}{k}}
$$

# Parameters

T

: Normal temperature of a fluid [K]

P

: Normal pressure of a fluid [Pa]

Pst

: Stagnation pressure of a fluid moving at a certain velocity [Pa]

k

: Isentropic coefficient []

# Returns

Tst

: Stagnation temperature of a fluid moving at a certain velocity [K]

# Notes

Assumes isentropic flow.

# Examples

Example 12-1 in [^footnote-1].

```pycon
>>> T_stagnation(286.8, 54050, 54050*8, 1.4)
519.5230938217768
```

# References

[^footnote-1]: Cengel, Yunus, and John Cimbala. Fluid Mechanics: Fundamentals and
    Applications. Boston: McGraw Hill Higher Education, 2006.

functions.name: fluids.compressible.T_stagnation_ideal
documentation:
Calculates the ideal stagnation temperature `Tst` calculated assuming
the fluid has a constant heat capacity `Cp` and with a specified
velocity `V` and temperature `T`.

$$
T^* = T + \frac{V^2}{2C_p}
$$

# Parameters

T

: Tempearture [K]

V

: Velocity [m/s]

Cp

: Ideal heat capacity [J/kg/K]

# Returns

Tst

: Stagnation temperature [J/kg]

# Examples

Example 12-1 in [^footnote-1].

```pycon
>>> T_stagnation_ideal(255.7, 250, 1005.)
286.79452736318405
```

# References

[^footnote-1]: Cengel, Yunus, and John Cimbala. Fluid Mechanics: Fundamentals and
    Applications. Boston: McGraw Hill Higher Education, 2006.

functions.name: fluids.compressible.isothermal_gas
documentation:
Calculation function for dealing with flow of a compressible gas in a
pipeline for the complete isothermal flow equation. Can calculate any of
the following, given all other inputs:

- Mass flow rate
- Upstream pressure (numerical)
- Downstream pressure (analytical or numerical if an overflow occurs)
- Diameter of pipe (numerical)
- Length of pipe

A variety of forms of this equation have been presented, differing in their
use of the ideal gas law and choice of gas constant. The form here uses
density explicitly, allowing for non-ideal values to be used.

$$
\dot m^2 = \frac{\left(\frac{\pi D^2}{4}\right)^2 \rho_{avg} \left(P_1^2-P_2^2\right)}{P_1\left(f_d\frac{L}{D} + 2\ln\frac{P_1}{P_2} \right)}
$$

# Parameters

rho

: Average density of gas in pipe, [kg/m^3]

fd

: Darcy friction factor for flow in pipe [-]

P1

: Inlet pressure to pipe, [Pa]

P2

: Outlet pressure from pipe, [Pa]

L

: Length of pipe, [m]

D

: Diameter of pipe, [m]

m

: Mass flow rate of gas through pipe, [kg/s]

# Returns

m, P1, P2, D, or L

: The missing input which was solved for [base SI]

# Notes

The solution for P2 has the following closed form, derived using Maple:

$$
P_2={P_1 \left( {{ e}^{0.5\cdot{\frac {1}{{m}^{2}} \left( -C{m}^{2} +\text{ lambertW} \left(-{\frac {BP_1}{{m}^{2}}{{ e}^{-{\frac {-C{m}^{ 2}+BP_1}{{m}^{2}}}}}}\right){}{m}^{2}+BP_1 \right) }}} \right) ^{-1}}
$$

$$
B = \frac{\pi^2 D^4}{4^2} \rho_{avg}
$$

$$
C = f_d \frac{L}{D}
$$

A wide range of conditions are impossible due to choked flow. See
`P_isothermal_critical_flow` for details. An exception is raised when
they occur.

The 2 multiplied by the logarithm is often shown as a power of the
pressure ratio; this is only the case when the pressure ratio is raised to
the power of 2 before its logarithm is taken.

A number of limitations exist for this model:

> - Density dependence is that of an ideal gas.
> - If calculating the pressure drop, the average gas density cannot
>   be known immediately; iteration must be used to correct this.
> - The friction factor depends on both the gas density and velocity,
>   so it should be solved for iteratively as well. It changes throughout
>   the pipe as the gas expands and velocity increases.
> - The model is not easily adapted to include elevation effects due to
>   the acceleration term included in it.
> - As the gas expands, it will change temperature slightly, further
>   altering the density and friction factor.

There are many commercial packages which perform the actual direct
integration of the flow, such as OLGA Dynamic Multiphase Flow Simulator,
or ASPEN Hydraulics.

This expression has also been presented with the ideal gas assumption
directly incorporated into it [^footnote-4] (note R is the specific gas constant, in
units of J/kg/K):

$$
\dot m^2 = \frac{\left(\frac{\pi D^2}{4}\right)^2 \left(P_1^2-P_2^2\right)}{RT\left(f_d\frac{L}{D} + 2\ln\frac{P_1}{P_2} \right)}
$$

# Examples

```pycon
>>> isothermal_gas(rho=11.3, fd=0.00185, P1=1E6, P2=9E5, L=1000, D=0.5)
145.4847572636031
```

# References

[^footnote-1]: Crane Co. Flow of Fluids Through Valves, Fittings, and Pipe. Crane,
    2009\.

[^footnote-2]: Kim, J. and Singh, N. "A Novel Equation for Isothermal Pipe Flow.".
    Chemical Engineering, June 2012, <http://www.chemengonline.com/a-novel-equation-for-isothermal-pipe-flow/?printmode=1>

[^footnote-3]: Wilkes, James O. Fluid Mechanics for Chemical Engineers with
    Microfluidics and CFD. 2 edition. Upper Saddle River, NJ: Prentice Hall,
    2005\.

[^footnote-4]: Rennels, Donald C., and Hobart M. Hudson. Pipe Flow: A Practical
    and Comprehensive Guide. 1st edition. Hoboken, N.J: Wiley, 2012.

functions.name: fluids.compressible.Panhandle_A
documentation:
Calculation function for dealing with flow of a compressible gas in a
pipeline with the Panhandle A formula. Can calculate any of the following,
given all other inputs:

- Flow rate
- Upstream pressure
- Downstream pressure
- Diameter of pipe
- Length of pipe

A variety of different constants and expressions have been presented
for the Panhandle A equation. Here, a new form is developed with all units
in base SI, based on the work of [^footnote-1].

$$
Q = 158.02053 E \left(\frac{T_s}{P_s}\right)^{1.0788}\left[\frac{P_1^2 -P_2^2}{L \cdot {SG}^{0.8539} T_{avg}Z_{avg}}\right]^{0.5394}D^{2.6182}
$$

# Parameters

SG

: Specific gravity of fluid with respect to air at the reference
  temperature and pressure `Ts` and `Ps`, [-]

Tavg

: Average temperature of the fluid in the pipeline, [K]

L

: Length of pipe, [m]

D

: Diameter of pipe, [m]

P1

: Inlet pressure to pipe, [Pa]

P2

: Outlet pressure from pipe, [Pa]

Q

: Flow rate of gas through pipe at `Ts` and `Ps`, [m^3/s]

Ts

: Reference temperature for the specific gravity of the gas, [K]

Ps

: Reference pressure for the specific gravity of the gas, [Pa]

Zavg

: Average compressibility factor for gas, [-]

E

: Pipeline efficiency, a correction factor between 0 and 1

# Returns

Q, P1, P2, D, or L

: The missing input which was solved for [base SI]

# Notes

[^footnote-1]'s original constant was 4.5965E-3, and it has units of km (length),
kPa, mm (diameter), and flowrate in m^3/day.

The form in [^footnote-2] has the same exponents as used here, units of mm
(diameter), kPa, km (length), and flow in m^3/hour; its leading constant is
1.9152E-4.

The GPSA [^footnote-3] has a leading constant of 0.191, a bracketed power of 0.5392,
a specific gravity power of 0.853, and otherwise the same constants.
It is in units of mm (diameter) and kPa and m^3/day; length is stated to be
in km, but according to the errata is in m.

[^footnote-4] has a leading constant of 1.198E7, a specific gravity of power of 0.8541,
and a power of diameter which is under the root of 4.854 and is otherwise
the same. It has units of kPa and m^3/day, but is otherwise in base SI
units.

[^footnote-5] has a leading constant of 99.5211, but its reference correction has no
exponent; other exponents are the same as here. It is entirely in base SI
units.

[^footnote-6] has pressures in psi, diameter in inches, length in miles, Q in
ft^3/day, T in degrees Rankine, and a constant of 435.87.
Its reference condition power is 1.07881, and it has a specific gravity
correction outside any other term with a power of 0.4604.

# Examples

```pycon
>>> Panhandle_A(D=0.340, P1=90E5, P2=20E5, L=160E3, SG=0.693, Tavg=277.15)
42.56082051195928
```

# References

[^footnote-1]: Menon, E. Shashi. Gas Pipeline Hydraulics. 1st edition. Boca Raton,
    FL: CRC Press, 2005.

[^footnote-2]: Crane Co. Flow of Fluids Through Valves, Fittings, and Pipe. Crane,
    2009\.

[^footnote-3]: GPSA. GPSA Engineering Data Book. 13th edition. Gas Processors
    Suppliers Association, Tulsa, OK, 2012.

[^footnote-4]: Campbell, John M. Gas Conditioning and Processing, Vol. 2: The
    Equipment Modules. 7th edition. Campbell Petroleum Series, 1992.

[^footnote-5]: Coelho, Paulo M., and Carlos Pinho. "Considerations about Equations
    for Steady State Flow in Natural Gas Pipelines." Journal of the
    Brazilian Society of Mechanical Sciences and Engineering 29, no. 3
    (September 2007): 262-73. doi:10.1590/S1678-58782007000300005.

[^footnote-6]: Ikoku, Chi U. Natural Gas Production Engineering. Malabar, Fla:
    Krieger Pub Co, 1991.

functions.name: fluids.compressible.Panhandle_B
documentation:
Calculation function for dealing with flow of a compressible gas in a
pipeline with the Panhandle B formula. Can calculate any of the following,
given all other inputs:

- Flow rate
- Upstream pressure
- Downstream pressure
- Diameter of pipe
- Length of pipe

A variety of different constants and expressions have been presented
for the Panhandle B equation. Here, a new form is developed with all units
in base SI, based on the work of [^footnote-1].

$$
Q = 152.88116 E \left(\frac{T_s}{P_s}\right)^{1.02}\left[\frac{P_1^2 -P_2^2}{L \cdot {SG}^{0.961} T_{avg}Z_{avg}}\right]^{0.51}D^{2.53}
$$

# Parameters

SG

: Specific gravity of fluid with respect to air at the reference
  temperature and pressure `Ts` and `Ps`, [-]

Tavg

: Average temperature of the fluid in the pipeline, [K]

L

: Length of pipe, [m]

D

: Diameter of pipe, [m]

P1

: Inlet pressure to pipe, [Pa]

P2

: Outlet pressure from pipe, [Pa]

Q

: Flow rate of gas through pipe at `Ts` and `Ps`, [m^3/s]

Ts

: Reference temperature for the specific gravity of the gas, [K]

Ps

: Reference pressure for the specific gravity of the gas, [Pa]

Zavg

: Average compressibility factor for gas, [-]

E

: Pipeline efficiency, a correction factor between 0 and 1

# Returns

Q, P1, P2, D, or L

: The missing input which was solved for [base SI]

# Notes

[^footnote-1]'s original constant was 1.002E-2, and it has units of km (length),
kPa, mm (diameter), and flowrate in m^3/day.

The form in [^footnote-2] has the same exponents as used here, units of mm
(diameter), kPa, km (length), and flow in m^3/hour; its leading constant is
4.1749E-4.

The GPSA [^footnote-3] has a leading constant of 0.339, and otherwise the same constants.
It is in units of mm (diameter) and kPa and m^3/day; length is stated to be
in km, but according to the errata is in m.

[^footnote-4] has a leading constant of 1.264E7, a diameter power of 4.961 which is
also under the 0.51 power, and is otherwise the same. It has units of kPa
and m^3/day, but is otherwise in base SI units.

[^footnote-5] has a leading constant of 135.8699, but its reference correction has
no exponent and its specific gravity has a power of 0.9608; the other
exponents are the same as here. It is entirely in base SI units.

[^footnote-6] has pressures in psi, diameter in inches, length in miles, Q in
ft^3/day, T in degrees Rankine, and a constant of 737 with the exponents
the same as here.

# Examples

```pycon
>>> Panhandle_B(D=0.340, P1=90E5, P2=20E5, L=160E3, SG=0.693, Tavg=277.15)
42.35366178004172
```

# References

[^footnote-1]: Menon, E. Shashi. Gas Pipeline Hydraulics. 1st edition. Boca Raton,
    FL: CRC Press, 2005.

[^footnote-2]: Crane Co. Flow of Fluids Through Valves, Fittings, and Pipe. Crane,
    2009\.

[^footnote-3]: GPSA. GPSA Engineering Data Book. 13th edition. Gas Processors
    Suppliers Association, Tulsa, OK, 2012.

[^footnote-4]: Campbell, John M. Gas Conditioning and Processing, Vol. 2: The
    Equipment Modules. 7th edition. Campbell Petroleum Series, 1992.

[^footnote-5]: Coelho, Paulo M., and Carlos Pinho. "Considerations about Equations
    for Steady State Flow in Natural Gas Pipelines." Journal of the
    Brazilian Society of Mechanical Sciences and Engineering 29, no. 3
    (September 2007): 262-73. doi:10.1590/S1678-58782007000300005.

[^footnote-6]: Ikoku, Chi U. Natural Gas Production Engineering. Malabar, Fla:
    Krieger Pub Co, 1991.

functions.name: fluids.compressible.Weymouth
documentation:
Calculation function for dealing with flow of a compressible gas in a
pipeline with the Weymouth formula. Can calculate any of the following,
given all other inputs:

- Flow rate
- Upstream pressure
- Downstream pressure
- Diameter of pipe
- Length of pipe

A variety of different constants and expressions have been presented
for the Weymouth equation. Here, a new form is developed with all units
in base SI, based on the work of [^footnote-1].

$$
Q = 137.32958 E \frac{T_s}{P_s}\left[\frac{P_1^2 -P_2^2}{L \cdot {SG} \cdot T_{avg}Z_{avg}}\right]^{0.5}D^{2.667}
$$

# Parameters

SG

: Specific gravity of fluid with respect to air at the reference
  temperature and pressure `Ts` and `Ps`, [-]

Tavg

: Average temperature of the fluid in the pipeline, [K]

L

: Length of pipe, [m]

D

: Diameter of pipe, [m]

P1

: Inlet pressure to pipe, [Pa]

P2

: Outlet pressure from pipe, [Pa]

Q

: Flow rate of gas through pipe at `Ts` and `Ps`, [m^3/s]

Ts

: Reference temperature for the specific gravity of the gas, [K]

Ps

: Reference pressure for the specific gravity of the gas, [Pa]

Zavg

: Average compressibility factor for gas, [-]

E

: Pipeline efficiency, a correction factor between 0 and 1

# Returns

Q, P1, P2, D, or L

: The missing input which was solved for [base SI]

# Notes

[^footnote-1]'s original constant was 3.7435E-3, and it has units of km (length),
kPa, mm (diameter), and flowrate in m^3/day.

The form in [^footnote-2] has the same exponents as used here, units of mm
(diameter), kPa, km (length), and flow in m^3/hour; its leading constant is
1.5598E-4.

The GPSA [^footnote-3] has a leading constant of 0.1182, and otherwise the same constants.
It is in units of mm (diameter) and kPa and m^3/day; length is stated to be
in km, but according to the errata is in m.

[^footnote-4] has a leading constant of 1.162E7, a diameter power of 5.333 which is
also under the 0.50 power, and is otherwise the same. It has units of kPa
and m^3/day, but is otherwise in base SI units.

[^footnote-5] has a leading constant of 137.2364; the other
exponents are the same as here. It is entirely in base SI units.

[^footnote-6] has pressures in psi, diameter in inches, length in miles, Q in
ft^3/hour, T in degrees Rankine, and a constant of 18.062 with the
exponents the same as here.

# Examples

```pycon
>>> Weymouth(D=0.340, P1=90E5, P2=20E5, L=160E3, SG=0.693, Tavg=277.15)
32.07729055913029
```

# References

[^footnote-1]: Menon, E. Shashi. Gas Pipeline Hydraulics. 1st edition. Boca Raton,
    FL: CRC Press, 2005.

[^footnote-2]: Crane Co. Flow of Fluids Through Valves, Fittings, and Pipe. Crane,
    2009\.

[^footnote-3]: GPSA. GPSA Engineering Data Book. 13th edition. Gas Processors
    Suppliers Association, Tulsa, OK, 2012.

[^footnote-4]: Campbell, John M. Gas Conditioning and Processing, Vol. 2: The
    Equipment Modules. 7th edition. Campbell Petroleum Series, 1992.

[^footnote-5]: Coelho, Paulo M., and Carlos Pinho. "Considerations about Equations
    for Steady State Flow in Natural Gas Pipelines." Journal of the
    Brazilian Society of Mechanical Sciences and Engineering 29, no. 3
    (September 2007): 262-73. doi:10.1590/S1678-58782007000300005.

[^footnote-6]: Ikoku, Chi U. Natural Gas Production Engineering. Malabar, Fla:
    Krieger Pub Co, 1991.

functions.name: fluids.compressible.Spitzglass_high
documentation:
Calculation function for dealing with flow of a compressible gas in a
pipeline with the Spitzglass (high pressure drop) formula. Can calculate
any of the following, given all other inputs:

- Flow rate
- Upstream pressure
- Downstream pressure
- Diameter of pipe (numerical solution)
- Length of pipe

A variety of different constants and expressions have been presented
for the Spitzglass (high pressure drop) formula. Here, the form as in [^footnote-1]
is used but with a more precise metric conversion from inches to m.

$$
Q = 125.1060 E \left(\frac{T_s}{P_s}\right)\left[\frac{P_1^2 -P_2^2}{L \cdot {SG} T_{avg}Z_{avg} (1 + 0.09144/D + \frac{150}{127}D)} \right]^{0.5}D^{2.5}
$$

# Parameters

SG

: Specific gravity of fluid with respect to air at the reference
  temperature and pressure `Ts` and `Ps`, [-]

Tavg

: Average temperature of the fluid in the pipeline, [K]

L

: Length of pipe, [m]

D

: Diameter of pipe, [m]

P1

: Inlet pressure to pipe, [Pa]

P2

: Outlet pressure from pipe, [Pa]

Q

: Flow rate of gas through pipe at `Ts` and `Ps`, [m^3/s]

Ts

: Reference temperature for the specific gravity of the gas, [K]

Ps

: Reference pressure for the specific gravity of the gas, [Pa]

Zavg

: Average compressibility factor for gas, [-]

E

: Pipeline efficiency, a correction factor between 0 and 1

# Returns

Q, P1, P2, D, or L

: The missing input which was solved for [base SI]

# Notes

This equation is often presented without any correction for reference
conditions for specific gravity.

This model is also presented in [^footnote-2] with a leading constant of 1.0815E-2,
the same exponents as used here, units of mm (diameter), kPa, km (length),
and flow in m^3/hour.

# Examples

```pycon
>>> Spitzglass_high(D=0.340, P1=90E5, P2=20E5, L=160E3, SG=0.693, Tavg=277.15)
29.42670246281681
```

# References

[^footnote-1]: Coelho, Paulo M., and Carlos Pinho. "Considerations about Equations
    for Steady State Flow in Natural Gas Pipelines." Journal of the
    Brazilian Society of Mechanical Sciences and Engineering 29, no. 3
    (September 2007): 262-73. doi:10.1590/S1678-58782007000300005.

[^footnote-2]: Menon, E. Shashi. Gas Pipeline Hydraulics. 1st edition. Boca Raton,
    FL: CRC Press, 2005.

functions.name: fluids.compressible.Spitzglass_low
documentation:
Calculation function for dealing with flow of a compressible gas in a
pipeline with the Spitzglass (low pressure drop) formula. Can calculate
any of the following, given all other inputs:

- Flow rate
- Upstream pressure
- Downstream pressure
- Diameter of pipe (numerical solution)
- Length of pipe

A variety of different constants and expressions have been presented
for the Spitzglass (low pressure drop) formula. Here, the form as in [^footnote-1]
is used but with a more precise metric conversion from inches to m.

$$
Q = 125.1060 E \left(\frac{T_s}{P_s}\right)\left[\frac{2(P_1 -P_2)(P_s+1210)}{L \cdot {SG} \cdot T_{avg}Z_{avg} (1 + 0.09144/D + \frac{150}{127}D)}\right]^{0.5}D^{2.5}
$$

# Parameters

SG

: Specific gravity of fluid with respect to air at the reference
  temperature and pressure `Ts` and `Ps`, [-]

Tavg

: Average temperature of the fluid in the pipeline, [K]

L

: Length of pipe, [m]

D

: Diameter of pipe, [m]

P1

: Inlet pressure to pipe, [Pa]

P2

: Outlet pressure from pipe, [Pa]

Q

: Flow rate of gas through pipe at `Ts` and `Ps`, [m^3/s]

Ts

: Reference temperature for the specific gravity of the gas, [K]

Ps

: Reference pressure for the specific gravity of the gas, [Pa]

Zavg

: Average compressibility factor for gas, [-]

E

: Pipeline efficiency, a correction factor between 0 and 1

# Returns

Q, P1, P2, D, or L

: The missing input which was solved for [base SI]

# Notes

This equation is often presented without any correction for reference
conditions for specific gravity.

This model is also presented in [^footnote-2] with a leading constant of 5.69E-2,
the same exponents as used here, units of mm (diameter), kPa, km (length),
and flow in m^3/hour. However, it is believed to contain a typo, and gives
results \<1/3 of the correct values. It is also present in [^footnote-2] in imperial
form; this is believed correct, but makes a slight assumption not done in
[^footnote-1].

This model is present in [^footnote-3] without reference corrections. The 1210
constant in [^footnote-1] is an approximation necessary for the reference correction
to function without a square of the pressure difference. The GPSA version
is as follows, and matches this formulation very closely:

$$
Q = 0.821 \left[\frac{(P_1-P_2)D^5}{L \cdot {SG} (1 + 91.44/D + 0.0018D)}\right]^{0.5}
$$

The model is also shown in [^footnote-4], with diameter in inches, length in feet,
flow in MMSCFD, pressure drop in inH2O, and a rounded leading constant of
0.09; this makes its predictions several percent higher than the model here.

# Examples

```pycon
>>> Spitzglass_low(D=0.154051, P1=6720.3199, P2=0, L=54.864, SG=0.6, Tavg=288.7)
0.9488775242530617
```

# References

[^footnote-1]: Coelho, Paulo M., and Carlos Pinho. "Considerations about Equations
    for Steady State Flow in Natural Gas Pipelines." Journal of the
    Brazilian Society of Mechanical Sciences and Engineering 29, no. 3
    (September 2007): 262-73. doi:10.1590/S1678-58782007000300005.

[^footnote-2]: Menon, E. Shashi. Gas Pipeline Hydraulics. 1st edition. Boca Raton,
    FL: CRC Press, 2005.

[^footnote-3]: GPSA. GPSA Engineering Data Book. 13th edition. Gas Processors
    Suppliers Association, Tulsa, OK, 2012.

[^footnote-4]: PetroWiki. "Pressure Drop Evaluation along Pipelines" Accessed
    September 11, 2016. <http://petrowiki.org/Pressure_drop_evaluation_along_pipelines#Spitzglass_equation_2>.

functions.name: fluids.compressible.Oliphant
documentation:
Calculation function for dealing with flow of a compressible gas in a
pipeline with the Oliphant formula. Can calculate any of the following,
given all other inputs:

- Flow rate
- Upstream pressure
- Downstream pressure
- Diameter of pipe (numerical solution)
- Length of pipe

This model is a more complete conversion to metric of the Imperial version
presented in [^footnote-1].

$$
Q = 84.5872\left(D^{2.5} + 0.20915D^3\right)\frac{T_s}{P_s}\left(\frac {P_1^2 - P_2^2}{L\cdot {SG} \cdot T_{avg}}\right)^{0.5}
$$

# Parameters

SG

: Specific gravity of fluid with respect to air at the reference
  temperature and pressure `Ts` and `Ps`, [-]

Tavg

: Average temperature of the fluid in the pipeline, [K]

L

: Length of pipe, [m]

D

: Diameter of pipe, [m]

P1

: Inlet pressure to pipe, [Pa]

P2

: Outlet pressure from pipe, [Pa]

Q

: Flow rate of gas through pipe at `Ts` and `Ps`, [m^3/s]

Ts

: Reference temperature for the specific gravity of the gas, [K]

Ps

: Reference pressure for the specific gravity of the gas, [Pa]

Zavg

: Average compressibility factor for gas, [-]

E

: Pipeline efficiency, a correction factor between 0 and 1

# Returns

Q, P1, P2, D, or L

: The missing input which was solved for [base SI]

# Notes

Recommended in [^footnote-1] for use between vacuum and 100 psi.

The model is simplified by grouping constants here; however, it is presented
in the imperial unit set inches (diameter), miles (length), psi, Rankine,
and MMSCFD in [^footnote-1]:

$$
Q = 42(24)\left(D^{2.5} + \frac{D^3}{30}\right)\left(\frac{14.4}{P_s} \right)\left(\frac{T_s}{520}\right)\left[\left(\frac{0.6}{SG}\right) \left(\frac{520}{T_{avg}}\right)\left(\frac{P_1^2 - P_2^2}{L}\right) \right]^{0.5}
$$

# Examples

```pycon
>>> Oliphant(D=0.340, P1=90E5, P2=20E5, L=160E3, SG=0.693, Tavg=277.15)
28.851535408143057
```

# References

[^footnote-1]: GPSA. GPSA Engineering Data Book. 13th edition. Gas Processors
    Suppliers Association, Tulsa, OK, 2012.

[^footnote-2]:
    6. 14. Oliphant, "Production of Natural Gas," Report. USGS, 1902.

functions.name: fluids.compressible.Fritzsche
documentation:
Calculation function for dealing with flow of a compressible gas in a
pipeline with the Fritzsche formula. Can calculate any of the following,
given all other inputs:

- Flow rate
- Upstream pressure
- Downstream pressure
- Diameter of pipe
- Length of pipe

A variety of different constants and expressions have been presented
for the Fritzsche formula. Here, the form as in [^footnote-1]
is used but with all inputs in base SI units.

$$
Q = 93.500 \frac{T_s}{P_s}\left(\frac{P_1^2 - P_2^2} {L\cdot {SG}^{0.8587} \cdot T_{avg}}\right)^{0.538}D^{2.69}
$$

# Parameters

SG

: Specific gravity of fluid with respect to air at the reference
  temperature and pressure `Ts` and `Ps`, [-]

Tavg

: Average temperature of the fluid in the pipeline, [K]

L

: Length of pipe, [m]

D

: Diameter of pipe, [m]

P1

: Inlet pressure to pipe, [Pa]

P2

: Outlet pressure from pipe, [Pa]

Q

: Flow rate of gas through pipe at `Ts` and `Ps`, [m^3/s]

Ts

: Reference temperature for the specific gravity of the gas, [K]

Ps

: Reference pressure for the specific gravity of the gas, [Pa]

Zavg

: Average compressibility factor for gas, [-]

E

: Pipeline efficiency, a correction factor between 0 and 1

# Returns

Q, P1, P2, D, or L

: The missing input which was solved for [base SI]

# Notes

This model is also presented in [^footnote-1] with a leading constant of 2.827,
the same exponents as used here, units of mm (diameter), kPa, km (length),
and flow in m^3/hour.

This model is shown in base SI units in [^footnote-2], and with a leading constant
of 94.2565, a diameter power of 2.6911, main group power of 0.5382
and a specific gravity power of 0.858. The difference is very small.

# Examples

```pycon
>>> Fritzsche(D=0.340, P1=90E5, P2=20E5, L=160E3, SG=0.693, Tavg=277.15)
39.421535157535565
```

# References

[^footnote-1]: Menon, E. Shashi. Gas Pipeline Hydraulics. 1st edition. Boca Raton,
    FL: CRC Press, 2005.

[^footnote-2]: Coelho, Paulo M., and Carlos Pinho. "Considerations about Equations
    for Steady State Flow in Natural Gas Pipelines." Journal of the
    Brazilian Society of Mechanical Sciences and Engineering 29, no. 3
    (September 2007): 262-73. doi:10.1590/S1678-58782007000300005.

functions.name: fluids.compressible.Muller
documentation:
Calculation function for dealing with flow of a compressible gas in a
pipeline with the Muller formula. Can calculate any of the following,
given all other inputs:

- Flow rate
- Upstream pressure
- Downstream pressure
- Diameter of pipe
- Length of pipe

A variety of different constants and expressions have been presented
for the Muller formula. Here, the form as in [^footnote-1]
is used but with all inputs in base SI units.

$$
Q = 15.7743\frac{T_s}{P_s}E\left(\frac{P_1^2 - P_2^2}{L \cdot Z_{avg} \cdot T_{avg}}\right)^{0.575} \left(\frac{D^{2.725}}{\mu^{0.15} SG^{0.425}}\right)
$$

# Parameters

SG

: Specific gravity of fluid with respect to air at the reference
  temperature and pressure `Ts` and `Ps`, [-]

Tavg

: Average temperature of the fluid in the pipeline, [K]

mu

: Average viscosity of the fluid in the pipeline, [Pa\*s]

L

: Length of pipe, [m]

D

: Diameter of pipe, [m]

P1

: Inlet pressure to pipe, [Pa]

P2

: Outlet pressure from pipe, [Pa]

Q

: Flow rate of gas through pipe at `Ts` and `Ps`, [m^3/s]

Ts

: Reference temperature for the specific gravity of the gas, [K]

Ps

: Reference pressure for the specific gravity of the gas, [Pa]

Zavg

: Average compressibility factor for gas, [-]

E

: Pipeline efficiency, a correction factor between 0 and 1

# Returns

Q, P1, P2, D, or L

: The missing input which was solved for [base SI]

# Notes

This model is presented in [^footnote-1] with a leading constant of 0.4937, the same
exponents as used here, units of inches (diameter), psi, feet (length),
Rankine, pound/(foot\*second) for viscosity, and 1000 ft^3/hour.

This model is also presented in [^footnote-2] in both SI and imperial form. The
SI form was incorrectly converted and yields much higher flow rates. The
imperial version has a leading constant of 85.7368, the same powers as
used here except with rounded values of powers of viscosity (0.2609) and
specific gravity (0.7391) rearranged to be inside the bracketed group;
its units are inches (diameter), psi, miles (length),
Rankine, pound/(foot\*second) for viscosity, and ft^3/day.

This model is shown in base SI units in [^footnote-3], and with a leading constant
of 15.7650, a diameter power of 2.724, main group power of 0.5747,
a specific gravity power of 0.74, and a viscosity power of 0.1494.

# Examples

```pycon
>>> Muller(D=0.340, P1=90E5, P2=20E5, L=160E3, SG=0.693, mu=1E-5,
... Tavg=277.15)
60.45796698148659
```

# References

[^footnote-1]: Mohitpour, Mo, Golshan, and Allan Murray. Pipeline Design and
    Construction: A Practical Approach. 3rd edition. New York: Amer Soc
    Mechanical Engineers, 2006.

[^footnote-2]: Menon, E. Shashi. Gas Pipeline Hydraulics. 1st edition. Boca Raton,
    FL: CRC Press, 2005.

[^footnote-3]: Coelho, Paulo M., and Carlos Pinho. "Considerations about Equations
    for Steady State Flow in Natural Gas Pipelines." Journal of the
    Brazilian Society of Mechanical Sciences and Engineering 29, no. 3
    (September 2007): 262-73. doi:10.1590/S1678-58782007000300005.

functions.name: fluids.compressible.IGT
documentation:
Calculation function for dealing with flow of a compressible gas in a
pipeline with the IGT formula. Can calculate any of the following,
given all other inputs:

- Flow rate
- Upstream pressure
- Downstream pressure
- Diameter of pipe
- Length of pipe

A variety of different constants and expressions have been presented
for the IGT formula. Here, the form as in [^footnote-1]
is used but with all inputs in base SI units.

$$
Q = 24.6241\frac{T_s}{P_s}E\left(\frac{P_1^2 - P_2^2}{L \cdot Z_{avg} \cdot T_{avg}}\right)^{5/9} \left(\frac{D^{8/3}}{\mu^{1/9} SG^{4/9}}\right)
$$

# Parameters

SG

: Specific gravity of fluid with respect to air at the reference
  temperature and pressure `Ts` and `Ps`, [-]

Tavg

: Average temperature of the fluid in the pipeline, [K]

mu

: Average viscosity of the fluid in the pipeline, [Pa\*s]

L

: Length of pipe, [m]

D

: Diameter of pipe, [m]

P1

: Inlet pressure to pipe, [Pa]

P2

: Outlet pressure from pipe, [Pa]

Q

: Flow rate of gas through pipe at `Ts` and `Ps`, [m^3/s]

Ts

: Reference temperature for the specific gravity of the gas, [K]

Ps

: Reference pressure for the specific gravity of the gas, [Pa]

Zavg

: Average compressibility factor for gas, [-]

E

: Pipeline efficiency, a correction factor between 0 and 1

# Returns

Q, P1, P2, D, or L

: The missing input which was solved for [base SI]

# Notes

This model is presented in [^footnote-1] with a leading constant of 0.6643, the same
exponents as used here, units of inches (diameter), psi, feet (length),
Rankine, pound/(foot\*second) for viscosity, and 1000 ft^3/hour.

This model is also presented in [^footnote-2] in both SI and imperial form. Both
forms are correct. The imperial version has a leading constant of 136.9,
the same powers as used here except with rounded values of powers of
viscosity (0.2) and specific gravity (0.8) rearranged to be inside the
bracketed group; its units are inches (diameter), psi, miles (length),
Rankine, pound/(foot\*second) for viscosity, and ft^3/day.

This model is shown in base SI units in [^footnote-3], and with a leading constant
of 24.6145, and the same powers as used here.

# Examples

```pycon
>>> IGT(D=0.340, P1=90E5, P2=20E5, L=160E3, SG=0.693, mu=1E-5, Tavg=277.15)
48.92351786788815
```

# References

[^footnote-1]: Mohitpour, Mo, Golshan, and Allan Murray. Pipeline Design and
    Construction: A Practical Approach. 3rd edition. New York: Amer Soc
    Mechanical Engineers, 2006.

[^footnote-2]: Menon, E. Shashi. Gas Pipeline Hydraulics. 1st edition. Boca Raton,
    FL: CRC Press, 2005.

[^footnote-3]: Coelho, Paulo M., and Carlos Pinho. "Considerations about Equations
    for Steady State Flow in Natural Gas Pipelines." Journal of the
    Brazilian Society of Mechanical Sciences and Engineering 29, no. 3
    (September 2007): 262-73. doi:10.1590/S1678-58782007000300005.

functions.name: fluids.numerics.linspace
documentation:
Port of numpy's linspace to pure python.

Does not support dtype, and returns lists of floats.

functions.name: fluids.numerics.derivative
documentation:
Reimplementation of SciPy's derivative function, with more cached
coefficients and without using numpy. If new coefficients not cached are
needed, they are only calculated once and are remembered.

Support for vector value functions has also been added.

functions.name: fluids.numerics.jacobian
documentation: legendre_points = {

functions.name: fluids.numerics.is_monotonic
documentation:
Checks if the input sequence of points is monotonic.

A sequence is monotonic if it is entirely increasing or ecreasing.
This function iterates through the provided sequence to determine if
it meets this criterion.

# Parameters

points

: A list of numerical values representing a sequence of points, [-]

# Returns

monotonic

: `True` if the sequence is monotonic, `False` if the sequence
  contains both increases and decreases.

# Examples

```pycon
>>> is_monotonic([1, 2, 2, 3])
True
>>> is_monotonic([3, 2, 1])
True
>>> is_monotonic([1, 3, 2, 4])
False
>>> is_monotonic([1e5, 1e7, 1e10])
True
```

# Notes

The function considers a single-element sequence as monotonic. It also
treats sequences with equal adjacent values as monotonic (e.g., [1, 2, 2, 3]).

functions.name: fluids.numerics.is_increasing
documentation:
Check if a given sequence of points is entirely increasing. Points
must be larger, not equal, by this criteria

# Parameters

points

: A list of numerical values representing a sequence of points, [-]

# Returns

increasing

: `True` if the sequence is entirely increasing (each element
  is strictly greater than its predecessor), `False` otherwise, [-]

# Examples

```pycon
>>> is_increasing([1, 2, 3, 4])
True
>>> is_increasing([1, 1, 2, 3])
False
>>> is_increasing([4, 3, 2, 1])
False
```

# Notes

Empty sequences or length 1 sequences are considered increasing.

functions.name: fluids.numerics.min_max_ratios
documentation:
Given known and calculated data, compare
the two and provide two numbers describing
how far away from the known data the calculated
data is.

The numbers are the ratio of the lowest relative
calc, and the highest relative calc.

functions.name: fluids.numerics.std
documentation:
Fast implementation of numpy's std function
for 1d inputs only, with double precision only always.

functions.name: fluids.numerics.chebder
documentation:
not quite a copy of numpy's version because this was faster to
implement.

This does not evaluate the value of a cheb series at a point; it returns
a new chebyshev seriese to be evaluated by chebval.

functions.name: fluids.numerics.isclose
documentation:
Pure python and therefore slow version of the standard library isclose.
Works on older versions of python though! Hasn't been unit tested, but has
been tested.

manual unit testing:

from math import isclose as isclose2
from random import uniform
for i in range(10000000):
    a = uniform(-1, 1)
    b = uniform(-1, 1)
    rel_tol = uniform(0, 1)
    abs_tol = uniform(0, .001)
    ans1 = isclose(a, b, rel_tol, abs_tol)
    ans2 = isclose2(a, b, rel_tol=rel_tol, abs_tol=abs_tol)
    try:
        assert ans1 == ans2
    except:
        print(a, b, rel_tol, abs_tol)

functions.name: fluids.numerics.interp
documentation:
One-dimensional linear interpolation routine inspired/ reimplemented from
NumPy for extra speed for scalar values (and also numpy).

Returns the one-dimensional piecewise linear interpolant to a function
with a given value at discrete data-points.

# Parameters

x

: X-coordinate of the interpolated values, [-]

dx

: X-coordinates of the data points, must be increasing, [-]

dy

: Y-coordinates of the data points; same length as `dx`, [-]

left

: Value to return for `x < dx[0]`, default is `dy[0]`, [-]

right

: Value to return for `x > dx[-1]`, default is `dy[-1]`, [-]

extrapolate

: If True, for the cases of `left` and/or `right` not given, a linear
  extrapolation will be performed outside of bounds, [-]

# Returns

y

: The interpolated value, [-]

# Notes

This function is "unsafe" in that it assumes the x-coordinates
are increasing. It also does not check for nan's, that `dx` and `dy`
are the same length, and that `x` is scalar.

Performance is 40-50% of that of NumPy under CPython.

# Examples

```pycon
>>> interp(2.5, [1, 2, 3], [3, 2, 0])
1.0
```

functions.name: fluids.numerics.implementation_optimize_tck
documentation:
Converts 1-d or 2-d splines calculated with SciPy's `splrep` or.

`bisplrep` to a format for fastest computation - lists in PyPy, and numpy
arrays otherwise.

Only implemented for 3 and 5 length `tck`s.

functions.name: fluids.numerics.damping_maintain_sign
documentation:
Damping function which will maintain the sign of the variable being
manipulated. If the step puts it at the other sign, the distance between `x`
and `step` will be shortened by the multiple of `factor`; i.e. if factor is
`x`, the new value of `x` will be 0 exactly.

The provided `damping` is applied as well.

# Parameters

x

: Previous value in iteration, [-]

step

: Change in `x`, [-]

damping

: The damping factor to be applied always, [-]

factor

: If the calculated step changes sign, this factor will be used instead
  of the step, [-]

# Returns

x_new

: The new value in the iteration, [-]

# Notes

# Examples

```pycon
>>> damping_maintain_sign(100, -200, factor=.5)
50.0
```

functions.name: fluids.numerics.best_bounding_bounds
documentation:
Given:
    1) A presumed bracketing interval such as very far out limits on
       physical bounds
    2) A history of a non-bounded search algorithm which did not converge

Find the best bracketing points which get the algorithm as close to the
solution as possible.

Parameters
----------
low : float
    Low bracketing interval (`f` has opposite sign at `low` than `high`),
    [-]
high : float
    High bracketing interval (`f` has opposite sign at `high` than `low`),
    [-]
f : callable, optional
    1D function to be solved, [-]
xs_pos : list[float]
    Unsorted list of `x` values of points with positive `y` values
    previously evaluated, [-]
ys_pos : list[float]
    Unsorted list of `y` values of points with positive `y` values
    previously evaluated, [-]
xs_neg : list[float]
    Unsorted list of `x` values of points with negative `y` values
    previously evaluated, [-]
ys_neg : list[float]
    Unsorted list of `y` values of points with negative `y` values
    previously evaluated, [-]
fa : float, optional
    Value of function at `low`, used instead of recalculating if provided,
    [-]
fb : float, optional
    Value of function at `high`, used instead of recalculating if provided,
    [-]

Returns
-------
low : float
    Low bracketing interval (`f` has opposite sign at `low` than `high`),
    [-]
high : float
    High bracketing interval (`f` has opposite sign at `high` than `low`),
    [-]
fa : float
    Value of function at `low`, [-]
fb : float, optional
    Value of function at `high`, [-]

Notes
-----
Negative and/or positive history values can be omitted, but both the `x`
and `y` lists should be skipped if so.

More work could be done to handle better the case if the bounds not
bracketing the root but the function history doing so.

functions.name: fluids.numerics.bisect

functions.name: fluids.numerics.newton
documentation:
Newton's method designed to be mostly compatible with SciPy's
implementation, with a few features added and others now implemented.

1) No tracking of how many iterations have progressed.
2) No ability to return a RootResults object
3) No warnings on some cases of bad input (low tolerance, no iterations)
4) Ability to accept True for either fprime or fprime2, which means that
   they are included in the return value of func
5) No handling for inf or nan!
6) Special handling for functions which need to ensure an evaluation at
   the final point
7) Damping as a constant or a fraction
8) Ability to perform bisection, optionally specifying a maximum range
9) Ability to specify minimum and maximum iteration values
10) Ability to specify a tolerance in the `y` direction
11) Ability to pass in keyword arguments as well as positional arguments

From scipy, with some modifications!
https://github.com/scipy/scipy/blob/v1.1.0/scipy/optimize/zeros.py#L66-L206

functions.name: fluids.numerics.check_jacobian

if it does contain those elements.

F0

functions.name: fluids.numerics.normalize
documentation:
Simple function which normalizes a series of values to be from 0 to 1,
and for their sum to add to 1.

$$
x = \frac{x}{sum_i x_i}
$$

# Parameters

values

: array of values

# Returns

fractions

: Array of values from 0 to 1

# Notes

Does not work on negative values, or handle the case where the sum is zero.

# Examples

```pycon
>>> normalize([3, 2, 1])
[0.5, 0.3333333333333333, 0.16666666666666666]
```

functions.name: fluids.numerics.func_35_splev
documentation: NOTE: the first value of each array is used; it is only the indexes that
are adjusted for fortran

functions.name: fluids.numerics.py_splev
documentation:
Evaluate a B-spline using a pure-python port of FITPACK's splev. This is
not fully featured in that it does not support calculating derivatives.
Takes the knots and coefficients of a B-spline tuple, and returns the value
of the smoothing polynomial.

Parameters
----------
x : float or list[float]
    An point or array of points at which to calculate and return the value
    of the  spline, [-]
tck : 3-tuple
    Sequence of length 3 returned by
    `splrep` containing the knots, coefficients, and degree
    of the spline, [-]
ext : int, optional, default 0
    If `x` is not within the range of the spline, this handles the
    calculation of the results.

    * For ext=0, extrapolate the value
    * For ext=1, return 0 as the value
    * For ext=2, raise a ValueError on that point and fail to return values
    * For ext=3, return the boundary value as the value

Returns
-------
y : list
    The array of calculated values; the spline function evaluated at
    the points in `x`, [-]

Notes
-----
The speed of this for a scalar value in CPython is approximately 15%
slower than SciPy's FITPACK interface. In PyPy, this is 10-20 times faster
than using it (benchmarked on PyPy 6).

There could be more bugs in this port.

functions.name: fluids.numerics.py_bisplev
documentation:
Evaluate a bivariate B-spline or its derivatives. For scalars, returns a
float; for other inputs, mimics the formats of SciPy's `bisplev`.

Parameters
----------
x : float or list[float]
    x value (rank 1), [-]
y : float or list[float]
    y value (rank 1), [-]
tck : tuple(list, list, list, int, int)
    Tuple of knot locations, coefficients, and the degree of the spline,
    [tx, ty, c, kx, ky], [-]
dx : int, optional
    Order of partial derivative with respect to `x`, [-]
dy : int, optional
    Order of partial derivative with respect to `y`, [-]

Returns
-------
values : float or list[list[float]]
    Calculated values from spline or their derivatives; according to the
    same format as SciPy's `bisplev`, [-]

Notes
-----
Use `bisplrep` to generate the `tck` representation; there is no Python
port of it.

functions.name: fluids.numerics.fpbspl
documentation:
subroutine fpbspl evaluates the (k+1) non-zero b-splines of degree k at
t(l) <= x < t(l+1) using the stable recurrence relation of de boor and cox.

All arrays are 1d! Optimized the assignment and order and so on.

functions.name: fluids.numerics.polylog2
documentation:
Simple function to calculate PolyLog(2, x) from ranges 0 \<= x \<= 1,
with relative error guaranteed to be < 1E-7 from 0 to 0.99999. This
is a Pade approximation, with three coefficient sets with splits at 0.7
and 0.99. An exception is raised if x is under 0 or above 1.

# Parameters

x

: Value to evaluate PolyLog(2, x) T

# Returns

y

: Evaluated result

# Notes

Efficient (2-4 microseconds). No implementation of this function exists in
SciPy. Derived with mpmath's pade approximation.
Required for the entropy integral of
{obj}`thermo.heat_capacity.Zabransky_quasi_polynomial`.

# Examples

```pycon
>>> polylog2(0.5)
0.5822405264516294
```

This module contains functionality for calculating the flow rate of fluids
in open channels. The Manning and Chezy methods are implemented Weirs as well
as several calculations for flow rate over weirs.

For reporting bugs, adding feature requests, or submitting pull requests,
please use the [GitHub issue tracker](https://github.com/CalebBell/fluids/)
or contact the author at <mailto:Caleb.Andrew.Bell@gmail.com>.

functions.name: fluids.open_flow.Q_weir_V_Shen
documentation:
Calculates the flow rate across a V-notch (triangular) weir from
the height of the liquid above the tip of the notch, and with the angle
of the notch. Most of these type of weir are 90 degrees. Model from [^footnote-1]
as reproduced in [^footnote-2].

Flow rate is given by:

$$
Q = C \tan\left(\frac{\theta}{2}\right)\sqrt{g}(h_1 + k)^{2.5}
$$

# Parameters

h1

: Height of the fluid above the notch [m]

angle

: Angle of the notch [degrees]

# Returns

Q

: Volumetric flow rate across the weir [m^3/s]

# Notes

angles = [20, 40, 60, 80, 100]
Cs = [0.59, 0.58, 0.575, 0.575, 0.58]
k = [0.0028, 0.0017, 0.0012, 0.001, 0.001]

The following limits apply to the use of this equation:

h1 >= 0.05 m
h2 > 0.45 m
h1/h2 \<= 0.4 m
b > 0.9 m

$$
\frac{h_1}{b}\tan\left(\frac{\theta}{2}\right) < 2
$$

Flows are lower than obtained by the curves at
<http://www.lmnoeng.com/Weirs/vweir.php>.

# Examples

```pycon
>>> Q_weir_V_Shen(0.6, angle=45)
0.21071725775478
```

# References

[^footnote-1]: Shen, John. "Discharge Characteristics of Triangular-Notch
    Thin-Plate Weirs : Studies of Flow to Water over Weirs and Dams."
    USGS Numbered Series. Water Supply Paper. U.S. Geological Survey :
    U.S. G.P.O., 1981

[^footnote-2]: Blevins, Robert D. Applied Fluid Dynamics Handbook. New York, N.Y.:
    Van Nostrand Reinhold Co., 1984.
## V-Notch Weirs (Triangular weir)

functions.name: fluids.open_flow.Q_weir_rectangular_Kindsvater_Carter
documentation:
Calculates the flow rate across rectangular weir from
the height of the liquid above the crest of the notch, the liquid depth
beneath it, and the width of the notch. Model from [^footnote-1] as reproduced in
[^footnote-2].

Flow rate is given by:

$$
Q = 0.554\left(1 - 0.0035\frac{h_1}{h_2}\right)(b + 0.0025) \sqrt{g}(h_1 + 0.0001)^{1.5}
$$

# Parameters

h1

: Height of the fluid above the crest of the weir [m]

h2

: Height of the fluid below the crest of the weir [m]

b

: Width of the rectangular flow section of the weir [m]

# Returns

Q

: Volumetric flow rate across the weir [m^3/s]

# Notes

The following limits apply to the use of this equation:

b/b1 ≤ 0.2
h1/h2 < 2
b > 0.15 m
h1 > 0.03 m
h2 > 0.1 m

# Examples

```pycon
>>> Q_weir_rectangular_Kindsvater_Carter(0.2, 0.5, 1)
0.15545928949179422
```

# References

[^footnote-1]: Kindsvater, Carl E., and Rolland W. Carter. "Discharge
    Characteristics of Rectangular Thin-Plate Weirs." Journal of the
    Hydraulics Division 83, no. 6 (December 1957): 1-36.

[^footnote-2]: Blevins, Robert D. Applied Fluid Dynamics Handbook. New York, N.Y.:
    Van Nostrand Reinhold Co., 1984.
## Rectangular Weirs

functions.name: fluids.open_flow.Q_weir_rectangular_SIA
documentation:
Calculates the flow rate across rectangular weir from
the height of the liquid above the crest of the notch, the liquid depth
beneath it, and the width of the notch. Model from [^footnote-1] as reproduced in
[^footnote-2].

Flow rate is given by:

$$
Q = 0.544\left[1 + 0.064\left(\frac{b}{b_1}\right)^2 + \frac{0.00626 - 0.00519(b/b_1)^2}{h_1 + 0.0016}\right] \left[1 + 0.5\left(\frac{b}{b_1}\right)^4\left(\frac{h_1}{h_1+h_2} \right)^2\right]b\sqrt{g}h^{1.5}
$$

# Parameters

h1

: Height of the fluid above the crest of the weir [m]

h2

: Height of the fluid below the crest of the weir [m]

b

: Width of the rectangular flow section of the weir [m]

b1

: Width of the full section of the channel [m]

# Returns

Q

: Volumetric flow rate across the weir [m^3/s]

# Notes

The following limits apply to the use of this equation:

b/b1 ≤ 0.2
h1/h2 < 2
b > 0.15 m
h1 > 0.03 m
h2 > 0.1 m

# Examples

```pycon
>>> Q_weir_rectangular_SIA(0.2, 0.5, 1, 2)
1.0408858453811165
```

# References

[^footnote-1]: Normen für Wassermessungen: bei Durchführung von Abnahmeversuchen
    an Wasserkraftmaschinen. SIA, 1924.

[^footnote-2]: Blevins, Robert D. Applied Fluid Dynamics Handbook. New York, N.Y.:
    Van Nostrand Reinhold Co., 1984.

functions.name: fluids.open_flow.Q_weir_rectangular_full_Ackers
documentation:
Calculates the flow rate across a full-channel rectangular weir from
the height of the liquid above the crest of the weir, the liquid depth
beneath it, and the width of the channel. Model from [^footnote-1] as reproduced in
[^footnote-2], confirmed with [^footnote-3].

Flow rate is given by:

$$
Q = 0.564\left(1+0.150\frac{h_1}{h_2}\right)b\sqrt{g}(h_1+0.001)^{1.5}
$$

# Parameters

h1

: Height of the fluid above the crest of the weir [m]

h2

: Height of the fluid below the crest of the weir [m]

b

: Width of the channel section [m]

# Returns

Q

: Volumetric flow rate across the weir [m^3/s]

# Notes

The following limits apply to the use of this equation:

h1 > 0.02 m
h2 > 0.15 m
h1/h2 ≤ 2.2

# Examples

Example as in [^footnote-3], matches. However, example is unlikely in practice.

```pycon
>>> Q_weir_rectangular_full_Ackers(h1=0.9, h2=0.6, b=5)
9.251938159899948
```

# References

[^footnote-1]: Ackers, Peter, W. R. White, J. A. Perkins, and A. J. M. Harrison.
    Weirs and Flumes for Flow Measurement. Chichester ; New York:
    John Wiley & Sons Ltd, 1978.

[^footnote-2]: Blevins, Robert D. Applied Fluid Dynamics Handbook. New York, N.Y.:
    Van Nostrand Reinhold Co., 1984.

[^footnote-3]: Cengel, Yunus, and John Cimbala. Fluid Mechanics: Fundamentals and
    Applications. Boston: McGraw Hill Higher Education, 2006.
## Rectangular Weirs, full channel

functions.name: fluids.open_flow.Q_weir_rectangular_full_SIA
documentation:
Calculates the flow rate across a full-channel rectangular weir from
the height of the liquid above the crest of the weir, the liquid depth
beneath it, and the width of the channel. Model from [^footnote-1] as reproduced in
[^footnote-2].

Flow rate is given by:

$$
Q = \frac{2}{3}\sqrt{2}\left(0.615 + \frac{0.000615}{h_1+0.0016}\right) b\sqrt{g} h_1 +0.5\left(\frac{h_1}{h_1+h_2}\right)^2b\sqrt{g} h_1^{1.5}
$$

# Parameters

h1

: Height of the fluid above the crest of the weir [m]

h2

: Height of the fluid below the crest of the weir [m]

b

: Width of the channel section [m]

# Returns

Q

: Volumetric flow rate across the weir [m^3/s]

# Notes

The following limits apply to the use of this equation:

0.025 < h < 0.8 m
b > 0.3 m
h2 > 0.3 m
h1/h2 < 1

# Examples

Example compares terribly with the Ackers expression - probable error
in [^footnote-2]. DO NOT USE.

```pycon
>>> Q_weir_rectangular_full_SIA(h1=0.3, h2=0.4, b=2)
1.1875825055400384
```

# References

[^footnote-1]: Normen für Wassermessungen: bei Durchführung von Abnahmeversuchen an
    Wasserkraftmaschinen. SIA, 1924.

[^footnote-2]: Blevins, Robert D. Applied Fluid Dynamics Handbook. New York, N.Y.:
    Van Nostrand Reinhold Co., 1984.

functions.name: fluids.open_flow.Q_weir_rectangular_full_Rehbock
documentation:
Calculates the flow rate across a full-channel rectangular weir from
the height of the liquid above the crest of the weir, the liquid depth
beneath it, and the width of the channel. Model from [^footnote-1] as reproduced in
[^footnote-2].

Flow rate is given by:

$$
Q = \frac{2}{3}\sqrt{2}\left(0.602 + 0.0832\frac{h_1}{h_2}\right) b\sqrt{g} (h_1 +0.00125)^{1.5}
$$

# Parameters

h1

: Height of the fluid above the crest of the weir [m]

h2

: Height of the fluid below the crest of the weir [m]

b

: Width of the channel section [m]

# Returns

Q

: Volumetric flow rate across the weir [m^3/s]

# Notes

The following limits apply to the use of this equation:

0.03 m < h1 < 0.75 m
b > 0.3 m
h2 > 0.3 m
h1/h2 < 1

# Examples

```pycon
>>> Q_weir_rectangular_full_Rehbock(h1=0.3, h2=0.4, b=2)
0.6486856330601333
```

# References

[^footnote-1]: King, H. W., Floyd A. Nagler, A. Streiff, R. L. Parshall, W. S.
    Pardoe, R. E. Ballester, Gardner S. Williams, Th Rehbock, Erik G. W.
    Lindquist, and Clemens Herschel. "Discussion of 'Precise Weir
    Measurements.'" Transactions of the American Society of Civil Engineers
    93, no. 1 (January 1929): 1111-78.

[^footnote-2]: Blevins, Robert D. Applied Fluid Dynamics Handbook. New York, N.Y.:
    Van Nostrand Reinhold Co., 1984.

functions.name: fluids.open_flow.Q_weir_rectangular_full_Kindsvater_Carter
documentation:
Calculates the flow rate across a full-channel rectangular weir from
the height of the liquid above the crest of the weir, the liquid depth
beneath it, and the width of the channel. Model from [^footnote-1] as reproduced in
[^footnote-2].

Flow rate is given by:

Q = \frac{2}{3}\sqrt{2}\left(0.602 + 0.0832\frac{h_1}{h_2}\right) b\sqrt{g} (h_1 +0.00125)^{1.5}

# Parameters


# References

[^footnote-1]: Kindsvater, Carl E., and Rolland W. Carter. "Discharge
    Characteristics of Rectangular Thin-Plate Weirs." Journal of the
    Hydraulics Division 83, no. 6 (December 1957): 1-36.

[^footnote-2]: Blevins, Robert D. Applied Fluid Dynamics Handbook. New York, N.Y.:
    Van Nostrand Reinhold Co., 1984.
print [Q_weir_rectangular_full_Rehbock(h1=0.3, h2=0.4, b=2)]

functions.name: fluids.open_flow.V_Manning
documentation:
Predicts the average velocity of a flow across an open channel of
hydraulic radius Rh and slope S, given the Manning roughness coefficient
n.

Flow rate is given by:

$$
V = \frac{1}{n} R_h^{2/3} S^{0.5}
$$

# Parameters

Rh

: Hydraulic radius of the channel, Flow Area/Wetted perimeter [m]

S

: Slope of the channel, m/m [-]

n

: Manning roughness coefficient; traditionally in the correct units,
  [s/m^(1/3)]

# Returns

V

: Average velocity of the channel [m/s]

# Notes

This is equation is often given in imperial units multiplied by 1.49.
Although `n` could be converted to be in imperial units, in practice this
has not been done and all tables keep it in the units of s/m^(1/3).

# Examples

Example is from [^footnote-2], matches.

```pycon
>>> V_Manning(0.2859, 0.005236, 0.03)
1.0467781958118971
```

# References

[^footnote-1]: Blevins, Robert D. Applied Fluid Dynamics Handbook. New York, N.Y.:
    Van Nostrand Reinhold Co., 1984.

[^footnote-2]: Cengel, Yunus, and John Cimbala. Fluid Mechanics: Fundamentals and
    Applications. Boston: McGraw Hill Higher Education, 2006.
print [Q_weir_rectangular_full_Kindsvater_Carter(h1=0.3, h2=0.4, b=2)]
## Open flow calculations - Manning and Chezy

functions.name: fluids.open_flow.n_Manning_to_C_Chezy
documentation:
Converts a Manning roughness coefficient to a Chezy coefficient,
given the hydraulic radius of the channel.

$$
C = \frac{1}{n}R_h^{1/6}
$$

# Parameters

n

: Manning roughness coefficient [s/m^(1/3)]

Rh

: Hydraulic radius of the channel, Flow Area/Wetted perimeter [m]

# Returns

C

: Chezy coefficient [m^0.5/s]

# Notes

# Examples

Custom example, checked.

```pycon
>>> n_Manning_to_C_Chezy(0.05, Rh=5)
26.15320972023661
```

# References

[^footnote-1]: Chow, Ven Te. Open-Channel Hydraulics. New York: McGraw-Hill, 1959.

functions.name: fluids.open_flow.C_Chezy_to_n_Manning
documentation:
Converts a Chezy coefficient to a Manning roughness coefficient,
given the hydraulic radius of the channel.

$$
n = \frac{1}{C}R_h^{1/6}
$$

# Parameters

C

: Chezy coefficient [m^0.5/s]

Rh

: Hydraulic radius of the channel, Flow Area/Wetted perimeter [m]

# Returns

n

: Manning roughness coefficient [s/m^(1/3)]

# Notes

# Examples

Custom example, checked.

```pycon
>>> C_Chezy_to_n_Manning(26.15, Rh=5)
0.05000613713238358
```

# References

[^footnote-1]: Chow, Ven Te. Open-Channel Hydraulics. New York: McGraw-Hill, 1959.

functions.name: fluids.open_flow.V_Chezy
documentation:
Predicts the average velocity of a flow across an open channel of
hydraulic radius Rh and slope S, given the Chezy coefficient C.

Flow rate is given by:

$$
V = C\sqrt{S R_h}
$$

# Parameters

Rh

: Hydraulic radius of the channel, Flow Area/Wetted perimeter [m]

S

: Slope of the channel, m/m [-]

C

: Chezy coefficient [m^0.5/s]

# Returns

V

: Average velocity of the channel [m/s]

# Notes

# Examples

Custom example, checked.

```pycon
>>> V_Chezy(Rh=5, S=0.001, C=26.153)
1.8492963648371776
```

# References

[^footnote-1]: Blevins, Robert D. Applied Fluid Dynamics Handbook. New York, N.Y.:
    Van Nostrand Reinhold Co., 1984.

[^footnote-2]: Cengel, Yunus, and John Cimbala. Fluid Mechanics: Fundamentals and
    Applications. Boston: McGraw Hill Higher Education, 2006.

[^footnote-3]: Chow, Ven Te. Open-Channel Hydraulics. New York: McGraw-Hill, 1959.

documentation:

/* -------------------------------------------------------------------- */
/* ---------  N R L M S I S E - 0 0    M O D E L    2 0 0 1  ---------- */
/* -------------------------------------------------------------------- */

/* This file is part of the NRLMSISE-00  C source code package - release
 * 20041227
 *
 * The NRLMSISE-00 model was developed by Mike Picone, Alan Hedin, and
 * Doug Drob. They also wrote a NRLMSISE-00 distribution package in
 * FORTRAN which is available at
 * http://uap-www.nrl.navy.mil/models_web/msis/msis_home.htm
 *
 * Dominik Brodowski implemented and maintains this C version. You can
 * reach him at mail@brodo.de. See the file "DOCUMENTATION" for details,
 * and check http://www.brodo.de/english/pub/nrlmsise/index.html for
 * updated releases of this package.
 */

classes.name: fluids.nrlmsise00.nrlmsise_00_header.nrlmsise_flags
documentation:
*   Switches: to turn on and off particular variations use these switches.
*   0 is off, 1 is on, and 2 is main effects off but cross terms on.
*
*   Standard values are 0 for switch 0 and 1 for switches 1 to 23. The
*   array "switches" needs to be set accordingly by the calling program.
*   The arrays sw and swc are set internally.
*
*   switches[i]:
*    i - explanation
*   -----------------
*    0 - output in centimeters instead of meters
*    1 - F10.7 effect on mean
*    2 - time independent
*    3 - symmetrical annual
*    4 - symmetrical semiannual
*    5 - asymmetrical annual
*    6 - asymmetrical semiannual
*    7 - diurnal
*    8 - semidiurnal
*    9 - daily ap [when this is set to -1 (!) the pointer
*                  ap_a in struct nrlmsise_input must
*                  point to a struct ap_array]
*   10 - all UT/long effects
*   11 - longitudinal
*   12 - UT and mixed UT/long
*   13 - mixed AP/UT/LONG
*   14 - terdiurnal
*   15 - departures from diffusive equilibrium
*   16 - all TINF var
*   17 - all TLB var
*   18 - all TN1 var
*   19 - all S var
*   20 - all TN2 var
*   21 - all NLB var
*   22 - all TN3 var
*   23 - turbo scale height var

classes.name: fluids.nrlmsise00.nrlmsise_00_header.ap_array
documentation:
* Array containing the following magnetic values:
*   0 : daily AP
*   1 : 3 hr AP index for current time
*   2 : 3 hr AP index for 3 hrs before current time
*   3 : 3 hr AP index for 6 hrs before current time
*   4 : 3 hr AP index for 9 hrs before current time
*   5 : Average of eight 3 hr AP indices from 12 to 33 hrs
*           prior to current time
*   6 : Average of eight 3 hr AP indices from 36 to 57 hrs
*           prior to current time

classes.name: fluids.nrlmsise00.nrlmsise_00_header.nrlmsise_input
documentation:
/*
*   NOTES ON INPUT VARIABLES:
*      UT, Local Time, and Longitude are used independently in the
*      model and are not of equal importance for every situation.
*      For the most physically realistic calculation these three
*      variables should be consistent (lst=sec/3600 + g_long/15).
*      The Equation of Time departures from the above formula
*      for apparent local time can be included if available but
*      are of minor importance.
*
*      f107 and f107A values used to generate the model correspond
*      to the 10.7 cm radio flux at the actual distance of the Earth
*      from the Sun rather than the radio flux at 1 AU. The following
*      site provides both classes of values:
*      ftp://ftp.ngdc.noaa.govS/STP/SOLAR_DATA/SOLAR_RADIO/FLUX/
*
*      f107, f107A, and ap effects are neither large nor well
*      established below 80 km and these parameters should be set to
*      150., 150., and 4. respectively.
*/

classes.name: fluids.nrlmsise00.nrlmsise_00_header.nrlmsise_output
documentation:
/*
*   OUTPUT VARIABLES:
*      d[0] - HE NUMBER DENSITY(CM-3)
*      d[1] - O NUMBER DENSITY(CM-3)
*      d[2] - N2 NUMBER DENSITY(CM-3)
*      d[3] - O2 NUMBER DENSITY(CM-3)
*      d[4] - AR NUMBER DENSITY(CM-3)
*      d[5] - TOTAL MASS DENSITY(GM/CM3) [includes d[8] in td7d]
*      d[6] - H NUMBER DENSITY(CM-3)
*      d[7] - N NUMBER DENSITY(CM-3)
*      d[8] - Anomalous oxygen NUMBER DENSITY(CM-3)
*      t[0] - EXOSPHERIC TEMPERATURE
*      t[1] - TEMPERATURE AT ALT
*
*
*      O, H, and N are set to zero below 72.5 km
*
*      t[0], Exospheric temperature, is set to global average for
*      altitudes below 120 km. The 120 km gradient is left at global
*      average value for altitudes below 72 km.
*
*      d[5], TOTAL MASS DENSITY, is NOT the same for subroutines GTD7
*      and GTD7D
*
*        SUBROUTINE GTD7 -- d[5] is the sum of the mass densities of the
*        species labeled by indices 0-4 and 6-7 in output variable d.
*        This includes He, O, N2, O2, Ar, H, and N but does NOT include
*        anomalous oxygen (species index 8).
*
*        SUBROUTINE GTD7D -- d[5] is the "effective total mass density
*        for drag" and is the sum of the mass densities of all species
*        in this model, INCLUDING anomalous oxygen.
*/

This module contains correlations for calculating the efficiency of a pump,
motor, or VFD. It also contains some functions for modeling the performance of
a pump, and has been adapted to contain electrical information relevant to
chemical engineering design.

For reporting bugs, adding feature requests, or submitting pull requests,
please use the [GitHub issue tracker](https://github.com/CalebBell/fluids/)
or contact the author at <mailto:Caleb.Andrew.Bell@gmail.com>.

```{contents}
:local: true
```
