functions.name: fluids.pump.Corripio_pump_efficiency
documentation:
Estimates pump efficiency using the method in Corripio (1982)
as shown in [^footnote-1] and originally in [^footnote-2]. Estimation only

$$
\eta_P = -0.316 + 0.24015\ln(Q) - 0.01199\ln(Q)^2
$$

# Parameters

Q

: Volumetric flow rate, [m^3/s]

# Returns

efficiency

: Pump efficiency, [-]

# Notes

For Centrifugal pumps only.
Range is 50 to 5000 GPM, but input variable is in metric.
Values above this range and below this range will go negative,
although small deviations are acceptable.
Example 16.5 in [^footnote-1].

# Examples

```pycon
>>> Corripio_pump_efficiency(461./15850.323)
0.705888867095162
```

# References

[^footnote-1]: Seider, Warren D., J. D. Seader, and Daniel R. Lewin. Product and
    Process Design Principles: Synthesis, Analysis, and Evaluation.
    2 edition. New York: Wiley, 2003.

[^footnote-2]: Corripio, A.B., K.S. Chrien, and L.B. Evans, "Estimate Costs of
    Centrifugal Pumps and Electric Motors," Chem. Eng., 89, 115-118,
    February 22 (1982).

functions.name: fluids.pump.Corripio_motor_efficiency
documentation:
Estimates motor efficiency using the method in Corripio (1982)
as shown in [^footnote-1] and originally in [^footnote-2]. Estimation only.

$$
\eta_M = 0.8  + 0.0319\ln(P_B) - 0.00182\ln(P_B)^2
$$

# Parameters

P

: Power, [W]

# Returns

efficiency

: Motor efficiency, [-]

# Notes

Example 16.5 in [^footnote-1].

# Examples

```pycon
>>> Corripio_motor_efficiency(137*745.7)
0.9128920875679222
```

# References

[^footnote-1]: Seider, Warren D., J. D. Seader, and Daniel R. Lewin. Product and
    Process Design Principles: Synthesis, Analysis, and Evaluation.
    2 edition. New York: Wiley, 2003.

[^footnote-2]: Corripio, A.B., K.S. Chrien, and L.B. Evans, "Estimate Costs of
    Centrifugal Pumps and Electric Motors," Chem. Eng., 89, 115-118,
    February 22 (1982).

functions.name: fluids.pump.VFD_efficiency
documentation:
Returns the efficiency of a Variable Frequency Drive according to [^footnote-1].
These values are generic, and not standardized as minimum values.
Older VFDs often have much worse performance.

# Parameters

P

: Power, [W]

load

: Fraction of motor's rated electrical capacity being used

# Returns

efficiency

: VFD efficiency, [-]

# Notes

The use of a VFD does change the characteristics of a pump curve's
efficiency, but this has yet to be quantified. The effect is small.
This value should be multiplied by the product of the pump and motor
efficiency to determine the overall efficiency.

Efficiency table is in units of hp, so a conversion is performed internally.
If load not specified, assumed 1 - where maximum efficiency occurs.
Table extends down to 3 hp and up to 400 hp; values outside these limits
are rounded to the nearest known value. Values between standardized sizes
are interpolated linearly. Load values extend down to 0.016.

The table used is for Pulse Width Modulation (PWM) VFDs.

# Examples

```pycon
>>> VFD_efficiency(10*hp)
0.96
>>> VFD_efficiency(100*hp, load=0.2)
0.92
```

# References

[^footnote-1]: GoHz.com. Variable Frequency Drive Efficiency.
    <http://www.variablefrequencydrive.org/vfd-efficiency>

functions.name: fluids.pump.motor_round_size
documentation:
Rounds up the power for a motor to the nearest NEMA standard power.
The returned power is always larger or equal to the input power.

# Parameters

P

: Power, [W]

# Returns

P_actual

: Actual power, equal to or larger than input [W]

# Notes

An exception is raised if the power required is larger than any of
the NEMA sizes. Larger motors are available, but are unstandardized.

# Examples

```pycon
>>> motor_round_size(1E5)
111854.98073734052
```

# References

[^footnote-1]: Natural Resources Canada. Electric Motors (1 to 500 HP/0.746 to
    375 kW). As modified 2015-12-17.
    <https://www.nrcan.gc.ca/energy/regulations-codes-standards/products/6885>

functions.name: fluids.pump.CSA_motor_efficiency
documentation:
Returns the efficiency of a NEMA motor according to [^footnote-1].
These values are standards, but are only for full-load operation.

# Parameters

P

: Power, [W]

closed

: Whether or not the motor is enclosed

poles

: The number of poles of the motor

high_efficiency

: Whether or not to look up the high-efficiency value

# Returns

efficiency

: Guaranteed full-load motor efficiency, [-]

# Notes

Criteria for being required to meet the high-efficiency standard is:

- Designed for continuous operation
- Operates by three-phase induction
- Is a squirrel-cage or cage design
- Is NEMA type A, B, or C with T or U frame; or IEC design N or H
- Is designed for single-speed operation
- Has a nominal voltage of less than 600 V AC
- Has a nominal frequency of 60 Hz or 50/60 Hz
- Has 2, 4, or 6 pole construction
- Is either open or closed

Pretty much every motor is required to meet the low-standard efficiency
table, however.

Several low-efficiency standard high power values were added to allow for
easy programming; values are the last listed efficiency in the table.

# Examples

```pycon
>>> CSA_motor_efficiency(100*hp)
0.93
>>> CSA_motor_efficiency(100*hp, closed=True, poles=6, high_efficiency=True)
0.95
```

# References

[^footnote-1]: Natural Resources Canada. Electric Motors (1 to 500 HP/0.746 to
    375 kW). As modified 2015-12-17.
    <https://www.nrcan.gc.ca/energy/regulations-codes-standards/products/6885>

functions.name: fluids.pump.motor_efficiency_underloaded
documentation:
Returns the efficiency of a motor operating under its design power
according to [^footnote-1].These values are generic; manufacturers usually list 4
points on their product information, but full-scale data is hard to find
and not regulated.

# Parameters

P

: Power, [W]

load

: Fraction of motor's rated electrical capacity being used

# Returns

efficiency

: Motor efficiency, [-]

# Notes

If the efficiency returned by this function is unattractive, use a VFD.
The curves used here are polynomial fits to [^footnote-1]'s graph, and curves were
available for the following motor power ranges:
0-1 hp, 1.5-5 hp, 10 hp, 15-25 hp, 30-60 hp, 75-100 hp
If above the upper limit of one range, the next value is returned.

# Examples

```pycon
>>> motor_efficiency_underloaded(1*hp)
0.8705179600980149
>>> motor_efficiency_underloaded(10.1*hp,  .1)
0.6728425932357025
```

# References

[^footnote-1]: Washington State Energy Office. Energy-Efficient Electric Motor
    Selection Handbook. 1993.

functions.name: fluids.pump.specific_speed
documentation:
Returns the specific speed of a pump operating at a specified Q, H,
and n.

$$
n_S = \frac{n\sqrt{Q}}{H^{0.75}}
$$

# Parameters

Q

: Flow rate, [m^3/s]

H

: Head generated by the pump, [m]

n

: Speed of pump [rpm]

# Returns

nS

: Specific Speed, [rpm\*m^0.75/s^0.5]

# Notes

Defined at the BEP, with maximum fitting diameter impeller, at a given
rotational speed.

# Examples

Example from [^footnote-1].

```pycon
>>> specific_speed(0.0402, 100, 3550)
22.50823182748925
```

# References

[^footnote-1]: HI 1.3 Rotodynamic Centrifugal Pumps for Design and Applications

functions.name: fluids.pump.specific_diameter
documentation:
Returns the specific diameter of a pump operating at a specified Q, H,
and D.

$$
D_s = \frac{DH^{1/4}}{\sqrt{Q}}
$$

# Parameters

Q

: Flow rate, [m^3/s]

H

: Head generated by the pump, [m]

D

: Pump impeller diameter [m]

# Returns

Ds

: Specific diameter, [m^0.25/s^0.5]

# Notes

Used in certain pump sizing calculations.

# Examples

```pycon
>>> specific_diameter(Q=0.1, H=10., D=0.1)
0.5623413251903492
```

# References

[^footnote-1]: Green, Don, and Robert Perry. Perry's Chemical Engineers' Handbook,
    Eighth Edition. McGraw-Hill Professional, 2007.

functions.name: fluids.pump.speed_synchronous
documentation:
Returns the synchronous speed of a synchronous AC motor according to [^footnote-1].

$$
N_s = \frac{120 f}{\text{poles}}
$$

# Parameters

f

: Line frequency, [Hz]

poles

: The number of poles of the motor

# Returns

Ns

: Speed of synchronous motor, [rpm]

# Notes

Synchronous motors have no slip. Large synchronous motors are not
self-starting.

# Examples

```pycon
>>> speed_synchronous(50, poles=12)
500.0
>>> speed_synchronous(60, poles=2)
3600.0
```

# References

[^footnote-1]: All About Circuits. Synchronous Motors. Chapter 13 - AC Motors
    <http://www.allaboutcircuits.com/textbook/alternating-current/chpt-13/synchronous-motors/>

functions.name: fluids.pump.current_ideal
documentation:
Returns the current drawn by a motor of power `P` operating at voltage
`V`, with line AC of phase `phase` and power factor `PF` according to [^footnote-1].

Single-phase power:

$$
I = \frac{P}{V \cdot \text{PF}}
$$

3-phase power:

$$
I = \frac{P}{V \cdot \text{PF} \sqrt{3}}
$$

# Parameters

P

: Power of the motor, [W]

V

: Voltage, [V]

phase

: Line AC phase, either 1 or 3

PF

: Power factor of motor

# Returns

I

: Power drawn by motor, [A]

# Notes

Does not include power used by the motor's fan, or startor, or internal
losses. These are all significant.

# Examples

```pycon
>>> current_ideal(V=120, P=1E4, PF=1, phase=1)
83.33333333333333
```

# References

[^footnote-1]: Electrical Construction, and Maintenance. "Calculating Single- and
    3-Phase Parameters." April 1, 2008.
    <http://ecmweb.com/basics/calculating-single-and-3-phase-parameters>.

classes.name: fluids.pump.CountryPower
documentation:
Class to hold information on the residential or electrical data of a
country. Data from Wikipedia, obtained in 2017.

Parameters
----------
plugs : tuple(str)
    Tuple of residential plug letter codes in use in the country, [-]
voltage : float or tuple(float)
    Voltage or voltages in common use of the country (residential data
    has one voltage; industrial data has multiple often), [V]
freq : float
    The electrical frequency in use in the country, [Hz]
country : str
    The name of the country, [-]

*/

*/

*/

functions.name: fluids.nrlmsise00.nrlmsise_00.gtd7
documentation:
The standard model subroutine (GTD7) always computes the.

`thermospheric` mass density by explicitly summing the masses of the
species in equilibrium at the thermospheric temperature T(z).

functions.name: fluids.nrlmsise00.nrlmsise_00.gtd7d
documentation:
A separate subroutine (GTD7D) computes the `effec- tive` mass density
by summing the thermospheric mass density and the mass density of the
anomalous oxygen component.

Below 500 km, the effective mass density is equivalent to the thermospheric
mass density, and we drop the distinction.

documentation:
/*     Thermospheric portion of NRLMSISE-00
*     See GTD7 for more extensive comments
*     alt > 72.5 km!
*/

This module contains functionality for calculating rating and designing
vapor-liquid separators.

For reporting bugs, adding feature requests, or submitting pull requests,
please use the [GitHub issue tracker](https://github.com/CalebBell/fluids/)
or contact the author at <mailto:Caleb.Andrew.Bell@gmail.com>.

```{contents}
:local: true
```

# Functions

```
.. autofunction :: v_Sounders_Brown
```

```
.. autofunction :: K_separator_Watkins
```

```
.. autofunction :: K_separator_demister_York
```

```
.. autofunction :: K_Sounders_Brown_theoretical
```

functions.name: fluids.separator.K_separator_Watkins
documentation:
Calculates the Sounders-Brown `K` factor as used in determining maximum
allowable gas velocity in a two-phase separator in either a horizontal or
vertical orientation. This function approximates a graph published in [^footnote-1]
to determine `K` as used in the following equation:

$$
v_{max} =  K_{SB}\sqrt{\frac{\rho_l-\rho_g}{\rho_g}}
$$

The graph has `K_{SB}` on its y-axis, and the following as its x-axis:

$$
\frac{m_l}{m_g}\sqrt{\rho_g/\rho_l} = \frac{(1-x)}{x}\sqrt{\rho_g/\rho_l}
$$

Cubic spline interpolation is the default method of retrieving a value
from the graph, which was digitized with Engauge-Digitizer.

Also supported are two published curve fits to the graph. The first is that
of Blackwell (1984) [^footnote-2], as follows:

$$
K_{SB} = \exp(-1.942936 -0.814894X -0.179390 X^2 -0.0123790 X^3 + 0.000386235 X^4 + 0.000259550 X^5)

X = \ln\left[\frac{(1-x)}{x}\sqrt{\rho_g/\rho_l}\right]
$$

The second is that of Branan (1999), as follows:

$$
K_{SB} = \exp(-1.877478097 -0.81145804597X -0.1870744085 X^2 -0.0145228667 X^3 -0.00101148518 X^4)

X = \ln\left[\frac{(1-x)}{x}\sqrt{\rho_g/\rho_l}\right]
$$

# Parameters

x

: Quality of fluid entering separator, [-]

rhol

: Density of liquid phase [kg/m^3]

rhog

: Density of gas phase [kg/m^3]

horizontal

: Whether to use the vertical or horizontal value; horizontal is 1.25
  higher

method

: One of 'spline, 'blackwell', or 'branan'

# Returns

K

: Sounders Brown horizontal or vertical `K` factor for two-phase
  separator design only, [m/s]

# Notes

Both the 'branan' and 'blackwell' models are used frequently. However,
the spline is much more accurate.

No limits checking is enforced. However, the x-axis spans only 0.006 to
5.4, and the function should not be used outside those limits.

# Examples

```pycon
>>> K_separator_Watkins(0.88, 985.4, 1.3, horizontal=True)
0.07951613600476297
```

# References

[^footnote-1]: Watkins (1967). Sizing Separators and Accumulators, Hydrocarbon
    Processing, November 1967.

[^footnote-2]: Blackwell, W. Wayne. Chemical Process Design on a Programmable
    Calculator. New York: Mcgraw-Hill, 1984.

[^footnote-3]: Branan, Carl R. Pocket Guide to Chemical Engineering. 1st edition.
    Houston, Tex: Gulf Professional Publishing, 1999.

functions.name: fluids.separator.K_separator_demister_York
documentation:
Calculates the Sounders Brown `K` factor as used in determining maximum
permissible gas velocity in a two-phase separator in either a horizontal or
vertical orientation, *with a demister*.
This function is a curve fit to [^footnote-2] published in [^footnote-1] and is widely used.

For 1 < P < 15 psia:

$$
K = 0.1821 + 0.0029P + 0.0460\ln P
$$

For 15 \<= P \<= 40 psia:

$$
K = 0.35
$$

For P < 5500 psia:

$$
K = 0.430 - 0.023\ln P
$$

In the above equations, P is in units of psia.

# Parameters

P

: Pressure of separator, [Pa]

horizontal

: Whether to use the vertical or horizontal value; horizontal is 1.25
  times higher, [-]

# Returns

K

: Sounders Brown Horizontal or vertical `K` factor for two-phase
  separator design with a demister, [m/s]

# Notes

If the input pressure is under 1 psia, 1 psia is used. If the
input pressure is over 5500 psia, 5500 psia is used.

# Examples

```pycon
>>> K_separator_demister_York(975*psi)
0.08281536035331669
```

# References

[^footnote-1]: Otto H. York Company, "Mist Elimination in Gas Treatment Plants and
    Refineries," Engineering, Parsippany, NJ.

[^footnote-2]: Svrcek, W. Y., and W. D. Monnery. "Design Two-Phase Separators
    within the Right Limits" Chemical Engineering Progress, (October 1,
    1993): 53-60.

functions.name: fluids.separator.v_Sounders_Brown
documentation:
Calculates the maximum allowable vapor velocity in a two-phase
separator to permit separation between entrained droplets and the gas
using an empirical `K` factor, named after Sounders and Brown [^footnote-1].
This is a simplifying expression for terminal velocity and drag on
particles.

$$
v_{max} =  K_{SB} \sqrt{\frac{\rho_l-\rho_g}{\rho_g}}
$$

# Parameters

K

: Sounders Brown `K` factor for two-phase separator design, [m/s]

rhol

: Density of liquid phase [kg/m^3]

rhog

: Density of gas phase [kg/m^3]

# Returns

v_max

: Maximum allowable vapor velocity in a two-phase separator to permit
  separation between entrained droplets and the gas, [m/s]

# Notes

The Sounders Brown K factor is related to the terminal velocity as shown in
the following expression.

$$
v_{term} = v_{max} = \sqrt{\frac{4 g d_p (\rho_p-\rho_f)}{3 C_D \rho_f }}

v_{term} = \sqrt{\frac{(\rho_p-\rho_f)}{\rho_f}} \sqrt{\frac{4 g d_p}{3 C_D}}

v_{term} = K_{SB} \sqrt{\frac{4 g d_p}{3 C_D}}
$$

Note this form corresponds to the Newton's law range (Re > 500), but in
reality droplets are normally in the intermediate or Stoke's law region
[^footnote-2]. For this reason using the drag coefficient expression directly is
cleaner, but identical results can be found with the Sounders Brown
equation.

# Examples

```pycon
>>> v_Sounders_Brown(K=0.08, rhol=985.4, rhog=1.3)
2.2010906387516167
```

# References

[^footnote-1]: Souders, Mott., and George Granger. Brown. "Design of Fractionating
    Columns I. Entrainment and Capacity." Industrial & Engineering Chemistry
    26, no. 1 (January 1, 1934): 98-103. <https://doi.org/10.1021/ie50289a025>.

[^footnote-2]: Vasude, Gael D. Ulrich and Palligarnai T. Chemical Engineering
    Process Design and Economics : A Practical Guide. 2nd edition. Durham,
    N.H: Process Publishing, 2004.

functions.name: fluids.separator.K_Sounders_Brown_theoretical
documentation:
Converts a known drag coefficient into a Sounders-Brown `K` factor
for two-phase separator design. This factor is the traditional way for
separator diameters to be obtained although it is unnecessary and the
theoretical drag coefficient method can be used instead.

$$
K_{SB} = \sqrt{\frac{(\rho_p-\rho_f)}{\rho_f}} = \sqrt{\frac{4 g d_p}{3 C_D}}
$$

# Parameters

D

: Design diameter of the droplets, [m]

Cd

: Drag coefficient [-]

g

: Acceleration due to gravity, [m/s^2]

# Returns

K

: Sounders Brown `K` factor for two-phase separator design, [m/s]

# Notes

Drag coefficient is a function of velocity; so iteration is needed to
obtain the most correct answer. The following example shows the use of
iteration to obtain the final velocity:

```pycon
>>> from fluids import *
>>> V = 2.0
>>> D = 150E-6
>>> rho = 1.3
>>> rhol = 700.
>>> mu = 1E-5
>>> for i in range(10):
...     Re = Reynolds(V=V, rho=rho, mu=mu, D=D)
...     Cd = drag_sphere(Re)
...     K = K_Sounders_Brown_theoretical(D=D, Cd=Cd)
...     V = v_Sounders_Brown(K, rhol=rhol, rhog=rho)
...     print('%.14f' %V)
0.76093307417658
0.56242939340131
0.50732895050696
0.48957142095508
0.48356021946899
0.48149076033622
0.48077414934614
0.48052549959141
0.48043916249756
0.48040917690193
```

The use of Sounders-Brown constants can be replaced as follows (the
v_terminal method includes its own solver for terminal velocity):

```pycon
>>> from fluids.drag import v_terminal
>>> v_terminal(D=D, rhop=rhol, rho=rho, mu=mu)
0.4803932186998
```

# Examples

```pycon
>>> K_Sounders_Brown_theoretical(D=150E-6, Cd=0.5)
0.06263114241333939
```

# References

[^footnote-1]: Svrcek, W. Y., and W. D. Monnery. "Design Two-Phase Separators
    within the Right Limits" Chemical Engineering Progress, (October 1,
    1993): 53-60.
