functions.name: fluids.flow_meter.flow_meter_discharge
documentation:
Calculates the flow rate of an orifice plate based on the geometry
of the plate, measured pressures of the orifice, and the density of the
fluid.

$$
m = \left(\frac{\pi D_o^2}{4}\right) C \frac{\sqrt{2\Delta P \rho_1}} {\sqrt{1 - \beta^4}}\cdot \epsilon
$$

# Parameters

D

: Upstream internal pipe diameter, [m]

Do

: Diameter of orifice at flow conditions, [m]

P1

: Static pressure of fluid upstream of orifice at the cross-section of
  the pressure tap, [Pa]

P2

: Static pressure of fluid downstream of orifice at the cross-section of
  the pressure tap, [Pa]

rho

: Density of fluid at `P1`, [kg/m^3]

C

: Coefficient of discharge of the orifice, [-]

expansibility

: Expansibility factor (1 for incompressible fluids, less than 1 for
  real fluids), [-]

# Returns

m

: Mass flow rate of fluid, [kg/s]

# Notes

This is formula 1-12 in [^footnote-1] and also [^footnote-2].

# Examples

```pycon
>>> flow_meter_discharge(D=0.0739, Do=0.0222, P1=1E5, P2=9.9E4, rho=1.1646,
... C=0.5988, expansibility=0.9975)
0.01120390943807026
```

# References

[^footnote-1]: American Society of Mechanical Engineers. Mfc-3M-2004 Measurement
    Of Fluid Flow In Pipes Using Orifice, Nozzle, And Venturi. ASME, 2001.

[^footnote-2]: ISO 5167-2:2003 - Measurement of Fluid Flow by Means of Pressure
    Differential Devices Inserted in Circular Cross-Section Conduits Running
    Full -- Part 2: Orifice Plates.

functions.name: fluids.flow_meter.orifice_expansibility
documentation:
Calculates the expansibility factor for orifice plate calculations
based on the geometry of the plate, measured pressures of the orifice, and
the isentropic exponent of the fluid.

$$
\epsilon = 1 - (0.351 + 0.256\beta^4 + 0.93\beta^8) \left[1-\left(\frac{P_2}{P_1}\right)^{1/\kappa}\right]
$$

# Parameters

D

: Upstream internal pipe diameter, [m]

Do

: Diameter of orifice at flow conditions, [m]

P1

: Static pressure of fluid upstream of orifice at the cross-section of
  the pressure tap, [Pa]

P2

: Static pressure of fluid downstream of orifice at the cross-section of
  the pressure tap, [Pa]

k

: Isentropic exponent of fluid, [-]

# Returns

expansibility

: Expansibility factor (1 for incompressible fluids, less than 1 for
  real fluids), [-]

# Notes

This formula was determined for the range of P2/P1 >= 0.80, and for fluids
of air, steam, and natural gas. However, there is no objection to using
it for other fluids.

It is said in [^footnote-1] that for liquids this should not be used. The result
can be forced by setting `k` to a really high number like 1E20.

# Examples

```pycon
>>> orifice_expansibility(D=0.0739, Do=0.0222, P1=1E5, P2=9.9E4, k=1.4)
0.9974739057343425
```

# References

[^footnote-1]: American Society of Mechanical Engineers. Mfc-3M-2004 Measurement
    Of Fluid Flow In Pipes Using Orifice, Nozzle, And Venturi. ASME, 2001.

[^footnote-2]: ISO 5167-2:2003 - Measurement of Fluid Flow by Means of Pressure
    Differential Devices Inserted in Circular Cross-Section Conduits Running
    Full -- Part 2: Orifice Plates.

functions.name: fluids.flow_meter.orifice_expansibility_1989
documentation:
Calculates the expansibility factor for orifice plate calculations
based on the geometry of the plate, measured pressures of the orifice, and
the isentropic exponent of the fluid.

$$
\epsilon = 1- (0.41 + 0.35\beta^4)\Delta P/\kappa/P_1
$$

# Parameters

D

: Upstream internal pipe diameter, [m]

Do

: Diameter of orifice at flow conditions, [m]

P1

: Static pressure of fluid upstream of orifice at the cross-section of
  the pressure tap, [Pa]

P2

: Static pressure of fluid downstream of orifice at the cross-section of
  the pressure tap, [Pa]

k

: Isentropic exponent of fluid, [-]

# Returns

expansibility

: Expansibility factor (1 for incompressible fluids, less than 1 for
  real fluids), [-]

# Notes

This formula was determined for the range of P2/P1 >= 0.75, and for fluids
of air, steam, and natural gas. However, there is no objection to using
it for other fluids.

This is an older formula used to calculate expansibility factors for
orifice plates.

In this standard, an expansibility factor formula transformation in terms
of the pressure after the orifice is presented as well. This is the more
standard formulation in terms of the upstream conditions. The other formula
is below for reference only:

$$
\epsilon_2 = \sqrt{1 + \frac{\Delta P}{P_2}} -  (0.41 + 0.35\beta^4) \frac{\Delta P}{\kappa P_2 \sqrt{1 + \frac{\Delta P}{P_2}}}
$$

[^footnote-2] recommends this formulation for wedge meters as well.

# Examples

```pycon
>>> orifice_expansibility_1989(D=0.0739, Do=0.0222, P1=1E5, P2=9.9E4, k=1.4)
0.9970510687411718
```

# References

[^footnote-1]: American Society of Mechanical Engineers. MFC-3M-1989 Measurement
    Of Fluid Flow In Pipes Using Orifice, Nozzle, And Venturi. ASME, 2005.

[^footnote-2]: Miller, Richard W. Flow Measurement Engineering Handbook. 3rd
    edition. New York: McGraw-Hill Education, 1996.

functions.name: fluids.flow_meter.C_Reader_Harris_Gallagher
documentation:
Calculates the coefficient of discharge of the orifice based on the
geometry of the plate, measured pressures of the orifice, mass flow rate
through the orifice, and the density and viscosity of the fluid.

$$
C = 0.5961 + 0.0261\beta^2 - 0.216\beta^8 + 0.000521\left(\frac{ 10^6\beta}{Re_D}\right)^{0.7}\\ + (0.0188 + 0.0063A)\beta^{3.5} \left(\frac{10^6}{Re_D}\right)^{0.3} \\ +(0.043 + 0.080\exp(-10L_1) -0.123\exp(-7L_1))(1-0.11A)\frac{\beta^4} {1-\beta^4} \\ -  0.031(M_2' - 0.8M_2'^{1.1})\beta^{1.3}
$$

$$
M_2' = \frac{2L_2'}{1-\beta}
$$

$$
A = \left(\frac{19000\beta}{Re_{D}}\right)^{0.8}
$$

$$
Re_D = \frac{\rho v D}{\mu}
$$

If D < 71.12 mm (2.8 in.) (Note this is a continuous addition; there is no
discontinuity):

$$
C += 0.11(0.75-\beta)\left(2.8-\frac{D}{0.0254}\right)
$$

If the orifice has corner taps:

$$
L_1 = L_2' = 0
$$

If the orifice has D and D/2 taps:

$$
L_1 = 1
$$

$$
L_2' = 0.47
$$

If the orifice has Flange taps:

$$
L_1 = L_2' = \frac{0.0254}{D}
$$

# Parameters

D

: Upstream internal pipe diameter, [m]

Do

: Diameter of orifice at flow conditions, [m]

rho

: Density of fluid at `P1`, [kg/m^3]

mu

: Viscosity of fluid at `P1`, [Pa\*s]

m

: Mass flow rate of fluid through the orifice, [kg/s]

taps

: The orientation of the taps; one of 'corner', 'flange', 'D', or 'D/2',
  [-]

# Returns

C

: Coefficient of discharge of the orifice, [-]

# Notes

The following limits apply to the orifice plate standard [^footnote-1]:

The measured pressure difference for the orifice plate should be under
250 kPa.

There are roughness limits as well; the roughness should be under 6
micrometers, although there are many more conditions to that given in [^footnote-1].

For orifice plates with D and D/2 or corner pressure taps:

- Orifice bore diameter muse be larger than 12.5 mm (0.5 inches)
- Pipe diameter between 50 mm and 1 m (2 to 40 inches)
- Beta between 0.1 and 0.75 inclusive
- Reynolds number larger than 5000 (for $0.10 \le \beta \le 0.56$)
  or for $\beta \ge 0.56, Re_D \ge 16000\beta^2$

For orifice plates with flange pressure taps:

- Orifice bore diameter muse be larger than 12.5 mm (0.5 inches)
- Pipe diameter between 50 mm and 1 m (2 to 40 inches)
- Beta between 0.1 and 0.75 inclusive
- Reynolds number larger than 5000 and also larger than
  $170000\beta^2 D$.

This is also presented in Crane's TP410 (2009) publication, whereas the
1999 and 1982 editions showed only a graph for discharge coefficients.

# Examples

```pycon
>>> C_Reader_Harris_Gallagher(D=0.07391, Do=0.0222, rho=1.165, mu=1.85E-5,
... m=0.12, taps='flange')
0.5990326277163659
```

# References

[^footnote-1]: American Society of Mechanical Engineers. Mfc-3M-2004 Measurement
    Of Fluid Flow In Pipes Using Orifice, Nozzle, And Venturi. ASME, 2001.

[^footnote-2]: ISO 5167-2:2003 - Measurement of Fluid Flow by Means of Pressure
    Differential Devices Inserted in Circular Cross-Section Conduits Running
    Full -- Part 2: Orifice Plates.

[^footnote-3]: Reader-Harris, M. J., "The Equation for the Expansibility Factor for
    Orifice Plates," Proceedings of FLOMEKO 1998, Lund, Sweden, 1998:
    209-214.

[^footnote-4]: Reader-Harris, Michael. Orifice Plates and Venturi Tubes. Springer,
    2015\.

functions.name: fluids.flow_meter.C_Miller_1996
documentation:
Calculates the coefficient of discharge of any of the orifice types
supported by the Miller (1996) [^footnote-1] correlation set. These correlations
cover a wide range of industrial applications and sizes. Most of them are
functions of `beta` ratio and Reynolds number. Unlike the ISO standards,
these correlations do not come with well defined ranges of validity, so
caution should be applied using there correlations.

The base equation is as follows, and each orifice type and range has
different values or correlations for $C_{\infty}$, `b`, and `n`.

$$
C = C_{\infty} + \frac{b}{{Re}_D^n}
$$

# Parameters

D

: Upstream internal pipe diameter, [m]

Do

: Diameter of orifice at flow conditions, [m]

rho

: Density of fluid at `P1`, [kg/m^3]

mu

: Viscosity of fluid at `P1`, [Pa\*s]

m

: Mass flow rate of fluid through the orifice, [kg/s]

subtype

: One of 'orifice', 'eccentric orifice', 'segmental orifice',
  'conical orifice', or 'quarter circle orifice', [-]

taps

: The orientation of the taps; one of 'corner', 'flange',
  'D and D/2', 'pipe', or 'vena contracta'; not all orifice subtypes
  support the all tap types [-]

tap_position

: The rotation of the taps, used **only for the eccentric orifice case**
  where the pressure profile is are not symmetric; '180 degree' for the
  normal case where the taps are opposite the orifice bore, and
  '90 degree' for the case where, normally for operational reasons, the
  taps are near the bore [-]

# Returns

C

: Coefficient of discharge of the orifice, [-]

# Notes

Many of the correlations transition at a pipe diameter of 100 mm to
different equations, which will lead to discontinuous behavior.

It should also be noted the author of these correlations developed a
commercial flow meter rating software package, at [^footnote-2].
He passed away in 2014, but contributed massively to the field of flow
measurement.

The numerous equations for the different cases are as follows:

For all **regular (concentric) orifices**, the `b` equation is as follows
and n = 0.75:

$$
b = 91.706\beta^{2.5}
$$

Regular (concentric) orifice, corner taps:

$$
C_{\infty} = 0.5959 + 0.0312\beta^2.1 - 0.184\beta^8
$$

Regular (concentric) orifice, flange taps, D > 58.4 mm:

$$
C_{\infty} = 0.5959 + 0.0312\beta^{2.1} - 0.184\beta^8 + \frac{2.286\beta^4}{(D_{mm}(1.0 - \beta^4))} - \frac{0.856\beta^3}{D_{mm}}
$$

Regular (concentric) orifice, flange taps, D < 58.4 mm:

$$
C_{\infty} = 0.5959 + 0.0312\beta^{2.1} - 0.184\beta^8 + \frac{0.039\beta^4}{(1.0 - \beta^4)} - \frac{0.856\beta^3}{D_{mm}}
$$

Regular (concentric) orifice, 'D and D/2' taps:

$$
C_{\infty} = 0.5959 + 0.0312\beta^{2.1} - 0.184\beta^8 + \frac{0.039\beta^4}{(1.0 - \beta^4)} - 0.01584
$$

Regular (concentric) orifice, 'pipe' taps:

$$
C_{\infty} = 0.5959 + 0.461\beta^{2.1} + 0.48\beta^8 + \frac{0.039\beta^4}{(1.0 - \beta^4)}
$$

For the case of a **conical orifice**, there is no tap dependence
and one equation (`b` = 0, `n` = 0):

$$
C_{\infty} = 0.734 \text{ if } 250\beta \le Re \le 500\beta \text{ else } 0.730
$$

For the case of a **quarter circle orifice**, corner and flange taps have
the same dependence (`b` = 0, `n` = 0):

$$
C_{\infty} = (0.7746 - 0.1334\beta^{2.1} + 1.4098\beta^8                + \frac{0.0675\beta^4}{(1 - \beta^4)} + 0.3865\beta^3)
$$

For all **segmental orifice** types, `b` = 0 and `n` = 0

Segmental orifice, 'flange' taps, D < 10 cm:

$$
C_{\infty} = 0.6284 + 0.1462\beta^{2.1} - 0.8464\beta^8 + \frac{0.2603\beta^4}{(1-\beta^4)} - 0.2886\beta^3
$$

Segmental orifice, 'flange' taps, D > 10 cm:

$$
C_{\infty} = 0.6276 + 0.0828\beta^{2.1} + 0.2739\beta^8 - \frac{0.0934\beta^4}{(1-\beta^4)} - 0.1132\beta^3
$$

Segmental orifice, 'vena contracta' taps, D < 10 cm:

$$
C_{\infty} = 0.6261 + 0.1851\beta^{2.1} - 0.2879\beta^8 + \frac{0.1170\beta^4}{(1-\beta^4)} - 0.2845\beta^3
$$

Segmental orifice, 'vena contracta' taps, D > 10 cm:

$$
C_{\infty} = 0.6276 + 0.0828\beta^{2.1} + 0.2739\beta^8 - \frac{0.0934\beta^4}{(1-\beta^4)} - 0.1132\beta^3
$$

For all **eccentric orifice** types, `n` = 0.75 and `b` is fit to a
polynomial of `beta`.

Eccentric orifice, 'flange' taps, 180 degree opposite taps, D < 10 cm:

$$
C_{\infty} = 0.5917 + 0.3061\beta^{2.1} + .3406\beta^8 -\frac{.1019\beta^4}{(1-\beta^4)} - 0.2715\beta^3
$$

$$
b = 7.3 - 15.7\beta + 170.8\beta^2 - 399.7\beta^3 + 332.2\beta^4
$$

Eccentric orifice, 'flange' taps, 180 degree opposite taps, D > 10 cm:

$$
C_{\infty} = 0.6016 + 0.3312\beta^{2.1} -1.5581\beta^8 + \frac{0.6510\beta^4}{(1-\beta^4)} - 0.7308\beta^3
$$

$$
b = -139.7 + 1328.8\beta - 4228.2\beta^2 + 5691.9\beta^3 - 2710.4\beta^4
$$

Eccentric orifice, 'flange' taps, 90 degree side taps, D < 10 cm:

$$
C_{\infty} = 0.5866 + 0.3917\beta^{2.1} + .7586\beta^8 - \frac{.2273\beta^4}{(1-\beta^4)} - .3343\beta^3
$$

$$
b = 69.1 - 469.4\beta + 1245.6\beta^2 -1287.5\beta^3 + 486.2\beta^4
$$

Eccentric orifice, 'flange' taps, 90 degree side taps, D > 10 cm:

$$
C_{\infty} = 0.6037 + 0.1598\beta^{2.1} -.2918\beta^8 + \frac{0.0244\beta^4}{(1-\beta^4)} - 0.0790\beta^3
$$

$$
b = -103.2 + 898.3\beta - 2557.3\beta^2 + 2977.0\beta^3 - 1131.3\beta^4
$$

Eccentric orifice, 'vena contracta' taps, 180 degree opposite taps, D < 10 cm:

$$
C_{\infty} = 0.5925 + 0.3380\beta^{2.1} + 0.4016\beta^8 - \frac{.1046\beta^4}{(1-\beta^4)} - 0.3212\beta^3
$$

$$
b = 23.3 -207.0\beta + 821.5\beta^2 -1388.6\beta^3 + 900.3\beta^4
$$

Eccentric orifice, 'vena contracta' taps, 180 degree opposite taps, D > 10 cm:

$$
C_{\infty} = 0.5922 + 0.3932\beta^{2.1} + .3412\beta^8 - \frac{.0569\beta^4}{(1-\beta^4)} - 0.4628\beta^3
$$

$$
b = 55.7 - 471.4\beta + 1721.8\beta^2 - 2722.6\beta^3 + 1569.4\beta^4
$$

Eccentric orifice, 'vena contracta' taps, 90 degree side taps, D < 10 cm:

$$
C_{\infty} = 0.5875 + 0.3813\beta^{2.1} + 0.6898\beta^8 - \frac{0.1963\beta^4}{(1-\beta^4)} - 0.3366\beta^3
$$

$$
b = -69.3 + 556.9\beta - 1332.2\beta^2 + 1303.7\beta^3 - 394.8\beta^4
$$

Eccentric orifice, 'vena contracta' taps, 90 degree side taps, D > 10 cm:

$$
C_{\infty} = 0.5949 + 0.4078\beta^{2.1} + 0.0547\beta^8 + \frac{0.0955\beta^4}{(1-\beta^4)} - 0.5608\beta^3
$$

$$
b = 52.8 - 434.2\beta + 1571.2\beta^2 - 2460.9\beta^3 + 1420.2\beta^4
$$

# Examples

```pycon
>>> C_Miller_1996(D=0.07391, Do=0.0222, rho=1.165, mu=1.85E-5, m=0.12, taps='flange', subtype='orifice')
0.599065557156788
```

# References

[^footnote-1]: Miller, Richard W. Flow Measurement Engineering Handbook.
    McGraw-Hill Education, 1996.

[^footnote-2]: "RW Miller & Associates." Accessed April 13, 2020.
    <http://rwmillerassociates.com/>.

functions.name: fluids.flow_meter.C_eccentric_orifice_ISO_15377_1998
documentation:
Calculates the coefficient of discharge of an eccentric orifice based
on the geometry of the plate according to ISO 15377, first introduced in
1998 and also presented in the second 2007 edition. It also appears in BS
1042-1.2: 1989.

$$
C = 0.9355 - 1.6889\beta + 3.0428\beta^2 - 1.7989\beta^3
$$

This type of plate is normally used to avoid obstructing entrained gas,
liquid, or sediment.

# Parameters

D

: Upstream internal pipe diameter, [m]

Do

: Diameter of orifice at flow conditions, [m]

# Returns

C

: Coefficient of discharge of the eccentric orifice, [-]

# Notes

No correction for where the orifice bore is located is included.

The following limits apply to the orifice plate standard [^footnote-1]:

- Bore diameter above 50 mm.
- Pipe diameter between 10 cm and 1 m.
- Beta ratio between 0.46 and 0.84
- $2\times 10^5 \beta^2 \le Re_D \le 10^6 \beta$

The uncertainty of this equation for `C` is said to be 1% if `beta` is
under 0.75, otherwise 2%.

The `orifice_expansibility` function should be used with this method as
well.

Additional specifications are:

- The thickness of the orifice should be between 0.005\`D\` and 0.02\`D\`.
- Corner tappings should be used, with hole diameter between 3 and 10 mm.
  The angular orientation of the tappings matters because the flow meter
  is not symmetrical. The angle should ideally be at the top or bottom of
  the plate, opposite which side the bore is on - but this can cause
  issues with deposition if the taps are on the bottom or gas bubbles if
  the taps are on the taps. The taps are often placed 30 degrees away from
  the ideal position to counteract this effect, with under an extra 2%
  error.

Some comparisons with CFD results can be found in [^footnote-2].

# Examples

```pycon
>>> C_eccentric_orifice_ISO_15377_1998(.2, .075)
0.6351923828125
```

# References

[^footnote-1]: TC 30/SC 2, ISO. ISO/TR 15377:1998, Measurement of Fluid Flow by
    Means of Pressure-Differential Devices - Guide for the Specification of
    Nozzles and Orifice Plates beyond the Scope of ISO 5167-1.

[^footnote-2]: Yashvanth, S., Varadarajan Seshadri, and J. YogeshKumarK. "CFD
    Analysis of Flow through Single and Multi Stage Eccentric Orifice Plate
    Assemblies," 2017.

functions.name: fluids.flow_meter.C_quarter_circle_orifice_ISO_15377_1998
documentation:
Calculates the coefficient of discharge of a quarter circle orifice based
on the geometry of the plate according to ISO 15377, first introduced in
1998 and also presented in the second 2007 edition. It also appears in BS
1042-1.2: 1989.

$$
C = 0.73823 + 0.3309\beta - 1.1615\beta^2 + 1.5084\beta^3
$$

# Parameters

D

: Upstream internal pipe diameter, [m]

Do

: Diameter of orifice at flow conditions, [m]

# Returns

C

: Coefficient of discharge of the quarter circle orifice, [-]

# Notes

The discharge coefficient of this type of orifice plate remains
constant down to a lower than normal `Re`, as occurs in highly
viscous applications.

The following limits apply to the orifice plate standard [^footnote-1]:

- Bore diameter >= 1.5 cm
- Pipe diameter \<= 50 cm
- Beta ratio between 0.245 and 0.6
- $Re_d \le 10^5 \beta$

There is also a table in [^footnote-1] which lists increased minimum
upstream pipe diameters for pipes of different roughnesses; the
higher the roughness, the larger the pipe diameter required,
and the table goes up to 20 cm for rusty cast iron.

Corner taps should be used up to pipe diameters of 40 mm;
for larger pipes, corner or flange taps can be used. No impact
on the flow coefficient is included in the correlation.

The recommended expansibility method for this type of orifice is
{obj}`orifice_expansibility`.

# Examples

```pycon
>>> C_quarter_circle_orifice_ISO_15377_1998(.2, .075)
0.77851484375000
```

# References

[^footnote-1]: TC 30/SC 2, ISO. ISO/TR 15377:1998, Measurement of Fluid Flow by
    Means of Pressure-Differential Devices - Guide for the Specification of
    Nozzles and Orifice Plates beyond the Scope of ISO 5167-1.

functions.name: fluids.flow_meter.discharge_coefficient_to_K
documentation:
Converts a discharge coefficient to a standard loss coefficient,
for use in computation of the actual pressure drop of an orifice or other
device.

$$
K = \left[\frac{\sqrt{1-\beta^4(1-C^2)}}{C\beta^2} - 1\right]^2
$$

# Parameters

D

: Upstream internal pipe diameter, [m]

Do

: Diameter of orifice at flow conditions, [m]

C

: Coefficient of discharge of the orifice, [-]

# Returns

K

: Loss coefficient with respect to the velocity and density of the fluid
  just upstream of the orifice, [-]

# Notes

If expansibility is used in the orifice calculation, the result will not
match with the specified pressure drop formula in [^footnote-1]; it can almost
be matched by dividing the calculated mass flow by the expansibility factor
and using that mass flow with the loss coefficient.

# Examples

```pycon
>>> discharge_coefficient_to_K(D=0.07366, Do=0.05, C=0.61512)
5.2314291729754
```

# References

[^footnote-1]: American Society of Mechanical Engineers. Mfc-3M-2004 Measurement
    Of Fluid Flow In Pipes Using Orifice, Nozzle, And Venturi. ASME, 2001.

[^footnote-2]: ISO 5167-2:2003 - Measurement of Fluid Flow by Means of Pressure
    Differential Devices Inserted in Circular Cross-Section Conduits Running
    Full -- Part 2: Orifice Plates.

functions.name: fluids.flow_meter.K_to_discharge_coefficient
documentation:
Converts a standard loss coefficient to a discharge coefficient.

$$
C = \sqrt{\frac{1}{2 \sqrt{K} \beta^{4} + K \beta^{4}} - \frac{\beta^{4}}{2 \sqrt{K} \beta^{4} + K \beta^{4}} }
$$

# Parameters

D

: Upstream internal pipe diameter, [m]

Do

: Diameter of orifice at flow conditions, [m]

K

: Loss coefficient with respect to the velocity and density of the fluid
  just upstream of the orifice, [-]

# Returns

C

: Coefficient of discharge of the orifice, [-]

# Notes

If expansibility is used in the orifice calculation, the result will not
match with the specified pressure drop formula in [^footnote-1]; it can almost
be matched by dividing the calculated mass flow by the expansibility factor
and using that mass flow with the loss coefficient.

This expression was derived with SymPy, and checked numerically. There were
three other, incorrect roots.

# Examples

```pycon
>>> K_to_discharge_coefficient(D=0.07366, Do=0.05, K=5.2314291729754)
0.6151200000000001
```

# References

[^footnote-1]: American Society of Mechanical Engineers. Mfc-3M-2004 Measurement
    Of Fluid Flow In Pipes Using Orifice, Nozzle, And Venturi. ASME, 2001.

[^footnote-2]: ISO 5167-2:2003 - Measurement of Fluid Flow by Means of Pressure
    Differential Devices Inserted in Circular Cross-Section Conduits Running
    Full -- Part 2: Orifice Plates.

functions.name: fluids.flow_meter.dP_orifice
documentation:
Calculates the non-recoverable pressure drop of an orifice plate based
on the pressure drop and the geometry of the plate and the discharge
coefficient.

$$
\Delta\bar w = \frac{\sqrt{1-\beta^4(1-C^2)}-C\beta^2} {\sqrt{1-\beta^4(1-C^2)}+C\beta^2} (P_1 - P_2)
$$

# Parameters

D

: Upstream internal pipe diameter, [m]

Do

: Diameter of orifice at flow conditions, [m]

P1

: Static pressure of fluid upstream of orifice at the cross-section of
  the pressure tap, [Pa]

P2

: Static pressure of fluid downstream of orifice at the cross-section of
  the pressure tap, [Pa]

C

: Coefficient of discharge of the orifice, [-]

# Returns

dP

: Non-recoverable pressure drop of the orifice plate, [Pa]

# Notes

This formula can be well approximated by:

$$
\Delta\bar w = \left(1 - \beta^{1.9}\right)(P_1 - P_2)
$$

The recoverable pressure drop should be recovered by 6 pipe diameters
downstream of the orifice plate.

# Examples

```pycon
>>> dP_orifice(D=0.07366, Do=0.05, P1=200000.0, P2=183000.0, C=0.61512)
9069.474705745388
```

# References

[^footnote-1]: American Society of Mechanical Engineers. Mfc-3M-2004 Measurement
    Of Fluid Flow In Pipes Using Orifice, Nozzle, And Venturi. ASME, 2001.

[^footnote-2]: ISO 5167-2:2003 - Measurement of Fluid Flow by Means of Pressure
    Differential Devices Inserted in Circular Cross-Section Conduits Running
    Full -- Part 2: Orifice Plates.

functions.name: fluids.flow_meter.velocity_of_approach_factor
documentation:
Calculates a factor for orifice plate design called the `velocity of
approach`.

$$
\text{Velocity of approach} = \frac{1}{\sqrt{1 - \beta^4}}
$$

# Parameters

D

: Upstream internal pipe diameter, [m]

Do

: Diameter of orifice at flow conditions, [m]

# Returns

velocity_of_approach

: Coefficient of discharge of the orifice, [-]

# Notes

# Examples

```pycon
>>> velocity_of_approach_factor(D=0.0739, Do=0.0222)
1.0040970074165514
```

# References

[^footnote-1]: American Society of Mechanical Engineers. Mfc-3M-2004 Measurement
    Of Fluid Flow In Pipes Using Orifice, Nozzle, And Venturi. ASME, 2001.

functions.name: fluids.flow_meter.flow_coefficient
documentation:
Calculates a factor for differential pressure flow meter design called
the `flow coefficient`. This should not be confused with the flow
coefficient often used when discussing valves.

$$
\text{Flow coefficient} = \frac{C}{\sqrt{1 - \beta^4}}
$$

# Parameters

D

: Upstream internal pipe diameter, [m]

Do

: Diameter of flow meter characteristic dimension at flow conditions, [m]

C

: Coefficient of discharge of the flow meter, [-]

# Returns

flow_coefficient

: Differential pressure flow meter flow coefficient, [-]

# Notes

This measure is used not just for orifices but for other differential
pressure flow meters [^footnote-2].

It is sometimes given the symbol K. It is also equal to the product of the
diacharge coefficient and the velocity of approach factor [^footnote-2].

# Examples

```pycon
>>> flow_coefficient(D=0.0739, Do=0.0222, C=0.6)
0.6024582044499308
```

# References

[^footnote-1]: American Society of Mechanical Engineers. Mfc-3M-2004 Measurement
    Of Fluid Flow In Pipes Using Orifice, Nozzle, And Venturi. ASME, 2001.

[^footnote-2]: Miller, Richard W. Flow Measurement Engineering Handbook. 3rd
    edition. New York: McGraw-Hill Education, 1996.

functions.name: fluids.flow_meter.nozzle_expansibility
documentation:
Calculates the expansibility factor for a nozzle or venturi nozzle,
based on the geometry of the plate, measured pressures of the orifice, and
the isentropic exponent of the fluid.

$$
\epsilon = \left\{\left(\frac{\kappa \tau^{2/\kappa}}{\kappa-1}\right) \left(\frac{1 - \beta^4}{1 - \beta^4 \tau^{2/\kappa}}\right) \left[\frac{1 - \tau^{(\kappa-1)/\kappa}}{1 - \tau} \right] \right\}^{0.5}
$$

# Parameters

D

: Upstream internal pipe diameter, [m]

Do

: Diameter of orifice of the venturi or nozzle, [m]

P1

: Static pressure of fluid upstream of orifice at the cross-section of
  the pressure tap, [Pa]

P2

: Static pressure of fluid downstream of orifice at the cross-section of
  the pressure tap, [Pa]

k

: Isentropic exponent of fluid, [-]

beta

: Optional `beta` ratio, which is useful to specify for wedge meters or
  flow meters which have a different beta ratio calculation, [-]

# Returns

expansibility

: Expansibility factor (1 for incompressible fluids, less than 1 for
  real fluids), [-]

# Notes

This formula was determined for the range of P2/P1 >= 0.75.

Mathematically the equation cannot be evaluated at `k` = 1, but if the
limit of the equation is taken the following equation is obtained and is
implemented:

$$
\epsilon = \sqrt{\frac{- D^{4} P_{1} P_{2}^{2} \log{\left(\frac{P_{2}} {P_{1}} \right)} + Do^{4} P_{1} P_{2}^{2} \log{\left(\frac{P_{2}}{P_{1}} \right)}}{D^{4} P_{1}^{3} - D^{4} P_{1}^{2} P_{2} - Do^{4} P_{1} P_{2}^{2} + Do^{4} P_{2}^{3}}}
$$

Note also there is a small amount of floating-point error around the range
of `k` ~1+1e-5 to ~1-1e-5, starting with 1e-7 and increasing to the point
of giving values larger than 1 or zero in the `k` ~1+1e-12 to ~1-1e-12
range.

# Examples

```pycon
>>> nozzle_expansibility(D=0.0739, Do=0.0222, P1=1E5, P2=9.9E4, k=1.4)
0.994570234456
```

# References

[^footnote-1]: American Society of Mechanical Engineers. Mfc-3M-2004 Measurement
    Of Fluid Flow In Pipes Using Orifice, Nozzle, And Venturi. ASME, 2001.

[^footnote-2]: ISO 5167-3:2003 - Measurement of Fluid Flow by Means of Pressure
    Differential Devices Inserted in Circular Cross-Section Conduits Running
    Full -- Part 3: Nozzles and Venturi Nozzles.

functions.name: fluids.flow_meter.C_long_radius_nozzle
documentation:
Calculates the coefficient of discharge of a long radius nozzle used
for measuring flow rate of fluid, based on the geometry of the nozzle,
mass flow rate through the nozzle, and the density and viscosity of the
fluid.

$$
C = 0.9965 - 0.00653\beta^{0.5} \left(\frac{10^6}{Re_D}\right)^{0.5}
$$

# Parameters

D

: Upstream internal pipe diameter, [m]

Do

: Diameter of long radius nozzle orifice at flow conditions, [m]

rho

: Density of fluid at `P1`, [kg/m^3]

mu

: Viscosity of fluid at `P1`, [Pa\*s]

m

: Mass flow rate of fluid through the nozzle, [kg/s]

# Returns

C

: Coefficient of discharge of the long radius nozzle orifice, [-]

# Notes

# Examples

```pycon
>>> C_long_radius_nozzle(D=0.07391, Do=0.0422, rho=1.2, mu=1.8E-5, m=0.1)
0.9805503704679863
```

# References

[^footnote-1]: American Society of Mechanical Engineers. Mfc-3M-2004 Measurement
    Of Fluid Flow In Pipes Using Orifice, Nozzle, And Venturi. ASME, 2001.

[^footnote-2]: ISO 5167-3:2003 - Measurement of Fluid Flow by Means of Pressure
    Differential Devices Inserted in Circular Cross-Section Conduits Running
    Full -- Part 3: Nozzles and Venturi Nozzles.

functions.name: fluids.flow_meter.C_ISA_1932_nozzle
documentation:
Calculates the coefficient of discharge of an ISA 1932 style nozzle
used for measuring flow rate of fluid, based on the geometry of the nozzle,
mass flow rate through the nozzle, and the density and viscosity of the
fluid.

$$
C = 0.9900 - 0.2262\beta^{4.1} - (0.00175\beta^2 - 0.0033\beta^{4.15}) \left(\frac{10^6}{Re_D}\right)^{1.15}
$$

# Parameters

D

: Upstream internal pipe diameter, [m]

Do

: Diameter of nozzle orifice at flow conditions, [m]

rho

: Density of fluid at `P1`, [kg/m^3]

mu

: Viscosity of fluid at `P1`, [Pa\*s]

m

: Mass flow rate of fluid through the nozzle, [kg/s]

# Returns

C

: Coefficient of discharge of the nozzle orifice, [-]

# Notes

# Examples

```pycon
>>> C_ISA_1932_nozzle(D=0.07391, Do=0.0422, rho=1.2, mu=1.8E-5, m=0.1)
0.9635849973250495
```

# References

[^footnote-1]: American Society of Mechanical Engineers. Mfc-3M-2004 Measurement
    Of Fluid Flow In Pipes Using Orifice, Nozzle, And Venturi. ASME, 2001.

[^footnote-2]: ISO 5167-3:2003 - Measurement of Fluid Flow by Means of Pressure
    Differential Devices Inserted in Circular Cross-Section Conduits Running
    Full -- Part 3: Nozzles and Venturi Nozzles.

functions.name: fluids.flow_meter.C_venturi_nozzle
documentation:
Calculates the coefficient of discharge of an Venturi style nozzle
used for measuring flow rate of fluid, based on the geometry of the nozzle.

$$
C = 0.9858 - 0.196\beta^{4.5}
$$

# Parameters

D

: Upstream internal pipe diameter, [m]

Do

: Diameter of nozzle orifice at flow conditions, [m]

# Returns

C

: Coefficient of discharge of the nozzle orifice, [-]

# Notes

# Examples

```pycon
>>> C_venturi_nozzle(D=0.07391, Do=0.0422)
0.9698996454169576
```

# References

[^footnote-1]: American Society of Mechanical Engineers. Mfc-3M-2004 Measurement
    Of Fluid Flow In Pipes Using Orifice, Nozzle, And Venturi. ASME, 2001.

[^footnote-2]: ISO 5167-3:2003 - Measurement of Fluid Flow by Means of Pressure
    Differential Devices Inserted in Circular Cross-Section Conduits Running
    Full -- Part 3: Nozzles and Venturi Nozzles.

functions.name: fluids.flow_meter.dP_venturi_tube
documentation:
Calculates the non-recoverable pressure drop of a venturi tube
differential pressure meter based on the pressure drop and the geometry of
the venturi meter.

$$
\epsilon =  \frac{\Delta\bar w }{\Delta P}
$$

The $\epsilon$ value is looked up in a table of values as a function
of beta ratio and upstream pipe diameter (roughness impact).

# Parameters

D

: Upstream internal pipe diameter, [m]

Do

: Diameter of venturi tube at flow conditions, [m]

P1

: Static pressure of fluid upstream of venturi tube at the cross-section
  of the pressure tap, [Pa]

P2
: Static pressure of fluid downstream of venturi tube at the

  : cross-section of the pressure tap, [Pa]

# Returns

dP

: Non-recoverable pressure drop of the venturi tube, [Pa]

# Notes

The recoverable pressure drop should be recovered by 6 pipe diameters
downstream of the venturi tube.

Note there is some information on the effect of Reynolds number as well
in [^footnote-1] and [^footnote-2], with a curve showing an increased pressure drop
from 1E5-6E5 to with a decreasing multiplier from 1.75 to 1; the multiplier
is 1 for higher Reynolds numbers. This is not currently included in this
implementation.

# Examples

```pycon
>>> dP_venturi_tube(D=0.07366, Do=0.05, P1=200000.0, P2=183000.0)
1788.5717754177406
```

# References

[^footnote-1]: American Society of Mechanical Engineers. Mfc-3M-2004 Measurement
    Of Fluid Flow In Pipes Using Orifice, Nozzle, And Venturi. ASME, 2001.

[^footnote-2]: ISO 5167-4:2003 - Measurement of Fluid Flow by Means of Pressure
    Differential Devices Inserted in Circular Cross-Section Conduits Running
    Full -- Part 4: Venturi Tubes.

functions.name: fluids.flow_meter.diameter_ratio_cone_meter
documentation:
Calculates the diameter ratio `beta` used to characterize a cone
flow meter.

$$
\beta = \sqrt{1 - \frac{d_c^2}{D^2}}
$$

# Parameters

D

: Upstream internal pipe diameter, [m]

Dc

: Diameter of the largest end of the cone meter, [m]

# Returns

beta

: Cone meter diameter ratio, [-]

# Notes

A mathematically equivalent formula often written is:

$$
\beta = \frac{\sqrt{D^2 - d_c^2}}{D}
$$

# Examples

```pycon
>>> diameter_ratio_cone_meter(D=0.2575, Dc=0.184)
0.6995709873957624
```

# References

[^footnote-1]: Hollingshead, Colter. "Discharge Coefficient Performance of Venturi,
    Standard Concentric Orifice Plate, V-Cone, and Wedge Flow Meters at
    Small Reynolds Numbers." May 1, 2011.
    <https://digitalcommons.usu.edu/etd/869>.

functions.name: fluids.flow_meter.cone_meter_expansibility_Stewart
documentation:
Calculates the expansibility factor for a cone flow meter,
based on the geometry of the cone meter, measured pressures of the orifice,
and the isentropic exponent of the fluid. Developed in [^footnote-1], also shown
in [^footnote-2].

$$
\epsilon = 1 - (0.649 + 0.696\beta^4) \frac{\Delta P}{\kappa P_1}
$$

# Parameters

D

: Upstream internal pipe diameter, [m]

Dc

: Diameter of the largest end of the cone meter, [m]

P1

: Static pressure of fluid upstream of cone meter at the cross-section of
  the pressure tap, [Pa]

P2

: Static pressure of fluid at the end of the center of the cone pressure
  tap, [Pa]

k

: Isentropic exponent of fluid, [-]

# Returns

expansibility

: Expansibility factor (1 for incompressible fluids, less than 1 for
  real fluids), [-]

# Notes

This formula was determined for the range of P2/P1 >= 0.75; the only gas
used to determine the formula is air.

# Examples

```pycon
>>> cone_meter_expansibility_Stewart(D=1, Dc=0.9, P1=1E6, P2=8.5E5, k=1.2)
0.9157343
```

# References

[^footnote-1]: Stewart, D. G., M. Reader-Harris, and NEL Dr RJW Peters. "Derivation
    of an Expansibility Factor for the V-Cone Meter." In Flow Measurement
    International Conference, Peebles, Scotland, UK, 2001.

[^footnote-2]: ISO 5167-5:2016 - Measurement of Fluid Flow by Means of Pressure
    Differential Devices Inserted in Circular Cross-Section Conduits Running
    Full -- Part 5: Cone meters.

functions.name: fluids.flow_meter.dP_cone_meter
documentation:
Calculates the non-recoverable pressure drop of a cone meter
based on the measured pressures before and at the cone end, and the
geometry of the cone meter according to [^footnote-1].

$$
\Delta \bar \omega = (1.09 - 0.813\beta)\Delta P
$$

# Parameters

D

: Upstream internal pipe diameter, [m]

Dc

: Diameter of the largest end of the cone meter, [m]

P1

: Static pressure of fluid upstream of cone meter at the cross-section of
  the pressure tap, [Pa]

P2

: Static pressure of fluid at the end of the center of the cone pressure
  tap, [Pa]

# Returns

dP

: Non-recoverable pressure drop of the orifice plate, [Pa]

# Notes

The recoverable pressure drop should be recovered by 6 pipe diameters
downstream of the cone meter.

# Examples

```pycon
>>> dP_cone_meter(1, .7, 1E6, 9.5E5)
25470.093437973323
```

# References

[^footnote-1]: ISO 5167-5:2016 - Measurement of Fluid Flow by Means of Pressure
    Differential Devices Inserted in Circular Cross-Section Conduits Running
    Full -- Part 5: Cone meters.

functions.name: fluids.flow_meter.diameter_ratio_wedge_meter
documentation:
Calculates the diameter ratio `beta` used to characterize a wedge
flow meter as given in [^footnote-1] and [^footnote-2].

$$
\beta = \left(\frac{1}{\pi}\left\{\arccos\left[1 - \frac{2H}{D} \right] - 2 \left[1 - \frac{2H}{D} \right]\left(\frac{H}{D} - \left[\frac{H}{D}\right]^2 \right)^{0.5}\right\}\right)^{0.5}
$$

# Parameters

D

: Upstream internal pipe diameter, [m]

H

: Portion of the diameter of the clear segment of the pipe up to the
  wedge blocking flow; the height of the pipe up to the wedge, [m]

# Returns

beta

: Wedge meter diameter ratio, [-]

# Notes

# Examples

```pycon
>>> diameter_ratio_wedge_meter(D=0.2027, H=0.0608)
0.5022531424646643
```

# References

[^footnote-1]: Hollingshead, Colter. "Discharge Coefficient Performance of Venturi,
    Standard Concentric Orifice Plate, V-Cone, and Wedge Flow Meters at
    Small Reynolds Numbers." May 1, 2011.
    <https://digitalcommons.usu.edu/etd/869>.

[^footnote-2]: IntraWedge WEDGE FLOW METER Type: IWM. January 2011.
    <http://www.intra-automation.com/download.php?file=pdf/products/technical_information/en/ti_iwm_en.pdf>

functions.name: fluids.flow_meter.C_wedge_meter_Miller
documentation:
Calculates the coefficient of discharge of an wedge flow meter
used for measuring flow rate of fluid, based on the geometry of the
differential pressure flow meter.

For half-inch lines:

$$
C = 0.7883 + 0.107(1 - \beta^2)
$$

For 1 to 1.5 inch lines:

$$
C = 0.6143 + 0.718(1 - \beta^2)
$$

For 1.5 to 24 inch lines:

$$
C = 0.5433 + 0.2453(1 - \beta^2)
$$

# Parameters

D

: Upstream internal pipe diameter, [m]

H

: Portion of the diameter of the clear segment of the pipe up to the
  wedge blocking flow; the height of the pipe up to the wedge, [m]

# Returns

C

: Coefficient of discharge of the wedge flow meter, [-]

# Notes

There is an ISO standard being developed to cover wedge meters as of 2018.

Wedge meters can have varying angles; 60 and 90 degree wedge meters have
been reported. Tap locations 1 or 2 diameters (upstream and downstream),
and 2D upstream/1D downstream have been used. Some wedges are sharp;
some are smooth. [^footnote-2] gives some experimental values.

# Examples

```pycon
>>> C_wedge_meter_Miller(D=0.1524, H=0.3*0.1524)
0.7267069372687651
```

# References

[^footnote-1]: Miller, Richard W. Flow Measurement Engineering Handbook. 3rd
    edition. New York: McGraw-Hill Education, 1996.

[^footnote-2]: Seshadri, V., S. N. Singh, and S. Bhargava. "Effect of Wedge Shape
    and Pressure Tap Locations on the Characteristics of a Wedge Flowmeter."
    IJEMS Vol.01(5), October 1994.

functions.name: fluids.flow_meter.C_wedge_meter_ISO_5167_6_2017
documentation:
Calculates the coefficient of discharge of an wedge flow meter
used for measuring flow rate of fluid, based on the geometry of the
differential pressure flow meter according to the ISO 5167-6 standard
(draft 2017).

$$
C = 0.77 - 0.09\beta
$$

# Parameters

D

: Upstream internal pipe diameter, [m]

H

: Portion of the diameter of the clear segment of the pipe up to the
  wedge blocking flow; the height of the pipe up to the wedge, [m]

# Returns

C

: Coefficient of discharge of the wedge flow meter, [-]

# Notes

This standard applies for wedge meters in line sizes between 50 and 600 mm;
and height ratios between 0.2 and 0.6. The range of allowable Reynolds
numbers is large; between 1E4 and 9E6. The uncertainty of the flow
coefficient is approximately 4%. Usually a 10:1 span of flow can be
measured accurately. The discharge and entry length of the meters must be
at least half a pipe diameter. The wedge angle must be 90 degrees, plus or
minus two degrees.

The orientation of the wedge meter does not change the accuracy of this
model.

There should be a straight run of 10 pipe diameters before the wedge meter
inlet, and two of the same pipe diameters after it.

# Examples

```pycon
>>> C_wedge_meter_ISO_5167_6_2017(D=0.1524, H=0.3*0.1524)
0.724792059539853
```

# References

[^footnote-1]: ISO/DIS 5167-6 - Measurement of Fluid Flow by Means of Pressure
    Differential Devices Inserted in Circular Cross-Section Conduits Running
    Full -- Part 6: Wedge Meters.

functions.name: fluids.flow_meter.dP_wedge_meter
documentation:
Calculates the non-recoverable pressure drop of a wedge meter
based on the measured pressures before and at the wedge meter, and the
geometry of the wedge meter according to [^footnote-1].

$$
\Delta \bar \omega = (1.09 - 0.79\beta)\Delta P
$$

# Parameters

D

: Upstream internal pipe diameter, [m]

H

: Portion of the diameter of the clear segment of the pipe up to the
  wedge blocking flow; the height of the pipe up to the wedge, [m]

P1

: Static pressure of fluid upstream of wedge meter at the cross-section
  of the pressure tap, [Pa]

P2

: Static pressure of fluid at the end of the wedge meter pressure tap, [
  Pa]

# Returns

dP

: Non-recoverable pressure drop of the wedge meter, [Pa]

# Notes

The recoverable pressure drop should be recovered by 5 pipe diameters
downstream of the wedge meter.

# Examples

```pycon
>>> dP_wedge_meter(1, .7, 1E6, 9.5E5)
20344.849697483587
```

# References

[^footnote-1]: ISO/DIS 5167-6 - Measurement of Fluid Flow by Means of Pressure
    Differential Devices Inserted in Circular Cross-Section Conduits Running
    Full -- Part 6: Wedge Meters.

functions.name: fluids.flow_meter.C_Reader_Harris_Gallagher_wet_venturi_tube
documentation:
Calculates the coefficient of discharge of the wet gas venturi tube
based on the geometry of the tube, mass flow rates of liquid and vapor
through the tube, the density of the liquid and gas phases, and an
adjustable coefficient `H`.

$$
C = 1 - 0.0463\exp(-0.05Fr_{gas, th}) \cdot \min\left(1, \sqrt{\frac{X}{0.016}}\right)
$$

$$
Fr_{gas, th} = \frac{Fr_{\text{gas, densionetric }}}{\beta^{2.5}}
$$

$$
\phi = \sqrt{1 + C_{Ch} X + X^2}
$$

$$
C_{Ch} = \left(\frac{\rho_l}{\rho_{1,g}}\right)^n + \left(\frac{\rho_{1, g}}{\rho_{l}}\right)^n
$$

$$
n = \max\left[0.583 - 0.18\beta^2 - 0.578\exp\left(\frac{-0.8 Fr_{\text{gas, densiometric}}}{H}\right),0.392 - 0.18\beta^2 \right]
$$

$$
X = \left(\frac{m_l}{m_g}\right) \sqrt{\frac{\rho_{1,g}}{\rho_l}}
$$

$$
{Fr_{\text{gas, densiometric}}} = \frac{v_{gas}}{\sqrt{gD}} \sqrt{\frac{\rho_{1,g}}{\rho_l - \rho_{1,g}}} =  \frac{4m_g}{\rho_{1,g} \pi D^2 \sqrt{gD}} \sqrt{\frac{\rho_{1,g}}{\rho_l - \rho_{1,g}}}
$$

# Parameters

mg

: Mass flow rate of gas through the venturi tube, [kg/s]

ml

: Mass flow rate of liquid through the venturi tube, [kg/s]

rhog

: Density of gas at `P1`, [kg/m^3]

rhol

: Density of liquid at `P1`, [kg/m^3]

D

: Upstream internal pipe diameter, [m]

Do

: Diameter of venturi tube at flow conditions, [m]

H

: A surface-tension effect coefficient used to adjust for different
  fluids, (1 for a hydrocarbon liquid, 1.35 for water, 0.79 for water in
  steam) [-]

# Returns

C

: Coefficient of discharge of the wet gas venturi tube flow meter
  (includes flow rate of gas ONLY), [-]

# Notes

This model has more error than single phase differential pressure meters.
The model was first published in [^footnote-1], and became ISO 11583 later.

The limits of this correlation according to [^footnote-2] are as follows:

$$
0.4 \le \beta \le 0.75
$$

$$
0 < X \le 0.3
$$

$$
Fr_{gas, th} > 3
$$

$$
\frac{\rho_g}{\rho_l} > 0.02
$$

$$
D \ge 50 \text{ mm}
$$

# Examples

```pycon
>>> C_Reader_Harris_Gallagher_wet_venturi_tube(mg=5.31926, ml=5.31926/2,
... rhog=50.0, rhol=800., D=.1, Do=.06, H=1)
0.9754210845876333
```

# References

[^footnote-1]: Reader-harris, Michael, and Tuv Nel. An Improved Model for
    Venturi-Tube Over-Reading in Wet Gas, 2009.

[^footnote-2]: ISO/TR 11583:2012 Measurement of Wet Gas Flow by Means of Pressure
    Differential Devices Inserted in Circular Cross-Section Conduits.

functions.name: fluids.flow_meter.dP_Reader_Harris_Gallagher_wet_venturi_tube
documentation:
Calculates the non-recoverable pressure drop of a wet gas venturi
nozzle based on the pressure drop and the geometry of the venturi nozzle,
the mass flow rates of liquid and gas through it, the densities of the
vapor and liquid phase, and an adjustable coefficient `H`.

$$
Y = \frac{\Delta \bar \omega}{\Delta P} - 0.0896 - 0.48\beta^9
$$

$$
Y_{max} = 0.61\exp\left[-11\frac{\rho_{1,g}}{\rho_l} - 0.045 \frac{Fr_{gas}}{H}\right]
$$

$$
\frac{Y}{Y_{max}} = 1 - \exp\left[-35 X^{0.75} \exp \left( \frac{-0.28Fr_{gas}}{H}\right)\right]
$$

$$
X = \left(\frac{m_l}{m_g}\right) \sqrt{\frac{\rho_{1,g}}{\rho_l}}
$$

$$
{Fr_{\text{gas, densiometric}}} = \frac{v_{gas}}{\sqrt{gD}} \sqrt{\frac{\rho_{1,g}}{\rho_l - \rho_{1,g}}} =  \frac{4m_g}{\rho_{1,g} \pi D^2 \sqrt{gD}} \sqrt{\frac{\rho_{1,g}}{\rho_l - \rho_{1,g}}}
$$

# Parameters

D

: Upstream internal pipe diameter, [m]

Do

: Diameter of venturi tube at flow conditions, [m]

P1

: Static pressure of fluid upstream of venturi tube at the cross-section
  of the pressure tap, [Pa]

P2

: Static pressure of fluid downstream of venturi tube at the cross-
  section of the pressure tap, [Pa]

ml

: Mass flow rate of liquid through the venturi tube, [kg/s]

mg

: Mass flow rate of gas through the venturi tube, [kg/s]

rhol

: Density of liquid at `P1`, [kg/m^3]

rhog

: Density of gas at `P1`, [kg/m^3]

H

: A surface-tension effect coefficient used to adjust for different
  fluids, (1 for a hydrocarbon liquid, 1.35 for water, 0.79 for water in
  steam) [-]

# Returns

C

: Coefficient of discharge of the wet gas venturi tube flow meter
  (includes flow rate of gas ONLY), [-]

# Notes

The model was first published in [^footnote-1], and became ISO 11583 later.

# Examples

```pycon
>>> dP_Reader_Harris_Gallagher_wet_venturi_tube(D=.1, Do=.06, H=1,
... P1=6E6, P2=6E6-5E4, ml=5.31926/2, mg=5.31926, rhog=50.0, rhol=800.,)
16957.43843129572
```

# References

[^footnote-1]: Reader-harris, Michael, and Tuv Nel. An Improved Model for
    Venturi-Tube Over-Reading in Wet Gas, 2009.

[^footnote-2]: ISO/TR 11583:2012 Measurement of Wet Gas Flow by Means of Pressure
    Differential Devices Inserted in Circular Cross-Section Conduits.

functions.name: fluids.flow_meter.differential_pressure_meter_beta
documentation:
Calculates the beta ratio of a differential pressure meter.

# Parameters

D

: Upstream internal pipe diameter, [m]

D2

: Diameter of orifice, or venturi meter orifice, or flow tube orifice,
  or cone meter end diameter, or wedge meter fluid flow height, [m]

meter_type

: One of {'conical orifice', 'orifice', 'machined convergent venturi tube',
  'ISO 5167 orifice', 'Miller quarter circle orifice', 'Hollingshead venturi sharp',
  'segmental orifice', 'Miller conical orifice', 'Miller segmental orifice',
  'quarter circle orifice', 'Hollingshead v cone', 'wedge meter', 'eccentric orifice',
  'venuri nozzle', 'rough welded convergent venturi tube', 'ISA 1932 nozzle',
  'ISO 15377 quarter-circle orifice', 'Hollingshead venturi smooth',
  'Hollingshead orifice', 'cone meter', 'Hollingshead wedge', 'Miller orifice',
  'long radius nozzle', 'ISO 15377 conical orifice', 'unspecified meter',
  'as cast convergent venturi tube', 'Miller eccentric orifice',
  'ISO 15377 eccentric orifice'}, [-]

# Returns

beta

: Differential pressure meter diameter ratio, [-]

# Notes

# Examples

```pycon
>>> differential_pressure_meter_beta(D=0.2575, D2=0.184,
... meter_type='cone meter')
0.6995709873957624
```

functions.name: fluids.flow_meter.differential_pressure_meter_C_epsilon
documentation:
Calculates the discharge coefficient and expansibility of a flow
meter given the mass flow rate, the upstream pressure, the second
pressure value, and the orifice diameter for a differential
pressure flow meter based on the geometry of the meter, measured pressures
of the meter, and the density, viscosity, and isentropic exponent of the
fluid.

# Parameters

D

: Upstream internal pipe diameter, [m]

D2

: Diameter of orifice, or venturi meter orifice, or flow tube orifice,
  or cone meter end diameter, or wedge meter fluid flow height, [m]

m

: Mass flow rate of fluid through the flow meter, [kg/s]

P1

: Static pressure of fluid upstream of differential pressure meter at the
  cross-section of the pressure tap, [Pa]

P2

: Static pressure of fluid downstream of differential pressure meter or
  at the prescribed location (varies by type of meter) [Pa]

rho

: Density of fluid at `P1`, [kg/m^3]

mu

: Viscosity of fluid at `P1`, [Pa\*s]

k

: Isentropic exponent of fluid, [-]

meter_type

: One of {'conical orifice', 'orifice', 'machined convergent venturi tube',
  'ISO 5167 orifice', 'Miller quarter circle orifice', 'Hollingshead venturi sharp',
  'segmental orifice', 'Miller conical orifice', 'Miller segmental orifice',
  'quarter circle orifice', 'Hollingshead v cone', 'wedge meter', 'eccentric orifice',
  'venuri nozzle', 'rough welded convergent venturi tube', 'ISA 1932 nozzle',
  'ISO 15377 quarter-circle orifice', 'Hollingshead venturi smooth',
  'Hollingshead orifice', 'cone meter', 'Hollingshead wedge', 'Miller orifice',
  'long radius nozzle', 'ISO 15377 conical orifice', 'unspecified meter',
  'as cast convergent venturi tube', 'Miller eccentric orifice',
  'ISO 15377 eccentric orifice'}, [-]

taps

: The orientation of the taps; one of 'corner', 'flange', 'D', or 'D/2';
  applies for orifice meters only, [-]

tap_position

: The rotation of the taps, used **only for the eccentric orifice case**
  where the pressure profile is are not symmetric; '180 degree' for the
  normal case where the taps are opposite the orifice bore, and
  '90 degree' for the case where, normally for operational reasons, the
  taps are near the bore [-]

C_specified

: If specified, the correlation for the meter type is not used - this
  value is returned for `C`

epsilon_specified

: If specified, the correlation for the fluid expansibility is not used -
  this value is returned for $\epsilon$, [-]

# Returns

C

: Coefficient of discharge of the specified flow meter type at the
  specified conditions, [-]

expansibility

: Expansibility factor (1 for incompressible fluids, less than 1 for
  real fluids), [-]

# Notes

This function should be called by an outer loop when solving for a
variable.

The latest ISO formulations for `expansibility` are used with the Miller
correlations.

# Examples

```pycon
>>> differential_pressure_meter_C_epsilon(D=0.07366, D2=0.05, P1=200000.0,
... P2=183000.0, rho=999.1, mu=0.0011, k=1.33, m=7.702338035732168,
... meter_type='ISO 5167 orifice', taps='D')
(0.6151252900244296, 0.9711026966676307)
```

functions.name: fluids.flow_meter.differential_pressure_meter_solver
documentation:
Calculates either the mass flow rate, the upstream pressure, the second
pressure value, or the orifice diameter for a differential
pressure flow meter based on the geometry of the meter, measured pressures
of the meter, and the density, viscosity, and isentropic exponent of the
fluid. This solves an equation iteratively to obtain the correct flow rate.

# Parameters

D

: Upstream internal pipe diameter, [m]

rho

: Density of fluid at `P1`, [kg/m^3]

mu

: Viscosity of fluid at `P1`, [Pa\*s]

k

: Isentropic exponent of fluid; required unless `epsilon_specified` is
  specified , [-]

D2

: Diameter of orifice, or venturi meter orifice, or flow tube orifice,
  or cone meter end diameter, or wedge meter fluid flow height, [m]

P1

: Static pressure of fluid upstream of differential pressure meter at the
  cross-section of the pressure tap, [Pa]

P2

: Static pressure of fluid downstream of differential pressure meter or
  at the prescribed location (varies by type of meter) [Pa]

m

: Mass flow rate of fluid through the flow meter, [kg/s]

meter_type

: One of {'conical orifice', 'orifice', 'machined convergent venturi tube',
  'ISO 5167 orifice', 'Miller quarter circle orifice', 'Hollingshead venturi sharp',
  'segmental orifice', 'Miller conical orifice', 'Miller segmental orifice',
  'quarter circle orifice', 'Hollingshead v cone', 'wedge meter', 'eccentric orifice',
  'venuri nozzle', 'rough welded convergent venturi tube', 'ISA 1932 nozzle',
  'ISO 15377 quarter-circle orifice', 'Hollingshead venturi smooth',
  'Hollingshead orifice', 'cone meter', 'Hollingshead wedge', 'Miller orifice',
  'long radius nozzle', 'ISO 15377 conical orifice', 'unspecified meter',
  'as cast convergent venturi tube', 'Miller eccentric orifice',
  'ISO 15377 eccentric orifice'}, [-]

taps

: The orientation of the taps; one of 'corner', 'flange', 'D', or 'D/2';
  applies for orifice meters only, [-]

tap_position

: The rotation of the taps, used **only for the eccentric orifice case**
  where the pressure profile is are not symmetric; '180 degree' for the
  normal case where the taps are opposite the orifice bore, and
  '90 degree' for the case where, normally for operational reasons, the
  taps are near the bore [-]

C_specified

: If specified, the correlation for the meter type is not used - this
  value is used for `C`

epsilon_specified

: If specified, the correlation for the fluid expansibility is not used -
  this value is used for $\epsilon$. Many publications recommend
  this be set to 1 for incompressible fluids [-]

# Returns

ans

: One of `m`, the mass flow rate of the fluid; `P1`, the pressure
  upstream of the flow meter; `P2`, the second pressure
  tap's value; and `D2`, the diameter of the measuring device; units
  of respectively, kg/s, Pa, Pa, or m

# Notes

See the appropriate functions for the documentation for the formulas and
references used in each method.

The solvers make some assumptions about the range of values answers may be
in.

Note that the solver for the upstream pressure uses the provided values of
density, viscosity and isentropic exponent; whereas these values all
depend on pressure (albeit to a small extent). An outer loop should be
added with pressure-dependent values calculated in it for maximum accuracy.

It would be possible to solve for the upstream pipe diameter, but there is
no use for that functionality.

If a meter has already been calibrated to have a known `C`, this may be
provided and it will be used in place of calculating one.

# Examples

```pycon
>>> differential_pressure_meter_solver(D=0.07366, D2=0.05, P1=200000.0,
... P2=183000.0, rho=999.1, mu=0.0011, k=1.33,
... meter_type='ISO 5167 orifice', taps='D')
7.70233803573
```

```pycon
>>> differential_pressure_meter_solver(D=0.07366, m=7.702338, P1=200000.0,
... P2=183000.0, rho=999.1, mu=0.0011, k=1.33,
... meter_type='ISO 5167 orifice', taps='D')
0.0499999999
```

functions.name: fluids.flow_meter.differential_pressure_meter_dP
documentation:
Calculates the non-recoverable pressure drop of a differential
pressure flow meter based on the geometry of the meter, measured pressures
of the meter, and for most models the meter discharge coefficient.

# Parameters

D

: Upstream internal pipe diameter, [m]

D2

: Diameter of orifice, or venturi meter orifice, or flow tube orifice,
  or cone meter end diameter, or wedge meter fluid flow height, [m]

P1

: Static pressure of fluid upstream of differential pressure meter at the
  cross-section of the pressure tap, [Pa]

P2

: Static pressure of fluid downstream of differential pressure meter or
  at the prescribed location (varies by type of meter) [Pa]

C

: Coefficient of discharge (used only in orifice plates, and venturi
  nozzles), [-]

meter_type

: One of {'conical orifice', 'orifice', 'machined convergent venturi tube',
  'ISO 5167 orifice', 'Miller quarter circle orifice', 'Hollingshead venturi sharp',
  'segmental orifice', 'Miller conical orifice', 'Miller segmental orifice',
  'quarter circle orifice', 'Hollingshead v cone', 'wedge meter', 'eccentric orifice',
  'venuri nozzle', 'rough welded convergent venturi tube', 'ISA 1932 nozzle',
  'ISO 15377 quarter-circle orifice', 'Hollingshead venturi smooth',
  'Hollingshead orifice', 'cone meter', 'Hollingshead wedge', 'Miller orifice',
  'long radius nozzle', 'ISO 15377 conical orifice', 'unspecified meter',
  'as cast convergent venturi tube', 'Miller eccentric orifice',
  'ISO 15377 eccentric orifice'}, [-]

# Returns

dP

: Non-recoverable pressure drop of the differential pressure flow
  meter, [Pa]

# Notes

See the appropriate functions for the documentation for the formulas and
references used in each method.

Wedge meters, and venturi nozzles do not have standard formulas available
for pressure drop computation.

# Examples

```pycon
>>> differential_pressure_meter_dP(D=0.07366, D2=0.05, P1=200000.0,
... P2=183000.0, meter_type='as cast convergent venturi tube')
1788.5717754177406
```

interpolating, and manipulation functions. It may be used with discrete
particle size distributions, or with statistical ones with parameters
specified.

please use the [GitHub issue tracker](https://github.com/CalebBell/fluids/)
or contact the author at <mailto:Caleb.Andrew.Bell@gmail.com>.

functions.name: fluids.particle_size_distribution.psd_spacing
documentation:
Create a particle spacing mesh in one of several ways for use in
modeling discrete particle size distributions. The allowable meshes are
'linear', 'logarithmic', a geometric series specified by a Renard number
such as 'R10', or the meshes available in one of several sieve standards.

# Parameters

d_min

: The minimum diameter at which the mesh starts, [m]

d_max

: The maximum diameter at which the mesh ends, [m]

pts

: The number of points to return for the mesh (note this is not respected
  by sieve meshes), [-]

method

: Either 'linear', 'logarithmic', a Renard number like 'R10' or 'R5' or
  'R2.5', or one of the sieve standards 'ISO 3310-1 R40/3',
  'ISO 3310-1 R20', 'ISO 3310-1 R20/3', 'ISO 3310-1', 'ISO 3310-1 R10',
  'ASTM E11', [-]

# Returns

ds

: The generated mesh diameters, [m]

# Notes

Note that when specifying a Renard series, only one of `d_min` or `d_max` can
be respected! Provide only one of those numbers.

Note that when specifying a sieve standard the number of points is not
respected!

# Examples

```pycon
>>> psd_spacing(d_min=5e-5, d_max=5e-4, method='ISO 3310-1 R20/3')
[6.3e-05, 9e-05, 0.000125, 0.00018, 0.00025, 0.000355, 0.0005]
```

# References

[^footnote-1]: ASTM E11 - 17 - Standard Specification for Woven Wire Test Sieve
    Cloth and Test Sieves.

[^footnote-2]: ISO 3310-1:2016 - Test Sieves -- Technical Requirements and Testing
    -- Part 1: Test Sieves of Metal Wire Cloth.

functions.name: fluids.particle_size_distribution.pdf_lognormal
documentation:
Calculates the probability density function of a lognormal particle
distribution given a particle diameter `d`, characteristic particle
diameter `d_characteristic`, and distribution standard deviation `s`.

$$
q(d) = \frac{1}{ds\sqrt{2\pi}} \exp\left[-0.5\left(\frac{ \ln(d/d_{characteristic})}{s}\right)^2\right]
$$

# Parameters

d

: Specified particle diameter, [m]

d_characteristic

: Characteristic particle diameter; often D[3, 3] is used for this
  purpose but not always, [m]

s

: Distribution standard deviation, [-]

# Returns

pdf

: Lognormal probability density function, [-]

# Notes

The characteristic diameter can be in terns of number density (denoted
$q_0(d)$), length density ($q_1(d)$), surface area density
($q_2(d)$), or volume density ($q_3(d)$). Volume density is
most often used. Interconversions among the distributions is possible but
tricky.

The standard distribution (i.e. the one used in Scipy) can perform the same
computation with `d_characteristic` as the value of `scale`.

```pycon
>>> import scipy.stats
>>> float(scipy.stats.lognorm.pdf(x=1E-4, s=1.1, scale=1E-5))
405.5420921
```

Scipy's calculation is over 300 times slower however, and this expression
is numerically integrated so speed is required.

# Examples

```pycon
>>> pdf_lognormal(1E-4, 1E-5, 1.1)
405.5420921
```

# References

[^footnote-1]: ISO 9276-2:2014 - Representation of Results of Particle Size
    Analysis - Part 2: Calculation of Average Particle Sizes/Diameters and
    Moments from Particle Size Distributions.

functions.name: fluids.particle_size_distribution.cdf_lognormal
documentation:
Calculates the cumulative distribution function of a lognormal particle
distribution given a particle diameter `d`, characteristic particle
diameter `d_characteristic`, and distribution standard deviation `s`.

$$
Q(d) = 0.5\left(1 + \text{err}\left[\left(\frac{\ln(d/d_c)}{s\sqrt{2}} \right)\right]\right)
$$

# Parameters

d

: Specified particle diameter, [m]

d_characteristic

: Characteristic particle diameter; often D[3, 3] is used for this
  purpose but not always, [m]

s

: Distribution standard deviation, [-]

# Returns

cdf

: Lognormal cumulative density function, [-]

# Notes

The characteristic diameter can be in terns of number density (denoted
$q_0(d)$), length density ($q_1(d)$), surface area density
($q_2(d)$), or volume density ($q_3(d)$). Volume density is
most often used. Interconversions among the distributions is possible but
tricky.

The standard distribution (i.e. the one used in Scipy) can perform the same
computation with `d_characteristic` as the value of `scale`.

```pycon
>>> import scipy.stats
>>> float(scipy.stats.lognorm.cdf(x=1E-4, s=1.1, scale=1E-5))
0.9818369875798177
```

Scipy's calculation is over 100 times slower however.

# Examples

```pycon
>>> cdf_lognormal(d=1E-4, d_characteristic=1E-5, s=1.1)
0.9818369875798
```

# References

[^footnote-1]: ISO 9276-2:2014 - Representation of Results of Particle Size
    Analysis - Part 2: Calculation of Average Particle Sizes/Diameters and
    Moments from Particle Size Distributions.

functions.name: fluids.particle_size_distribution.pdf_lognormal_basis_integral
documentation:
Calculates the integral of the multiplication of d^n by the lognormal
pdf, given a particle diameter `d`, characteristic particle
diameter `d_characteristic`, distribution standard deviation `s`, and
exponent `n`.

$$
\int d^n\cdot q(d)\; dd = -\frac{1}{2} \exp\left(\frac{s^2 n^2}{2} \right)d^n \left(\frac{d}{d_{characteristic}}\right)^{-n} \text{erf}\left[\frac{s^2 n - \ln(d/d_{characteristic})} {\sqrt{2} s} \right]
$$

This is the crucial integral required for interconversion between different
bases such as number density (denoted $q_0(d)$), length density
($q_1(d)$), surface area density ($q_2(d)$), or volume density
($q_3(d)$).

# Parameters

d

: Specified particle diameter, [m]

d_characteristic

: Characteristic particle diameter; often D[3, 3] is used for this
  purpose but not always, [m]

s

: Distribution standard deviation, [-]

n

: Exponent of the multiplied n

# Returns

pdf_basis_integral

: Integral of lognormal pdf multiplied by d^n, [-]

# Notes

This integral has been verified numerically. This integral is itself
integrated, so it is crucial to obtain an analytical form for at least
this integral.

Note overflow or zero division issues may occur for very large values of
`s`, larger than 10. No mathematical limit was able to be obtained with
a CAS.

# Examples

```pycon
>>> pdf_lognormal_basis_integral(d=1E-4, d_characteristic=1E-5, s=1.1, n=-2)
56228306549.26362
```

functions.name: fluids.particle_size_distribution.pdf_Gates_Gaudin_Schuhman
documentation:
Calculates the probability density of a particle
distribution following the Gates, Gaudin and Schuhman (GGS) model given a
particle diameter `d`, characteristic (maximum) particle
diameter `d_characteristic`, and exponent `m`.

$$
q(d) = \frac{n}{d}\left(\frac{d}{d_{characteristic}}\right)^m \text{ if } d < d_{characteristic} \text{ else } 0
$$

# Parameters

d

: Specified particle diameter, [m]

d_characteristic

: Characteristic particle diameter; in this model, it is the largest
  particle size diameter in the distribution, [m]

m

: Particle size distribution exponent, [-]

# Returns

pdf

: GGS probability density function, [-]

# Notes

The characteristic diameter can be in terns of number density (denoted
$q_0(d)$), length density ($q_1(d)$), surface area density
($q_2(d)$), or volume density ($q_3(d)$). Volume density is
most often used. Interconversions among the distributions is possible but
tricky.

# Examples

```pycon
>>> pdf_Gates_Gaudin_Schuhman(d=2E-4, d_characteristic=1E-3, m=2.3)
283.8355768512045
```

# References

[^footnote-1]: Schuhmann, R., 1940. Principles of Comminution, I-Size Distribution
    and Surface Calculations. American Institute of Mining, Metallurgical
    and Petroleum Engineers Technical Publication 1189. Mining Technology,
    volume 4, p. 1-11.

[^footnote-2]: Bayat, Hossein, Mostafa Rastgo, Moharram Mansouri Zadeh, and Harry
    Vereecken. "Particle Size Distribution Models, Their Characteristics and
    Fitting Capability." Journal of Hydrology 529 (October 1, 2015): 872-89.

functions.name: fluids.particle_size_distribution.cdf_Gates_Gaudin_Schuhman
documentation:
Calculates the cumulative distribution function of a particle
distribution following the Gates, Gaudin and Schuhman (GGS) model given a
particle diameter `d`, characteristic (maximum) particle
diameter `d_characteristic`, and exponent `m`.

$$
Q(d) = \left(\frac{d}{d_{characteristic}}\right)^m \text{ if } d < d_{characteristic} \text{ else } 1
$$

# Parameters

d

: Specified particle diameter, [m]

d_characteristic

: Characteristic particle diameter; in this model, it is the largest
  particle size diameter in the distribution, [m]

m

: Particle size distribution exponent, [-]

# Returns

cdf

: GGS cumulative density function, [-]

# Notes

The characteristic diameter can be in terns of number density (denoted
$q_0(d)$), length density ($q_1(d)$), surface area density
($q_2(d)$), or volume density ($q_3(d)$). Volume density is
most often used. Interconversions among the distributions is possible but
tricky.

# Examples

```pycon
>>> cdf_Gates_Gaudin_Schuhman(d=2E-4, d_characteristic=1E-3, m=2.3)
0.024681354508800397
```

# References

[^footnote-1]: Schuhmann, R., 1940. Principles of Comminution, I-Size Distribution
    and Surface Calculations. American Institute of Mining, Metallurgical
    and Petroleum Engineers Technical Publication 1189. Mining Technology,
    volume 4, p. 1-11.

[^footnote-2]: Bayat, Hossein, Mostafa Rastgo, Moharram Mansouri Zadeh, and Harry
    Vereecken. "Particle Size Distribution Models, Their Characteristics and
    Fitting Capability." Journal of Hydrology 529 (October 1, 2015): 872-89.

functions.name: fluids.particle_size_distribution.pdf_Gates_Gaudin_Schuhman_basis_integral
documentation:
Calculates the integral of the multiplication of d^n by the Gates,
Gaudin and Schuhman (GGS) model given a particle diameter `d`,
characteristic (maximum) particle diameter `d_characteristic`, and exponent
`m`.

$$
\int d^n\cdot q(d)\; dd =\frac{m}{m+n} d^n \left(\frac{d} {d_{characteristic}}\right)^m
$$

# Parameters

d

: Specified particle diameter, [m]

d_characteristic

: Characteristic particle diameter; in this model, it is the largest
  particle size diameter in the distribution, [m]

m

: Particle size distribution exponent, [-]

n

: Exponent of the multiplied n, [-]

# Returns

pdf_basis_integral

: Integral of Rosin Rammler pdf multiplied by d^n, [-]

# Notes

This integral does not have any numerical issues as `d` approaches 0.

# Examples

```pycon
>>> pdf_Gates_Gaudin_Schuhman_basis_integral(d=2E-4, d_characteristic=1E-3, m=2.3, n=-3)
-10136984887.543015
```

functions.name: fluids.particle_size_distribution.pdf_Rosin_Rammler
documentation:
Calculates the probability density of a particle
distribution following the Rosin-Rammler (RR) model given a
particle diameter `d`, and the two parameters `k` and `m`.

$$
q(d) = k m d^{(m-1)} \exp(- k d^{m})
$$

# Parameters

d

: Specified particle diameter, [m]

k

: Parameter in the model, [(1/m)^m]

m

: Parameter in the model, [-]

# Returns

pdf

: RR probability density function, [-]

# Notes

# Examples

```pycon
>>> pdf_Rosin_Rammler(1E-3, 200, 2)
0.3999200079994667
```

# References

[^footnote-1]: Rosin, P. "The Laws Governing the Fineness of Powdered Coal." J.
    Inst. Fuel. 7 (1933): 29-36.

[^footnote-2]: Bayat, Hossein, Mostafa Rastgo, Moharram Mansouri Zadeh, and Harry
    Vereecken. "Particle Size Distribution Models, Their Characteristics and
    Fitting Capability." Journal of Hydrology 529 (October 1, 2015): 872-89.

functions.name: fluids.particle_size_distribution.cdf_Rosin_Rammler
documentation:
Calculates the cumulative distribution function of a particle
distribution following the Rosin-Rammler (RR) model given a
particle diameter `d`, and the two parameters `k` and `m`.

$$
Q(d) = 1 - \exp\left(-k d^m\right)
$$

# Parameters

d

: Specified particle diameter, [m]

k

: Parameter in the model, [(1/m)^m]

m

: Parameter in the model, [-]

# Returns

cdf

: RR cumulative density function, [-]

# Notes

The characteristic diameter can be in terns of number density (denoted
$q_0(d)$), length density ($q_1(d)$), surface area density
($q_2(d)$), or volume density ($q_3(d)$). Volume density is
most often used. Interconversions among the distributions is possible but
tricky.

# Examples

```pycon
>>> cdf_Rosin_Rammler(5E-2, 200, 2)
0.3934693402873667
```

# References

[^footnote-1]: Rosin, P. "The Laws Governing the Fineness of Powdered Coal." J.
    Inst. Fuel. 7 (1933): 29-36.

[^footnote-2]: Bayat, Hossein, Mostafa Rastgo, Moharram Mansouri Zadeh, and Harry
    Vereecken. "Particle Size Distribution Models, Their Characteristics and
    Fitting Capability." Journal of Hydrology 529 (October 1, 2015): 872-89.

functions.name: fluids.particle_size_distribution.pdf_Rosin_Rammler_basis_integral
documentation:
Calculates the integral of the multiplication of d^n by the Rosin
Rammler (RR) pdf, given a particle diameter `d`, and the two parameters `k`
and `m`.

$$
\int d^n\cdot q(d)\; dd =-d^{m+n} k(d^mk)^{-\frac{m+n}{m}}\Gamma \left(\frac{m+n}{m}\right)\text{gammaincc}\left[\left(\frac{m+n}{m} \right), kd^m\right]
$$

# Parameters

d

: Specified particle diameter, [m]

k

: Parameter in the model, [(1/m)^m]

m

: Parameter in the model, [-]

n

: Exponent of the multiplied n, [-]

# Returns

pdf_basis_integral

: Integral of Rosin Rammler pdf multiplied by d^n, [-]

# Notes

This integral was derived using a CAS, and verified numerically.
The `gammaincc` function is that from scipy.special, and `gamma` from the
same.

For very high powers of `n` or `m` when the diameter is very low,
exceptions may occur.

# Examples

```pycon
>>> "{:g}".format(pdf_Rosin_Rammler_basis_integral(5E-2, 200, 2, 3))
'-0.000452399'
```
