functions.name: fluids.packed_bed.Brauer
documentation:
Calculates pressure drop across a packed bed of spheres using a
correlation developed in [^footnote-1], as shown in [^footnote-2]. Seventh most accurate
correlation overall in the review of [^footnote-2]. Also shown in [^footnote-3].

$$
f_p = \left(160 + 3\left(\frac{Re}{1-\epsilon}\right)^{0.9}\right) \frac{(1-\epsilon)^2}{\epsilon^3 Re}
$$

$$
f_p = \frac{\Delta P d_p}{\rho v_s^2 L}
$$

$$
Re = \frac{\rho v_s  d_p}{\mu}
$$

# Parameters

dp

: Particle diameter of spheres [m]

voidage

: Void fraction of bed packing [-]

vs

: Superficial velocity of the fluid (volumetric flow rate/cross-sectional
  area)[m/s]

rho

: Density of the fluid [kg/m^3]

mu

: Viscosity of the fluid, [Pa\*s]

L

: Length the fluid flows in the packed bed [m]

# Returns

dP

: Pressure drop across the bed [Pa]

# Notes

The original article [^footnote-1] has not been reviewed.
In [^footnote-2], is stated as for a range of $2 < Re_{Erg} < 20,000$.
In [^footnote-3], is stated as for a range of $0.01 < Re_{Erg} < 40,000$.

# Examples

```pycon
>>> Brauer(dp=8E-4, voidage=0.4, vs=1E-3, rho=1E3, mu=1E-3)
1441.5479196020563
```

# References

[^footnote-1]: H. Brauer, Grundlagen der Einphasen -und Mehrphasenstromungen,
    Sauerlander AG, Aarau, 1971.

[^footnote-2]: Erdim, Esra, Ömer Akgiray, and İbrahim Demir. "A Revisit of Pressure
    Drop-Flow Rate Correlations for Packed Beds of Spheres." Powder
    Technology 283 (October 2015): 488-504. doi:10.1016/j.powtec.2015.06.017.

[^footnote-3]: Allen, K. G., T. W. von Backstrom, and D. G. Kroger. "Packed Bed
    Pressure Drop Dependence on Particle Shape, Size Distribution, Packing
    Arrangement and Roughness." Powder Technology 246 (September 2013):
    590-600. doi:10.1016/j.powtec.2013.06.022.

functions.name: fluids.packed_bed.KTA
documentation:
Calculates pressure drop across a packed bed of spheres using a
correlation developed in [^footnote-1], as shown in [^footnote-2]. Third most accurate
correlation overall in the review of [^footnote-2].

$$
f_p = \left(160 + 3\left(\frac{Re}{1-\epsilon}\right)^{0.9}\right) \frac{(1-\epsilon)^2}{\epsilon^3 Re}
$$

$$
f_p = \frac{\Delta P d_p}{\rho v_s^2 L}
$$

$$
Re= \frac{\rho v_s  d_p}{\mu}
$$

# Parameters

dp

: Particle diameter of spheres [m]

voidage

: Void fraction of bed packing [-]

vs

: Superficial velocity of the fluid (volumetric flow rate/cross-sectional
  area)[m/s]

rho

: Density of the fluid [kg/m^3]

mu

: Viscosity of the fluid, [Pa\*s]

L

: Length the fluid flows in the packed bed [m]

# Returns

dP

: Pressure drop across the bed [Pa]

# Notes

Developed for gas flow through pebbles in nuclear reactors.

In [^footnote-2], stated as for a range of $1 < RE_{Erg} <100,000$.
In [^footnote-1], a limit on porosity is stated as $0.36 < \epsilon < 0.42$.

# Examples

```pycon
>>> KTA(dp=8E-4, voidage=0.4, vs=1E-3, rho=1E3, mu=1E-3)
1440.409277034248
```

# References

[^footnote-1]: KTA. KTA 3102.3 Reactor Core Design of High-Temperature Gas-Cooled
    Reactors Part 3: Loss of Pressure through Friction in Pebble Bed Cores.
    Germany, 1981.

[^footnote-2]: Erdim, Esra, Ömer Akgiray, and İbrahim Demir. "A Revisit of Pressure
    Drop-Flow Rate Correlations for Packed Beds of Spheres." Powder
    Technology 283 (October 2015): 488-504. doi:10.1016/j.powtec.2015.06.017.

functions.name: fluids.packed_bed.Erdim_Akgiray_Demir
documentation:
Calculates pressure drop across a packed bed of spheres using a
correlation developed in [^footnote-1], claiming to be the best model to date.

$$
f_v = 160 + 2.81Re_{Erg}^{0.904}
$$

$$
f_v = \frac{\Delta P d_p^2}{\mu v_s L}\frac{\epsilon^3}{(1-\epsilon)^2}
$$

$$
Re_{Erg} = \frac{\rho v_s  d_p}{\mu(1-\epsilon)}
$$

# Parameters

dp

: Particle diameter of spheres [m]

voidage

: Void fraction of bed packing [-]

vs

: Superficial velocity of the fluid (volumetric flow rate/cross-sectional
  area)[m/s]

rho

: Density of the fluid [kg/m^3]

mu

: Viscosity of the fluid, [Pa\*s]

L

: Length the fluid flows in the packed bed [m]

# Returns

dP

: Pressure drop across the bed [Pa]

# Notes

Developed with data in the range of:

$$
2 < Re_{Erg} <3582\\ 4 < d_t/d_p < 34.1\\ 0.377 < \epsilon <0.470
$$

# Examples

```pycon
>>> Erdim_Akgiray_Demir(dp=8E-4, voidage=0.4, vs=1E-3, rho=1E3, mu=1E-3)
1438.2826958844414
```

# References

[^footnote-1]: Erdim, Esra, Ömer Akgiray, and İbrahim Demir. "A Revisit of Pressure
    Drop-Flow Rate Correlations for Packed Beds of Spheres." Powder
    Technology 283 (October 2015): 488-504. doi:10.1016/j.powtec.2015.06.017.

functions.name: fluids.packed_bed.Fahien_Schriver
documentation:
Calculates pressure drop across a packed bed of spheres using a
correlation developed in [^footnote-1], as shown in [^footnote-2]. Second most accurate
correlation overall in the review of [^footnote-2].

$$
f_p = \left(q\frac{f_{1L}}{Re_{Erg}} + (1-q)\left(f_2 + \frac{f_{1T}} {Re_{Erg}}\right)\right)\frac{1-\epsilon}{\epsilon^3}
$$

$$
q = \exp\left(-\frac{\epsilon^2(1-\epsilon)}{12.6}Re_{Erg}\right)
$$

$$
f_{1L}=\frac{136}{(1-\epsilon)^{0.38}}
$$

$$
f_{1T} = \frac{29}{(1-\epsilon)^{1.45}\epsilon^2}
$$

$$
f_2 = \frac{1.87\epsilon^{0.75}}{(1-\epsilon)^{0.26}}
$$

$$
f_p = \frac{\Delta P d_p}{\rho v_s^2 L}
$$

$$
Re_{Erg} = \frac{\rho v_s  d_p}{\mu(1-\epsilon)}
$$

# Parameters

dp

: Particle diameter of spheres [m]

voidage

: Void fraction of bed packing [-]

vs

: Superficial velocity of the fluid (volumetric flow rate/cross-sectional
  area)[m/s]

rho

: Density of the fluid [kg/m^3]

mu

: Viscosity of the fluid, [Pa\*s]

L

: Length the fluid flows in the packed bed [m]

# Returns

dP

: Pressure drop across the bed [Pa]

# Notes

No range of validity available.

# Examples

```pycon
>>> Fahien_Schriver(dp=8E-4, voidage=0.4, vs=1E-3, rho=1E3, mu=1E-3)
1470.6175541844
```

# References

[^footnote-1]: R.W. Fahien, C.B. Schriver, Paper presented at the 1961 Denver
    meeting of AIChE, in: R.W. Fahien, Fundamentals of Transport Phenomena,
    McGraw-Hill, New York, 1983.

[^footnote-2]: Erdim, Esra, Ömer Akgiray, and İbrahim Demir. "A Revisit of Pressure
    Drop-Flow Rate Correlations for Packed Beds of Spheres." Powder
    Technology 283 (October 2015): 488-504. doi:10.1016/j.powtec.2015.06.017.

functions.name: fluids.packed_bed.Idelchik
documentation:
Calculates pressure drop across a packed bed of spheres as in [^footnote-2],
originally in [^footnote-1].

$$
\frac{\Delta P}{L\rho v_s^2} d_p  = \frac{0.765}{\epsilon^{4.2}} \left(\frac{30}{Re_l} + \frac{3}{Re_l^{0.7}} + 0.3\right)
$$

$$
Re_l = (0.45/\epsilon^{0.5})Re_{Erg}
$$

$$
Re_{Erg} = \frac{\rho v_s  D_p}{\mu(1-\epsilon)}
$$

# Parameters

dp

: Particle diameter of spheres [m]

voidage

: Void fraction of bed packing [-]

vs

: Superficial velocity of the fluid (volumetric flow rate/cross-sectional
  area)[m/s]

rho

: Density of the fluid [kg/m^3]

mu

: Viscosity of the fluid, [Pa\*s]

L

: Length the fluid flows in the packed bed [m]

# Returns

dP

: Pressure drop across the bed [Pa]

# Notes

$0.001 < Re_{Erg} <1000$
This equation is valid for void fractions between 0.3 and 0.8. Cited as
by Bernshtein.

# Examples

```pycon
>>> Idelchik(dp=8E-4, voidage=0.4, vs=1E-3, rho=1E3, mu=1E-3)
1571.909125999067
```

# References

[^footnote-1]: Idelchik, I. E. Flow Resistance: A Design Guide for Engineers.
    Hemisphere Publishing Corporation, New York, 1989.

[^footnote-2]: Allen, K. G., T. W. von Backstrom, and D. G. Kroger. "Packed Bed
    Pressure Drop Dependence on Particle Shape, Size Distribution, Packing
    Arrangement and Roughness." Powder Technology 246 (September 2013):
    590-600. doi:10.1016/j.powtec.2013.06.022.

functions.name: fluids.packed_bed.Harrison_Brunner_Hecker
documentation:
Calculates pressure drop across a packed bed of spheres using a
correlation developed in [^footnote-1], also shown in [^footnote-2]. Fourth most accurate
correlation overall in the review of [^footnote-2].
Applies a wall correction if diameter of tube is provided.

$$
f_p = \left(119.8A + 4.63B\left(\frac{Re}{1-\epsilon}\right)^{5/6} \right)\frac{(1-\epsilon)^2}{\epsilon^3 Re}
$$

$$
A = \left(1 + \pi \frac{d_p}{6(1-\epsilon)D_t}\right)^2
$$

$$
B = 1 - \frac{\pi^2 d_p}{24D_t}\left(1 - \frac{0.5d_p}{D_t}\right)
$$

$$
f_p = \frac{\Delta P d_p}{\rho v_s^2 L}
$$

$$
Re = \frac{\rho v_s  d_p}{\mu}
$$

# Parameters

dp

: Particle diameter of spheres [m]

voidage

: Void fraction of bed packing [-]

vs

: Superficial velocity of the fluid (volumetric flow rate/cross-sectional
  area)[m/s]

rho

: Density of the fluid [kg/m^3]

mu

: Viscosity of the fluid, [Pa\*s]

L

: Length the fluid flows in the packed bed [m]

Dt

: Diameter of the tube, [m]

# Returns

dP

: Pressure drop across the bed [Pa]

# Notes

Uses data from other sources only. Correlation will underestimate pressure
drop if tube diameter is not provided. Limits are specified in [^footnote-1] as:

$$
0.72 < Re < 7700 \\ 8.3 < d_t/d_p < 50 \\ 0.33 < \epsilon < 0.88
$$

# Examples

```pycon
>>> Harrison_Brunner_Hecker(dp=8E-4, voidage=0.4, vs=1E-3, rho=1E3, mu=1E-3, Dt=1E-2)
1255.1625662
```

# References

[^footnote-1]: KTA. KTA 3102.3 Reactor Core Design of High-Temperature Gas-Cooled
    Reactors Part 3: Loss of Pressure through Friction in Pebble Bed Cores.
    Germany, 1981.

[^footnote-2]: Erdim, Esra, Ömer Akgiray, and İbrahim Demir. "A Revisit of Pressure
    Drop-Flow Rate Correlations for Packed Beds of Spheres." Powder
    Technology 283 (October 2015): 488-504. doi:10.1016/j.powtec.2015.06.017.

functions.name: fluids.packed_bed.Montillet_Akkari_Comiti
documentation:
Calculates pressure drop across a packed bed of spheres as in [^footnote-2],
originally in [^footnote-1]. Wall effect adjustment is used if `Dt` is provided.

$$
\frac{\Delta P}{L\rho V_s^2} D_p \frac{\epsilon^3}{(1-\epsilon)} = a\left(\frac{D_c}{D_p}\right)^{0.20} \left(\frac{1000}{Re_{p}} + \frac{60}{Re_{p}^{0.5}} + 12 \right)
$$

$$
Re_{p} = \frac{\rho v_s  D_p}{\mu}
$$

# Parameters

dp

: Particle diameter of spheres [m]

voidage

: Void fraction of bed packing [-]

vs

: Superficial velocity of the fluid (volumetric flow rate/cross-sectional
  area)[m/s]

rho

: Density of the fluid [kg/m^3]

mu

: Viscosity of the fluid, [Pa\*s]

L

: Length the fluid flows in the packed bed [m]

Dt

: Diameter of the tube, [m]

# Returns

dP

: Pressure drop across bed [Pa]

# Notes

$10 < REp <2500$
if Dc/D > 50, set to 2.2.
a = 0.061 for epsilon < 0.4, 0.050 for > 0.4.

# Examples

Custom example:

```pycon
>>> Montillet_Akkari_Comiti(dp=0.0008, voidage=0.4, L=0.5, vs=0.00132629120, rho=1000., mu=1.00E-003)
1148.19052440
```

# References

[^footnote-1]: Montillet, A., E. Akkari, and J. Comiti. "About a Correlating
    Equation for Predicting Pressure Drops through Packed Beds of Spheres
    in a Large Range of Reynolds Numbers." Chemical Engineering and
    Processing: Process Intensification 46, no. 4 (April 2007): 329-33.
    doi:10.1016/j.cep.2006.07.002.

[^footnote-2]: Allen, K. G., T. W. von Backstrom, and D. G. Kroger. "Packed Bed
    Pressure Drop Dependence on Particle Shape, Size Distribution, Packing
    Arrangement and Roughness." Powder Technology 246 (September 2013):
    590-600. doi:10.1016/j.powtec.2013.06.022.

functions.name: fluids.packed_bed.Guo_Sun
documentation:
Calculates pressure drop across a packed bed of spheres using a
correlation developed in [^footnote-1]. This is valid for highly-packed particles
at particle/tube diameter ratios between 2 and 3, where a ring packing
structure occurs. If a packing ratio is so low, it is important to use this
model because in some cases its predictions are as low as half those of
other models!

$$
f_v = 180 + \left(9.5374\frac{d_p}{D_t} - 2.8054\right)Re_{Erg}^{0.97}
$$

$$
f_v = \frac{\Delta P d_p^2}{\mu v_s L}\frac{\epsilon^3}{(1-\epsilon)^2}
$$

$$
Re_{Erg} = \frac{\rho v_s  d_p}{\mu(1-\epsilon)}
$$

# Parameters

dp

: Particle diameter of spheres [m]

voidage

: Void fraction of bed packing [-]

vs

: Superficial velocity of the fluid (volumetric flow rate/cross-sectional
  area)[m/s]

rho

: Density of the fluid [kg/m^3]

mu

: Viscosity of the fluid, [Pa\*s]

Dt

: Diameter of the tube, [m]

L

: Length the fluid flows in the packed bed [m]

# Returns

dP

: Pressure drop across the bed [Pa]

# Notes

Developed with data in the range of:

$$
100 < Re_{m} <33000\\ 2 < d_t/d_p < 3 1\\ 0.476 < \epsilon <0.492
$$

# Examples

```pycon
>>> Guo_Sun(dp=14.2E-3, voidage=0.492, vs=0.6, rho=1E3, mu=1E-3, Dt=40.9E-3)
42019.529911
```

# References

[^footnote-1]: Guo, Zehua, Zhongning Sun, Nan Zhang, Ming Ding, and Jiaqing Liu.
    "Pressure Drop in Slender Packed Beds with Novel Packing Arrangement."
    Powder Technology 321 (November 2017): 286-92.
    doi:10.1016/j.powtec.2017.08.024.

functions.name: fluids.packed_bed.dP_packed_bed_methods
documentation:
This function handles determining which pressure drop in a packed bed
correlation are suitable for the provided inputs.

Preferred correlations are 'Erdim, Akgiray & Demir' when tube
diameter is not provided, and 'Harrison, Brunner & Hecker' when tube
diameter is provided.

# Examples

```pycon
>>> dP_packed_bed_methods(dp=8E-4, voidage=0.4, vs=1E-3, rho=1E3, mu=1E-3, L=1.0)[0]
'Erdim, Akgiray & Demir'
>>> dP_packed_bed_methods(dp=8E-4, voidage=0.4, vs=1E-3, rho=1E3, mu=1E-3, L=1, Dt=1e-2)[0]
'Harrison, Brunner & Hecker'
```

# Parameters

dp

: Particle diameter of spheres [m]

voidage

: Void fraction of bed packing [-]

vs

: Superficial velocity of the fluid (volumetric flow rate/cross-sectional
  area) [m/s]

rho

: Density of the fluid [kg/m^3]

mu

: Viscosity of the fluid, [Pa\*s]

L

: Length the fluid flows in the packed bed [m]

Dt

: Diameter of the tube, [m]

check_ranges

: Added for Future use only

# Returns

methods

: List of methods which can be used to calculate `dP` with the given inputs

functions.name: fluids.packed_bed.dP_packed_bed
documentation:
This function handles choosing which pressure drop in a packed bed
correlation is used. Automatically select which correlation
to use if none is provided. Returns None if insufficient information is
provided.

Preferred correlations are 'Erdim, Akgiray & Demir' when tube
diameter is not provided, and 'Harrison, Brunner & Hecker' when tube
diameter is provided. If you are using a particles in a narrow tube
between 2 and 3 particle diameters, expect higher than normal voidages
(0.4-0.5) and used the method 'Guo, Sun, Zhang, Ding & Liu'.

# Examples

```pycon
>>> dP_packed_bed(dp=8E-4, voidage=0.4, vs=1E-3, rho=1E3, mu=1E-3)
1438.2826958844
>>> dP_packed_bed(dp=8E-4, voidage=0.4, vs=1E-3, rho=1E3, mu=1E-3, Dt=0.01)
1255.1625662548
>>> dP_packed_bed(dp=0.05, voidage=0.492, vs=0.1, rho=1E3, mu=1E-3, Dt=0.015, Method='Guo, Sun, Zhang, Ding & Liu')
18782.499710673
```

# Parameters

dp

: Particle diameter of spheres [m]

voidage

: Void fraction of bed packing [-]

vs

: Superficial velocity of the fluid (volumetric flow rate/cross-sectional
  area) [m/s]

rho

: Density of the fluid [kg/m^3]

mu

: Viscosity of the fluid, [Pa\*s]

L

: Length the fluid flows in the packed bed [m]

Dt

: Diameter of the tube, [m]

sphericity

: Sphericity of the particles [-]

# Returns

dP

: Pressure drop across the bed [Pa]

# Other Parameters

Method

: A string of the function name to use, as in the dictionary
  packed_beds_correlations

functions.name: fluids.packed_bed.voidage_Benyahia_Oneil
documentation:
Calculates voidage of a bed of arbitrarily shaped uniform particles
packed into a bed or tube of diameter `Dt`, with equivalent sphere diameter
`Dp`. Shown in [^footnote-1], and cited by various authors. Correlations exist
also for spheres, solid cylinders, hollow cylinders, and 4-hole cylinders.
Based on a series of physical measurements.

$$
\epsilon = 0.1504 + \frac{0.2024}{\phi} + \frac{1.0814} {\left(\frac{d_{t}}{d_{pe}}+0.1226\right)^2}
$$

# Parameters

Dpe

: Equivalent spherical particle diameter (diameter of a sphere with the
  same volume), [m]

Dt

: Diameter of the tube, [m]

sphericity

: Sphericity of particles in bed []

# Returns

voidage

: Void fraction of bed packing []

# Notes

Average error of 5.2%; valid 1.5 < dtube/dp < 50 and 0.42 < sphericity < 1

# Examples

```pycon
>>> voidage_Benyahia_Oneil(Dpe=1E-3, Dt=1E-2, sphericity=.8)
0.41395363849210065
```

# References

[^footnote-1]: Benyahia, F., and K. E. O\`Neill. "Enhanced Voidage Correlations for
    Packed Beds of Various Particle Shapes and Sizes." Particulate Science
    and Technology 23, no. 2 (April 1, 2005): 169-77.
    doi:10.1080/02726350590922242.
import matplotlib.pyplot as plt
import numpy as np
voidage = 0.4
mu = 1E-3
methods = dP_packed_bed(dp, voidage, vs, rho, mu, L=1, AvailableMethods=True)
dps = np.logspace(-4, -1, 100)
for method in methods:
dPs = [dP_packed_bed(dp, voidage, vs, rho, mu, Method=method) for dp in dps]
plt.semilogx(dps, dPs, label=method)
plt.legend()
plt.show()
## Voidage correlations

functions.name: fluids.packed_bed.voidage_Benyahia_Oneil_spherical
documentation:
Calculates voidage of a bed of spheres
packed into a bed or tube of diameter `Dt`, with sphere diameters
`Dp`. Shown in [^footnote-1], and cited by various authors. Correlations exist
also for solid cylinders, hollow cylinders, and 4-hole cylinders.
Based on a series of physical measurements.

$$
\epsilon = 0.390+\frac{1.740}{\left(\frac{d_{cyl}}{d_p}+1.140\right)^2}
$$

# Parameters

Dp

: Spherical particle diameter, [m]

Dt

: Diameter of the tube, [m]

# Returns

voidage

: Void fraction of bed packing []

# Notes

Average error 1.5%, 1.5 < ratio < 50.

# Examples

```pycon
>>> voidage_Benyahia_Oneil_spherical(Dp=.001, Dt=.05)
0.3906653157443224
```

# References

[^footnote-1]: Benyahia, F., and K. E. O\`Neill. "Enhanced Voidage Correlations for
    Packed Beds of Various Particle Shapes and Sizes." Particulate Science
    and Technology 23, no. 2 (April 1, 2005): 169-77.
    doi:10.1080/02726350590922242.

functions.name: fluids.packed_bed.voidage_Benyahia_Oneil_cylindrical
documentation:
Calculates voidage of a bed of cylindrical uniform particles
packed into a bed or tube of diameter `Dt`, with equivalent sphere diameter
`Dpe`. Shown in [^footnote-1], and cited by various authors. Correlations exist
also for spheres, solid cylinders, hollow cylinders, and 4-hole cylinders.
Based on a series of physical measurements.

$$
\epsilon = 0.373+\frac{1.703}{\left(\frac{d_{cyl}}{d_p}+0.611\right)^2}
$$

# Parameters

Dpe

: Equivalent spherical particle diameter (diameter of a sphere with the
  same volume), [m]

Dt

: Diameter of the tube, [m]

sphericity

: Sphericity of particles in bed []

# Returns

voidage

: Void fraction of bed packing []

# Notes

Average error 1.6%; 1.7 < ratio < 26.3.

# Examples

```pycon
>>> voidage_Benyahia_Oneil_cylindrical(Dpe=.01, Dt=.1, sphericity=.6)
0.38812523109607894
```

# References

[^footnote-1]: Benyahia, F., and K. E. O\`Neill. "Enhanced Voidage Correlations for
    Packed Beds of Various Particle Shapes and Sizes." Particulate Science
    and Technology 23, no. 2 (April 1, 2005): 169-77.
    doi:10.1080/02726350590922242.

This module contains correlations for the loss coefficient of various types
of filters in a pipe or channel.

For reporting bugs, adding feature requests, or submitting pull requests,
please use the [GitHub issue tracker](https://github.com/CalebBell/fluids/)
or contact the author at <mailto:Caleb.Andrew.Bell@gmail.com>.

```{contents}
:local: true
```

# Loss Coefficients for Screens

```
.. autofunction:: round_edge_screen
```

```
.. autofunction:: round_edge_open_mesh
```

```
.. autofunction:: square_edge_screen
```

# Loss Coefficients for Grills

```
.. autofunction:: square_edge_grill
```

```
.. autofunction:: round_edge_grill
```

functions.name: fluids.filters.round_edge_screen
documentation:
Returns the loss coefficient for a round edged wire screen or bar
screen, as shown in [^footnote-1]. Angle of inclination may be specified as well.

# Parameters

alpha

: Fraction of screen open to flow [-]

Re

: Reynolds number of flow through screen with D = space between rods, []

angle

: Angle of inclination, with 0 being straight and 90 being parallel to
  flow [degrees]

# Returns

K

: Loss coefficient [-]

# Notes

Linear interpolation between a table of values. Re table extends
from 20 to 400, with constant values outside of the table. This behavior
should be adequate.
alpha should be between 0.05 and 0.8.
If angle is over 85 degrees, the value at 85 degrees is used.

The velocity the loss coefficient relates to is the approach velocity
before the screen.

# Examples

```pycon
>>> round_edge_screen(0.5, 100)
2.0999999999999996
>>> round_edge_screen(0.5, 100, 45)
1.05
```

# References

[^footnote-1]: Blevins, Robert D. Applied Fluid Dynamics Handbook. New York, N.Y.:
    Van Nostrand Reinhold Co., 1984.

functions.name: fluids.filters.round_edge_open_mesh
documentation:
Returns the loss coefficient for a round edged open net/screen
made of one of the following patterns, according to [^footnote-1]:

'round bar screen':

$$
K = 0.95(1-\alpha) + 0.2(1-\alpha)^2
$$

'diamond pattern wire':

$$
K = 0.67(1-\alpha) + 1.3(1-\alpha)^2
$$

'knotted net':

$$
K = 0.70(1-\alpha) + 4.9(1-\alpha)^2
$$

'knotless net':

$$
K = 0.72(1-\alpha) + 2.1(1-\alpha)^2
$$

# Parameters

alpha

: Fraction of net/screen open to flow [-]

subtype

: One of 'round bar screen', 'diamond pattern wire', 'knotted net' or
  'knotless net'.

angle

: Angle of inclination, with 0 being straight and 90 being parallel to
  flow [degrees]

# Returns

K

: Loss coefficient [-]

# Notes

`alpha` should be between 0.85 and 1 for these correlations.
Flow should be turbulent, with Re > 500.

The velocity the loss coefficient relates to is the approach velocity
before the mesh.

# Examples

```pycon
>>> round_edge_open_mesh(0.96, angle=33.)
0.02031327712601458
```

# References

[^footnote-1]: Blevins, Robert D. Applied Fluid Dynamics Handbook. New York, N.Y.:
    Van Nostrand Reinhold Co., 1984.

functions.name: fluids.filters.square_edge_screen
documentation:
Returns the loss coefficient for a square wire screen or square bar
screen or perforated plate with squared edges, as shown in [^footnote-1].

# Parameters

alpha

: Fraction of screen open to flow [-]

# Returns

K

: Loss coefficient [-]

# Notes

Linear interpolation between a table of values.
The velocity the loss coefficient relates to is the approach velocity
before the screen.

# Examples

```pycon
>>> square_edge_screen(0.99)
0.008000000000000007
```

# References

[^footnote-1]: Blevins, Robert D. Applied Fluid Dynamics Handbook. New York, N.Y.:
    Van Nostrand Reinhold Co., 1984.

functions.name: fluids.filters.square_edge_grill
documentation:
Returns the loss coefficient for a square grill or square bar
screen or perforated plate with squared edges of thickness l, as shown in
[^footnote-1].

for Dh < l < 50D

$$
K = \frac{0.5(1-\alpha) + (1-\alpha^2)}{\alpha^2}
$$

else:

$$
K = \frac{0.5(1-\alpha) + (1-\alpha^2) + f{l}/D}{\alpha^2}
$$

# Parameters

alpha

: Fraction of grill open to flow [-]

l

: Thickness of the grill or plate [m]

Dh

: Hydraulic diameter of gap in grill, [m]

fd

: Darcy friction factor [-]

# Returns

K

: Loss coefficient [-]

# Notes

If l, Dh, or fd is not provided, the first expression is used instead.
The alteration of the expression to include friction factor is there
if the grill is long enough to have considerable friction along the
surface of the grill.

The velocity the loss coefficient relates to is the approach velocity
before the grill.

# Examples

```pycon
>>> square_edge_grill(.45)
5.296296296296296
>>> square_edge_grill(.45, l=.15, Dh=.002, fd=.0185)
12.148148148148147
```

# References

[^footnote-1]: Blevins, Robert D. Applied Fluid Dynamics Handbook. New York, N.Y.:
    Van Nostrand Reinhold Co., 1984.

functions.name: fluids.filters.round_edge_grill
documentation:
Returns the loss coefficient for a rounded square grill or square bar
screen or perforated plate with rounded edges of thickness l, as shown in
[^footnote-1].

for Dh < l < 50D

$$
K = lookup(alpha)
$$

else:

$$
K = lookup(alpha) + \frac{fl}{\alpha^2D}
$$

# Parameters

alpha

: Fraction of grill open to flow [-]

l

: Thickness of the grill or plate [m]

Dh

: Hydraulic diameter of gap in grill, [m]

fd

: Darcy friction factor [-]

# Returns

K

: Loss coefficient [-]

# Notes

If l, Dh, or fd is not provided, the first expression is used instead.
The alteration of the expression to include friction factor is there
if the grill is long enough to have considerable friction along the
surface of the grill.
alpha must be between 0.3 and 0.7.

The velocity the loss coefficient relates to is the approach velocity
before the grill.

# Examples

```pycon
>>> round_edge_grill(.4)
1.0
>>> round_edge_grill(.4, l=.15, Dh=.002, fd=.0185)
2.3874999999999997
```

# References

[^footnote-1]: Blevins, Robert D. Applied Fluid Dynamics Handbook. New York, N.Y.:
    Van Nostrand Reinhold Co., 1984.

modules.name: fluids.optional.spa

functions.name: fluids.optional.spa.julian_day_dt
documentation:
This is the original way to calculate the julian day from the NREL paper.

However, it is much faster to convert to unix/epoch time and then convert to
julian day. Note that the date must be UTC.

functions.name: fluids.optional.spa.mean_anomaly_sun
documentation: + 445267.111480 * julian_ephemeris_century
- 0.0019142 * julian_ephemeris_century**2
+ julian_ephemeris_century**3 / 189474.0)
return x0

functions.name: fluids.optional.spa.local_hour_angle
documentation: Measured westward from south.

functions.name: fluids.optional.spa.equation_of_time
documentation: @jcompile('float64(float64, float64, float64, float64)', nopython=True)

functions.name: fluids.optional.spa.earthsun_distance
documentation:
Calculates the distance from the earth to the sun using the NREL SPA
algorithm described in [1].

Parameters
----------
unixtime : numpy array
    Array of unix/epoch timestamps to calculate solar position for.
    Unixtime is the number of seconds since Jan. 1, 1970 00:00:00 UTC.
    A pandas.DatetimeIndex is easily converted using .astype(np.int64)/10**9
delta_t : float
    Difference between terrestrial time and UT. USNO has tables.

Returns
-------
R : array
    Earth-Sun distance in AU.

References
----------
[1] Reda, I., Andreas, A., 2003. Solar position algorithm for solar
radiation applications. Technical report: NREL/TP-560- 34302. Golden,
USA, http://www.nrel.gov.

functions.name: fluids.optional.spa.solar_position
documentation:
Calculate the solar position using the NREL SPA algorithm described in
[1].

If numba is installed, the functions can be compiled
and the code runs quickly. If not, the functions
still evaluate but use numpy instead.

# Parameters

unixtime

: Array of unix/epoch timestamps to calculate solar position for.
  Unixtime is the number of seconds since Jan. 1, 1970 00:00:00 UTC.
  A pandas.DatetimeIndex is easily converted using .astype(np.int64)/10\*\*9

lat

: Latitude to calculate solar position for

lon

: Longitude to calculate solar position for

elev

: Elevation of location in meters

pressure

: avg. yearly pressure at location in millibars;
  used for atmospheric correction

temp

: avg. yearly temperature at location in
  degrees C; used for atmospheric correction

delta_t

: If delta_t is None, uses spa.calculate_deltat
  using time.year and time.month from pandas.DatetimeIndex.
  For most simulations specifying delta_t is sufficient.
  Difference between terrestrial time and UT1.
  Note: delta_t = None will break code using nrel_numba,
  this will be fixed in a future version.
  By default, use USNO historical data and predictions

atmos_refrac

: The approximate atmospheric refraction (in degrees)
  at sunrise and sunset.

numthreads: int, optional, default None

: Number of threads to use for computation if numba>=0.17
  is installed.

sst

: If True, return only data needed for sunrise, sunset, and transit
  calculations.

# Returns

list with elements:

: apparent zenith,
  zenith,
  elevation,
  apparent_elevation,
  azimuth,
  equation_of_time

# References

[^footnote-1]:
    1. Reda and A. Andreas, Solar position algorithm for solar radiation

applications. Solar Energy, vol. 76, no. 5, pp. 577-589, 2004.
.. [2] I. Reda and A. Andreas, Corrigendum to Solar position algorithm for
solar radiation applications. Solar Energy, vol. 81, no. 6, p. 838, 2007.

functions.name: fluids.optional.spa.solar_position_loop
documentation: Loop through the time array and calculate the solar position.
This is 3x slower without nogil

functions.name: fluids.optional.spa.solar_position_numba
documentation:
Calculate the solar position using the numba compiled functions
and multiple threads.

Very slow if functions are not numba compiled.

functions.name: fluids.optional.spa.transit_sunrise_sunset
documentation:
Calculate the sun transit, sunrise, and sunset for a set of dates at a
given location.

# Parameters

dates

: Numpy array of ints/floats corresponding to the Unix time
  for the dates of interest, must be midnight UTC (00:00+00:00)
  on the day of interest.

lat

: Latitude of location to perform calculation for

lon

: Longitude of location

delta_t

: Difference between terrestrial time and UT. USNO has tables.

# Returns

tuple : (transit, sunrise, sunset) localized to UTC

```pycon
>>> transit_sunrise_sunset(1523836800, 51.0486, -114.07, 70.68302220312503)
(1523907360.3863413, 1523882341.570479, 1523932345.7781625)
```

At present, this consists of several functions from API 520.

For reporting bugs, adding feature requests, or submitting pull requests,

functions.name: fluids.safety_valve.API520_round_size
documentation:
Rounds up the area from an API 520 calculation to an API526 standard
valve area. The returned area is always larger or equal to the input area.

# Parameters

A

: Minimum discharge area [m^2]

# Returns

area

: Actual discharge area [m^2]

# Notes

To obtain the letter designation of an input area, lookup the area with
the following:

API526_letters[API526_A.index(area)]

An exception is raised if the required relief area is larger than any of
the API 526 sizes.

# Examples

From [^footnote-1], checked with many points on Table 8.

```pycon
>>> API520_round_size(1E-4)
0.00012645136
>>> API526_letters[API526_A.index(API520_round_size(1E-4))]
'E'
```

# References

[^footnote-1]: API Standard 526.

functions.name: fluids.safety_valve.API520_C
documentation:
Calculates coefficient C for use in API 520 critical flow relief valve
sizing.

$$
C = 0.03948\sqrt{k\left(\frac{2}{k+1}\right)^\frac{k+1}{k-1}}
$$

# Parameters

k

: Isentropic coefficient or ideal gas heat capacity ratio [-]

# Returns

C

: Coefficient `C` [-]

# Notes

If C cannot be established, assume a coefficient of 0.0239,
the highest value possible for C.

Although not dimensional, C varies with the units used.

If k is exactly equal to 1, the expression is undefined, and the formula
must be simplified as follows from an application of L'Hopital's rule.

$$
C = 0.03948\sqrt{\frac{1}{e}}
$$

# Examples

From [^footnote-1], checked with many points on Table 8.

```pycon
>>> API520_C(1.35)
0.02669419967057233
```

# References

[^footnote-1]: API Standard 520, Part 1 - Sizing and Selection.

functions.name: fluids.safety_valve.API520_F2
documentation:
Calculates coefficient F2 for subcritical flow for use in API 520
subcritical flow relief valve sizing.

$$
F_2 = \sqrt{\left(\frac{k}{k-1}\right)r^\frac{2}{k} \left[\frac{1-r^\frac{k-1}{k}}{1-r}\right]}
$$

$$
r = \frac{P_2}{P_1}
$$

# Parameters

k

: Isentropic coefficient or ideal gas heat capacity ratio [-]

P1

: Upstream relieving pressure; the set pressure plus the allowable
  overpressure, plus atmospheric pressure, [Pa]

P2

: Built-up backpressure; the increase in pressure during flow at the
  outlet of a pressure-relief device after it opens, [Pa]

# Returns

F2

: Subcritical flow coefficient `F2` [-]

# Notes

F2 is completely dimensionless.

# Examples

From [^footnote-1] example 2, matches.

```pycon
>>> API520_F2(1.8, 1E6, 7E5)
0.8600724121105563
```

# References

[^footnote-1]: API Standard 520, Part 1 - Sizing and Selection.

functions.name: fluids.safety_valve.API520_N
documentation:
Calculates correction due to steam pressure for steam flow for use in
API 520 relief valve sizing.

For pressures below 10339 kPa, the correction factor is 1.

$$
K_N = \frac{0.02764P_1-1000}{0.03324P_1-1061}
$$

# Parameters

P1

: Upstream relieving pressure; the set pressure plus the allowable
  overpressure, plus atmospheric pressure, [Pa]

# Returns

KN

: Correction due to steam temperature [-]

# Notes

Although not dimensional, KN varies with the units used.

For temperatures above 922 K or pressures above 22057 kPa, KN is not defined.

Internally, units of kPa are used to match the equation in the standard.

# Examples

```pycon
>>> API520_N(10500e3)
0.9969100255
```

# References

[^footnote-1]: API Standard 520, Part 1 - Sizing and Selection.

functions.name: fluids.safety_valve.API520_SH
documentation:
Calculates correction due to steam superheat for steam flow for use in
API 520 relief valve sizing. 2D interpolation among a table with 28
pressures and 10 temperatures is performed.

# Parameters

T1

: Temperature of the fluid entering the valve [K]

P1

: Upstream relieving pressure; the set pressure plus the allowable
  overpressure, plus atmospheric pressure, [Pa]

edition

: One of '10E', '7E', [-]

# Returns

KSH

: Correction due to steam superheat [-]

# Notes

For P above 20679 kPag, use the critical flow model.
Superheat cannot be above 649 degrees Celsius.
If T1 is above 149 degrees Celsius, returns 1.

# Examples

Custom example from table 9, 7th edition:

```pycon
>>> API520_SH(593+273.15, 1066.325E3, '7E')
0.7201800000
```

# References

[^footnote-1]: API Standard 520, Part 1 - Sizing and Selection.

functions.name: fluids.safety_valve.API520_B
documentation:
Calculates capacity correction due to backpressure on balanced
spring-loaded PRVs in vapor service. For pilot operated valves,
this is always 1. Applicable up to 50% of the percent gauge backpressure,
For use in API 520 relief valve sizing. 1D interpolation among a table with
53 backpressures is performed.

# Parameters

Pset

: Set pressure for relief [Pa]

Pback

: Backpressure, [Pa]

overpressure

: The maximum fraction overpressure; one of 0.1, 0.16, or 0.21, [-]

# Returns

Kb

: Correction due to vapor backpressure [-]

# Notes

If the calculated gauge backpressure is less than 30%, 38%, or 50% for
overpressures of 0.1, 0.16, or 0.21, a value of 1 is returned.

Percent gauge backpressure must be under 50%.

# Examples

Custom examples from figure 30:

```pycon
>>> API520_B(1E6, 5E5)
0.7929945420944432
```

# References

[^footnote-1]: API Standard 520, Part 1 - Sizing and Selection.

functions.name: fluids.safety_valve.API520_A_g
documentation:
Calculates required relief valve area for an API 520 valve passing
a gas or a vapor, at either critical or sub-critical flow.

For critical flow:

$$
A = \frac{m}{CK_dP_1K_bK_c}\sqrt{\frac{TZ}{M}}
$$

For sub-critical flow:

$$
A = \frac{17.9m}{F_2K_dK_c}\sqrt{\frac{TZ}{MP_1(P_1-P_2)}}
$$

# Parameters

m

: Mass flow rate of vapor through the valve, [kg/s]

T

: Temperature of vapor entering the valve, [K]

Z

: Compressibility factor of the vapor, [-]

MW

: Molecular weight of the vapor, [g/mol]

k

: Isentropic coefficient or ideal gas heat capacity ratio [-]

P1

: Upstream relieving pressure; the set pressure plus the allowable
  overpressure, plus atmospheric pressure, [Pa]

P2

: Built-up backpressure; the increase in pressure during flow at the
  outlet of a pressure-relief device after it opens, [Pa]

Kd

: The effective coefficient of discharge, from the manufacturer or for
  preliminary sizing, using 0.975 normally or 0.62 when used with a
  rupture disc as described in [^footnote-1], []

Kb

: Correction due to vapor backpressure [-]

Kc

: Combination correction factor for installation with a rupture disk
  upstream of the PRV; 1.0 when a rupture disk is not installed, and
  0.9 if a rupture disk is present and the combination has not been
  certified, []

# Returns

A

: Minimum area for relief valve according to [^footnote-1], [m^2]

# Notes

Units are interlally kg/hr, kPa, and mm^2 to match [^footnote-1].

# Examples

Example 1 from [^footnote-1] for critical flow, matches:

```pycon
>>> API520_A_g(m=24270/3600., T=348., Z=0.90, MW=51., k=1.11, P1=670E3, Kb=1, Kc=1)
0.0036990460646834414
```

Example 2 from [^footnote-1] for sub-critical flow, matches:

```pycon
>>> API520_A_g(m=24270/3600., T=348., Z=0.90, MW=51., k=1.11, P1=670E3, P2=532E3, Kd=0.975, Kb=1, Kc=1)
0.004248358775943481
```

The mass flux in (kg/(s\*m^2)) can be found by dividing the specified mass
flow by the calculated area:

```pycon
>>> (24270/3600.)/API520_A_g(m=24270/3600., T=348., Z=0.90, MW=51., k=1.11, P1=670E3, Kb=1, Kc=1)
1822.541960488834
```

# References

[^footnote-1]: API Standard 520, Part 1 - Sizing and Selection.

functions.name: fluids.safety_valve.API520_A_steam
documentation:
Calculates required relief valve area for an API 520 valve passing
a steam, at either saturation or superheat but not partially condensed.

$$
A = \frac{190.5m}{P_1 K_d K_b K_c K_N K_{SH}}
$$

# Parameters

m

: Mass flow rate of steam through the valve, [kg/s]

T

: Temperature of steam entering the valve, [K]

P1

: Upstream relieving pressure; the set pressure plus the allowable
  overpressure, plus atmospheric pressure, [Pa]

Kd

: The effective coefficient of discharge, from the manufacturer or for
  preliminary sizing, using 0.975 normally or 0.62 when used with a
  rupture disc as described in [^footnote-1], []

Kb

: Correction due to backpressure, see {obj}`API520_B` [-]

Kc

: Combination correction factor for installation with a rupture disk
  upstream of the PRV; 1.0 when a rupture disk is not installed, and
  0.9 if a rupture disk is present and the combination has not been
  certified, []

edition

: One of '10E', '7E', [-]

# Returns

A

: Minimum area for relief valve according to [^footnote-1], [m^2]

# Notes

Units are interlally kg/hr, kPa, and mm^2 to match [^footnote-1].
With the provided temperature and pressure, the KN coefficient is
calculated with the function API520_N; as is the superheat correction KSH,
with the function API520_SH.

# Examples

Example 4 from [^footnote-1] 7th edition, matches:

```pycon
>>> API520_A_steam(m=69615/3600., T=592.5, P1=12236E3, Kd=0.975, Kb=1, Kc=1, edition='7E')
0.001103471242369
```

Example 4 from the 10th edition of [^footnote-1]:

```pycon
>>> API520_A_steam(m=69615/3600., T=707.0389, P1=12236E3, Kd=0.975, Kb=1, Kc=1, edition='10E')
0.00128518893191
```

# References

[^footnote-1]: API Standard 520, Part 1 - Sizing and Selection.

functions.name: fluids.safety_valve.API520_Kv
documentation:
Calculates correction due to viscosity for liquid flow for use in
API 520 relief valve sizing.

From the 7th to 9th editions, the formula for this calculation is as
follows:

$$
K_v = \left(0.9935 + \frac{2.878}{Re^{0.5}} + \frac{342.75} {Re^{1.5}}\right)^{-1}
$$

Startign in the 10th edition, the formula is

$$
K_v = \left(1 + \frac{170}{Re}\right)^{-0.5}
$$

In the 10th edition, the formula is applicable for Re > 80. It is also
recommended there that if the viscosity is < 0.1 Pa\*s, this correction
should be set to 1.

# Parameters

Re

: Reynolds number for flow out the valve [-]

edition

: One of '10E', '7E', [-]

# Returns

Kv

: Correction due to viscosity [-]

# Notes

Reynolds number in the standard is defined as follows, with Q in L/min, G1
as specific gravity, mu in centipoise, and area in mm^2:

$$
Re = \frac{Q(18800G_1)}{\mu \sqrt{A}}
$$

The constant 18800 is derived as follows, combining multiple unit
conversions and the formula from diameter from area together. The precise
value is shown below.

```pycon
>>> from scipy.constants import *
>>> liter/minute*1000./(0.001*(milli**2)**0.5)*sqrt(4/pi)
18806.319451591
```

Note that 4 formulas are provided in API 520 part 1; two metric and two
imperial. One pair of formulas uses viscosity in conventional units; the
other uses it in Saybolt Universal Seconds. A conversion is essentially
embedded in the the Saybolt Universal Seconds formula. A more precise
conversion can be obtained from
{obj}`chemicals.viscosity.viscosity_converter`.

In both editions, if the formula is used below the recommended Re range
and into the very low Re region this correction tends towards 0.

In the 10th edition, the formula tends to 1 exactly as Re increases. In the
7th edition, the formula can actually produce corrections above 1; this is
handled by truncating the factor to 1.

# Examples

From [^footnote-1] 7E, checked with example 5.

```pycon
>>> API520_Kv(100, edition='7E')
0.615744589
```

From [^footnote-2] 10E, checked with example 5:

```pycon
>>> API520_Kv(4525, edition='10E')
0.9817287137013179
```

Example in [^footnote-3], using the 7th edition formula:

```pycon
>>> API520_Kv(2110, edition='7E')
0.943671807
```

# References

[^footnote-1]: API Standard 520, Part 1 - Sizing and Selection, 7E

[^footnote-2]: API Standard 520, Part 1 - Sizing and Selection, 10E

[^footnote-3]: CCPS. Guidelines for Pressure Relief and Effluent Handling Systems.
    2nd edition. New York, NY: Wiley-AIChE, 2017.
## Liquids

functions.name: fluids.safety_valve.API520_W
documentation:
Calculates capacity correction due to backpressure on balanced
spring-loaded PRVs in liquid service. For pilot operated valves,
this is always 1. Applicable up to 50% of the percent gauge backpressure,
For use in API 520 relief valve sizing. 1D interpolation among a table with
53 backpressures is performed.

# Parameters

Pset

: Set pressure for relief [Pa]

Pback

: Backpressure, [Pa]

# Returns

KW

: Correction due to liquid backpressure [-]

# Notes

If the calculated gauge backpressure is less than 15%, a value of 1 is
returned.

# Examples

Custom example from figure 31 in [^footnote-1]:

```pycon
>>> API520_W(1E6, 3E5) # 22% overpressure
0.95114718480085
```

Example 5 from [^footnote-2], set pressure 250 psig and backpressure up to 50 psig:

```pycon
>>> API520_W(Pset=1825014, Pback=446062)
0.97242133397677
```

# References

[^footnote-1]: API Standard 520, Part 1 - Sizing and Selection. 7E

[^footnote-2]: API Standard 520, Part 1 - Sizing and Selection. 10E

functions.name: fluids.safety_valve.API520_A_l
documentation:
Calculates required relief valve area for an API 520 valve passing
a liquid in sub-critical flow.

$$
A = \frac{11.78Q}{K_d K_w K_c K_v}\left(\frac{G_1}{P1 - P2}\right)^{0.5}
$$

# Parameters

m

: Mass flow rate of liquid through the valve, [kg/s]

rho

: Liquid density, [kg/m^3]

P1

: Upstream relieving pressure; the set pressure plus the allowable
  overpressure, plus atmospheric pressure, [Pa]

P2

: Built-up backpressure; the increase in pressure during flow at the
  outlet of a pressure-relief device after it opens, [Pa]

overpressure

: The maximum fraction overpressure; used if `Kw` is not specified, [-]

Kd

: The effective coefficient of discharge, from the manufacturer or for
  preliminary sizing, using 0.65 normally or 0.62 when used with a
  rupture disc as described in [^footnote-1], []

Kc

: Combination correction factor for installation with a rupture disk
  upstream of the PRV; 1.0 when a rupture disk is not installed, and
  0.9 if a rupture disk is present and the combination has not been
  certified, []

Kw

: Correction due to liquid backpressure [-]

Kv

: Correction due to viscosity [-]

edition

: One of '10E', '7E', [-]

mu

: If provided and `Kv` is None, `Kv` will be calculated automatically,
  [Pa\*s]

# Returns

A

: Minimum area for relief valve according to [^footnote-1], [m^2]

# Notes

Units are interlally kg/hr, kPa, and mm^2 to match [^footnote-1].

This expression is essentially a form of the Loss coefficient `K`
expression, with many factors and unit conversions. The raw expression in
SI units, with `K` the true loss coefficient, is as follows:

$$
A = \frac{\sqrt{2} m \sqrt{\frac{K}{\rho \left(P_{1} - P_{2}\right)}}}{2}
$$

The constant 11.78 is the result of the following conversions:

> - 60000, converting from m^3/s to L/min
> - sqrt(2)/2 as a factor from algebra
> - 1e6 converting from m^2 to mm^2
> - sqrt(1e-3\*(rho0)) converting from Pa to kPa and kg/m^3 to specific gravity

The full precise value is (depending on the reference density chosen)

```pycon
>>> sqrt(1e-3*(999.0107539518483))/60000*sqrt(2)/2*1e6
11.779282389196
```

The K value from a relief valve sized with this method can be calculated
as follows:

$$
K = \frac{2 A^{2} \rho \left(P_{1} - P_{2}\right)}{m^{2}}
$$

The K value can also be directly calculated from the coefficients Kd, Kc,
Kw, and Kv. The calculation is as follows, making use of the correction
above.

$$
K = \left(\frac{1}{K_d K_w K_c K_v\cdot (11.779282389196/11.78)}\right)^2
$$

# Examples

Example 5 in [^footnote-1], 10th edition. The calculation involves numerous steps,
shown below and ending with a recalculation with a viscosity correction.

```pycon
>>> Q = 6814*1.6666666666666667e-05 # L/min to m^3/s
>>> rho = 0.9*999 # specific gravity times density of water kg/m^3
>>> m = rho*Q # mass flow rate, kg/s
>>> overpressure = 0.1
>>> P_design_g = 1724E3 # design pressure, guage
>>> P1 = (1+overpressure)*P_design_g + 101325.0 # upstream relieving pressure, Pa
>>> backpressure = 0.2
>>> mu = 0.388 # viscosity, Pa*s, converted from 2000 Saybolt Universal Seconds
>>> P2 = backpressure*P_design_g + 101325.0 # backpressure, Pa
```

Do the first calculation, using the value of Kw=0.97 shown in [1]

```pycon
>>> A0 = API520_A_l(m=m, rho=rho, P1=P1, P2=P2, overpressure=overpressure, Kd=0.65, Kw=0.97, Kc=1.0, Kv=1.0)
>>> A0
0.0030661356203
```

This value matches the 3066 mm^2 shown in the example calculation.

Do the same calculation but allow the calculation of `Kw` automatically:

```pycon
>>> A0 = API520_A_l(m=m, rho=rho, P1=P1, P2=P2, overpressure=overpressure, Kd=0.65, Kc=1.0, Kv=1.0)
>>> A0
0.0030585022573
```

There is a slight deviation with a more precise `Kw` value.

Compute Reynolds number from this original area

```pycon
>>> from math import pi
>>> D = (A0*4/pi)**0.5
>>> v = Q/A0
>>> Re = rho*v*D/mu
>>> Re
5369.4253339
```

The reynolds number shown in [1] is 4525; the difference comes from the less
precise Saybolt Universal Seconds conversion.

Compute the viscosity correction:

```pycon
>>> Kv = API520_Kv(Re, '10E')
>>> Kv
0.984535878488
```

Compute the final area

```pycon
>>> A = API520_A_l(m=m, rho=rho, P1=P1, P2=P2, overpressure=overpressure, Kd=0.65, Kc=1.0, Kv=Kv)
>>> A
0.003106542203
```

The final answer given in API 520 example 5 is 3122 mm^2, a very similar
value despite the small differences.

If is also possible to have `Kv` be calculated by this routine
automatically, by setting `Kv` to None and providing the fluid's viscosity.

```pycon
>>> A = API520_A_l(m=m, rho=rho, P1=P1, P2=P2, overpressure=overpressure, Kd=0.65, Kc=1.0, Kv=None, mu=mu)
>>> A
0.003106542203
```

As described in the note, an overall K value can be calculated for the
valve

```pycon
>>> K = 2*A**2*rho*(P1 - P2)/m**2
>>> K
2.5825844233354602
```

We can check the calculation

```pycon
>>> from fluids.core import dP_from_K
>>> v = Q/A
>>> dP_from_K(K=K, rho=rho, V=v), P1-P2
(1551600.000, 1551600.00)
```

# References

[^footnote-1]: API Standard 520, Part 1 - Sizing and Selection.

functions.name: fluids.safety_valve.API521_noise_graph
documentation:
Calculate the `L` parameter used in the API 521
noise calculation, from their Figure 18, Sound
Pressure Level at 30 m from the stack tip.

# Parameters

P_ratio

: The ratio of relieving pressure to atmospheric pressure [-]

# Returns

L

: Sound pressure level at 30 m from the stack tip [decibels]

# Notes

Two logarithmic linear polynomials are used. The function is
continious throughout. The pressure ratio should be more than 1
for physical reasons; the value is checked for this case.

# References

[^footnote-1]: API Standard 521.

functions.name: fluids.safety_valve.API521_noise
documentation:
Calculate the the noise coming from a flare tip at a
specified distance according to API 521. A graphical technique
is used to get the noise at 30 m from the tip, and it is then
adjusted for distance.

$$
L_{30 \text{m}} = L - 10 \log_{10}(0.5 m c^2)
$$

$$
L_p = L_{30 \text{m}} - 20 \log_{10}(r/(30 \text{m}))
$$

# Parameters

m

: Mass flow rate of relieving fluid, [kg/s]

P1

: Upstream pressure at the source, before the relieving
  device [Pa]

P2

: Atmospheric pressure, [Pa]

c

: Speed of sound of the fluid at the relieving device [m/s]

r

: Distance from the flare stack, [m]

# Returns

L

: Sound pressure level at the specified distance from the
  stack tip [decibels]

# Notes

# Examples

Example as shown in [^footnote-1]:

```pycon
>>> API521_noise(m=14.6, P1=330E3, P2=101325, c=353.0, r=30)
113.6841057
```

# References

[^footnote-1]: API Standard 521.

functions.name: fluids.safety_valve.VDI_3732_noise_ground_flare
documentation:
Calculate the the noise at the flare tip of a ground flare
[^footnote-1], [^footnote-2].

$$
L = 100 + 15\log_{10}\left(\frac{m}{\text{tonne/hour}}\right)
$$

# Parameters

m

: Mass flow rate of relieving fluid, [kg/s]

# Returns

noise

: Sound pressure level at the relieving flare stack [decibels]

# Notes

# Examples

```pycon
>>> VDI_3732_noise_ground_flare(3.0)
145.501356332
```

# References

[^footnote-1]: VDI 3732 - Standard Noise Levels of Technical Sound
    Sources - Flares, 1999.
    <https://www.vdi.de/en/home/vdi-standards/details/vdi-3732-standard-noise-levels-of-technical-sound-sources-flares>.

[^footnote-2]: AdminFlare Noise Calculator. WKC Group (blog).
    <https://www.wkcgroup.com/tools-room/flare-noise-calculator/>.

functions.name: fluids.safety_valve.VDI_3732_noise_elevated_flare

OUT OF OR IN CONNECTION WITH THE SOFTWARE OR THE USE OR OTHER DEALINGS IN THE
SOFTWARE.

This module contains functionality for calculating parameters about different
geometrical forms that come up in engineering practice.

Implemented geometry objects are tanks, helical coils, cooling towers,
air coolers, compact heat exchangers, and plate and frame heat exchangers.

Additional functionality for typical catalyst/adsorbent pellet shapes is also
included.

For reporting bugs, adding feature requests, or submitting pull requests,
please use the [GitHub issue tracker](https://github.com/CalebBell/fluids/)
or contact the author at <mailto:Caleb.Andrew.Bell@gmail.com>.

functions.name: fluids.geometry.SA_partial_sphere
documentation:
Calculates surface area of a partial sphere according to [^footnote-1].
If h is half of D, the shape is half a sphere. No bottom is considered in
this function. Valid inputs are positive values of D and h, with h always
smaller or equal to D.

$$
a = \sqrt{h(2r - h)}
$$

$$
A = \pi(a^2 + h^2)
$$

# Parameters

D

: Diameter of the sphere, [m]

h

: Height, as measured from the cap to where the sphere is cut off [m]

# Returns

SA

: Surface area [m^2]

# Examples

```pycon
>>> SA_partial_sphere(1., 0.7)
2.199114857512855
```

# References

[^footnote-1]: Weisstein, Eric W. "Spherical Cap." Text. Accessed December 22, 2015.
    <http://mathworld.wolfram.com/SphericalCap.html>.
## Spherical Vessels, partially filled

functions.name: fluids.geometry.V_partial_sphere
documentation:
Calculates volume of a partial sphere according to [^footnote-1].
If h is half of D, the shape is half a sphere. No bottom is considered in
this function. Valid inputs are positive values of D and h, with h always
smaller or equal to D.

$$
a = \sqrt{h(2r - h)}
$$

$$
V = 1/6 \pi h(3a^2 + h^2)
$$

# Parameters

D

: Diameter of the sphere, [m]

h

: Height, as measured up to where the sphere is cut off, [m]

# Returns

V

: Volume [m^3]

# Examples

```pycon
>>> V_partial_sphere(1., 0.7)
0.4105014400690663
```

# References

[^footnote-1]: Weisstein, Eric W. "Spherical Cap." Text. Accessed December 22, 2015.
    <http://mathworld.wolfram.com/SphericalCap.html>.

functions.name: fluids.geometry.V_horiz_conical
documentation:
Calculates volume of a tank with conical ends, according to [^footnote-1].

$$
V_f = A_fL + \frac{2aR^2}{3}K, \;\;0 \le h < R\\
$$

$$
V_f = A_fL + \frac{2aR^2}{3}\pi/2,\;\; h = R\\
$$

$$
V_f = A_fL + \frac{2aR^2}{3}(\pi-K), \;\; R< h \le 2R
$$

$$
K = \cos^{-1} M + M^3\cosh^{-1} \frac{1}{M} - 2M\sqrt{1 - M^2}
$$

$$
M = \left|\frac{R-h}{R}\right|
$$

$$
A_f = R^2\cos^{-1}\frac{R-h}{R} - (R-h)\sqrt{2Rh - h^2}
$$

# Parameters

D

: Diameter of the main cylindrical section, [m]

L

: Length of the main cylindrical section, [m]

a

: Distance the cone head extends on one side, [m]

h

: Height, as measured up to where the fluid ends, [m]

headonly

: Function returns only the volume of a single head side if True

# Returns

V

: Volume [m^3]

# Examples

Matching example from [^footnote-1], with inputs in inches and volume in gallons.

```pycon
>>> V_horiz_conical(D=108., L=156., a=42., h=36)/231
2041.1923581273443
```

# References

[^footnote-1]: Jones, D. "Calculating Tank Volume." Text. Accessed December 22, 2015.
    <http://www.webcalc.com.br/blog/Tank_Volume.PDF>
## Functions as developed by Dan Jones

functions.name: fluids.geometry.V_horiz_ellipsoidal
documentation:
Calculates volume of a tank with ellipsoidal ends, according to [^footnote-1].

$$
V_f = A_fL + \pi a h^2\left(1 - \frac{h}{3R}\right)
$$

$$
A_f = R^2\cos^{-1}\frac{R-h}{R} - (R-h)\sqrt{2Rh - h^2}
$$

# Parameters

D

: Diameter of the main cylindrical section, [m]

L

: Length of the main cylindrical section, [m]

a

: Distance the ellipsoidal head extends on one side, [m]

h

: Height, as measured up to where the fluid ends, [m]

headonly

: Function returns only the volume of a single head side if True

# Returns

V

: Volume [m^3]

# Examples

Matching example from [^footnote-1], with inputs in inches and volume in gallons.

```pycon
>>> V_horiz_ellipsoidal(D=108, L=156, a=42, h=36)/231.
2380.9565415578145
```

# References

[^footnote-1]: Jones, D. "Calculating Tank Volume." Text. Accessed December 22, 2015.
    <http://www.webcalc.com.br/blog/Tank_Volume.PDF>

functions.name: fluids.geometry.V_horiz_guppy
documentation:
Calculates volume of a tank with guppy heads, according to [^footnote-1].

$$
V_f = A_fL + \frac{2aR^2}{3}\cos^{-1}\left(1 - \frac{h}{R}\right) +\frac{2a}{9R}\sqrt{2Rh - h^2}(2h-3R)(h+R)
$$

$$
A_f = R^2\cos^{-1}\frac{R-h}{R} - (R-h)\sqrt{2Rh - h^2}
$$

# Parameters

D

: Diameter of the main cylindrical section, [m]

L

: Length of the main cylindrical section, [m]

a

: Distance the guppy head extends on one side, [m]

h

: Height, as measured up to where the fluid ends, [m]

headonly

: Function returns only the volume of a single head side if True

# Returns

V

: Volume [m^3]

# Examples

Matching example from [^footnote-1], with inputs in inches and volume in gallons.

```pycon
>>> V_horiz_guppy(D=108., L=156., a=42., h=36)/231.
1931.7208029476762
```

# References

[^footnote-1]: Jones, D. "Calculating Tank Volume." Text. Accessed December 22, 2015.
    <http://www.webcalc.com.br/blog/Tank_Volume.PDF>

functions.name: fluids.geometry.V_horiz_spherical
documentation:
Calculates volume of a tank with spherical heads, according to [^footnote-1].

$$
V_f = A_fL + \frac{\pi a}{6}(3R^2 + a^2),\;\; h = R, |a|\le R
$$

$$
V_f = A_fL + \frac{\pi a}{3}(3R^2 + a^2),\;\; h = D, |a|\le R
$$

$$
V_f = A_fL + \pi a h^2\left(1 - \frac{h}{3R}\right),\;\; h = 0, \text{ or } |a| = 0, R, -R
$$

$$
V_f = A_fL + \frac{a}{|a|}\left\{\frac{2r^3}{3}\left[\cos^{-1} \frac{R^2 - rw}{R(w-r)} + \cos^{-1}\frac{R^2 + rw}{R(w+r)} - \frac{z}{r}\left(2 + \left(\frac{R}{r}\right)^2\right) \cos^{-1}\frac{w}{R}\right] - 2\left(wr^2 - \frac{w^3}{3}\right) \tan^{-1}\frac{y}{z} + \frac{4wyz}{3}\right\} ,\;\; h \ne R, D; a \ne 0, R, -R, |a| \ge 0.01D
$$

$$
V_f = A_fL + \frac{a}{|a|}\left[2\int_w^R(r^2 - x^2)\tan^{-1} \sqrt{\frac{R^2-x^2}{r^2-R^2}}dx - A_f z\right] ,\;\; h \ne R, D; a \ne 0, R, -R, |a| < 0.01D
$$

$$
A_f = R^2\cos^{-1}\frac{R-h}{R} - (R-h)\sqrt{2Rh - h^2}
$$

$$
r = \frac{a^2 + R^2}{2|a|}
$$

$$
w = R - h
$$

$$
y = \sqrt{2Rh-h^2}
$$

$$
z = \sqrt{r^2 - R^2}
$$

# Parameters

D

: Diameter of the main cylindrical section, [m]

L

: Length of the main cylindrical section, [m]

a

: Distance the spherical head extends on one side, [m]

h

: Height, as measured up to where the fluid ends, [m]

headonly

: Function returns only the volume of a single head side if True

# Returns

V

: Volume [m^3]

# Examples

Matching example from [^footnote-1], with inputs in inches and volume in gallons.

```pycon
>>> V_horiz_spherical(D=108., L=156., a=42., h=36)/231.
2303.96151169861
```

# References

[^footnote-1]: Jones, D. "Calculating Tank Volume." Text. Accessed December 22, 2015.
    <http://www.webcalc.com.br/blog/Tank_Volume.PDF>

functions.name: fluids.geometry.V_horiz_torispherical
documentation:
Calculates volume of a tank with torispherical heads, according to [^footnote-1].

$$
V_f  = A_fL + 2V_1, \;\; 0 \le h \le h_1\\ V_f  = A_fL + 2(V_{1,max} + V_2 + V_3), \;\; h_1 < h < h_2\\ V_f  = A_fL + 2[2V_{1,max} - V_1(h=D-h) + V_{2,max} + V_{3,max}] , \;\; h_2 \le h \le D
$$

$$
V_1 = \int_0^{\sqrt{2kDh - h^2}} \left[n^2\sin^{-1}\frac{\sqrt {n^2-w^2}}{n} - w\sqrt{n^2-w^2}\right]dx
$$

$$
V_2 = \int_0^{kD\cos\alpha}\left[n^2\left(\cos^{-1}\frac{w}{n} - \cos^{-1}\frac{g}{n}\right) - w\sqrt{n^2 - w^2} + g\sqrt{n^2 - g^2}\right]dx
$$

$$
V_3 = \int_w^g(r^2 - x^2)\tan^{-1}\frac{\sqrt{g^2 - x^2}}{z}dx - \frac{z}{2}\left(g^2\cos^{-1}\frac{w}{g} - w\sqrt{2g(h-h_1) - (h-h_1)^2}\right)
$$

$$
V_{1,max} = v_1(h=h_1)
$$

$$
v_{2,max} = v_2(h=h_2)
$$

$$
v_{3,max} = \frac{\pi a_1}{6}(3g^2 + a_1^2)
$$

$$
a_1 = fD(1-\cos\alpha)
$$

$$
\alpha = \sin^{-1}\frac{1-2k}{2(f-k)}
$$

$$
n = R - kD + \sqrt{k^2D^2-x^2}
$$

$$
g = r\sin\alpha
$$

$$
r = fD
$$

$$
h_2 = D - h_1
$$

$$
w = R - h
$$

$$
z = \sqrt{r^2- g^2}
$$

# Parameters

D

: Diameter of the main cylindrical section, [m]

L

: Length of the main cylindrical section, [m]

f

: Dimensionless dish-radius parameter; also commonly given as the
  product of `f` and `D` (`fD`), which is called both dish radius and
  also crown radius and has units of length, [-]

k

: Dimensionless knuckle-radius parameter; also commonly given as the
  product of `k` and `D` (`kD`), which is called the knuckle radius
  and has units of length, [-]

h

: Height, as measured up to where the fluid ends, [m]

headonly

: Function returns only the volume of a single head side if True

# Returns

V

: Volume [m^3]

# Examples

Matching example from [^footnote-1], with inputs in inches and volume in gallons.

```pycon
>>> V_horiz_torispherical(D=108., L=156., f=1., k=0.06, h=36)/231.
2028.62667
```

# References

[^footnote-1]: Jones, D. "Calculating Tank Volume." Text. Accessed December 22, 2015.
    <http://www.webcalc.com.br/blog/Tank_Volume.PDF>

functions.name: fluids.geometry.V_vertical_conical
documentation:
Calculates volume of a vertical tank with a convex conical bottom,
according to [^footnote-1]. No provision for the top of the tank is made here.

$$
V_f = \frac{\pi}{4}\left(\frac{Dh}{a}\right)^2\left(\frac{h}{3}\right),\; h < a
$$

$$
V_f = \frac{\pi D^2}{4}\left(h - \frac{2a}{3}\right),\; h\ge a
$$

# Parameters

D

: Diameter of the main cylindrical section, [m]

a

: Distance the cone head extends under the main cylinder, [m]

h

: Height, as measured up to where the fluid ends, [m]

# Returns

V

: Volume [m^3]

# Examples

Matching example from [^footnote-1], with inputs in inches and volume in gallons.

```pycon
>>> V_vertical_conical(132., 33., 24)/231.
250.67461381371024
```

# References

[^footnote-1]: Jones, D. "Calculating Tank Volume." Text. Accessed December 22, 2015.
    <http://www.webcalc.com.br/blog/Tank_Volume.PDF>
## Begin vertical tanks

functions.name: fluids.geometry.V_vertical_ellipsoidal
documentation:
Calculates volume of a vertical tank with a convex ellipsoidal bottom,
according to [^footnote-1]. No provision for the top of the tank is made here.

$$
V_f = \frac{\pi}{4}\left(\frac{Dh}{a}\right)^2 \left(a - \frac{h}{3}\right),\; h < a
$$

$$
V_f = \frac{\pi D^2}{4}\left(h - \frac{a}{3}\right),\; h \ge a
$$

# Parameters

D

: Diameter of the main cylindrical section, [m]

a

: Distance the ellipsoid head extends under the main cylinder, [m]

h

: Height, as measured up to where the fluid ends, [m]

# Returns

V

: Volume [m^3]

# Examples

Matching example from [^footnote-1], with inputs in inches and volume in gallons.

```pycon
>>> V_vertical_ellipsoidal(132., 33., 24)/231.
783.3581681678445
```

# References

[^footnote-1]: Jones, D. "Calculating Tank Volume." Text. Accessed December 22, 2015.
    <http://www.webcalc.com.br/blog/Tank_Volume.PDF>

functions.name: fluids.geometry.V_vertical_spherical
documentation:
Calculates volume of a vertical tank with a convex spherical bottom,
according to [^footnote-1]. No provision for the top of the tank is made here.

$$
V_f = \frac{\pi h^2}{4}\left(2a + \frac{D^2}{2a} - \frac{4h}{3}\right),\; h < a
$$

$$
V_f = \frac{\pi}{4}\left(\frac{2a^3}{3} - \frac{aD^2}{2} + hD^2\right),\; h\ge a
$$

# Parameters

D

: Diameter of the main cylindrical section, [m]

a

: Distance the spherical head extends under the main cylinder, [m]

h

: Height, as measured up to where the fluid ends, [m]

# Returns

V

: Volume [m^3]

# Examples

Matching example from [^footnote-1], with inputs in inches and volume in gallons.

```pycon
>>> V_vertical_spherical(132., 33., 24)/231.
583.6018352850442
```

# References

[^footnote-1]: Jones, D. "Calculating Tank Volume." Text. Accessed December 22, 2015.
    <http://www.webcalc.com.br/blog/Tank_Volume.PDF>

functions.name: fluids.geometry.V_vertical_torispherical
documentation:
Calculates volume of a vertical tank with a convex torispherical bottom,
according to [^footnote-1]. No provision for the top of the tank is made here.

$$
V_f = \frac{\pi h^2}{4}\left(2a_1 + \frac{D_1^2}{2a_1} - \frac{4h}{3}\right),\; 0 \le h \le a_1
$$

$$
V_f = \frac{\pi}{4}\left(\frac{2a_1^3}{3} + \frac{a_1D_1^2}{2}\right) +\pi u\left[\left(\frac{D}{2}-kD\right)^2 +s\right] + \frac{\pi tu^2}{2} - \frac{\pi u^3}{3} + \pi D(1-2k)\left[ \frac{2u-t}{4}\sqrt{s+tu-u^2} + \frac{t\sqrt{s}}{4} + \frac{k^2D^2}{2}\left(\cos^{-1}\frac{t-2u}{2kD}-\alpha\right)\right] ,\; a_1 < h \le a_1 + a_2
$$

$$
V_f = \frac{\pi}{4}\left(\frac{2a_1^3}{3} + \frac{a_1D_1^2}{2}\right) +\frac{\pi t}{2}\left[\left(\frac{D}{2}-kD\right)^2 +s\right] +\frac{\pi  t^3}{12} + \pi D(1-2k)\left[\frac{t\sqrt{s}}{4} + \frac{k^2D^2}{2}\sin^{-1}(\cos\alpha)\right] + \frac{\pi D^2}{4}[h-(a_1+a_2)] ,\;  a_1 + a_2 < h
$$

$$
\alpha = \sin^{-1}\frac{1-2k}{2(f-k)}
$$

$$
a_1 = fD(1-\cos\alpha)
$$

$$
a_2 = kD\cos\alpha
$$

$$
D_1 = 2fD\sin\alpha
$$

$$
s = (kD\sin\alpha)^2
$$

$$
t = 2a_2
$$

$$
u = h - fD(1-\cos\alpha)
$$

# Parameters

D

: Diameter of the main cylindrical section, [m]

f

: Dimensionless dish-radius parameter; also commonly given as the
  product of `f` and `D` (`fD`), which is called dish radius and
  has units of length, [-]

k

: Dimensionless knuckle-radius parameter; also commonly given as the
  product of `k` and `D` (`kD`), which is called the knuckle radius
  and has units of length, [-]

h

: Height, as measured up to where the fluid ends, [m]

# Returns

V

: Volume [m^3]

# Examples

Matching example from [^footnote-1], with inputs in inches and volume in gallons.

```pycon
>>> V_vertical_torispherical(D=132., f=1.0, k=0.06, h=24)/231.
904.0688283793
```

# References

[^footnote-1]: Jones, D. "Calculating Tank Volume." Text. Accessed December 22, 2015.
    <http://www.webcalc.com.br/blog/Tank_Volume.PDF>

functions.name: fluids.geometry.V_vertical_conical_concave
documentation:
Calculates volume of a vertical tank with a concave conical bottom,
according to [^footnote-1]. No provision for the top of the tank is made here.

$$
V = \frac{\pi D^2}{12} \left(3h + a - \frac{(a+h)^3}{a^2}\right) ,\;\; 0 \le h < |a|
$$

$$
V = \frac{\pi D^2}{12} (3h + a ),\;\; h \ge |a|
$$

# Parameters

D

: Diameter of the main cylindrical section, [m]

a

: Negative distance the cone head extends inside the main cylinder, [m]

h

: Height, as measured up to where the fluid ends, [m]

# Returns

V

: Volume [m^3]

# Examples

Matching example from [^footnote-1], with inputs in inches and volume in gallons.

```pycon
>>> V_vertical_conical_concave(D=113., a=-33, h=15)/231
251.158255657951
```

# References

[^footnote-1]: Jones, D. "Compute Fluid Volumes in Vertical Tanks." Chemical
    Processing. December 18, 2003.
    <http://www.chemicalprocessing.com/articles/2003/193/>
## Begin vertical tanks with concave heads

functions.name: fluids.geometry.V_vertical_ellipsoidal_concave
documentation:
Calculates volume of a vertical tank with a concave ellipsoidal bottom,
according to [^footnote-1]. No provision for the top of the tank is made here.

$$
V = \frac{\pi D^2}{12} \left(3h + 2a - \frac{(a+h)^2(2a-h)}{a^2}\right) ,\;\; 0 \le h < |a|
$$

$$
V = \frac{\pi D^2}{12} (3h + 2a ),\;\; h \ge |a|
$$

# Parameters

D

: Diameter of the main cylindrical section, [m]

a

: Negative distance the eppilsoid head extends inside the main cylinder, [m]

h

: Height, as measured up to where the fluid ends, [m]

# Returns

V

: Volume [m^3]

# Examples

Matching example from [^footnote-1], with inputs in inches and volume in gallons.

```pycon
>>> V_vertical_ellipsoidal_concave(D=113., a=-33, h=15)/231
44.84968851034856
```

# References

[^footnote-1]: Jones, D. "Compute Fluid Volumes in Vertical Tanks." Chemical
    Processing. December 18, 2003.
    <http://www.chemicalprocessing.com/articles/2003/193/>

functions.name: fluids.geometry.V_vertical_spherical_concave
documentation:
Calculates volume of a vertical tank with a concave spherical bottom,
according to [^footnote-1]. No provision for the top of the tank is made here.

$$
V = \frac{\pi}{12}\left[3D^2h + \frac{a}{2}(3D^2 + 4a^2) + (a+h)^3 \left(4 - \frac{3D^2 + 12a^2}{2a(a+h)}\right)\right],\;\; 0 \le h < |a|
$$

$$
V = \frac{\pi}{12}\left[3D^2h + \frac{a}{2}(3D^2 + 4a^2) \right] ,\;\;  h \ge |a|
$$

# Parameters

D

: Diameter of the main cylindrical section, [m]

a

: Negative distance the spherical head extends inside the main cylinder, [m]

h

: Height, as measured up to where the fluid ends, [m]

# Returns

V

: Volume [m^3]

# Examples

Matching example from [^footnote-1], with inputs in inches and volume in gallons.

```pycon
>>> V_vertical_spherical_concave(D=113., a=-33, h=15)/231
112.81405437348528
```

# References

[^footnote-1]: Jones, D. "Compute Fluid Volumes in Vertical Tanks." Chemical
    Processing. December 18, 2003.
    <http://www.chemicalprocessing.com/articles/2003/193/>

functions.name: fluids.geometry.V_vertical_torispherical_concave
documentation:
Calculates volume of a vertical tank with a concave torispherical bottom,
according to [^footnote-1]. No provision for the top of the tank is made here.

$$
V = \frac{\pi D^2 h}{4} - v_1(h=a_1+a_2) + v_1(h=a_1 + a_2 -h),\; 0 \le h < a_2
$$

$$
V = \frac{\pi D^2 h}{4} - v_1(h=a_1+a_2) + v_2(h=a_1 + a_2 -h),\; a_2 \le h < a_1 + a_2
$$

$$
V = \frac{\pi D^2 h}{4} - v_1(h=a_1+a_2) + 0,\; h \ge a_1 + a_2
$$

$$
v_1 = \frac{\pi}{4}\left(\frac{2a_1^3}{3} + \frac{a_1D_1^2}{2}\right) +\pi u\left[\left(\frac{D}{2}-kD\right)^2 +s\right] + \frac{\pi tu^2}{2} - \frac{\pi u^3}{3} + \pi D(1-2k)\left[ \frac{2u-t}{4}\sqrt{s+tu-u^2} + \frac{t\sqrt{s}}{4} + \frac{k^2D^2}{2}\left(\cos^{-1}\frac{t-2u}{2kD}-\alpha\right)\right]
$$

$$
v_2 = \frac{\pi h^2}{4}\left(2a_1 + \frac{D_1^2}{2a_1} - \frac{4h}{3}\right)
$$

$$
\alpha = \sin^{-1}\frac{1-2k}{2(f-k)}
$$

$$
a_1 = fD(1-\cos\alpha)
$$

$$
a_2 = kD\cos\alpha
$$

$$
D_1 = 2fD\sin\alpha
$$

$$
s = (kD\sin\alpha)^2
$$

$$
t = 2a_2
$$

$$
u = h - fD(1-\cos\alpha)
$$

# Parameters

D

: Diameter of the main cylindrical section, [m]

f

: Dimensionless dish-radius parameter; also commonly given as the
  product of `f` and `D` (`fD`), which is called dish radius and
  has units of length, [-]

k

: Dimensionless knuckle-radius parameter; also commonly given as the
  product of `k` and `D` (`kD`), which is called the knuckle radius
  and has units of length, [-]

h

: Height, as measured up to where the fluid ends, [m]

# Returns

V

: Volume [m^3]

# Examples

Matching example from [^footnote-1], with inputs in inches and volume in gallons.

```pycon
>>> V_vertical_torispherical_concave(D=113., f=0.71, k=0.081, h=15)/231
103.88569287163769
```

# References

[^footnote-1]: Jones, D. "Compute Fluid Volumes in Vertical Tanks." Chemical
    Processing. December 18, 2003.
    <http://www.chemicalprocessing.com/articles/2003/193/>

functions.name: fluids.geometry.SA_ellipsoidal_head
documentation:
Calculates the surface area of an ellipsoidal head according to [^footnote-1] and [^footnote-2].
The formula below is for the full shape, the result of which is halved. The
formula is for $a < R$. In the equations, `a` is the same and `c` is `D`.

$$
\text{SA} = 2\pi a^2 + \frac{\pi c^2}{e_1}\ln\left(\frac{1+e_1}{1-e_1} \right)
$$

$$
e_1 = \sqrt{1 - \frac{c^2}{a^2}}
$$

For the case of $a \ge R$ from [^footnote-2], which is needed to make the tank
head volume grow linearly with length:

$$
\text{SA} = 2\pi R^2 + \frac{2\pi a^2 R}{\sqrt{a^2 - R^2}}\cos^{-1}\frac{R}{|a|}
$$

# Parameters

D

: Diameter of the main cylindrical section, [m]

a

: Distance the ellipsoidal head extends, [m]

# Returns

SA

: Surface area [m^2]

# Examples

Spherical case

```pycon
>>> SA_ellipsoidal_head(2, 1)
6.283185307179586
>>> SA_ellipsoidal_head(2, 1.5)
8.459109081729984
```

# References

[^footnote-1]: Weisstein, Eric W. "Spheroid." Text. Accessed March 14, 2016.
    <http://mathworld.wolfram.com/Spheroid.html>.

[^footnote-2]: Jones, D. "Calculating Tank Wetted Area." Text. Chemical Processing.
    April 2017. <https://www.chemicalprocessing.com/assets/Uploads/calculating-tank-wetted-area.pdf>
## Total surface area of heads, orientation-independent

functions.name: fluids.geometry.SA_conical_head
documentation:
Calculates the surface area of a conical head according to [^footnote-1].

$$
SA = \frac{\pi D}{2} \sqrt{a^2 + \left(\frac{D}{2}\right)^2}
$$

# Parameters

D

: Diameter of the main cylindrical section, [m]

a

: Distance the conical head extends, [m]

# Returns

SA

: Surface area [m^2]

# Examples

```pycon
>>> SA_conical_head(2, 1)
4.442882938158366
```

# References

[^footnote-1]: Weisstein, Eric W. "Cone." Text. Accessed March 14, 2016.
    <http://mathworld.wolfram.com/Cone.html>.

functions.name: fluids.geometry.SA_guppy_head
documentation:
Calculates the surface area of a guppy head according to [^footnote-1].
Some work was involved in combining formulas for the ellipse of the head,
and the conic section on the sides.

$$
SA = \frac{\pi  D}{4}\sqrt{D^2 + a^2} + \frac{\pi D}{2}a
$$

# Parameters

D

: Diameter of the main cylindrical section, [m]

a

: Distance the conical head extends, [m]

# Returns

SA

: Surface area [m^2]

# Examples

```pycon
>>> SA_guppy_head(2, 1)
6.654000019110157
```

# References

[^footnote-1]: Weisstein, Eric W. "Cone." Text. Accessed March 14, 2016.
    <http://mathworld.wolfram.com/Cone.html>.

functions.name: fluids.geometry.SA_torispheroidal
documentation:
Calculates surface area of a torispherical head according to [^footnote-1].
Somewhat involved. Equations are adapted to be used for a full head.

$$
SA = S_1 + S_2
$$

$$
S_1 = 2\pi D^2 f_d \alpha
$$

$$
S_2 = 2\pi D^2 f_k\left(\alpha - \alpha_1 + (0.5 - f_k)\left(\sin^{-1} \left(\frac{\alpha-\alpha_2}{f_k}\right) - \sin^{-1}\left(\frac{ \alpha_1-\alpha_2}{f_k}\right)\right)\right)
$$

$$
\alpha_1 = f_d\left(1 - \sqrt{1 - \left(\frac{0.5 - f_k}{f_d-f_k} \right)^2}\right)
$$

$$
\alpha_2 = f_d - \sqrt{f_d^2 - 2f_d f_k + f_k - 0.25}
$$

$$
\alpha = \frac{a}{D_i}
$$

# Parameters

D

: Diameter of the main cylindrical section, [m]

f

: Dimensionless dish-radius parameter; also commonly given as the
  product of `f` and `D` (`fD`), which is called dish radius and
  has units of length, [-]

k

: Dimensionless knuckle-radius parameter; also commonly given as the
  product of `k` and `D` (`kD`), which is called the knuckle radius
  and has units of length, [-]

# Returns

SA

: Surface area [m^2]

# Examples

Example from [^footnote-1].

```pycon
>>> SA_torispheroidal(D=2.54, f=1.039370079, k=0.062362205)
6.00394283477063
```

# References

[^footnote-1]: Honeywell. "Calculate Surface Areas and Cross-sectional Areas in
    Vessels with Dished Heads". <https://www.honeywellprocess.com/library/marketing/whitepapers/WP-VesselsWithDishedHeads-UniSimDesign.pdf>
    Whitepaper. 2014.

functions.name: fluids.geometry.SA_tank
documentation:
Calculates the surface are of a cylindrical tank with optional heads.
In the degenerate case of being provided with only `D` and `L`, provides
the surface area of a cylinder.

# Parameters

D

: Diameter of the cylindrical section of the tank, [m]

L

: Length of the main cylindrical section of the tank, [m]

sideA

: The left (or bottom for vertical) head of the tank's type; one of
  [None, 'conical', 'ellipsoidal', 'torispherical', 'guppy', 'spherical'].

sideB

: The right (or top for vertical) head of the tank's type; one of
  [None, 'conical', 'ellipsoidal', 'torispherical', 'guppy', 'spherical'].

sideA_a

: The distance the head as specified by sideA extends down or to the left
  from the main cylindrical section, [m]

sideB_a

: The distance the head as specified by sideB extends up or to the right
  from the main cylindrical section, [m]

sideA_f

: Dish-radius parameter for side A; fD = dish radius [1/m]

sideA_k

: knuckle-radius parameter for side A; kD = knuckle radius [1/m]

sideB_f

: Dish-radius parameter for side B; fD = dish radius [1/m]

sideB_k

: knuckle-radius parameter for side B; kD = knuckle radius [1/m]

# Returns

SA

: Surface area of the tank [m^2]

sideA_SA

: Surface area of only `sideA` [m^2]

sideB_SA

: Surface area of only `sideB` [m^2]

lateral_SA

: Surface area of cylindrical section of tank [m^2]

# Examples

Cylinder, Spheroid, Long Cones, and spheres. All checked.

```pycon
>>> SA_tank(D=2, L=2)[0]
18.84955592153876
>>> SA_tank(D=1., L=0, sideA='ellipsoidal', sideA_a=2, sideB='ellipsoidal',
... sideB_a=2)[0]
10.124375616183
>>> SA_tank(D=1., L=5, sideA='conical', sideA_a=2, sideB='conical',
... sideB_a=2)[0]
22.18452243965
>>> SA_tank(D=1., L=5, sideA='spherical', sideA_a=0.5, sideB='spherical',
... sideB_a=0.5)[0]
18.8495559215
```

functions.name: fluids.geometry.V_tank
documentation:
Calculates the total volume of a vertical or horizontal tank with
different head types.

# Parameters

D

: Diameter of the cylindrical section of the tank, [m]

L

: Length of the main cylindrical section of the tank, [m]

horizontal

: Whether or not the tank is a horizontal or vertical tank

sideA

: The left (or bottom for vertical) head of the tank's type; one of
  [None, 'conical', 'ellipsoidal', 'torispherical', 'guppy', 'spherical'].

sideB

: The right (or top for vertical) head of the tank's type; one of
  [None, 'conical', 'ellipsoidal', 'torispherical', 'guppy', 'spherical'].

sideA_a

: The distance the head as specified by sideA extends down or to the left
  from the main cylindrical section, [m]

sideB_a

: The distance the head as specified by sideB extends up or to the right
  from the main cylindrical section, [m]

sideA_f

: Dimensionless dish-radius parameter for side A; also commonly given as
  the product of `f` and `D` (`fD`), which is called dish radius and
  has units of length, [-]

sideA_k

: Dimensionless knuckle-radius parameter for side A; also commonly given
  as the product of `k` and `D` (`kD`), which is called the knuckle
  radius and has units of length, [-]

sideB_f

: Dimensionless dish-radius parameter for side B; also commonly given as
  the product of `f` and `D` (`fD`), which is called dish radius and
  has units of length, [-]

sideB_k

: Dimensionless knuckle-radius parameter for side B; also commonly given
  as the product of `k` and `D` (`kD`), which is called the knuckle
  radius and has units of length, [-]

# Returns

V

: Total volume [m^3]

sideA_V

: Volume of only `sideA` [m^3]

sideB_V

: Volume of only `sideB` [m^3]

lateral_V

: Volume of cylindrical section of tank [m^3]

# Examples

```pycon
>>> V_tank(D=1.5, L=5., horizontal=False, sideA='conical',
... sideB='conical', sideA_a=2., sideB_a=1.)
(10.602875205865551, 1.1780972450961726, 0.5890486225480863, 8.835729338221293)
```

functions.name: fluids.geometry.SA_partial_cylindrical_body
documentation:
Calculates the partial area of a cylinder's body in the context of
a horizontal cylindrical vessel and liquid partially
filling it. This computes the wetted surface area of the bottom of the
cylinder.

$$
\text{SA} = L D \cos^{-1}\left(\frac{D - 2h}{D}\right)
$$

# Parameters

L

: Length of the cylinder, [m]

D

: Diameter of the cylinder, [m]

h

: Height measured from bottom of cylinder to liquid level, [m]

# Returns

SA_partial

: Partial (wetted) surface area, [m^2]

# Notes

This method is undefined for $h > D$. and $h < 0$, but those
cases are handled by returning the full surface area and the zero
respectively.

# Examples

```pycon
>>> SA_partial_cylindrical_body(L=200.0, D=96., h=22.0)
19168.852890279868
```

# References

[^footnote-1]: Weisstein, Eric W. "Circular Segment." Text. Wolfram Research, Inc.
    Accessed May 10, 2020. <https://mathworld.wolfram.com/CircularSegment.html>.

functions.name: fluids.geometry.A_partial_circle
documentation:
Calculates the partial area of a circle, in the context of the circle
being an end cap to a horizontal cylindrical vessel and liquid partially
filling it. This computes the wetted surface area of one of the end caps.

Multiply this by two to obtain the wetted area of two end caps.

$$
\text{SA} = R^2\cos^{-1}\frac{(R - h)}{R} - (R - h)\sqrt{(2Rh - h^2)}
$$

# Parameters

D

: Diameter of the circle, [m]

h

: Height measured from bottom of circle to liquid level, [m]

# Returns

SA_partial

: Partial (wetted) surface area, [m^2]

# Notes

This method is undefined for $h > D$ and $h < 0$, but those
cases are handled by returning the full surface area and the zero
respectively.

# Examples

```pycon
>>> A_partial_circle(D=96., h=22.0)
1251.2018147383194
```

# References

[^footnote-1]: Weisstein, Eric W. "Circular Segment." Text. Wolfram Research, Inc.
    Accessed May 10, 2020. <https://mathworld.wolfram.com/CircularSegment.html>.

functions.name: fluids.geometry.circle_segment_h_from_A
documentation:
Calculates the height of a chord of a circle given the area of that
circle segment. This is a numerical problem, solving the
following equation for `h`.

$$
\text{A} = R^2\cos^{-1}\frac{(R - h)}{R} - (R - h)\sqrt{(2Rh - h^2)}
$$

# Parameters

A

: Circle section area, [m^2]

D

: Diameter of the circle, [m]

# Returns

h

: Height measured from bottom of circle to the end of the circle section,
  [m]

# Notes

# Examples

```pycon
>>> circle_segment_h_from_A(A=1251.2018147383194, D=96.)
22.0
```

# References

[^footnote-1]: Weisstein, Eric W. "Circular Segment." Text. Wolfram Research, Inc.
    Accessed May 10, 2020. <https://mathworld.wolfram.com/CircularSegment.html>.

functions.name: fluids.geometry.SA_partial_horiz_conical_head
documentation:
